First ask yourself is it cyanotic or not? They will have to tell you this in the stem. Look for this in the stem every time, then cross out answers that are not cyanotic. Cyanotic Not Cyanotic TOF ASD TAPVR VSD Transposition PDA Truncus PAPVR Tricuspid Atresia Aortic Coarctation (adult type - post ductal) Example: Patient “X ” is a newborn cyanotic, what is the most likely Dx? A -V SD B- ASD C- Demonic Possession D -T O F Without even looking at a picture (which they will probably show), you know the answer is D, because that is the only cyanotic one listed. If you were wondering about C - 1 did a google scholar search for “Demonic Possession causing cyanosis ”, and although there were a few case reports none come down hard on cyanosis. 533

B attle Tactics: Neonatal Chest Lung Volume Symmetric (looks like pulmonary edema) HIGH Pattern Transient Tachypnea “Pulmonary Edema + Effusions” Less Symmetric Perihilar / Streaky Meconium Aspiration or Non GB Neonatal Pneumonia Pleural Effusions \ Beta Hemolytic Pneumonia “Diffuse Granular” Not Usually Surfactant- Deficient Disease (SDD) “Diffuse Granular” THIS vs THAT : Meconium Aspiration or Non Group B Strep Pneumonia This is super tough without any history, and because of that I feel like the test writer has two options: (1) Stop being an asshole and give you some history, (2) not include both as answer choices - assuming only one is correct. Now, along those lines if you saw both as choices and the question header gives you no history you could eliminate them both as distractors - because they both can’t be correct. Example: What color is this box A - B l u e B - Looks Red C - It has a Red Appearance \l. I Don’t See a BOX! How can I answer the question?!? There can be situations wliere tine picture is not necessary to get tiie question right. They could show you a picture of a grilled cheese sandwich and call it a chest x-ray It could still be possible to get the question right by eliminating all but one possible choice. The question header can help you disqualify (as we discussed with cyanotic heart disease) or you can try and find choices that cannot be distinguished from each other. Both answers can’t be right - so they must both be wrong. 534

Battle Tactics: Peds Chest & Misc Prematurity — Guessing that the kid is premature can be helpful for eliminating choices (Meconium Aspiration is more of a post term thing), and raising your pretest probability (SDD, or NEC in a belly film). There are two main clues: (1) Humeral Head Ossification - This tends to occur closer to term. If the humeral head is NOT ossified you can assume (in the world of multiple choice) that the kid is likely premature. (2) Lack of Subcutaneous Fat - Premature kids tend to be very skinny, although I think of this more of a soft sign that is useful when absent more than present. I’ll just say that if the kid appears chubby he is probably NOT premature. The Thing in the Lung: Left Upper Lobe: Think Congenital Lobar Emphysema (CLE) first. But, remember CCAM has no lobar prevalence, so it can be anywhere Left Lower Lobe: Think Sequestration First. Congenital Diaphragmatic Hernia (CDHs) favors this side too Case 1. Newborn with congenital heart disease Case 2. 10 year old with recurrent pneumonia A. Intralobar Sequestration A. Intralobar B. Extralobar Sequestration Sequestration B. Extralobar Sequestration C. Congenital Lobar C. Congenital Lobar Emphysema Emphysema Vntralobar is seen older kids, **Extralobar is seen in infants with co- morbids ** CLE is in the upper lobe 535

NG Tube Tricks: The presence of an NG tube (especially if not placed correctly) should alert you to some form of trickery. The NG tube stops in the upper thoracic esophagus: Think esophageal atresia (probably in the setting of VACTERL). The NG tube curling into the chest — it’s either (1) in the lung, or (2) it’s in a congenital diaphragmatic hernia. If I had to pick between the two (and it wasn’t obvious), I’d say left side hernia, right side lung - just because those are the more common sides. NG Tube Curls in the upper thoracic esophagus. First think Esophageal Atresia. Then think VACTERL The Classic Congenital Lobar Em physema Trick: They can show you a series of CXRs. The first one has an opacity in the lung (the affected lung is fluid filled). The next x-ray will show the opacity resolved. The following x-ray will show it getting more lucent, and more lucent. Until it’s actually pushing the heart over. This is the classic way to show it in case conference, or case books The School Aged CXR: Things to look for: • Big Heart - Probably showing you a sickle cell case. Look for bone infarcts in shoulders. • Lucent Lung - Think foreign Body (air trapping). Remember you put the affected side down (if it remains lucent- that confirms it). THIS vs THAT: Cystic Fibrosis vs Primary Ciliary Dyskinesia CF Abnormal Mucus - Ciha can’t clear it Bronchiectasis (upper lobes) PCD I Normal Mucus - Cilia don’t work Bronchicctasis (lower lobes) Normal sperm, obliterated vas deferens Noinial vas deferens, sperm carmot swim normally 536

The Mandible: There are only a few things that a mandible will be shown for with regards to Peds. Think Caffeys first - especially if the picture looks blurry and old (there hasn’t been a case of this in 50 years). If it’s osteonecrosis think about O.I. on bisphosphonates. If it’s a dwarf case, think wide angled mandible with Pyknodysostosis. A “floating tooth” could be EG. Cysts Hemangioma Focal Nodular Hyperplasia Hepatic Adenoma Hepatocellular Carcinoma Ultrasound Rapid Review Anechoic with well defined, thin walls. Single or multiple - less than 2 cm. Things to suggest maybe it's not a cyst and instead possibly a parasite (i.e. Hydatid)? • Bigger than 2 cm. • Presence of membranes. • Abundant sediment in cysts. Most common liver tumor. Classic: well defined, homogeneously echogenic. No intemal doppler flow (because it is a slow flow lesion). Will see posterior enhancement (because like a cyst, the blood is just slow flowing, so the signal passes through it quickly.) Things to suggest maybe it's not a hemangioma; • Intemal flow Mets Young woman - most classic Seen after chemotherapy treatment with oxaliplatin (chemotherapy for bowel cancer) Can occur with other lesions (most commonly hemangioma) Classic: Isoechoic to the liver and very difficult to see - so they must either show the lesion; • Bulging the liver, • Presence of a cen tral scar, or • Classic spoke wheel appearance on doppler. - Development is induced by intake of anabolic hormones and oral contraceptives. - Association: Glycogen storage diseases - Association: Being a fat ass with diabetes - Classic ; Round mass with well defined borders. : Hvpoechoic halo of fatty sparring. **Appearance is highly variably because of hemorrhage, fat, calcification, necrosis - Classic Doppler; perilesional increased flow (although this is also variable) - Basically anything with a shit ton of intemal flow or peripheral flow on Doppler (other than your typical spoke wheel pattern of FNH) you gotta be worried. - A thin peripheral halo of hypoechogenicity can represent either focal fatty sparing (in the case of an Adenoma) or... a fibrous capsule (target) as is often seen in HCC - so this should also be concerning. Multiple lesions - Especially if there is a halo (target sign). ~ 40% Hypoechoic ~ 30% Hyperechoic Note tne Uirrtiotic Liver Contour (that is a huge hint) 542

I I U S > Pulmonary Interstitial Emphysema (PIE) - put the bad side down Bronchial Foreign Body - put the lucency side down (if it stays that way, it’s positive) Papillomatosis has a small (2%) risk of squamous cell CA Pulmonary sling is the only variant that goes between the esophagus and the trachea. This is associated with trachea stenosis. Thymic Rebound - Seen after stress (chemotherapy) - Can be PET-Avid Lymphoma - Most common mediastinal mass in child (over 10) Anterior Mediastinal Mass with Calcification - Either treated lymphoma, or Thymic Lesion (lymphoma doesn’t calcify unless treated). Neuroblastoma is the most common posterior mediastinal mass in child under 2 (primary thoracic does better than abdo). Hypertrophic Pyloric Stenosis - NOT at birth, NOT after 3 months (3 weeks to 3 months ) Criteria for HPS - 4 mm and 14 mm (4mm single wall, 14mm length). Annular Pancreas presents as duodenal obstruction in children and pancreatitis in adults. Most common cause of bowel obstruction in child over 4 = Appendicitis Intussusception - 3 months to 3 years is ok, earlier or younger think lead point Gastroschisis is ALWAYS on the right side Omphalocele has associated anomalies (gastroschisis does not). Physiologic Gut Hernia normal at 6-8 weeks AFP is elevated with Hepatoblastoma Endothelial growth factor is elevated with Hemangioendothelioma Most Common cause of pancreatitis in a kid = Trauma (seatbelt) Weigert Meyer Rule - Duplicated ureter on top inserts inferior and medial Most common tumor of the fetus or infant - Sacrococcygeal Teratoma Most common cause of idiopathic scrotal edema - HSP Most common cause of acute scrotal pain age 7-14 - Torsion of Testicular Appendages Bell Clapper Deformity is the etiology for testicular torsion. SCFE is a Salter Harris Type 1 Physiologic Periostitis of the Newborn doesn’t occur in a newborn - seen around 3 months Acetabular Angle should be < 30, and Alpha angle should be more than 60. Most Common benign mucosal lesion of the esophagus = Papilloma Esophageal Webs have increased risk for cancer, and Plummer-Vinson Syndrome (anemia + web) Dysphagia Lusoria is from compression by a right subclavian artery (most patients with aberrant rights don’t have symptoms). Achalasia has an increased risk of squamous cell cancer (20 years later) Most common mesenchymal tumor of the GI tract = GIST Most common location for GIST = Stomach Abscesses are almost exclusively seen in Crohns (rather than UC) Nodes + UC = Common in the setting of active disease Nodes (larger than 1cm) + Crohns = Cancer 573

Diverticulosis + Nodes = Cancer (maybe) -> next step endoscopy. Krukenberg Tumor = Stomach (GI) met to the ovary Menetrier’s involves flindus and spares the antrum The stomach is the most common location for sarcoid (in the GI tract) Gastric Remnants have an increased risk of cancer years after Billroth Most common internal hernia = Left sided paraduodenal Most common site of peritoneal carcinomatosis = retrovesical space An injury to the bare area of the liver can cause a retroperitoneal bleed Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis associated with Ulcerative Colitis Extrahepatic ducts are nornial with Primary Biliary Cirrhosis Anti-mitochondrial Antibodies - positive with primary biliary cirrhosis Mirizzi Syndrome - the stone in the cystic duct obstructs the CBD Mirizzi has a 5x increased risk of GB cancer. The most common cause of a benign bile duct stricture is iatrogenic trauma during a lap cholecystectomy The most common treatment for a benign bile duct stricture caused by iatrogenic trauma during a lap cholecystectomy is endoscopic dilation Dorsal pancreatic agenesis - associated with diabetes and polysplenia Hereditary and Tropical Pancreatitis - early age of onset, increased risk of cancer Felty’s Syndrome - Big Spleen, RA, and Neutropenia Splenic Artery Aneurysm - more common in women, and more likely to rupture in pregnant women. Insulinoma is the most common islet cell tumor Gastrinoma is the most common islet cell tumor with MEN On T2, a normal liver should always have a signal higher than muscle. Chemotherapy is a classic cause of fatty liver In an adult, the most common cause of a persistent small bowel intussuception is a mucosal mass In a kid, the most common cause of a persistent small bowel intussuception is lymph node hypertrophy (usually from a prior viral infection) Ulcerative Colitis has an increased risk of colon cancer (if it involves colon past the splenic flexure). UC involving the rectum only does not increase risk of CA. Calcifications in a renal CA - are associated with an improved survival RCC bone mets are “always” lytic There is an increased risk of malignancy with dialysis Horseshoe kidneys are more susceptible to trauma Most common location for TCC is the bladder Second most common location for TCC is the upper urinary tract Upper Tract TCC is more commonly multifocal (12%) - as opposed to bladder (4%) The cysts in acquired renal cystic disease improve after renal transplant, although the risk of renal CA in the native kidney remains elevated. In fact, the cancers tend to be more aggressive because of the immunosuppressive therapy needed to not reject a transplant. Weigert Meyer Rule - Upper Pole inserts medial and inferior Ectopic Ureters are associated with incontinence in women (not men) Leukoplakia is pre-malignant; Malakoplakia is not pre-malignant Extraperitoneal bladder rupture is more common, and managed medically 574

Intraperitoneal bladder rupture is less common, and managed surgically Indinavir (HIV medication) stones are the only ones not seen on CT. Uric Acid stones are not seen on plain film Endometrial tissue in a rudimentary horn (even one that does NOT communicate) increases the risk of miscarriage Arcuate Uterus does NOT have an increased risk of infertility (it’s a normal variant) Fibroids with higher T2 signal respond better to UAE Hyaline Fibroid Degeneration is the most common subtype Adenomyosis - favors the posterior wall, spares the cervix Hereditary Non-Polyposis Colon Cancer (HNPCC) - have a 30-50x increased risk of endometrial cancer Tamoxifen increases the risk of endometrial cancer, and endometrial polyps Cervical Cancer that has parametrial involvement (2B) - is treated with chemo/radiation. Cervical Cancer without parametrial involvement (2A) - is treated with surgery Vaginal cancer in adults is usually squamous cell Vaginal Rhabdomyosarcoma occurs in children / teenagers Premenopausal ovaries can be hot on PET (depending on the phase of cycle). Post menopausal ovaries should Never be hot on PET. Transformation subtypes; Endometrioma = Clear Cell, Dermoid = Squamous Postpartum fever can be from ovarian vein thrombophlebitis You see an endometrioma on MRI. Next Step = Look for more endometriomas in the deep pelvis and along the c-section scar. The most common gynecological malignancy in the united states = Endometrial carcinoma Fractured penis = rupture of the corpus cavemosum and the surrounding tunica albuginea. Prostate Cancer is most commonly in the peripheral zone, - ADC dark BPH nodules are in the central zone Hypospadias is the most common association with prostatic utricle Seminal Vesicle cysts are associated with renal agenesis, and ectopic ureters Cryptorchidism increases the risk of cancer (in both testicles), and the risk is not reduced by orchiopexy Immunosuppressed patients can get testicular lymphoma -hiding behind blood testes barrier Most common cause of correctable infertility in a man is a varicocele. Undescended testicles are more common in premature kids. Membranes disrupted before 10 weeks, increased risk for amniotic bands The earliest visualization of the embryo is the “double bleb sign” Hematoma greater than 2/3 the circumference of the chorion has a 2x increased risk of abortion. Biparietal Diameter - Recorded at the level of the thalamus from the outermost edge of the near skull to the inner table of the far skull. Abdominal Circumference - does not include the subcutaneous soft tissues Abdominal Circumference is recorded at the the level of the junction of the umbilical v. and left portal v. Abdominal Circumference is the parameter classically involved with asymmetric lUGR Femur Length does NOT include the epiphysis Umbilical Artery Systolic / Diastolic Ratio should NOT exceed 3 at 34 weeks - makes you think pre- eclampsia and lUGR A full bladder can mimic a placenta previa Nuchal lucency is measured between 9-12 weeks, and should be < 3 mm. More than 3mm is associated with Downs. Lemon sign will disappear after 24 weeks Aqueductal Stenosis is the most common cause of non-communicating hydrocephalus in a neonate The tricuspid valve is the most anterior The pulmonic valve is the most superior There are 10 lung segments on the right, and 8 lung segments on the left If it goes above the clavicles, it’s in the posterior mediastinum (cervicothoracic sign) 575

Azygos Lobe has 4 layers of pleura Most common pulmonary vein variant is a separate vein draining the right middle lobe Most common cause of pneumonia in AIDS patient is Strep Pneumonia Most common opportunistic infection in AIDS = PCP. Aspergilloma is seen in a normal immune patient Invasive Aspergillus is seen in an immune compromised patient Lung Cancer Screening CTs have a minimum size thickness of 2.5mm (although most everyone says 1.5mm or thinner is preferred). AIDS + Bilateral Ground Glass Opacities + Thin Walled Cysts + Pneumothorax = PCP Prevascular Lymph nodes can NOT easily be reached by mediastinoscopy. Fleischner Society Recommendations do NOT apply to patient’s with known cancers The most common location for an echinococcal cyst is the liver The second most common site of involvement for echinococcus is the lung Eccentric calcifications in a solitary pulmonary nodule pattern is considered the most suspicious. A part solid nodule with a ground glass component is the most suspicious morphology you can have Most common early presentation of lung C A is a solitary nodule (right upper lobe) Lung Fibrosis patients (UIP, etc...) more commonly have lower lobe CA Stage 3B lung CA is unresectable (contralateral nodal involvement; ipsilateral or contralateral scalene or supraclavicular nodal involvement, tumor in different lobes). The most common cause of unilateral lymphangitic carcinomatosis is bronchogenic carcinoma lung cancer invading the lymphatics There is a 20 year latency between initial exposure and development of lung cancer or pleural mesothelioma Pleural efifiision is the earliest and most common finding with asbestosis exposure. Silicosis actually raises your risk of TB by about 3 fold. Nitrogen Dioxide exposure is “Silo Filler’s Disease,” gives you a pulmonary edema pattern. Reticular pattern in the posterior costophrenic angle is supposedly the first finding of UIP on CXR Sarcoidosis is the most common recurrent primary disease after lung transplant Pleural plaque of asbestosis typically spares the costophrenic angles. Pleural effusion is the most common manifestation of mets to the pleura. There is an association with mature teratomas and Klinefelter Syndrome. Injury close to the carina is going to cause a pneumomediastinum rather than a pneumothorax Hodgkin Lymphoma spreads in a contiguous fashion from the mediastinum and is most often unilateral. Non-Hodgkin Lymphoma is typically bilateral with associated abdominal lymphadenopathy MRI is superior for assessing superior sulcus tumors because you need to look at the brachial plexus. Leiomyoma is the most common benign esophageal tumor (most common in the distal third). Esophageal Leiomyomatosis may be associated with Alport’s Syndrome Bronchial / Tracheal injury must be evaluated with bronchoscopy If you say COP also say Eosinophilic Pneumonia If you say BAC also say lymphoma Bronchial Atresia is classically in the LUL Pericardial cysts MUST be simple, Bronchogenic cysts don’t have to be simple PAP follows a rule of I/3s post treatment; 1/3 gets better, 1/3 doesn’t, 1/3 progresses to fibrosis Dysphagia Lusoria presents later in life as atherosclerosis develops Carcinoid is COLD on PET Wegener’s is now called Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis - Wegener was a Nazi. Apparently he was not just a Nazi, he was a real asshole. I heard the guy used to take up tw'o parking spots at the grocery store on Sunday afternoons. The right atrium is defined by the I VC. The right ventricle is defined by the moderator band. The tricuspid papillary muscles insert on the septum (mitral ones do not). Lipomatous Hypertrophy of the Intra-Atrial Septum - can be PET Avid (it’s brown fat) LAD gives off diagonals 576

RCA gives off acute marginals LCX gives off obtuse marginals RCA perfuses SA and AV nodes (most of the time) Dominance is decided by which vessel gives off the posterior descending - it’s the right 85% LCA from the Right Coronary Cusp - always gets repaired RCA from the Left Coronary Cusp - repaired if symptoms Most common location of myocardial bridging is in the mid portion of the LAD. Coronary Artery Aneurysm - most common cause in adult = Atherosclerosis Coronary Artery Aneurysm - most common cause in child = Kawasaki Left Sided SVC empties into the coronary sinus Rheumatic heart disease is the most common cause of mitral stenosis Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension is the most common cause of tricuspid atresia. Most common vascular ring is the double aortic arch Most common congenital heart disease is a VSD Most common ASD is the Secundum Infracardiac TAPVR classically shown with pulmonary edema in a newborn “L” Transposition type is congenitally corrected (they are “L”ucky). “D” Transposition type is doomed. Truncus is associated with CATCH-22 (DiGeorge) Rib Notching from coarctation spares the 1st and 2nd Ribs Infarct with > 50% involvement is unlikely to recover function Microvascular Obstruction is NOT seen in chronic infarct Amyloid is the most common cause of restricted cardiomyopathy Primary amyloid can be seen in multiple myeloma Most common neoplasm to involve the cardiac valves = Fibroelastoma Most commonly the congenital absence of the pericardium is partial and involves the pericardium over the left atrium and adjacent pulmonary artery {the left atrial appendage is the most at risk to become strangulated). Glenn shunt - SVC to pulmonary artery (vein to artery) Blalock-Taussig Shunt - Subclavian Artery to Pulmonary Artery (artery - artery) Ross Procedure - Replaces aortic valve with pulmonic, and pulmonic with a graft (done for kids). Aliasing is common with Cardiac MRl. You can fix it by: (1) opening your FOV, (2) oversampling the frequency encoding direction, or (3) switching phase and frequency encoding directions. Giant Coronary Artery Aneurysms (> 8mm) don’t regress, and are associated with Mis. Wet Beriberi (thiamine def) can cause a dilated cardiomyopathy. Most common primary cardiac tumor in children = Rhabdomyoma. 2"d most common primary cardiac tumor in children = Fibroma Most common complication of MI is myocardial remodeling. Unroofed coronary sinus is associated with Persistent left SVC. Most common source of cardiac mets = Lung Cancer (lymphoma #2). PART 1: PHYSICS

I want to begin by talking about what exactly an “x-ray” is. That discussion will lead into a review of some high school science. Then we can pivot into how x-rays are produced and manipulated for the purpose of medical imaging. Throughout the chapter, I’ll make an effort to discuss how these topics can be tested. What is this “x-ray” I’ve heard so much about ? X-rays are a type of Electromagnetic Radiation” defined by a wavelength of a specific size (between 0.01 - 10 nm). What is this “Electromagnetic Radiation? ” This is basically a wave of energy that does not require a media to travel in (it can travel in a vacuum). Its’ velocity is fixed at 3 x 108 m/s Remember these from general chemistry? Velocity = Frequency x Wavelength Energy = Frequency x some constant “h” Because the velocity is fixed, frequency and wavelength have an inverse relationship. As one goes up, the other goes down. As these factors change, the quality of the wave changes. Depending on the frequency and wavelength - an electromagnetic wave has different qualities. For example, long wavelengths (low frequency) can carry radiant heat. Whereas shorter wavelengths (high frequency) can carry radio, tv, and radar signals. People who makes their living begging other people for money (grant writing) decided to arbitrarily pick certain ranges to have certain names. Increasing Frequency (Energy) Radiowaves Microwaves Infrared Visible Light UV X-Rays / Gamma Rays Non-Ionizing Ionizing 8

Medical imaging is going to use stuff from both ends of the spectrum. X-Rays and Gamma Rays are obvious (and ionizing), but don’t forget about Radio-waves, those things are used with MR. Testable Point = MRI does NOT utilize ionizing radiation (the RF pulse is NOT ionizing) WTF does “Ionizing” even mean ? As the frequency increases, the wave gets more energetic and will have the ability to remove electrons from an atom. This is called “ionizing radiation.” Since it takes 15eV of energy to remove an electron from an atom, a photon with energy greater than 15ev is ionizing (one with less will be non-ionizing). HARD vs SOFT: From time to time, you may hear shifty-eyed Physicists (or other people pretending to know what they are talking about) use the words “hard x-rays” and “soft x-rays.” Remember when I said x-rays are defined by having a wavelength from 0.01 - 10 nm ? You can drawn another line in the middle of that range and say any x-rays with a wavelength below 0.2-0.1 nm are going to be hard. Don’t forget: • Small Wavelength = High Energy These smaller wavelength “hard” x- rays are going to be the ones used in medical imaging. The lower energy (longer wavelength) x-rays are going to be the “soft ones.” Soft x-rays are the wimp x-rays. They have no practical value for imaging. If you tried to image with them they would never reach the target because they’d get annihilated in the air (absorption in air is typically at a distance of 2 nm). They don’t do any better in the body - with a similar absorption distance in water. This may seem like trivia (and it is) but I’m taking the time to talk about it because it illustrates an important point. You need a certain energy in your x-ray to make a diagnostic image. If the x-ray can’t get to the patient, and some of them can’t get through the body to the receptor, then you don’t make a picture. Very low energy “soft” x-rays are contributing nothing to the image, they are just adding dose. This concept is highly testable. “ Soft” - The Asshole - Low Energy - (Low Frequency, Long Wavelength) - Shit for making pictures - Just adds dose “Hard” - High Energy - (High Frequency, Short Wavelength) - Good for making pictures - Still adds dose, but also makes a picture 9

This vs That: X-Rays vs G Rays Both are used for medical origin stories. and both are ionizing - the difference lies in their Gamma Rays - This type of radiation turned Bruce Banner into the Incredible Hulk. Aside from creating giant green rage monsters, it is also the primary tool of the nuclear medicine imager. These rays originate from the nuclei of an atom. They are excess energy given off as an atom decays in an attempt to become more stable. More on this in the Nukes chapter. X-Rays - This type of radiation allows Superman to see what color Lois Lane’s underwear is (as long as she’s not standing behind a lead flower pot). X-Rays originate from interactions between fast-moving electrons and atoms. Gamma - Rays Emitted by Nucleus .'''T; ■' <Vv. LA T':- X - Rays Emitted by Electrons 10

Quiz Time - We said that X-rays and Gamma Rays can ionize stuff (remove elections from atoms). Is there anything else that Radiologists deal with that can cause ionization??? Answer Time - Yup... Alpha and Beta Particles. WTF are those? Alpha Particles - The Alpha Male of the particles. The particle is composed of two protons and two neutrons (same thing as a helium nucleus). As these things have two protons, their net charge is two. This heavy positive charge allows them to suck electrons up (electrons that are attracted to their Alpha male swagger). When these electrons are pulled off, ions get created. Nucleus Alpha Particle Alpha Particle Testable Trivia: • They can’t travel far (they are too buff - shitty cardio) • They can’t penetrate far (that’s what she said) • They can still fuck stuff up (hungry hungry hippos style) and that’s why they are used for treatment - not imaging. Beta Particles - This is an electron emitted from the nucleus. Why not just call it an electron? Because it came from the nucleus instead of an orbital. It’s a totally beta move. Real men don’t originate from the nucleus. These things are much faster and lighter than alpha particles. They can travel way faster and penetrate deeper. Beta Particle (Don’t call it an electron) Horny Electrons Attracted to Alpha Dog Alpha Particle 11

P rom ethean Dialogue on A tom ic S tru ctu re - A discourse on the nucleus, electron shells, and the habits o f tiny particles It is necessary to first have an understanding of atomic structure and the various movements of electrons before we move to a meaningful discussion about the interactions between tungsten targets and fast moving electrons. This topic can be confusing, so I’m going to take a little extra time to explain things. The level of depth in this discussion is not likely to be directly tested but it is my hope that an improved understanding of this process will make more testable sections easier to understand and make the likelihood of being tricked on a multiple choice question decrease. Let us begin first by thinking about the atom as having an organization similar to a solar system. Planets which orbit the sun. The sun being the nucleus, composed of protons and neutrons. The planets being the electrons. Yes... I realize modem theory on atomic structure disagrees with this simplification of the Bohr model, but for the purpose of multiple choice exams: atoms are organized like a solar system. The number of protons in a given atom is referred to as the ‘‘atomic number” and typically referred to as the letter Z. The total number of protons + neutrons is referred to as the “mass number” and typically referred to as the letter A. All atoms of a given element (carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, etc...) have the same atomic number “Z.” However, they may have different numbers of neutrons. This leads us to the introduction of “Isotopes.” Isotopes (which will be important when we discuss nuclear imaging) have the same number of protons “Z,” but have a different number o f neutrons and therefore different atomic mass “A.” Alright... now with those basics out of the way. We can pivot to a discussion of electron orbits and energy levels. The very mention of electron orbits typically gives general chemistry professors a raging hard-on. Personally, I never enjoyed the topic, but it is critical to understanding x-ray generation. So let us proceed once more unto the breach, dear friends, once more... As we discussed above, the simplest way to conceptualize an atom is a central positive nucleus with electrons orbiting in a circular orbit - similar to a solar system. However, unlike a solar system an “atomic system” has a specific order to how things can line up. Specifically, the “rules” state that you can only have 2 electrons in the inner most orbit, up to 8 in the second, 18 in the third, 32 in the fourth, and 50 in the fifth. Letters - K, L, M, N, O etc... etc... are used to designate these orbital levels. 1 12

“K Shell” = Hold 2 Electrons “L Shell” = Hold 8 Electrons “M Shell” = Hold 18 Electrons “Binding Force” -The attractive force between the negatively charged electrons and the positively charge protons acts as the glue which holds the atom together. One might say that this “binding force” surrounds us and penetrates us; it binds the galaxy together. The strength of this force is inversely proportional to the square of the distance between the nucleus and the electrons. In other words, a K shell electron has a larger binding force than an electron in the L or M shells. The direction of this force is pointed towards the nucleus (that is where the positive charge originates), just like the planets being pulled towards the sun. Bound Particles Always Have NEGATIVE Energy - An object in motion is said to have kinetic energy. Shifty-eyed Physicists have decided that kinetic energy must be described as positive. Bound objects (like an electron in a certain orbital shell) have negative potential energy. This is an important concept, because it helps you realize that energy must be added to these bound objects to bring them to zero. At zero they have reached the “binding energy” where they can break free of the previously desci The Closer to the Nucleus the Greater the Negative Potential Energy - The energy required to raise the electron to the “binding energy” level varies depending on the individual element and the level at which the electron is living. For example, in Tungsten atoms the K-shell energy is -69.5 keV, and the L shell energy is -10.2 keV. It is critical to understand that these are NEGATIVE energy values. In other words, to free the K shell electron (raising it to the binding energy) you would need to transfer it 69.5 keV of energy. To free the L shell electron you would only need to transfer it 10.2 keV. id “binding force.’ L Shell Electron -Lower Negative Potential -Higher Energy -Tungsten -10.2 keV K Shell Electron -Highest Negative Potential -Lowest Energy -Tungsten -69.5 keV 13

Electrons Live in a Marxists Society - An electron is only allowed to have the energy associated with the shell it lives in (no more, no less). In other words, the addition or subtraction of small quantities of energy are prohibited by the Peoples Republic of Tungsten. The application of this idea is super important because this is how x-ray production can occur. If an electron moves to a lower shell (example from L to K) the difference is energy must be given away to the state. “Characteristic” X-Ray Photon of 59.3 keV L-shell • L-shell Electrons live at -10.2 keV • K-shell Electrons live at -69.5 keV • L-shell Notices a Vacancy in the K-shell and is drawn in the direction of the binding force of the nucleus. NEGATIVE numbers. • The difference between the two must be paid off to the state as a photon (x-ray) of energy • (-10.2) - (-69.5) = 59.3 • L-shell electron moves • The L-shell electron has too to the K-shell much energy (-10.2 keV) to live in the K-shell -69.5 kev) and the Peoples Republic of Tungsten will not allow it. • Remember these are The difference in energy (and therefore characteristic energy of the emitted x-ray / photon) will depend on the atom involved, and the electron shells involved. L-shell Characteristic x-ray photon Characteristic x-ray photon 14

Putting it Together: Let us look at a series of events starting with a fast moving electron striking one of our inner shell electrons. The collision transfers enough energy to the electron to overcome the binding force. The electron leaves the solar system creating a vacancy. This vacancy is then filled with an outer shell electron. The movement creates an energy imbalance which is rectified by the emission of a characteristic x-ray photon. M' ©"* Electron moves in and strikes an inner shell electron (K Shell). It has enough energy to remove it (needs 69.5 keV for tungsten). Inner Shell Electron gets ejected, leaving a vacancy in the K Shell. An outer shell electron moves down to that prime real-estate K shell and a characteristic X-ray is released. That x-ray is then used in diagnostic imaging to diagnose a life threatening condition. Years later the same x-ray is blamed for causing cancer in a 90 year old by CNN in another expose against the dangers of medical imaging. A 3rd year medical student writes an abstract inspired by the CNN article generating a line on his CV. He brags constantly about the publication. That medical student one day becomes the editor in chief of the New England Journal of Medicine. He buys a custom license plate for his Honda Civic that says NEJM. A nurse who works at his family medicine clinic notices the plate and wonders who “Nejm” is. 15

- X-Ray Production - I want to first give a general overview of how x-rays are produced. Then I’ll go piece by piece through the various components and discuss the trivia. I’ll wrap it up with a more in depth look at the actual processes of x-ray production (which occurs at the target site). Thermionic Emissions of Electrons - An Overview: A “small” current is applied to a filament. The filament is typically made of Tungsten - because Tungsten has a very high melting temperature (it can tolerate a lot of heat). Sometimes Rhenium (10%) is added to keep the Tungsten from cracking during the repeated cycles of heating and cooling. This current is considered “small,” but it still makes the Tungsten really hot (over 2000 degrees). This extreme temperature means the electrons in the Tungsten filament are very energetic (like seriously fucking energetic) - so energetic they “boil off.” This “boiling off’ of electrons is referred to as “thermionic emission.” The filament is a cathode (negatively charged). This repels the electrons as they boil off. They fire down a vacuum sealed chamber towards a target (also usually made of Tungsten). The free electrons accelerate toward this target (on the anode) because of the potential difference between the cathode and anode. As they accelerate, they gain kinetic energy (keV). When the energetic electrons strike the Tungsten target, they lose their kinetic energy via 3 different methods (excitation, ionization, and radiative losses i.e. Bremsstrahlung). Excitation just makes heat. The other two processes can produce x-rays. I’ll get into the nuts of bolts of that in bit. First, let us go through the components in a little more detail. 16

Tube (Glass Enclosure / Envelope): This is a bubble around the anode and cathode. What’s the testable purpose o f the envelope? The primary purpose is to maintain a vacuum. Why do you need vacuum ? There are a couple of reasons actually. Probably the most important (testable) reason is that without the vacuum the electrons you are accelerating towards the anode would collide with gas molecules. Now, I’m sure at least one of you nerds is thinking to yourself - yeah but wouldn’t those interactions create secondary electrons which could produce even more interactions at the target. They might. In fact, if you spent some time in a radiology museum you might find some of these things called “gas x-ray tubes,” that were actually designed to take advantage of that. The reason this “gas tube” was abandoned is you lose control over your process. These secondary electrons will be of variable reduced speeds when striking the target, and that will result in a wide variation in the energy of the x-rays produced. The true purpose is to allow the amount and speed of the electrons to be controlled independently. Cathode: This is the negatively charged part of the tube. Why wouldn’t the test writer just call it the filament? Short Answer: Because they are fucking with you. It’s basically the filament. Long Answer: Technically, the cathode is the filament plus the wires which provide the voltage / amperage to heat the thing up, and the focusing cup. I will talk (write) briefly about the focusing cup, but I feel it’s probably a low yield concept. The filament is a much higher yield topic, so lets start the discussion with that. “The Filament” - This is a thin coiled wire of tungsten. When a current is placed through this wire it gets hotter than Vulcan’s dick (like 2000 degrees). Vulcan as in the ancient Roman God of Fire (not the star trek character). The extreme temperature conveys in the electrons enough energy to escape the metal and form a cloud of electrons. This electron cloud will hover closely to the filament and is called a “space charge. ” The space charge actually limits the emission of electrons. This phenomenon is called the “space charge effect. ” 17

“Space charge effect” - The general idea is that in a normal atom the charge is balanced (equal protons and electrons). Since you are “boiling” off electrons from tungsten atoms you are creating an imbalance. Now you have more protons than electrons and thus a net positive charge. This net positive charge in the filament is going to exert some “pull” on the electron cloud back onto itself. “Electron Cloud” As electrons continue to boil off the cloud reaches an equilibrium size (the number of electrons in the cloud is constant). As an electron is added to the cloud, one leaves for the anode. <a O < O But Prometheus?! How can the Tungsten filament have a slight positive charge? I thought the cathode was negatively charged ? The definition of the word cathode is actually somewhat confusing. In this situation the simplest way to think about the cathode is the place in the device where the electrons enter. The filament is the source of electrons, so this is where they enter the device. Electrons are negatively charged. The space charge (electron cloud) is negatively charged. Thus this is the negative end of the tube, and will help create a potential difference between the cathode and anode to drive electron flow towards the target. in A What is this “m. I " '? Il stands for “milliampere” - with “ampere” being the key word. An “ampere” is a unit a measurement used to describe “current” or the movement of electrons. In other words, it is a description of the QUANTITY of electrons moving from the cathode to the anode. Key Concepts: (1) “mA” is the current of the tube. This is a fancy way of describing the number of electrons per second moving from the filament to the target. (2) This relationship is direct. In other words, a mA of 100 has twice the electrons as a mA of 50. (3) The quantity (total number of) x-rays produced by the tube depends totally on the number of electrons that flow from the filament (cathode) to the target (anode). (4) kVp affects both the quality (ability to penetrate and energy) and the quantity (number of x-rays). mA and exposure time (mAs) affect only the quantity. **77/ talk more about this later in the chapter. 18

“Focusing Cup” - Electrons have negative charges. Negative charges repel each other. This mutual repulsion creates a spreading / separation. If nothing was done to correct this you’d end up with a big ugly splat on the target. For reasons that I’ll discuss later, this is suboptimal. The solution is a negatively charged metal “focusing cup” placed around the filament. What’s the testable purpose o f the focusing cup? The purpose is to help the electron beam strike the target in an acceptable size. Anode: The anode is the “target” of Tungsten that is assaulted by the stream of electrons. I briefly mentioned earlier in the chapter that when the energetic electrons strike the tungsten target, they lose their kinetic energy via 3 different methods (excitation, ionization, and radiative losses i.e. Bremsstrahlung). Of these processes, only about 1% of the electrons which strike the target will result in the production of x-rays. The rest of the energy is converted to heat (via excitation). This is a critical concept. Electrons on the target = heat. Even though Tungsten has a melting temperature of around 3300 C, the amount of heat generated at the anode can actually be a limiting point. Flere is the dilemma - you need a large focal area (target zone) to disperse the heat or you risk melting your anode. At the same time you need a small focal area to produce diagnostic quality radiographs. Solution 1: “Rotating Anode” - Anodes basically come in two flavors. There are stationary anodes made of a small plate (target) of Tungsten embedded in a larger block of some other metal (often Copper). Then you have rotating anodes. In this situation, you have a disc of tungsten which rotates super fast (like several thousand RPMs) while the tube is active. What’s the testable purpose o f a rotating anode? The purpose is to spread the heat produced over a larger surface area (prevent the anode from melting). Solution 2: “Line Focus Principal” - The second solution is to create a slope or angle in the target. This also allows for heat to be spread. The testable implications of this run deep because spatial resolution, the heel effect, and several other factors become involved. To understand these testable facts we need to dive a little deeper. 19

Focus on the Focal Spot: Placing an angle in the anode will allow for a greater surface area to disperse heat. However, this angle will alter the size of the “effective focal spot” - which has implications in spatial resolution. There is some terminology for the anatomy of this set up that is potentially testable. • Electron Stream: This is the crowd of electrons being pulled (pushed) towards the anode. • Actual Focal Spot: This is where the electrons actually land on the anode (the angled part) • Apparent (effective) Focal Spot: This is where the x-rays land on the patient. This defines the amount of blur (not the actual spot). Electron Stream Actual Focal Spot Apparent (Effective) Focal Spot So what is this “focal spot”? The focal spot is the area of electron bombardment (and x-ray production) on the anode. The big take-home point is that a smaller anode has better spatial resolution. The trade-off is that a smaller anode can’t disperse heat as well and can melt (so you deal with more heat limitation). As we discussed, this is addressed in two main ways: (1) angling the anode and (2) using a rotating anode. Testable Trivia: • Mammo uses a focal spot of 0.3 and 0.1 mm General X-ray uses a focal spot of 0.6 and 1.2 mm • Portable x-ray device often use a stationary anode (doesn’t rotate to dissipate heat). This limits their tube rating. 20

Changes in Angle / Line Focus Principal: The goal is the smallest focal spot possible (best resolution) - the trade-off is heat tolerance. “Line Focus Principal” - Here is the general idea.... You want a small effective focal spot (the spot on the patient). This is one of the rare situations where bigger is not better. The smaller the better (that’s not what she said) - because that yields better spatial resolution. You might think the way to achieve this would be to tighten up your actual focal spot (the spot where the electrons hit the target) to then make a smaller effective focal spot. This would actually work. The problem is you would bum up your target. You have to spread the pain around. That’s where this idea of the “Line Focus Principal” comes in. Someone smarter than me (and probably you) figured out that if you angle the target that it will keep your large actual focal spot (for the purpose of dispersing heat) but narrow up your effective focal spot. Critical (Testable) Trivia: Small Angle = Small Effective Focal Spot Smaller Effective Focal Relatively Larger Effective Focal Trade-offs (also highly testable): • If the target angle is made too small, then the x-ray beam might not be large enough to expose a standard image receptor at a typical 40 inch source-to-image distance. • The angle in the target creates a “heel effect” (I’ll explain what that means on the next page), just know that Steeper (smaller) Angle = Greater Heel Effect. This is sometimes called the “Heel Cut-Off’ 21

The Heel (Anode-Heel) Effect: Because x-rays on the anode side must pass through a greater thickness of the anode, you have a reduction in the intensity of these x-rays. In other words, the intensity of the x-ray beam is not uniform. Critical Concept 1: Smaller Angles = Worsening Heal Effect The steeper the angle, the more abrupt the change in intensity. Critical Concept 2: Cathode Side = Strong Side The cathode side will generate the more intense beam. This factor can be taken advantage of to create balanced penetration in tissues of variable thickness. Critical Concept 3: Larger FFD = Less Heel Effect The larger the Focus to Film Distance (distance between the tube and the film) the less heel effect. Less More At shorter distances variation (heel effect) is more pronounced Less Intensity More Intensity Critical Concept 4: Smaller Film = Less Heel Effect (assuming same FFD) The idea is that the central beam is the most uniform. A larger percentage of the central beam will contribute to the exposure if the film is smaller. 22

Rapid Review: Heel Effect is WORSE with • Smaller angles - “heel cut off’ • Smaller Focus to Film Distance • Larger Film Size (Field of View) — field is less uniform when spread out Another Way to Ask this - Heel Effect is IMPROVED with • Larger Anode Angle • Larger Focus to Film Distance • Smaller Film Size (Field of View)- field is more uniform in the center. Applying Concept 2 (Cathode Side = Strong Side) Mammo - This is used in mammo, where the thicker part of the breast / chest wall are aligned with the cathode ( “C”athode on the “C”hest wall). Cathode Anode Less Intense on Anode Side Nipple # More Intense on Cathode Side Chest Chest X-Ray - The classic way to show the heel effect is with a mammogram (cathode on the chest wall). However, there are other sneaky ways to show this principal. Proper Technique: -Anode - Cathode Oriented Vertically With the Cathode Down Shit Technique: -Anode - Cathode Oriented Horizontal Creates a unilateral lucent lung AP Thoracic Spine X-Ray - This is essentially the same concept as the chest x-ray. The soft tissues tend to be increased over the lower thoracic spine, so that is where you want the cathode side. n Anode = Upper Thoracic Spine Cathode = Lower Thoracic Spine. 23

OK, that concludes our slight tangent. Glance at this diagram and rapidly review the components o f the x-ray tube and then will will conclude the discussion by diving into a little more depth on the interactions that occur at the target to create the x-rays. Tungsten Target *note the angle Envelope *keeps the vacuum Tungsten Filament *Boiling Hot \ Focusing Cup X-Rays Produced At Variable Intensity Stronger Ones are Closer to the Cathode 24

W Excitation — ^ Heat Electron Collides with ^ Radiative Losses i.e. Tungsten Atom Bremsstrahlung P “General” X-Rays (at the target anode) ^ Ionization ------------^ Characteristic X-Rays Interactions at the Target (Actual X-Ray Production): Excitation - This is the dominant process and represents the vast majority of the interactions at the anode. This occurs when you have energy transfer from the incident particle to an orbital electron in the target. The transfer moves the electron to a high energy state, but not high enough to actually remove it from the shell. When it finally calms down (returns to a lower energy state) there is emission of heat - but no x-ray production. I think about this process as “wiggling but not moving.” The other two processes (ionization and Bremsstrahlung) can actually produce x-rays. Before I get into depth with those I want to discuss some general concepts on x-ray generation. Concept 1: X-rays are generated through the conversion o f energy. You are taking the kinetic energy of a fast moving electron and transferring it to the atoms of the tungsten target. Assuming the conditions are correct that transferred energy will then be converted into an x-ray. Concept 2: “Voltage” The easiest way to conceptualize voltage is water pressure. More water pressure means the water is pushed out of the pipe faster. More voltage means the power source (voltage generator) is exerting more pressure on the electrons through the electric circuit, making them move faster. In Radiology you see the term “kVp” a lot. That term is describing the PEAK kilo-voltage. It’s important to understand that not all the electrons are traveling at the same speed (the system is too complex to be totally uniform) but the fastest electrons in the stream will have the peak potential energy. If you set the kVp to 100 not every electron in the stream is at 100 kVp (the number describes the peak). What about keV ? Is that the same as kVp? No it is not. keV describes the energy of a single electron in the beam. If the kVp was set to 100, a few of the electrons in the stream will have a keV of 100, most will be at some value less than that. kVp describes the stream as a whole with the number selected being the peak. keV is a description of a single individual electron within that stream, of which you might have randomly selected out to describe. Concept 3: “Electrons strike the target with different energies. ” This is an extension of concept 2. The idea that the electron stream is not uniform in energy. If you set the device to kVp of 100 some will be at that peak energy, but the others will be at variable energies levels less than that. So, when they reach the target they will collide and transfer variable degrees of energy. 25

X-Ray Production - How Babies are Made There are two processes that can result in the production of x-ray photons via the interaction of high speed electrons and the target atoms. Process 1: Bremsstrahlung “Brems” (radiative losses) - Think about it like this. You are traveling across country and suddenly you are overwhelmed with the intense urgency to shit - code brown - impending massive diarrhea. This is a real emergency. So you pull off the highway and start looking for a gas station / fast food joint (preferably not a taco bell — as you suspect that was the genesis of your problem). The sense of urgency and desperation is building when you see a Hooters on your left. You slam on the brakes and cut across three lanes of traffic to make it into the parking lot. This process of “breaking” causes your car to lose kinetic energy. Now - for the parallel. You are a fast moving electron going in a relatively straight line. You see the nucleus full of protons. Even though your Dad told you to stay away from protons you just can’t resist them. So you slam on the breaks and curve towards the nucleus. This process of breaking causes you to lose kinetic energy. As the electrons slow down and approach the nucleus they have basically three responses. They can (1) strike the nucleus and give off maximum energy to the x-ray, (2) come close to the nucleus and give off medium energy to the x-ray, or (3) travel distant from the nucleus and give off a little energy to the x-ray. Most (80%) x-rays are produced via this process. The amount of bremsstrahlung interactions is proportional to the energy of the incoming charged particle and the atomic number Z of the absorber (larger Z = more). High Energy X-Ray Electron Travels Near Nucleus - Gives Off High Energy X-Ray Electron Travels Farther Away From the Nucleus - Gives Off Low Energy X-Ray 26

The Spectrum: As we discussed, there is a spectrum of options for the breaking electron (maximum, medium, low) depending on the interaction it has while curving at the nucleus. I think about this like our friend who needs to poop. He could make it all the way to the bathroom and shit in the toilet (best result). He could make it into the bathroom but fumble wildly with his zipper prior to shitting himself just outside the bathroom stall (medium result). He could make it into the parking lot but fumble wildly with his seatbelt prior to shitting himself in the car (low result). The pants pooping could occur at any point from the parking lot to the toilet. Because you have this spectrum of results you are going to produce a spectrum of x-ray energies that looks something like a door stop or hill when graphed on a multiple choice exam. The last point I want to make on this (for now) is that the highest achievable energy x-ray is dictated by the original energy of the electron - which is your kVp. You can’t produce an x-ray with a higher keV than your kVp. Testable Trivia: Max energy = Max kVp. Gamesmanship: “Highest Energy” vs “Lowest Energy” X-Ray. H: The Highest Energy X-ray leaving the tube will depend on the selected kVp. As above this sets the upper threshold. L: The Lowest Energy X-ray leaving the tube will depend on the placement of an x-ray filter (or low energy x-rays absorbed by the envelope - if no filter is used). This is because a spectrum of x-rays will be generated. Testable Trivia: Higher Z = More Bremsstrahlung (more protons to make the electrons “break”). Testable Trivia: In nuclear medicine, low Z materials (plastic) are used to shield Beta emitters (classical example = 90 Yttrium) in order to minimize bremsstrahlung production. Lead would make this much worse. 27

Process 2: Ionization - If the energy of the incident particle is enough to eject an electron then you have ionization. This ionization will then lead to one of two process (only one of which results in the generation of an x-ray) Characteristic Radiation (characteristic x- rays): Characteristic x-rays are “characteristic” because they depend on the binding energy of the shell involved, and this binding energy is different for different targets. So it is unique... or “characteristic.” Incident Electron Strikes K Shell Incident Electron and Former K Electrons cascade downward to fill lower Electron with enough energy to Shell Electron are expelled. shell vacancies. Excess energy produces x- knock it loose (at least 69.5) The atom is now ionized. rays which are characteristic of that transition You may recall I mentioned (page 13) that the K shell is the inner most shell, and therefore has the largest negative potential energy. Yes, there are lots of different letter named shells (L, M, N, etc...) but for the purpose of medical imaging nobody gives a shit about those. K Shell is where it’s at. When a vacancy in an inner shell is created, an electron in an outer shell promptly jumps in to fill the vacancy. Energy released in this process is equal to the difference in binding energies between the two shells. The energy may appear as an x-ray photon. Since the binding energies have exact characteristic values, the emitted x-rays carry exact and discrete energies and are called “characteristic x-rays.” The characteristic x-rays are depicted as sharp peaks over the Bremsstrahlung continuum. The peaks noted on the figure correspond to the energy difference between the inner vacated shell (secondary to the K shell electron being ejected upon collision with an incident high energy electron) and an outer shell which is vacated to fill the inner shell. More Meat on the Spectrum: Test writers love spectrum questions so I want to be complete in my discussion. I’ve diagramed out a spectrum of Tungsten x-ray production. Notice there are multiple little spikes. This is because not every event is an L —> K transition to fill that vacancy. You can have M —> K, N —-> K , etc... Remember that the difference between these two shells negative potential energies is going to translate to the energy of the produced x-rays. Also, remember that the energies of these x-rays are “characteristic” to the target element. K shell binding energies of Copper are different that Tungsten. So, this pattern could be thought of as a fingerprint for that element. keV 28 Characteristic X-Rays

Testable Point: The K-shell binding energy of tungsten is -69.5 keV. Gamesmanship: We said the binding energy of the K shell electron of Tungsten was -69.5 keV. So we need to transfer at least that much energy to liberate that electron and start the process of generating a characteristic x-ray. If we only had electrons with 69.0 keV we would not generate K-shell characteristic x-rays. A way to ask this would be to say “what percentage o f the x-ray spectrum will K-shell characteristic x-rays make when the tube is set to 69.0 kVp ? ” The answer would be zero. There are a few other ways to ask this same question by drawling spectrum diagrams, and I’ll cover those tricks along with other spectrum diagram related fuckery later in the chapter. kVp Manipulation Trivia: • Below 69.5 kVp = Zero K-Shell Characteristic X-Rays • Between 80-150 kVp = 10-25% K-Shell Characteristic X-Ray Contribution • Between 150-300 kVp = Progressive Decrease in Contribution • Above 300 kVp = Negligible Contribution K-shell electrons prefer an energy that is fairly close to the binding energy. In other words, a crazy high energy of like 300 kVp will actually make characteristic x-ray generation less likely. “Beam Intensity” —This is described as the number of x-rays multiplied by the energy. You will typically see this written as Roentgens per min (R/min). There are multiple factors that are going to contribute to this process including; mA, kVp, target material, and filtrations. You can imagine how easily this type of thing would lend itself to multiple choice. Potential Fuckery: “Beam Intensity” is NOT the same as “total number of x-rays.” Remember that beam intensity factors in the “pushing power” i.e. energy of the spectrum. Be very careful when you read factor manipulation questions for what terminology they are using. More on this later. 29

QUALITY and QUANTITY ~ Critical Concept ~ QUANTITY The total number of x-rays or area under the curve in a spectrum diagram. QUALITY ; The overall energy of the beam. Changes in average energy reflect changes in quality. How Does the Target Material Influence Quantity and Quality ? Short Answer: The larger the atomic number (Z) the more x-rays will be produced. Long Answer: An increase in atomic number only applies to Bremsstrahlung generated x-rays (which is still the majority of x-rays). Just think more protons, more pull means greater breaking / curving, more curving means more interactions, more interactions means more x-rays. A change in atomic number will effect the characteristic x-rays also but NOT the number produced. Instead changing the Z will change the various energies at the shell level (most importantly the K shell). Remember this is why characteristic x-rays are “characteristic.” Large Z = More Overall X-Rays via Bremsstrahlung. This is a change in Q U A N TITY . Large Z = Difference Characteristic Energy Peaks. No change in the amount produced. This is a change is Q U A LITY . Practice Target Change Example: I’ll discuss this more in the mammo chapter, but this is a good place to introduce the concept. Tungsten: This is the target used in general radiology. Tungsten has a relatively high Z (74). This high Z is why it is chosen (that and its’ high melting point). Because the Z is relatively high it is efficient at producing x-rays, but remember that Z only effects the QUANTITY of Bremsstrahlung x-rays. In fact, the Tungsten spectrum is always all Bremsstrahlung (with some variation at kVps in the 80-150 range). Molybdenum: This is a common target in mammography. “Moly” has a lower Z (42) which decreases the Bremsstrahlung contribution and in combination with the use of lower kVp increases the characteristic x-ray contribution. The K shell binding energy is 20 keV (vs 69.5 for Tungsten) - so the spectrum of characteristic peaks is much lower. This is done on purpose to maximize tissue contrast. 30

How Does the kVp Influence Quantity and Quality ? Remember the kVp is the maximum (or peak) energy of the electron stream. As this increases the average energy of the electron stream will go up as well. The result of this increase has three testable results: (1) The maximum x-ray energy will go up to match the kVp. (2) The average x-ray energy will go up - a change in QUALITY (3) The total number of x-rays produced will go up (more energy was available in those bremsstrahlung x-rays) - a change in QUANTITY. The beam intensity will increase as a square of kVp. Intensity is proportional to (kVp)2 There will NOT be a change in the position of the characteristic x-ray peaks with an increase in kVp. Although, if you decreased the kVp you could potentially drop below the necessary K- shell binding energy and lose them. How Does the mAs Influence Quantity and Quality ? Remember that the mA describes the number of moving electrons. In other words, it is a description of the QUANTITY of electrons moving from the cathode to the anode. This relationship is direct. In other words, a mA of 100 has twice the electrons as a mA of 50. The “s” at the end of mA“s” is a measurement of time. So, what is really being said with mAs is “there are ‘x’ amount of electrons moving towards an anode for a certain amount of time.” It is all about QUANTITY. mAs has not role in the average energy or peak energy of the spectrum. QUANTITY but NOT Quality. 15% Rule This is a described rule for discussing the relationship between kVp and mA. It goes like this: If you increase the kVp by 15%, you can decrease the mAby half and maintain the same x-ray density on the film. If you decrease the kVp by 15% you need to double the mA to maintain the same density on the fdm. I will discuss the application of this more in chapter 2. 31

How Does the Voltage Generator Influence Quantity and Quality ? We haven’t talked much about generators or generator waveforms because I believe this to be a very low yield topic. However, the concept of “voltage ripple” does lend itself to multiple choice because it is a factor that effects QUALITY and QUANTITY. Let’s rapidly discuss single phase vs three phase generators. Single Phase: This type of generator will produce a voltage that / \ r \ A starts at zero, peaks, then drops back to zero again. This cycle repeats throughout the exposure ( 0 - max - 0 - max - 0 - max ). kVp will follow the curve of the rainbow - with the “peak” at the peak of the rainbow. This is one of the reasons you have a spectrum of energies (poly) and not a uniform energy (mono). Three Phase: This type of generator will produce three separate repeating curves slightly out of phase with each other. The idea is to try and maintain a uniform peak output. There is still some variability - “ripple” - so it is not totally uniform. Overall, since the voltage produced remains higher throughout the exposure, the average electron energy will be increased. This is going to result in a change in quality (increase in the average x- ray energy), and quality (more energy electrons = more Bremsstrahlung). Single Phase vs Triple Phase Generator: Keeping the kVp constant, the Triple Phase will produce more x-rays, and produce a curve with a higher average energy, The peak of the curve will NOT change (as that is limited by kVp). Single Phase: More Ripple = Less QUANTITY and QUALITY. Triple Phase: Less Ripple = More QUANTITY and QUALITY. 32

We haven’t talked much about filters. That is not because it isn’t high yield. I just haven’t got to it yet. This is actually very high yield because the topic overlaps Quantity / Quality questions with Spectrum questions. The best way to discuss filters is via the concept of “H alf Value Layer. ” How Does the Addition of a Filter Influence Quantity and Quality ? ‘‘H alf Value Layer” is a measurement done to help understand beam quality. It is the amount of material required to attenuate an x-ray to 1/2 the original output. The higher the average photon energy the more penetrating it will be, and the larger its HVL. If the beam is filtered the wimpy low energy photons will be removed first (leaving the higher ones). This will increase the average photon energy. Since average photon energy determines penetration capacity this will increase the HVL. HVL of an x-ray beam does NOT depend on mAs HVL does depend on beam filtration HVL does depend on anode material (e.g. Tungsten) HVL does depend on kVp More Filtration = Higher HVL Less Filtration = Lower HVL Testable Trivia: With each HVL the average photon energy goes up. 3rd HVL > 2nd HVL > 1st HVL Lets look at some sneaky ways this can get graphed out: 1.5 mm Al Filter 2.0 mm Al Filter Average Energy Average Energy Shifting More Average Energy Shifting •Notice the average energy increases with more filtration - change in QUALITY •Notice the area under the curve is decreasing (you are losing x-rays) - change in QUANTITY Testable Trivia: A mono-energetic beam would have a higher HVL than a poly-energetic beam (at the same kVp). 10th HVL (“TVL”) : This is the thickness of material that can attenuate an x-ray to 90%. This is used for shielding calculations. No Filtration 33

QUALITY and QUANTITY - Trivia Summary Increased Target Atomic Number (Z) Increases Quality and Quantity Increased kVp Increases Quality and Quantity Increased mAs Increased Quantity Increased Voltage Ripple Decrease in Quantity and Quality Added Filtration Increased Quality, Decreased Quantity Testable Trivia Recap and Rapid Review • Electrons from a heated filament will be pushed / pulled towards a target. When they strike the target 3 basic things happen: (1) Heat Production, (2) Characteristic Interactions, and (3) Brems Interactions • “Characteristic Interactions” = Occur when an inner electron is forced out, and its’ spot is filled by an outer electron. The exchange results in release of potential energy in the form of a “characteristic photon.” This makes the spiky thing on the spectrum diagrams. • The energy of the characteristic photon is specific to the shell and the target. The classic -69.5 K shell Tungsten. • Brems interactions occur from “Breaking” or “Slowing Down” of the electrons around a target nucleus. It occurs in a spectrum - with different energy photons given off depending on high close the electron is to the nucleus (how much slowing down it does). This makes the fat mountain looking part on the spectrum diagrams. • The average Brems energy is equal to one third of the kVp selected. The peak energy is the kVp. • Quantity (total number of x-rays) is directly proportional to mAs. Double = Double. Quantity varies as the approximate square of the change in kVp. Double = Increased Quantity by 5 ish. Quantity decreases with distance - via the inverse square law (change as the inverse of the square of the distance). • Quality (penetrating power) is affected by (1) kVp -directly, and (2) Filtration. Quality is measuring in “half value thickness” hence penetrating power. • FIVE Factors will change the x-ray spectrum • (1) mA = Beam Quantity • (2) kVp = Beam Quality (and quantity) • (3) Filtration = Beam Quality (and quantity) • (4) Target Material = Changes Characteristic Peaks. Also increasing the atomic number of the target will increase x-ray quantity * one of the reasons Tungsten is used. Also the quality is changed or “shifted to the right” • (5) Generator Type = Improving the efficiency (less ripple) of the generator increases the x- ray beam quantity and quality. 34

- Misc Topics - A Collection o f Random Topics o f Uncertain Yield Auger Electrons: Remember we said that if the energy of the incident particle is enough to eject an electron then you have ionization. This ionization will then lead to one of two process (only one of which results in the generation of an x-ray). The x-ray generating process was the “characteristic x-ray” production process. The other process (the one that doesn’t make x-rays) pertains to Auger Electrons. If the energy released from the filling of an inner shell vacancy by an outer shell electron is imparted to another electron (instead of being emitted as a photon), which is then also ejected, the second ejected electron is referred to as an Auger electron. No x-rays are emitted in this process. In general, heavy elements (like Tungsten) are likely to emit x-rays and lighter elements (like biological tissues) are more likely to emit Auger electrons. Incident Particle Transferred Energy to Inner Orbital Electron and Ejects the Sucka ^ Instead of Releasing a Characteristic Radiation - Energy Is Given to an Outer Shell The Vacancy Attracts An Outer Electron To Drop This Energy Results in the Ejection of a second Electron - An “Auger” Electron Secondary Ionization: Secondary ionization results when the electron which is ejected from the atom has enough kinetic energy to cause additional ionization events (i.e. eject additional electrons). The ejected electrons are sometimes called “delta rays.” 38

More mA means more photons, or more photons on a crowded street. People (especially patients) are icky, and I certainly don’t want to stand next to them. So people / crowds tend to spread out. More spreading out or “blooming'’ = wider focal spot. Low mA High mA *Crowded Street ! A ? f t • A (2) Increasing the kVp. They tend to thin out (remember it as weight loss, or further away from each other). Low kVp (Walking) • • • A A A t f A A I High kVp (Running) 1 1 I--/ 39

Off-Focal Radiation: This is scatter from the anode outside the focal area. This leads to an increase in patient exposure and blurring. This can be reduced with a small lead collimator near the tube output. Tubes constructed with metal enclosures and/or with the anode at electric ground potential will have less off -focal radiation (metal envelope attracts the scattered electrons). X-Ray Tube Insert: X-ray tube is sealed under high vacuum. This vacuum is needed to prevent electrons from interacting with gas molecules. The x-ray port is necessary because x-rays are produces in all directions. Only the x-rays heading towards the patient are “useful.” The port is typically made of the same stuff as the housing (except with mammography in where it’s made of beryllium to minimize low energy x-ray absorption). Unwanted Radiation Vocab: • Leakage - X-rays that are transmitted though the housing • Secondary - Characteristic radiation that is made from electron interaction with materials other than the target (glass, housing, etc.). • Scattered - X rays that are deflected in direction once they leave the tube • Stray - The sum total of leakage and scatter Collimation: This is the process of restricting the size and shape of the x-ray beam emerging from the port. It’s done to reduce both primary and secondary radiation. It improves image quality (less fog). The tech can gauge how much they are collimating by using a beam of light reflected by a mirror of low x-ray attenuation that mimics the x-ray beam. By order of various regulatory bodies, the light field and x-ray field must closely align. Trivia'. The terms “beam restriction ” and “collimation ” are used interchangeable. They both means the same thing - decreasing the size of the projected radiation field. • Increasing Collimation = Decreasing Field Size • Decreasing Collimation = Increasing Field Size Grid Controlled: This has nothing to do with an actual grid. Instead it refers to a way to turn the tube on and off fast by using a little cup around the filament to “suck electrons out.” You are not actually shifting the big voltage across the tube, just diverting the electrons. This is how pulse fluoro is done (more on this in the fluoro chapter). 40

DEXA Scan Now is as good of a time as any to talk about the DEXA scan. DEXA or Dual Energy X-Ray Absorption is a technique that relies on the differences in transmission through different tissues at different energies. Let’s just look at it from an engineering standpoint - for the purpose of understanding the principals, then I’ll talk about what I think is testable. Basically you have two unknown quantities (1) Area of bone mineral, and (2) Area of soft tissue. It’s easy enough to calculate how much a single beam will be attenuated through the mass of both (you know what you put in, and you can measure what you got out). But how do you tell which was attenuated by bone, and which was attenuated by soft tissue? There were some ancient methods that I’m not going to talk about (submerging people in water, using gamma rays, etc..). The eventual solution was to use two different energies - one high and one low. The idea is that the bone is gonna block the low energy ones, but not the high energy ones. The soft tissue is gonna let them both through. Then you can do math to subtract out what is just bone. You make a bunch of measurements - average them out and come up with a density (BMD). Trivia: DEXA relies on transmission measurements made at two different photon energies. Two main methods for doing this: o Use a filter that drops the k-edge right in the middle of the spectrum - essentially separating the curve into two peaks - or two different energies (usually around 40 and 70). o Switch the tube voltage between low (70) and high (140) - placing the effecting average energy around 45 and 100. • Dose for a DEXA scan is VERY low - we are talking 0.001 mSv (for perspective, a spine x-ray is like 1.5 mSv) 41
