The most general idea is this: Collides with Atom Inside Person Scattered / Person f \ X-Ray Passes Straight Through Absorbed Deflected in a Random „ Direction X-Ray is Fucking Gone ► Impact on the Target ‘Noise” NO Impact on the Target The title of this chapter used to be called “Interactions with Matter.” I decided to change it because what “matter” really means is interactions with “people.” X-Rays can interact with people (matter) in 5 ways - only 3 of which occur in the range of energy used with diagnostic radiology. Occur at Dx Energy: • Classical (Coherent) • Compton • Photoelectric Occurs Way Above Dx Energy • Pair Production • Photodisintegration For completeness (and because I cannot exclude some serious fuckery) - I’m going mention all 5 - but focus on the ones that are more likely to be tested (Compton and Photoelectric). 44

Classical There are a bunch of names for this; “Classical” , “Coherent”, “Thomson” - remember that the one name you don’t remember will be the one they call it on the test. The situation is that your x-ray strikes an orbital electron and essentially bounces off - changing direction. technically it gets absorbed and re-emitted without a loss o f energy, but it’s easier to think about it just bouncing o ff and changing direction. The key point is these x-rays are low energy (less than 10 keV). Because they are low energy they don’t cause ionization (they can’t shake an electron loose). This is the most important thing to remember - the vast majority of these are absorbed by the body and DO NOT CONTRIBUTE SIGNIFICANTLY TO THE IMAGE, BUT DO ADD A SMALL AMOUNT OF DOSE TO THE PATIENT. Key Points: Does NOT result in ionization (no electron is liberated/ejected). Does NOT result in the net transfer of energy. Does NOT result in a contribution to the image Only adds a SMALL (TINY) amount of dose to the patient. • Seen primarily at low energies - i.e. mammography; wasting about 15% of photon interaction below 30 keV. Scattered X-Ray *No Energy Lost *No Ionization *Only a Change in Direction Incident Wimpy (Low Energy) X-Ray 45

Compton S cattering (“The Asshole”): Just so we are clear - this is highly highly testable. Unlike Classical Interaction (Coherent) which occurs at low energies, Compton scatter occurs at higher energies. The situation is that your x-ray strikes an outer shell electron. This interaction is strong enough to remove it from the shell (orbit). The incoming or “incident” x-ray loses some Incident X-Ray energy and is reflected in a new direction. This interaction causes 3 things: (1) It Frees an Electron (the one it knocked loose). This “compton electron” can go off and wreak havoc elsewhere in the body (2) It Ionizes an Atom (because it is missing an electron) (3) It deflects the incoming x-ray “Scattered Photon” What are the implications of this mess? Overall it’s bad. Pretty much all bad. The free electron can go off and undergo other interactions - none of which are meaningful for image creation and some of which could be harmful. The ionized atom will likely behave badly with the potential for biological harm to the patient. Lastly, the scattered photon may strike the image receptor in the wrong spot - and contribute zero helpful information. In fact it’s just creating a “fog” to the image. Bottom Line? • Compton = Bad (Straight out of Compton... those guys are trouble) • Compton Contributes to Dose, and Fogs Up the Image. Additional Testable Trivia: • Predominant interaction in the diagnostic energy range with soft tissue (100 keV and up) • Probability of Compton scatter does technically decrease with increased X-ray energy, although it occurs at all energies (in tissue) that arc used in medical imaging. • Compton interactions are the DOMINANT force contributing to SCATTER/FOG • Compton is the MAJOR SOURCE of OCCUPATIONAL Exposure - all the scatter inside the patient is being emitted all around - towards you / the tech/ etc... • Probability of a Compton scatter does NOT depend on the Z of the atom - because the energy of these outer shell electrons is low. • Probability of a Compton scatter is dependent on the density of the material - elements contain around the same amount of electrons per gram - regardless of their atomic number. • Hydrogenous materials have a higher probability for Compton scattering. “Compton” Electron Scattered Photon 46

Photoelectric In teractio n s (“T h e Good Guy”): Just so we are clear - this is highly highly testable. Unlike Classical Interaction (Coherent) which occurs at low energies (lOish), or Compton scatter which occurs at higher energies, Photoelectric occurs throughout the diagnostic range (20-120). BUT-and this is the major testable point - IT DOMINATES AT LOWER ENERGY (relative to Compton). The situation is that your x-ray strikes an inner shell electron. For the P.E. interaction to occur the energy of the x-ray must be equal (or greater) than the orbital shell binding energy. You have to have enough juice to let it loose. This interaction is strong enough to remove the electron from the shell (orbit). The incoming or “incident” x-ray loses all it’s energy and is totally absorbed. This interaction is referred to as “all or nothing” - either it can release that inner shell electron or it can’t. With this inner electron spot missing you get downward cascade and release of a characteristic x-ray or you get the production of an Auger electron. Because Auger electron production tends to dominate in biologic tissue (unlike Tungsten, a target with a high Z) you still end up with biologic damage from these free electrons. P.E. is still good because this actually contributes to image contrast (Compton just makes noise). P.E. Effect Yields: Inc (1) Characteristic Radiation (2) Negative Ion X-Ray (“photoelectron”) (3) Positive Ion (atom missing an electron) Ejected “Photo” - Electron 47

When does the P.E. Occur? • Energy of interaction - You have to have an energy higher (or equal) to the inner shell binding energy. Although (and I’ll talk about this later) the peak energy for P.E. is right around the binding energy for that shell. The probably of a P.E. interaction actually starts to decline after that — x-rays (incident photon “IP”) with a ton of juice just pass right through. The function is inverse to the third power and that is testable. • Atomic Number (Z) of the Absorber - Remember people aren’t all made out of the same shit. You have fat, water, bone, etc.. (Basically “Soft Tissue” and “Bone”). The higher the Z - the more likely you will have a photoelectric effect, and the change is significant - because it’s also cubed (to the third power). Why is this P.E. so important in Radiology? It is the fundamental method by which contrast is developed. For example, bone has a higher Z than soft tissue. Higher Z means more P.E. More P.E. means more absorbed photons, so fewer photons hitting the imaging receptor. The end result? Bones are white on an x-ray... it’s science. More critical than that is the smaller differences between stuff are amplified because it’s not 1:1 - it is cubed. So a small change in Z is amplified. This helps with soft tissue delineation. Using your practical understanding of bones being white on an x-ray, I’m going to try and help you remember the energy differences. Ever see an x-ray that’s “burnt out” or “over exposed” ? The reason it looks like shit is because the tech selected a kVp that was too high. Remember that photoelectric effect dominates at or around the binding energy of the inner shell electron - and above that it drops off rapidly. So, too much kVp = not enough P.E. and therefore not enough contrast (everything looks the same). Probability of P.E. (1) Minimal Incident Photon Energy: It has to be enough to free the K shell electron. For Iodine this would be around 33 keV. (2) “Matching ” Incident Photon Energy: Probability goes up when the binding energy and incident photon energy are about the same (assuming IP still > BE). Probability will drop off big time as IP > > > BE. • Probability o f PE. is inversely proportional to the IP Energy cubed: 1/ E3 (3) Z of the Atom: “Tighter bound” K electrons - i.e. those in higher Z material are more likely to undergo P.E. interactions. • Probability o f P.E. is directly proportional to the atomic number cubed'. Z3 48

Why Can’t Superman See What Color Lois Lane’s Underwear Are? In the 1978 classic Superman movie - Lois Lane interviews Superman on the roof top during which she challenges him to use his x-ray vision to tell her the color of her underwear, but because she is standing behind a lead flower pot he can’t see them. The reason he can’t see them is because Lead has a High Z. High Z = More Photoelectric Effect (more absorption). She then steps out from behind the pot... and he tells her they are pink. Then like a promiscuous girl she says “do you like pink?” Girl, I’m a freak you shouldn't say those things Concept o f the flower pot is the same reason shields, gowns, gloves etc... made to shield x-rays are made o f lead. It s all about the P.E. What about these Secondary X-Rays? You may have recognized that this P.E. process is the same process we talked about in the first chapter with high speed electrons striking K shell Tungsten and creating a cascade of characteristic x-rays. The difference here is that instead of using a high speed electron as our projectile and Tungsten as our target we are using x-ray photons and human tissue atoms (mostly carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, and calcium). So what about the characteristic x-rays that are produced during the P.E. interactions with human tissues? Remember that the energy of the generated x-ray photons is dependent on the K-shell binding energy ? Tungsten was around 70. Well, in the human body Calcium has the highest atomic number (of any element found in a relevant quantity). The K shell photon emitted from Calcium is 4 keV. That is the highest one. So, the Hydrogen, Oxygen, Carbon ones are all less than that. What I’m getting at here is these secondary x-rays are total pieces of shit. Those things are so wimpy they aren’t every going to make it to the film / detector (typically they are absorbed within millimeters of their origin). This is NOT the case with Barium and Iodine (contrast agents). Those higher Z elements can actually generate secondary x-rays with enough juice to leave the patient and fog the image. Shifty eyed PhDs will make a big deal about calling this “secondary radiation,” and not “scatter radiation” although clinically the distinction is meaningless since they both result in noise / fog. 49

The “K-Edge” - A Game of Shadows Attenuated K Edge - Spike Corresponds with ling Energy Higher Energy - Pass Right I want to start by defining a term that we throw around a lot - but often don’t think about what it actually means. “Contrast” What is this “Contrast” ? Contrast is the ability to tell that two things standing next to each other are different. The more contrast - the easier it is to tell them apart. For example, the white lion and the black lion - standing next to each other - the difference is obvious. Even if they were very small you’d be able to tell them apart. There is a high “contrast” between white compare that to the Black Lion vs the Dark Grey Lion. Yes it’s not as easy. There is less contrast between In Radiology we try and maximize contrast - so we can tell things apart. The easiest example would be the use of IV contrast or Oral Contrast. Both of these things have high Z’s - and therefore more photoelectric effect. Remember more RE. = more contrast. What you get is a white thing (contrast) next to darker things. Low Energy - Easily But the game is even more complicated than that. Remember when I said P.E. peaks right around the binding energy for that inner shell electron? That peak is called the “ K- Edge” and exploiting it is an important part of imaging (and more importantly - something that can be testable). This is taken advantage of by selecting a kVp between 65-90 when using contrast agents like barium (K edge 37 keV) and iodine (K edge 33 keV). But Prometheus Why 65-90??? Why wouldn’t you pick 37 or 33? Because the beam is “polychromatic” - with the kVp representing the peak, and a relative minority of photons. The average energy is going to be between 1/3 to 1/2 the maximum energy - placing the k edge right in the meat of the band - maximizing that spike in attenuation via the P.E. Photon Energy 50

P ho toelectric In teractio n s -It was the best o f times, It was the worst o f times- I want to spend a little more time talking about the pros and cons of P.E. interactions in medical imaging. “The Best o f Times ” Photoelectric interactions are great for producing crisp diagnostic images. Certainly this interaction is superior to Compton Scatter (the asshole) which just makes noise. The reason the P.E. interactions produce such high quality images is the amplification of natural contrast. The human body is made up of different tissues and the entire point of imaging is to try and separate out what is where. Since P.E. interactions depend on the Z of tissues, the absorption rates are going to vary. The differences in these absorptions generates “contrast.” This contrast is amplified by the third power of the atomic number - essentially creating a magnification of the differences between tissues. “The Worst o f Times” Dose. P.E. interactions are going to generate more dose that any other type of interaction. The reason is all the energy of that incident x-ray photon gets absorbed. People talk a mess of shit about Compton Scatter (the asshole) but only part of the energy gets absorbed by the patient in those interactions. To address this higher kVp settings are used. You still need / want some P.E. You don’t want scatter (which will fuck up the image), but you will tolerate some to keep the dose reasonable. Bottom line is that the general practice is to try and select the highest kVp possible that will still yield a diagnostic image. This is part of the balance between dose and diagnostic quality. Probability of P.E. is directly proportional to the atomic number cubed: Z3 51

Rule of 15 **Not to be confused with Darth Bane’s Rule of 2 Back in Chapter 1 - 1 briefly mentioned this thing called the “15% Rule of Thumb.” Basically, it says that X-Ray production increases in a linear direction with mA. Doubling mA will double intensity of the x-ray spectrum. BUT Increasing kVp by 15% will double the intensity of the spectrum. This is because kVp is increasing the amount of x-rays produced (via Brems) and the average energy. In other words - if you want to increase the x-ray exposure you can either (1) double your mAs or (2) increase the kVp by 15%. Why you might do one vs the other depends on the needs of the study. Don’t forget that the average energy of the x-ray spectrum defines the attenuation characteristics and defines the contrast between objects and tissue. Want to visualize low contrast objects / tissue ? - Keep the kVp constant and increase the mAs. You go too high on that kVp and you are going to lose all your RE. Want to lower dose but maintain a constant exposure ? Raise the kVp by 15%, then lower the mAs by 50%. *This works because the higher kVp x-rays will penetrate more easily and delivers a lower dose (*skin dose) - less P.E. interactions (remember we said they are worse for dose). The study is contrasted ? Remember you are going to want the kVp set at least twice the binding energy of the contrast agent being used. Iodine - K edge is 33 keV; so you want it set at least 66 kVp. Barium - K edge is 37 kev, so you want it set at least 74 kVp 3rd Grade Math (something doctors don’t know how to do... usually). If you wanted to increase the kVp by a certain percentage you need to multiply it by the number with a “1.0” in front of it. Example: kVp is 80. Want to increase by 15%. 80 x 1.15 = 92 If you want to decrease the kVp by a certain percentage you need to multiply if by the number you would add to the percent amount to make 1.0. Example: kVp is 80. Want to decrease by 15%. 80 x 0.85 = 68 52

Absorption Differential Absorption = The difference between the x-rays that get absorbed (via P.E. etc..) and those that pass right through. The word “differential” refers to the fact that not all body parts absorb equally (example: bone > lung). Vocab: • Transmission = The X-Rays that make it through the body and reach the imaging receptor • Absorption = The X-Rays that are attenuated by the body and don’t make it to Valhalla (the imaging receptor). Anything that increases P.E, increases absorption (and decreases transmission). Remember the big ones are, kVp near the k-edge, higher Z, more density. In other words - Attenuation in Tissue Depends on 3 things: • Effective Atomic Number in Tissue • X Ray Beam Quality (energy) • Tissue Density Linear Attenuation vs Mass Attenuation: • Linear: This is the actual fraction of photons interacting per unit thickness of an absorber. In other words, it is the fraction of photons removed from the x-ray in a certain distance factoring in effects from compton scatter, PE effect, and coherent scatter. In contrast to “mass attenuation,” the linear attenuation of ice, water, and water vapor is different (they have different lengths for the same amount of molecules). Factors to consider * More attenuation occurs with denser object * More attenuation occurs with higher Z material * Lower attenuation occurs with higher kVp * Higher attenuation occurs at K-edge • Mass: This is the fraction of photons interacting - scaled per gram of tissue. This is supposed to reflect the attenuation. The important point is that the mass attenuation of ice, water, and water vapor is the same. 55

Linear Attenuation and Half Value Layer People will sometimes describe the relationship between these two things. It’s fairly intuitive but I’ll walk you through it real fast. Scenario 1: You have a high energy photon • The photon has lots of energy and will penetrate further. • Further Penetration means you have “less attenuation” - remember lower attenuation at higher kVP. • The half value layer - which is a term for the amount of material required to attenuate an x-ray to 1/2 the original output, will need to be increased. ^ ENERGY (kVp) ■ Linear W Attenuation j Coefficient A Half Value Layer Scenario 2: You have a super dense tissue • More Dense Tissue = More Collisions • More Collisions = More Rapid Attenuation • More Rapid Attenuation = Don’t Need as Thick of a Layer ^ Density A Linear A Attenuation Coefficient X Half Value j Layer 56

A pplied A bsorption T h eo ry It is important to realize that a transmitted photon (one that makes it through the patient onto the film / detector) is just as important as an attenuated photon (on that got blocked). For example, if every photon made it through the film would be uniformly black. If every photon got blocked you’d have a totally white film. You need a mix of both to make a diagnostic image. Let’s imagine we (the techs) are taking an x-ray of a 400 pound Diabetic’s stinky foot. That smelly sloppy foot is going to be composed primarily of infected osteomyelitis ridden bones, soft tissue (atrophic fatty infiltrated neuropathic muscles, actual fat, water, etc...) and a candy com that has been lodged between the 2nd and 3rd toe. The point of this exercise is to look at how attenuation changes between tissues are various keV. Behold the follow charts: c9? a> o O c o •+—' 05 3 C (D 05<1)C keV The lower the energy the greater the contribution of P.E. Higher energies are dominated by Compton interactions. keV At a low keV you can see the contrast between the two is greatest. As keV increases the attenuation difference between soft tissue and bone become less and less. So, you are thinking - obviously you’d want a low keV to maximize your contrast. But wait... remember at lower diagnostic energies the P.E. effect is going to predominate, and we said that this interaction deposits more dose to the patient (more absorbed energy). You can now see the need for balance between maximizing diagnostic quality and controlling dose to the patient. Thus a slightly higher keV would be selected as a compromise. We could make the problem more complex by trying to differentiate water and fat. With tissues that similar you’d need to rely heavily on the P.E. effect, and to maximize this you’d need to use lower energy. More on this problem in the mammo chapter. 57

V ariables and Entrance Skin Dose Entrance Skin Dose (ESD) is a term used to describe the radiation absorbed by the skin as the bean strikes the patient. Absorbed is a key concept here, and something we will continue to discuss later in the text - I just thought this was a good place to sneak in this concept since we already discussed a lot of the factors involved. Speaking of those factors, quick review of the variables that will determine how much radiation gets blasted into the skin of the innocent patient by the reckless and cruel Radiology department. Factors that determine ESD: •Tube Current (mA) - Proportion Relationship •Time of Exposure (“s”) - Proportional Relationship •Peak Kilovoltage (kVp) - Square •Distance - Regulated for the inverse square law (double the distance, dose reduced by 4) As above, the ESD will change as the square of the change in kVp. More kVp = higher average energy and therefore more penetration. As we have discussed, this higher kVp could allow for the use of a lower mA or reduced time of exposure “s’ - making those modifications could actually reduce dose to the patient. This kind of complex thinking can get you into trouble on the exam. Should I assume that the mA has been reduced??? N o... never assume. It is bad manners. If you are lucky, the question writer will give the qualifier “all variables held constant.” Now let us take a look at some possible math problems using this concept. Warning! This will require 9th Grade M ath... so pucker your assholes (Doctors almost always suck at math, otherwise they’d be engineers. Engineers hate memorizing and repeating trivia — Doctors are great at that). Changing mA (direct proportion) Ii = mAsi h = mAs2 I f the dose is 28 mR at 25 mAs - what will the dose be at 35 mAs ? 28 mR = 25 mAs First Cross Multiply: 28 x 35 = ? x 25 ? = 35 mAs Then divide to isolate the variable: 28 x 35 / 25 = ? You should get 39.2 mR 58

Changing the kVp (squared - relationship) 1, - (kVp,)2 h = (kVp2)2 If the dose is 20 mR at 75 kVp, what will the dose be at 125 kVp ? 20 mR = (75 kvP)2 First Cross Multiply: 20 x 1252 = ? x 752 ? = (125)2 Then divide to isolate the variable: 20 x 1252 / 752 = ? You should be 55.6 mR Is this your homework, Larry? 59

CHAPTER I: © IN IIR M I ° i A T C O N C E P T ! - PROMETHEUS LIONHART, M.D. - In this chapter I’m going to fill in some knowledge gaps regarding general x-ray stuff (what makes the image good vs crap). There are 4 main image characteristics people talk about. (1) Noise, (2) Spatial Resolution, (3) Contrast, and (4) Brightness. I’m gonna cover those, and related trivia. Old people hate a lot of things - including (but not limited too) “Change” , Vegans, Popular Music, Black Presidents, the Internet, Cold Weather, Gay People (except Ellen - everyone loves Ellen), and “NOISE !!!” Radiologist Young and Old also hate “Noise”. So what is this “Noise” ? Noise is basically data that contributes nothing useful to the image, and serves only as a distraction. “Quantum Noise ” is the more fancy term is the term / concern for digital imaging. Think of it as background randomness. The piece of paper you are drawling on is not clean and white, it’s got some background smudges on it. If you draw with a thin pencil you’ll notice the background dirt a lot more than if you draw with a thick magic marker. The key point to know is that this occurs as a function of the number of photons hitting the imaging receptor. • More photons (thick marker) = More Signal relative to Noise • Less Photons (thin pencil) = Low Signal relative to Noise That background variation (noise) is always there - you just drown it out with a heavier exposure. Bottom line = anything that increases the number of photons on the imaging receptor will cut down on the “mottle” and therefore reduce noise. Analogy: You are in the waiting room trying to talk to a patient. In the waiting room there is a TV and Fox News is playing very loud. To make sure your patient can hear you, you speak very loud (you increased the signal, background noise is constant). The patient is a nervous little guy and he whispers when he talks (low signal, background noise is constant). You have trouble hearing him. (1) Noise 60

“Quantum Mottle” - A very popular term thrown around by people pretending to be smart / know physics. Basic concept is that the x-ray beam is not perfectly uniform and at low doses you notice it more. To use another analogy think about rain on a sidewalk. When it first starts to rain the few drops on the sidewalk might appear random, but once the downpour starts the sidewalk is saturated and you’d swear the rain is coming down in a totally homogenous pattern (even though its not really - but almost). Scatter - Remember the definition of “noise” is data that does not contribute to the image. So not all “noise” is quantum mottle (although that is the major contributor). Let us discuss further... for the purpose of multiple choice. The vast majority of the scatter is via Compton interactions. Essentially no characteristic radiation is going to make the film because it is so low energy. The only exception would be the characteristic radiation generated from heavier elements like Iodinated contrast and Barium. What determines the about o f scatter? There are 3 primary kVp: As discussed in the prior chapter, RE. interactions dominate at lower energy levels and Compton scatter plays a much large role in the higher diagnostic ranges {above 26 kVp in soft tissue, and above 35 kVp in bone). Higher kVp — More Scatter Field of View: A smaller field of view will irradiate a smaller volume of tissue and therefore create less scatter interactions. Additionally, a smaller FOV will have a larger “angle of escape.” The angle of escape basically refers to the idea that scatter photons can exit in any direction, but only the ones that land on the film matter. So, smaller film size (or FOV) smaller target for the scatter to land on. Thickness: Similar to the field size, a thicker body part will have more irradiated tissue and therefore more Compton scatter interactions. factors. Scatter is Maximized With: - High kVp Technique - Large Field of View - Thick Parts (or People) Vocab “Radiographic Density” Also called “optical density”, “photographic density”, or “film density” by various geriatric practitioners. This is essentially a description of how dark the film is. More x-rays on the film = darker = more radiographic density. 61

Noise Reduction - Part A - “Collimation” -Dealing with Scatter- Collimators are a type of x-ray beam restriction device that is attached to the housing of the x-ray tube. Beam Restriction or Collimation is done to limit patient exposure and reduce scatter. If you increase collimation you decrease field size, and if you decrease collimation you increase field size. Increased Field Size (decreased collimation) decreases quantum noise — although dose and scatter are increased. To compensate for the overall decreased photons hitting the image receptor (decreased signal to quantum noise ratio) when you collimate - the mAs is typically increased. For the purpose o f multiple choice: Collimation decreases noise. 62

Noise Reduction - Part B - “Grid” -Dealing with Scatter- Grids are used to improve contrast (by reducing scatter). Scatter is a “violation” of the most basic law of radiography, because it doesn’t originate in a straight line from the focal spot. Instead it is the result of Compton scatter in the body (which will squirt out like a wild fireman’s hose or radiate like light bulb). Off Angle How does the Grid work? A grid is a v Scatter Is device that has multiple thin metallic 4 Blocked (usually lead) strips with interspersed radiolucent spaces (holes). It is placed behind the patient and in front of the image receptor (I.R.). The primary action of the grid is to block off angle (presumed scattered) x-rays, and allow the ones the are coming straight in to contact the image receptor. Patient Grid ll.R . Grid Ratio: This is a term to describe how dense the grid is. It’s basically the ratio of the height of the lead to the distance between them. The higher the ratio the less scatter and the better the contrast. What does the Grid do to Dose ? The tradeoff to using a grid is that dose is increased (because the automatic brightness control turns up the juice to compensate). A higher grid ratio will create a larger increase in dose. There is a thing called the “bucky factor” which is basically the mAs required with the grid / mAs without the grid. “Bucky Grids ” - This is a moving grid. It wiggles back and forth rapidly - too fast to be seen. If the grid motion fails (and it stops moving) you will end up with grid lines. Grid Cut Off: This is a possible “risk” of using a grid. Essentially what happens is that you block so many photons that you cause a quantum mottle - or noisy image. This is classically described as happening when the grid is aligned incorrectly (put on upside-down, off level, off center, or off focus / at a bad source to image distance). Air Gap Technique — There is another method similar to a grid that involves separating the patient from the film / receptor. In this strategy scatter radiation decreases not from filtration but from the radiation missing the target. More on this in the mammo chapter. Dose Increases (1) Using a Grid (as opposed to not using a Grid) (2) Using a Higher Grid Ratio 63

mA and kVp: This is where the money is. Quantum Mottle’s mortal enemy is more photons. Less quantum mottle = less noise. So logically increasing both the mA and kVp would decrease mottle - but there is some confusion and some potential trickery. If given the choice between increasing mA or increasing kVp to decrease noise you should choice increase mA. Why not kVp ??? I thought there was a rule of 15% and all that bullshit. Yes... increasing kVp does increase the exposure more the mA - pound for pound, BUT if you go too high you will shift over into Compton scatter territory and actually create more nose via scatter. So technically increased kVp would probably decrease mottle - but may increase overall noise. You can see how this would lead to a very poorly written multiple choice question. • Not enough mAs = Quantum Mottle • Excessive kVp = Excessive Scatter Radiation and a loss of contrast. Testable Trivia: Noise will increase as the distance between the tube and detector increase, with the increase in noise described by the inverse square law. Noise Reduction - Part C - “Increasing Dose” -Dealing with Quantum Mottle- What is this “inverse square law ” ? Essentially this says that energy twice as far from the source is spread out over four times the area (in other words l/4th the intensity). Signal to Noise Ratio (SNR) This is a quantitative method for comparing the signal (stuff that contributes to the image) vs the noise (stuff that doesn’t contribute to the image). If your signal is really strong - you can tolerate more noise. If your signal is weak - even a little bit of noise can wreck your image. Sometimes the term "contrast to noise ratio” is used with a similar meaning. This is how people typically talk about noise. If you say I’m improving/reducing the “noise” - you are really improving the SNR. It’s a ratio. Things like higher kVp may increase scatter - but do also improve overall SNR. I’ll talk more about this in later chapters. 64

Gamesmanship Summary: If the question is - "which of the following will reduce noise ?” All of the following would be correct: • Use a Grid • Use an AirGap (this works like a grid — it’s mostly a mammo thing) • Increase the mA If all the choices seem correct and you have to pick just one - I’d pick increase the mA. How could that happen? If your text writer is using noise and mottle (photon starvation) interchangeable. Really noise should include scatter - but there is no guarantee that the people writing the questions actually understand physics. Be weary of (1) Questions about field size / collimation questions, because that mixes the results. Collimation reduces scatter, but it also reduces photons hitting the target. Typically there is an automatic increase in mA with collimation so your signal to noise ratio improves - that’s why collimation is good (plus the reduced dose). But there is the potential for mind reading / fuckery - because if you don’t increase the mA although you may reduce scatter the overall SNR may worse secondary to mottle. Just be careful - read the wording of the question carefully. Be weary of (2) Any answer choices asking you to reduce noise with post processing - unless you thinks it’s talking about iterative reconstruction technique - in which case they better say it. Just be careful, it seems like this could be a trap. “It’s a trap!” - Admiral Gial Ackbar If the question mentions a grid - and you suspect a “grid cut off” - the next step is to reposition the grid. Don’t immediately jump to increasing the mA. Remember most grid cut offs are from incorrect placement of the grid. “Slap the tech around” - would also be a good option - if that’s available. It’s second order, assuming you know the tech put the grid on wrong. If the question mentions increasing (or decreasing) the distance between source and detector, remember this relationship is not 1:1 - it’s governed by the inverse square law - as described on the prior page. 65

(2) Spatial Resolution What is this “spatial resolution ? ” Think about this as how close two lines can be to each other and still be visibly resolved as separate things. This is often quantified with “line pairs per mm.” You see that thrown around mammo MQSA all the time. actually 2 things W hat is this “spatial frequency ? ” For all intensive purposes just consider this the same thing as spatial resolution W hat is this “unsharpness ? ” For all intensive purposes “unsharpness” means a loss of spatial resolution. PhDs describe 3 subtypes of unsharpness (1) Motion Unsharpness - This is basically loss of spatial resolution that is attributable to patient m otion. A good way to ask questions about this would be to say “how do you decrease motion unsharpness” or address motion artifact? Good Answers would be (a) To say something like “Sir!! Hold Still!! Yes I know you’re leg is broken - but you don’t see me screaming!” (b) Tie his ass down, (c) Decrease the exposure time. Less “s” on the mAs. (2) System Unsharpness - 1 prefer to blame someone anytime something goes wrong. If I was an academic I would blame the resident / fellow. Since I’m not an academic I usually blame the tech or the nurse (for farts I usually blame the dog). In this case system unsharpness is the fault o f the detector. Every detector type has a limiting factor which determines the maximum spatial resolution. • Film = Size of the Grain of Photographic Chemical • Computed Radiography (CR) = Size of the Laser used to read the phosphor plate in the cassette reader • Digital Radiography (DR) = Size of the individual thermoluminescent transistor. (3) Geometric Unsharpness - This is caused by geometry. *More on the next page. 66

Geom etric Relationship - Influences Perform ance: Geometric Unsharpness: This occurs because of several factors: • Focal Spot: Small Focal Spot = Less Blur • Source-Object Distance: Closer the source is to the image = More Blur • Object Detector Distance: Closer the object is to the detector = Less Blur • Magnification: More Magnification = More Blur Below is a diagram showing how a change in focal spot will change the size of geometric unsharpness. As you can see, a smaller focal spot has less “unsharpness.” Focal Spot Source to Object Distance Object to Detector Distance Geometric Unsharpness Focal Spot Geometric Unsharpness Strategy: As a practical point of advice I’ve found it much much much easier to simply remember how to draw this type of diagram than to remember the working parts. If you can draw this thing there is a very good chance, no matter what demented mind was involved in writing the question, if it pertains to the material you can figure it out. 67

Now let’s look at some different distances. If you are asked about “source to object”, or “object to detector” distances again I recommend you draw them out. If you can draw it, it will save some room in your brain for memorizing stuff that really maters (like the dose for temporary epilation, or how often you do the “flood test”). Focal Spot Source to Object Distance Object to Detector Distance Focal Spot Geometric Unsharpness Geometric Unsharpness Magnification: Let’s take another look at that diagram. Notice the magnification difference between when you change the source to object distance? Magnification: Increases when: • The patient is far away from the detector. “Greater Object to Detector Distance” • The source is closer to the patient. “Less Source to Object Distance” M agnification is calculated by: “ SID” over “ SOD” H _ SOD J Source to Detector (Image) Distance [>—SID Magnification Factor: Source to Object (Patient) Distance *More on Magnification in the Mammo & Fluoro Chapter 68

Modulation Transfer Function (MTF) Sharpness and Resolution are two terms that are often used synonymously but are slightly different. “Sharpness” describes the ability of the x-ray system to define an edge. Resolution describes the ability of the x-ray system to differentiate two closely approximated things. Modulation Transfer Function (MTF) describes the relationship between sharpness and resolution. This is a confusing and often poorly taught subject that simultaneous seems to be high yield on multiple choice exams. I will now give my best effort to help you understand this M F’r (Modulation Function). Let us first try and understand what “MTF” is actually describing. Imagine you have a jar of peanut butter and you want to know how much peanut butter is in the jar because that will help you budget not starving to death on your resident salary. You look on the label but it’s in grams and because you are a real American you don’t use the fucking metric system - take that shit back to Canada or France. That label should say ounces! So you are forced to “transfer” the peanut butter one teaspoon at a time into another jar which is properly labeled in ounces - like peanut butter should be. At the end of the transfer there will still be a small amount of peanut butter stuck to the sides of the jar. So, when you count up the total number of ounces in the second container the number you get will be slightly less than the original amount of peanut butter you started with. Your “transfer” has been “modulated. ” If you were going to describe this as a mathematical “function ” you might say Peanut Butter in the First Jar (labeled in grams) MTF (peanut butter) — ------------------------------------------- Peanut Butter in the Second Jar (labeled properly in ounces) You would get some ratio less than 1. You could never get a number greater than 1.0 because you couldn’t end up with more peanut butter than you started. A perfectly disciplined peanut butter transfer would land you at exactly 1 .0 , but we both know that is just not possible (you gotta lick those fingers). Now - in Radiology the transfer we are talking about is the original / perfect x-ray data that has exited the patient to the product that the digital detector / film has produced. Just like the peanut butter transfer the data is never perfectly replicated ( 1 .0 ). Information Recorded MTF (image) = ------------------------------------------- Information Available 69

Modulation Transfer Function (MTF) - Continued 1.0 MTF Spatial Resolution (Frequency) You will see curves that look like this in various radiology text books. An in-depth discussion of the math and physics required to development that curve is completely beyond the scope of the exam, and honestly maybe 2-3 people reading this would understand it. It’s probably better to just say this: Q: Why does the curve look like that? A: Because I’m Your Mother! That’s Why!! Which is what my Mom would say when I would demand and explanation for things as a child. It worked then, and it works now. Now having said that, I do want to point out the somewhat intuitive relationship that this curve is demonstrating. As objects get closer together (the gap between them gets smaller) the ability to demonstrate that gap will stress the system more and more until eventually it will reach a limit and make two things look like 1. The better the system, the more accurate the transfer (higher MTF) at a respective spatial resolution demand. X-Rays Lead Bars Recorded Info Available Info Spatial Resolution Demand Increasing Who Gives a Shit? Besides the fact that you could get asked questions about this, there is some practical value to this. Primarily the rating (function) derived from the recorded / available data basically tells you how good the system is. So you could use that to compare different digital fdm systems or plain film etc. 70

D etective Quantum Efficiency (DQE) DQE is essentially an estimate of the required exposure level that will be necessary to create an optimal image. In other words, it’s a “prediction of dose.” DQE is evaluated by comparing the image noise of a detector with that of the “ideal detector,” and in that way it is a measure of the efficiency of a detector in converting x-ray energy into an image signal. People who like math calculate this by comparing the signal to noise ratio at output with the signal to noise ratio at input as a function of spatial frequency. In a perfect world you want this to be “ 1 .0 ” (all radiation energy is absorbed and converted to image), at any spatial resolution - but this isn’t what happens. Factors that influence it are the radiation exposure, spatial frequency (resolution), modular transfer function, detector material, kVp, and mAs. The better your DQE the less radiation you need to maintain your signal. • High DQE = Low Dose • Low DQE = High Dose Here are some things to remember about DQE: • It’s a measurement of efficiency • DQE is directly proportional to MTF • DQE is inversely proportional to the Signal to Noise Ratio • DQE is better at low spatial resolution • In general the DQE of DR is around 0.45, significantly better than that of CR or plain films - which is around 0.25. ** more on this in the next chapter. DQE and spatial resolution: DQE is inversely proportional to spatial resolution (frequency). Why would that be? Just reason that you would need more dose (more photon density) to see fine details. If you are asking the system to see more fine detail - then you are going to need more juice per square inch - therefore a lower DQE. Remember : High DOE = Low Dose 71

Spatial Resolution Concepts Related to Digital Im aging I’m going to talk all about the digital imaging receptors in the next chapter, but for completing the discussion of spatial resolution I want to touch on a few points regarding the hardware. I’ll rehash this again in the next chapter so if it doesn’t make sense you don’t need to spaz it will all make sense by the time you finish the book (probably). Even though film is ancient technology relative to digital imaging one of the few (probably only) advantage it sort of has is in spatial resolution. Film has the advantage of being composed of a continuous thing, where as digital receptors have discrete information in the form of pixels. Now these pixels are really fucking small (they are measured in microns), but they are still separate things. I’ll touch on this again later but the pixel size is a function of the matrix (more pixels in a matrix - the smaller they are). The more pixels you have per unit area “pixel density’’ the better your spatial resolution. Another vocabulary word worth knowing is “pixelpitch” - which is the measurement from the center of one pixel to the next (more easily understood as the pixel spacing). • Testable Point: A major determinant of spatial resolution of digital images is the pixel size and spacing (pixel pitch). • Testable Point: Increased Pixel Density = Better Spatial Resolution • Testable Point: Decreased Pixel Pitch = Better Spatial Resolution 72

(3) Contrast (“Contrast Resolution”) I introduced the concept of contrast in the prior chapter by showing you 3 lions of different shades standing next to each other. Remember the general idea is that it is easier to tell two thing apart if they are totally different shades (black and white) and it is more difficult to tell things apart when they are close to the same shade (one shade of grey vs a slightly lighter shade of grey). This is the idea of imaging contrast. More is good. kVp - Remember that the kVp (kilovoltage peak) doesn’t just play a role in how much the beam penetrates but also plays a large role in image contrast because of the photoelectric effect and k edge concepts discussed in prior chapters. Another way a person who writes multiple choice questions may ask this is by using the term “quality.” Remember that quality is a kVp thing (where as quantity is a mAs thing ... and a kVp thing). • Testable Point: Low kVp = High Contrast • Testable Point: High kVp = Low Contrast Scatter - Anything that increases scatter reduces contrast. So use of a Grid and Collimation (smaller field of view) reduces scatter, and improves contrast resolution. Vocab Radiographic Contrast” - Radiographic contrast is a result of both “subject contrast” and “image receptor contrast” (or film contrast - if the year is 1973). Film Contrast - This is controlled by the design and manufacturing of the film components. “Sensitometric curves” are a thing that is used to describe the inherent quality of the film - which compare changes in density and exposure etc... If I lived in rural Africa or the year was 1973 then 1 might give a shit about this. Since neither of those things are true - 1 give exactly zero shits and more importantly I think it’s very low yield for the test. Just know that film and the subject play a role and they had curves to describe this quality in film. Vocab “High vs Low Subject Contrast” - Certain anatomy regions are intrinsically referred to as either “High” or “Low” contrast. For example, the chest has bones and lung - two things with very different density (and Z) resulting in big contrast difference - white bones next to black lungs. The chest is a region of “high subject contrast.” An example of “low subject contrast” would be the belly - where you have soft tissue organs, next to other soft tissue organs and everything is a blob of grey. The breast is also a classic example of low contrast, where the difference in tissue composition / density between parenchyma and cancer are very very small. 73

On the prior page, I defined the term “subject contrast ” Let’s look at an example of how this could work if we only considered the variable of thickness. For example, two blobs of fat on a table. One that is 10 cm thick, next to another that is only 3 cm thick. Choosing the correct kVP is critical to exploiting the difference in this regions and allowing for a balance between dose and adequate diagnostic quality. In this example, think about contrast as the the ratio of penetrating x-rays that can get through a thin part over a thick part when exposed to the same kVp. Thin Thick 100 kVp Thin Thick A A 40 20 80 70 40 / 20 = 2.0 8 0 /7 0 = 1.1 Regardless of the kVp, you would expect more x-rays to escape a thinner part and strike the target, relative to the thicker part. The change you see is in the ratio. As you increase the kVp, more and more, you will have a larger number of x-rays that are powerful enough to penetrate through the thicker part. Eventually, the ratio will get closer to 1 .0 — which means you have basically no contrast. The point is even without factoring in all the fancy physics with RE. vs Compton interactions, a lower kVp will accentuate contrast differences just based on thickness alone — relative to a higher kVp. Frat Version: Higher kVp —» Smaller Attenuation Coefficients —> Less Contrast 74

Image Receptor Contrast - Digital imaging has an advantage over film with regard to contrast. I’ll touch on this again in the next chapter - but it’s worth knowing the depth of the pixel is determined by the number of bits (10, 12, 14 etc..). This determines the number of shades of gray available for imaging. More bits = more contrast resolution. Window Width - I’ll talk about this in more depth in the CT chapter - but I am going to touch on it here too, because it’s an important contrast topic. You can adjust the radiographic contrast - post processed - by adjusting the range of densities visible. • Testable Point: A narrow (decreased) window width increases contrast. • Testable Point: A wider (increased) window width decreases contrast. • Testable Point: The center point (or midpoint) of the level is going to determine the brightness Digital Imaging Specific Concept “The LUTs”- A major difference between plain film and digital is the primary factor influencing contrast. With plain film kVp is the primary factor. With digital the primary factor influencing contrast is the Look-Up Table or “LUT.” WTF is a “LU T”? The LUT is a histogram of known input intensities and corresponding grayscale. A rescaling process allows for adjustment to present an optimal image (underexposed images are scaled darker, overexposed images are scaled lighter). As a result it’s less of a problem to adjust the kVp to correct for dose when you are using digital - rather than plain film. Key Points Regarding Digital Imaging / LUTs • The primary factor influencing image contrast (in digital systems) is the LUT. • The primary factor influencing image contrast (in film systems) is the kVp. • kVp still influences contrast, but digital systems have a much wider dynamic range. • Imaging-processing software is capable of producing an image with a specified level of contrast even when higher kVp • A higher kVp can be used (adjusted by the 15% rule) and a quality image still produced. WTF is a “B it” ? Digital Transistors (like a Sith Lord) only deal in absolutes, it’s either a zero (off) or a one (on). Each 0 and 1 is called a bit and refers to the computer’s basic unit of information. These binary digits are used to display the shades of gray in a digital image. The greater the number of bits - the more shades of gray available. 75

(4) Brightness Brightness can mean slightly different things depending on if you are talking about film or digital. For film “brightness” refers to how many x-rays are blocked. In other words, “bones are bright.” White Stuff = Bright Stuff. Digital is a little more complicated. Well not really... for the purpose of multiple choice what you need to know (or may not need to know) isn’t very fucking much. It’s like this much - imagine me, a 7 foot tall red bearded man with steel blue eyes, holding up my hand with my thumb and my index finger pretty close to each other. This much... Q: Prometheus are you really 7 foot tall??? A: Of course 1 am! A short person could never write a physics review book this good! Here is the idea - if you have managed to make a digital image then you are gonna be reading it on a monitor (not a stupid light box). When you are dealing with a monitor “brightness ” refers to the amount of luminance (light emission) of the display. Digital imaging provides a wider dynamic range than film screen. Because of this you have the ability to adjust for exposure errors, and make acceptable image densities - optimized for the human eyeball. This process of optimizing is “windowing.” Specifically, the window level (or center). This lets you set a midpoint in the range of densities that are visible on the image. Setting the level (center) allows the image brightness to be increased or decreased throughout the entire range of densities. Level Up (high pixel value) lets you see darker regions (like the lung) better - by increasing the overall brightness. Level Down (lower pixel value) lets you see lighter regions better. Frat Version / Trivia Summary: • Digital imaging provides a wider dynamic range than film screen • Window Level (or Center) = Thing you change for Brightness • Window Width = Thing you change for Contrast • Level Up (higher pixel value) = For lookin’ at dark stuff (lungs) • Level Down (lower pixel value) = For lookin’ at light stuff (bones) 76

Prometheus, if a film is overexposed does it mean both mA and kVP are high or can it be either or both? An overexposed film can be described as having a “high density number” or suffering from “blackening” of the film. In other words, too many x-ray photons have made it to the film / detector. As we have discussed, the density and contrast of an image on film is controlled by the kV, mAs and other exposure factors. Having said that, in general an over exposed image will have an mAs that is too high, and/or a focal spot to detector/film distance that is too small. The kV primarily determines the contrast of an image - but does play a role in putting X-rays on the detector (higher energy = more on the detector). However, there are contrast factors to consider depending on the structure you are trying to optimize. Short Answer = increasing the mA and the kVp will both make the image darker - however you want to use kV to maximize your image contrast. If forced to choice a single factor I would probably pick mA - but this would be a very shitty question. 77

Techs talk a lot about “film density” - or how black the image is. The training they get basically has them focus on balancing “density” (blackness) and contrast. Part 1: “Density” mAs is the weapon of choice for controlling density. Film isn’t “black” enough = increase the mAs. How much? Techs use a thing called the “30% rule.” This is different than the “15%” rule for kVp and mAs. The “30% rule” states that you have to increase the mAs by at least 30% if you want to make a visible change in density. kVp also has an affect on density. Increasing the kVp will increase the quantity (and quality) of the x-ray spectrum. But, remember that a shift into higher kVp ranges is going to result in increased Compton scatter (relative to PE interactions). Another issue that is stressed in Tech training is that increases in kVp are not all equal. You get more bang for your buck increasing the kVp at lower energy levels. Example: 40 kVp increased to 50 kVp = big increase in blackness (density). Example: 95 kVp increased to 105 kVp = trivial increase in blackness. Changing kVp while maintaining density (blackness) is performed with the 15% rule. As discussed previously, that would mean that if you decreased the kVp by 15%, you would need to double the mA to keep the same degree of “blackness” on the film. Image Optimization Tactics -Things You’d Do If You Were a Tech- Fat People and Fat Parts: In general, Techs will say that every 4 cms of patient tissue will require them to double the mA - starting from a standard mA technique for that particular study. Do they use a tape measure ?Even before automatic technique adjustment equipment they didn’t measure people... they just guessed and repeated the study as needed. 78

Image Optimization Tactics -Things You’d Do If You Were a Tech- Part 2: “Contrast” When considering the optimization of contrast the primary enemy is scatter. Scatter is like Skeletor - Lord of Destruction and Overlord of Evil. kVp is the weapon of choice for contrast. It's the equivalent of He-Man's "power sword," in the fight against Skeletor (scatter). Let’s look at some general techniques and see what we can learn. Examples: **These are general techniques for a rough estimate. kVp mAs Thickness of the Body Part Grid Y/N PA CXR 120 5 21 cm Yes AP ABD 80 20 20 cm Yes AP Foot 60 2 9 cm No A few things worth noticing. First, when you have a structure that has high internal contrast (lungs vs ribs - in a CXR) you can use a higher kVP because you don’t need to accentuate that intrinsic contrast as much. When we look at the abdomen (which in this example is similar in thickness) you can see the kVp has dropped. Abdominal soft tissues are closer in density so you need to rely on your P.E. interactions. Remember, those will occur more at a lower keV. More P.E. will also mean less scatter. Scatter is the bad guy (Skeletor). Also, notice the mA was increased on the ABD film. This was done to maintain “density / blackness” on the film given the lower kVp. The grid is an awesome tool for reducing scatter. If you are wondering why the foot x-ray isn’t getting a grid, it’s because the foot is much thinner. Thicker parts make more scatter. Thinner things (extremities, babies) typically don’t get a grid. Grids are typically used when the object is thicker than 10 cm or you are using more than 60 kVp. Don’t forget the grid will add dose. 79

Image Optimization Tactics -Things You’d Do If You Were a Tech- Part 3: “Contrast Continued” “S/P Ratio” — On occasion you may hear people mention a thing called the "S/P Ratio. ” This is a ratio of the Secondary X-Rays (scatter) vs the Primary X- Rays (useful information) that strike the film / detector. So “improving your S/P Ratio” means reducing scatter. Low numbers are better (because the bad guy is the numerator). S/P Ratio essentially depends on two things: (1) The Thickness of the Body Part, and (2) The Area of the Beam. Thicker bodies and bigger areas are going to create more scatter - and increase the S/P ratio. Ways to Reduce Scatter (Improve Contrast): 1 - Collimate: This reduces the area of the beam. 2 - Compress the Part: This reduces the thickness (this is why mammograms are done in compression). 3 - Lower kVp: This reduces Compton scatter (increase P.E.) - trade off is increased dose (skin*) 4 - Grid / Air Gap: Reduces the number of scatter photons that strike the film / detector. Part 4: “Clinical Scenarios” Scenario 1: The Patient is a Big Fat Ass - Whale / T-Rex *Remember Techs will says that every 4 cms of patient tissue will require them to double the mA. *Using a higher kVp of a “thicker” patient will only add scatter. 80

Image Optimization Tactics -Things You’d Do If You Were a Tech- Part 4: “Clinical Scenarios Continued” Scenario 2: Newborns When you x-ray an infant you are going to do a couple of things differently (which makes them testable). (1) You do NOT use a Grid. (2) You lower the kVp - “good technique is around 65 kVp.” They are small and don’t require a lot of juice to penetrate. *Most adult CXRs are around 120-140 kVp. (3) You use around the same or lower mAs - “good technique is around 2-4 mAs.” Adults are typically around 4 mAs (portables are usually closer to 2.5). Scenario 3: Wiggly Babies/Kids Perquisite Knowledge: To continue using this book you must now open google images. You need to google image two things - and the order in which you do it is critical. This literally will decide if you pass or fail boards. First: Google Image “Pigg o Stat" Second: Google Image "Pigg o Stat Blender" You are welcome. So, you could use one of those things or the other one that looks like that thing they would wheel Plannibal the Cannibal Lecter around in - but made for a 5 year old (possibly a future cannibal). Or you could increase the kVp slightly to allow for a shorter "s" on the mAs. Remember the "s" is for seconds - it's the time you are exposing the patient. Longer times are more susceptible to motion / blurring. 81

Image Optimization Tactics -Things You’d Do If You Were a Tech- Part 4: “Clinical Scenarios More Continuing” Scenario 4: Misc Random (possibly testable) Scenarios: Peds Age Adaptation: • 0-5 Years Old = Use 25% of the mAs that an adult would get • 6-12 Years Old = Use 50% of the mAs that an adult would get Casts: • Fiberglass casts require no technique adjustment • Plaster casts require an increase in mAs (2x when dry, 3x when wet) Random silly shit I read in X-Ray Tech books, that I thought some asshole might write a test question about: • History of Splenomegaly = Increase kVp • History of Ascites = Increase kVp • History of Sclerotic Phase Pagets = Increase kVp • History of Lytic Phase Pagets = Decrease kVp • History of Emphysema = Decrease kVp • History of Osteoporosis = Decrease kVp • Soft Tissue Neck XR = Increase mAs We must not accept inferior radiographic technique. If we accept inferior radiographic technique, the terrorists win. We're talking about unchecked aggression here, dude. 82
