O r... instead of scribbling you could make a list of creative curses you might want/need to use on your enemies if you one day acquire the powers of a wizard (which should be everyones eventual goal). I’ll help you get started. 1) Whenever my enemy sees a school bus he/she will shit their pants. 2) Whenever my enemy hears the word “Thursday” he/she will be compelled to pretend they are a dragon for 20-30 seconds. 3) 4) 5) 6) V) 8) 9) 10) 83

^ CHAPTER 4: OMA©l DETECT®®! - PROMETHEUS UONHART, M.D. - I Digital vs Analog Long ago, when dinosaurs roamed the earth, radiology was done on film screen. Now in the modem age it is digital. The comparison of film screen vs digital radiology could lend itself to multiple choice questions. “Larger Dynamic Range” One of the major limitations of film is its limited dynamic range. What that means is that there is a limit to the range of exposure intensities that it can accurately detect. In other words - at the extreme ends (not enough and too much) film screen sucks, leaving it very sensitive to under or overexposure. The consequence of that is more repeat studies (more radiation for the patient). A second consequence of that is for situations when you want to look at two structures that differ greatly in x-ray attenuation (example - bones and soft tissue) - film doesn’t optimize this as well. PhDs love to diagram this comparison of the dynamic range - this vs that, and I’m a firm believer that comparative radiology makes for good test questions, so I think it’s worth being familiar with the graph. Testable points: • Digital = Larger Dynamic Range + Linear Response to Exposure Detector • Film = Narrow Dynamic Range + Response Curvilinear Response to Exposure X-Ray Exposure I touched on this at the end of the last chapter (repetition = memory); remember that because digital has this larger dynamic range you can correct moderately under or overexposed images by adjusting the brightness (via the window level / center). I also mentioned in the prior chapter that you can try and fix the contrast by adjusting the window width. Trivia: Digital lets you try and fix contrast and brightness. If you have film you have to break out the fucking “hot light” - yes that was a thing - google it. Dynamic Range Digital (Linear) Film (Curvilinear) 84

Prom etheus, I don’t understand why film screen is not able to optimize seeing bone and soft tissue (structures that differ greatly in X-ray attenuation) ? By popular demand, I’m gonna make an attempt to explain this confusing topic. Before we discuss the limitation, we should quickly review this concept of “contrast.” When we talk about contrast we are referring to the differences in density (white vs black) that are present on a single image. For example, if you have big differences (example 1) you would say the image has “high radiographic contrast.” If you have a more narrow range of differences (example 2 ) you would say the image has “low radiographic contrast.” Example 2 : So why might one image have high contrast and another image have low contrast? Well, in the setting of an old fashion film screen it is going to depend on multiple factors including the choice of intensifying screen (the actual film is a poor x-ray detector) and kVp. A high contrast exam (example - MSK plain film of an extremity) would be performed by choosing a narrow film/screen dynamic range and a low x-ray beam energy (kVp). In general, film screen combinations produce curves that look something like this. Exposure to X-Rays Narrow Latitude - High Contrast Potential Wider Latitude - Lower Contrast Potential Now, what this is showing is the change in density (how white exposed parts of the image look) in response to exposure to x-rays. The broader (wider) the latitude the lower the contrast. In other words, if small difference in exposure result in large differences in density change, you will see more dramatic shifts from black to white. 85

This is why the fixed narrow dynamic range issue is usually cited as the primary weakness in film imaging. If you look at the curve the bottom part (the toe) will result in an image with no significant contrast as the image is UNDER exposure. The top part of the curve (the shoulder) will also result in an image with no significant contrast as this image is OVER exposed. So - again this is the primary advantage of digital imaging over screen film. The wide dynamic range allow you to compensate for sub-optimal settings, and allows you to maximize contrast regardless of the technique that is used (within reason). 86

Dose Another element of “Digital vs Analog" that I want to point out is the superior dose efficiency - given: (1) The wider dynamic range, which would allow you to underexpose a little bit knowing that you can correct it by windowing, (2) You don’t have to depend on kVp so very much for contrast. With film kVp was super super important for getting your contrast (remember small differences in Z = big differences in photoelectric absorption). But with digital, since you can adjust contrast and brightness afterwards, you can go up on the kVp (by 15%) and half your mA to decrease dose. Frat Version = Digital has the potential to have lower dose relative to film because of post processing options. Gamesmanship = Another way to word this is “Higher Dose Efficiency.” Thats a fancy way to say less radiation. Spatial Resolution — “Pixels” Film radiographs were/are actually made up of tiny deposits of black metallic silver (imported from the Byzantine Empire), smeared on as a single blob-like unit. This is different than a digital image which is recorded in individual units called “pixels” as a “matrix.” Unfortunately, no one can be told what the matrix is... you have to see it for yourself. OK fine. The matrix is a combination of very small (usually) “picture elements” arranged into rows and columns. These “picture elements” are your “pixels”. Think of each one of these pixels as a recorded numerical value, which is going to be used to represent a brightness level on the display. The matrix size varies - so the amount of pixels you have varies. For example, a 1024 matrix has 1,048,576 individual pixels (1024 x 1024). So as I mentioned previously pixel size is a function of the matrix (more pixels in a matrix - the smaller they are). The more pixels you have per unit area {“pixel density”), the better your spatial resolution. Another vocabulary word worth knowing is “pixelpitch” - which is the measurement from the center of one pixel to the next (more easily understood as the pixel spacing). • Testable Point: A major determinant of spatial resolution of digital images is the pixel size and spacing (pixel pitch). • Testable Point: Increased Pixel Density = Better Spatial Resolution • Testable Point: Decreased Pixel Pitch = Better Spatial Resolution • Testable Point: Plain Film may have better spatial resolution than digital, because they don’t have the limitation of individual units (pixels). 87

Rapid Review - Differences in Plain Film vs Digital • Digital has “larger dynamic range” which means you can over or under expose the film (within reason) and it’s still diagnostic. This means less repeats (so less radiation overall). • Digital has a linear response curve, Film has a curvilinear response curve. • Film depends on kVp for contrast. Digital doesn’t as much - so you can use the 15% rule (increase your kVp by 15%, decrease your mA by 50%) and cut down on dose. • Spatial Resolution of Film may still be better than Digital Autom atic Exposure Control - (AEC) Honestly, I probably should have described this thing already - but I might need to mention it later in the chapter so I’ll go head and do it now. During the Cretaceous Period the exposure timing was done with an actual timer. Now a thing called the automatic exposure control (AEC) is used. It essentially serves the same role as a timer but operates differently depending on the patient body part - to determine the ideal amount of exposure. The underlying mechanism is actually an ionization chamber - which I’ll talk about again in the nukes chapter. Basically, when x-rays interact with the atoms within the chamber they ionize them (through the same mechanisms discussed earlier in the book). These ionized electrons are now charged, which creates a signal to terminate the exposure. The AEC is calibrated to know how much of a charge is good enough to terminate exposure. Things to know: • AEC acts like a timer which decides when to terminate exposure • AEC works by using an ionization chamber, placed between the patient and the image receptor • The duration of the exposure is determined by the density and thickness of the area of the patient placed over the ionization chamber. • AEC controls only the quantity of radiation reaching the image receptor - no effect on kYp. The “timer” function is dealing with the “s” in mAs. (Fluoro Automatic Brightness systems - can mess with kVp) • AEC must be re-calibrated if that receptor changes (you upgrade from film to a CR digital system) 88

D igital Nuts and Bolts - Types Trivia Etc.. So on and So Forth - If you are working anywhere in the United States this is all you will use. If you are working in certain parts of Africa or have traveled back in time about 30 years you may still be using film. Let us do a quick rundown on some vocab. I already introduced the idea of a matrix and a pixel. Now is probably the right time to go into a bit of depth on bit depth. So what is this thing called “bit depth ” ? Bit Depth Pixels have what is called “bit depth, ” which is basically the number of bits that determine the number of shades of gray that can be displayed on a computer monitor. For example a 12-bit depth can display 4096 shades of gray, whereas a 14- and 16-bit depth can display 16,384 and 65,536 shades of gray, respectively. Where do those numbers come from ? It is basically 2 to the “x” power. So 2 12 = 4096. Take-home point: The greater the pixel bit depth (the bigger the number) the more shades of gray available for image display. The more shades of gray (“punish me”) available to display on a digital image, the better the contrast resolution. Just don’t forget your safe word. Classifications Digital detectors can be broken down like this: Storage Phosphor (CR) - Type of Indirect • Flat Panel Detectors (DR) • Direct Indirect What is the difference between direct and indirect? Matrix: Images are displayed as a combination of columns and rows — “array.” A bigger matrix improves spatial resolution. Pixel: The smallest component of the matrix. A greater number of smaller pixels improves spatial resolution. Pixel Bit Depth: Number of bits that determine the number of shades of gray that can be displayed on a computer monitor. Can be thought of as a precision component controlling the exact pixel brightness that can be specified. Indirect (scintillators) = X rays — ► Light — ► Charge Direct (photoconductors) = X rays — Charge 89

- CR - Computed Radiography or “The C assette Based System" Storage Phosphors (CR) : These can work with a conventional x-ray machine (just like a normal cassette) The system uses a special phosphor (Barium Fluorohalides) that does not emit the absorbed energy as light after interaction with an x-ray. Instead, the x- ray causes an electron in the phosphor to change to a metastable state (one that it can hold for several days). Essentially when the plate is exposed to x-rays some of electrons within the phosphor are removed. Those liberated electrons get trapped in a “conduction band.” A magical metal called “europium” helps convince some of the liberated electrons to stay trapped in this conduction band for hours. The idea is that the storage phosphor is holding a “latent” x-ray image. When the cassette is returned back to a docking station a readout is performed. You can think of this like developing the film. The process is called “photostimulable luminescence ” - To read it out, the plate is exposed to a laser that sweeps back and forth. The red light of the laser liberates the trapped electrons and they return to their shells. As they return they release energy as blue-green light. The light is directed onto a photodetector which then amplifies the light signal and converts it to an electronic signal. The signal is then digitalized and divided into a matrix (a bunch of squares). Each one of these squares is called a “pixel.” Remember the size of the matrix is going to determine your spatial resolution (larger matrix = greater number of pixels , because each pixel is smaller, = greater spatial resolution). “Sampling Pitch” is a vocab word that describes the distance between laser positions as it’s reading out the plate. With direct systems (not CR) it means something slightly different - and instead refers to the distance between adjacent DELs (Detector Elements). “Pixel Pitch” is a vocab word I’ve mentioned before that means the distance between adjacent pixels. Remember this is also important for spatial resolution. Testable Trivia: • The amount of light detected is proportional to the intensity of the incident x-ray. • The photostimulated phosphors have a wide detectable range, tolerating x-ray intensities 100 times higher and 100 times lower than the 5 micro Gy needed for an old school screen film. • The plate is reset by forceable exposure to bright white light - which erases it. If you forget to do this you will get ghosting artifacts. WTF is a “Phosphor ” ? A phosphor is a substance that can emit light when exposed to a radiation source. “Luminescence,” they call it. 90

- DR - Digital Radiography or “The C assetteless System " Flat Panel Detectors (DR): When most people say “digital detector,” this is what they are talking about. It’s much faster than conventional fdm development or CR plate reading. As mentioned before you can have direct or indirect versions of digital systems. What does that mean again ? Indirect systems (like CR) have an intermediate step where the latent image has to be released as light and then converted into a digital signal (0 ’s and l ’s) to be displayed on the screen. • Indirect (scintillators) = X rays — ► Light — ► Charge • Direct (photoconductors) = X rays — ► Charge Indirect: The x-ray activates the Thallium-doped Cesium Iodide (Csl). Csl is the poster child scintillator. Scintillators Gonna Scintillate (emit light in response to an absorbed x-ray) Light gets emitted - which is then converted by a photodiode into an electric charge. The electric charge is in turn captured and transmitted by the TFT (Thin-Film Transistor) array to the workstation for processing / display. Is a “Scintillator” the same as a “Phosphor” ? Pretty much - 1 mean you can get into the weeds with some major PhD bullshit trying to distinguish them. For the purpose of this exam all you need to know is they are processes of light emission. If you really need to know - this is the way I think about them: • In radioluminescence, the ionizing radiation produces free electrons which recombine at a luminescence center to produce luminescence. • In scintillation, the ionized atom itself relaxes via a series of excited levels to produce luminescence. (Readout Process) Indirect DR System 91

Lateral Dispersion of Light = Loss of Spatial Resolution A plague upon the indirect method is this idea of lateral light dispersion. What you hope would happen What actually happens Light goes straight down Lateral Dispersion of the Light What is this “Lateral Dispersion ” ? This is a problem you run into with indirect methods that use phosphors. Light tends to diffuse laterally after it leaves the site of conversion from an x-ray. This creates issues with spatial resolution, which get worse with increasing thickness of the crystal. However, making the crystal thin has its own problems as your sensitivity for collecting x-rays is going to drop off (they just fly right through). Is there a difference in “Lateral Dispersion ” between types o f phosphors? Yes - Gadolinium Oxysulfide has more lateral dispersion (light scatter) than Csl. How does one solve the problem o f “Lateral Dispersion ” ? Luckily, someone much smarter than you or me already did. They invented a columnar structure that forms a “lead pipe” like matrix. With this design you can make a nice thick crystal without worry. Having said that - a better answer would probably be to use a Direct System which doesn’t deal with phosphors / scintillation etc... 92

Direct: As mentioned on the prior page, one of the issues with indirect methods is this extra step where x-rays are converted to light first, prior to being converted to an electric signal. This step is removed with direct methodology. • This uv That: Indirect uses a scintillator. Direct uses a photoconductor • This vs That: Indirect uses Cesium Iodine (a scintillator). Direct uses Amorphous Selenium, (the photoconductor) • This vs That: Both Indirect and Direct use a TFT read out array How it Works: Step 1: Apply a homogeneous “bias” charge to the surface of the selenium. Step 2: Fire the x-rays through the patient, into the selenium. The x-rays are absorbed by the selenium and electrons are released. Step 3: Released electrons ( “electron hole pair”) travel to the surface of the selenium and neutralize a portion of the applied charged. This is done in proportion to the radiation intensity. An important testable point is there is no lateral dispersion. Step 4: These electrical charges are drawn in along the electric field lines to the charge storage capacitor electrodes connected to the TFT. Step 5: The pattern of charges is scanned and converted to a digital signal stored by each TFT. Amorphous Selenium The material is magical, likely originating from the underground dwarf mines of middle earth. Selenium is essentially a photoconductor. When it is exposed to radiation, its electrical conductivity is altered in proportion to the intensity of the radiation. It is very susceptible to humidity and temperature. Don’t try and use it outside in the Amazon jungle. * Amorphous Selenium Photon (from x-ray) jh j Field Electrode Dielectric Layer * - Collection Array Direct DR System 93

F ill Factor What is this “Fill Factor” ? This is the area of the detector which is sensitive to x-rays (in relation to the entire detector area). The higher the fill factor, the more efficient the detector. Fill Factor Differences: With DR systems, the electric field shaping essentially allows for a fill factor of nearly 100%. This is not seen with Indirect (CR) systems. Remember, differences between things make good multiple choice questions. Direct - Amorphous Selenium Indirect - Cesium Iodide Detective Q uantum E fficiency (DQE) - In the prior chapter, 1 went on and on about this confusing topic. As a rapid recap DQE is essentially an estimate of the required exposure level that will be necessary to create an optimal image. In other words, it’s an efficiency measurement. The better your DQE the less radiation you need to maintain your signal (High DQE = Low Dose). Another important point is that your DQE struggles as your demand for spatial resolution increases. As I mentioned above, DR systems have a better fill factor (near 100%). That means they are more efficient. So you could correctly infer that DR has a higher DQE. Testable Trivia: • DQE of DR » CR (and plain film) • DQE of DR is around 0.45, significantly better than that of CR or plain films - which is around 0.25. D isplay Trivia: The typical standard o f care for a digital display is 3 M ega-Pixels. 94

Trivia C harts I Tables Specific Factors Affecting the Spatial Resolution of CR • Laser Spot Size: Smaller = Better • Phosphor Plate Density / Thickness: More Thick = More Light Spreading = Less Resolution • Sampling Frequency (Rate of Light Sampling): Increasing the sampling frequency results in a smaller pixel pitch with improves the spatial resolution. • Imaging Plate: You are supposed to use the SMALLEST plate size reasonable for the anatomic area of interest. The reason is that for fixed-matrix-size CR systems, using a smaller plate for a given field of view improves your spatial resolution. • Increasing x-rays will NOT improve Maximum spatial resolution Specific Factors Affecting the Spatial Resolution of DR • Spatial Resolution for DR is Superior to CR because the pixel detector is built into the DR flat panel - • Direct systems that avoid lateral dispersion of light have better spatial resolution • Spatial Resolution for Flat Panel Detectors is limited to the DEL (detector element); smaller detector elements = better spatial resolution. Dircc, Conversion Indirect Conversion Directly converts X-Rays to electrical signal X-Ray -> Light -> Electrical Signal Detector material is amorphous selenium Phospor material is usually thallium doped cesium iodine Signal does not “laterally disperse”, as the applied voltage separates the electrons and holes made by X-Rays Light can scatter (worse with thicker crystal), better if columnar structure is used. Fill Factor is high (near 100%) Moderate fill factor (depends on size of pixel) Higher Detector Quantum Efficiency (DQE) Moderate Detector Quantum Efficiency (DQE) “Centralized vs Decentralized W orkflow ” • CR is a cassette based “centralized system” = that is essentially the same workflow as conventional film. (CR = Centralized) • DR allows for a “decentralized system” where the image can be acquired, adjusted, and transferred to PACS without the tech leaving the x-ray room. Each room is its own “center” - therefore it’s “de— centralized” (De = DR) 95

C larification 1: Increasing X-ray w ill not im prove m axim um spatial resolution in CR system What I’m trying to say here is that the maximum spatial resolution of the CR system is capped by the design of the system. Specifically the problem of light scattering from the laser light, and the size of the pixels. X-Ray density can never overcome these intrinsic limitations. Primary Spatial Resolution Summary: • Screen Films = Screen Thickness • CR = Pixel Size and Light Scattering / Spreading • DR = Spread of Light Photons in the X-ray to Light Conversion (indirect DR), and the size of the detector “del.” C larification 2: In direct FPD has m oderate fill fa c to r (depends on th e size of th e pixel). There is an established relationship with fill factor and pixel size. In general, the larger the pixel the better the fill factor. It is complicated and has a lot to do with construction methods, but you will see charts like this one floating around in the literature. Pixel Size I myself dabbled in pacifism once 96

CHAPTER S: M A M M ® - PROMETHEUS LIONHART, M.D. - Gamesmanship - When you are studying mammo for physics I want you to concentrate on what makes mammo unique - relative to regular plain old diagnostic x-ray. Remember by learning via comparison you are training yourself to exclude distractors - a key tactic for multiple choice exams. Overview — Why does mammo need to be different? The difference in attenuation of a breast cancer and just regular breast tissue is very small. So you have to use lower energy and a nearly mono-energetic beam to enhance the attenuation differences. Additionally, an increase in spatial resolution is required to see micro calcifications. fill® Low er Energy Why lower energy? - Remember the photoelectric effect dominates at lower kVp. Small differences really amplify the chance of the P.E. because of the cubed mathematic relationship (probability o f P.E. directly proportional to the atomic number cubed: Z 3 and indirectly with energy 1/E3). Glandular Tissue Cancer Glandular Tissue Normal Dx Range - 50 keV Lower Mammo Range - 20 keV What is the Optimal kVp: The ideal energy for mammo is between 16-23 keV. this energy, a voltage of 25-30 kVp is used (general Dx uses between 50-120). To get 98

D ifferent T arg et Target Anodes: Mammo uses a molybdenum or rhodium anode (general radiology uses tungsten). Why? Remember, characteristic x-rays are “characteristic” because they depend on the binding energy of the shell involved, and this binding energy is different for different targets. So its unique... or “characteristic.” Tungsten , which is used in conventional imaging, has characteristic x-rays around 69.5 keV. Mammo needs energies way lower than that (ideally between 16 -23 keV). So the solution is to use a low atomic number target. The classic examples being molybdenum and rhodium. These targets generate lower K-characteristic x-rays which are required to generate all those false positives and unnecessary biopsies that mammo uses to fund its section. I mean... to find the cancers. The Normal Spectrum o f Molybdenum: The combination of low kVp and a low Z give Moly a high peak of characteristic x-rays, with low Bremsstrahlung. Notice that energy is around 18 keV (instead of 69.5 keV like Tungsten). 99

' J K-Edge Filtration K Edge Filtration - A K. edge filter is placed on the outside of the tube with the goal of creating a nearly mono-energetic beam in the target range of 16-23 keV. Moly is classically used for this, taking advantage of the 20 keV K edge and subsequent rise in photoelectric absorption. Energies lower than 15 and greater the 20 get filtered out. : Spectrum of Molybdenum with Superimposed Mo Filter 18 keV 18 keV Rhodium: An alternative to Moly is Rhodium, which has a similar (slightly stronger) spectrum, and a binding energy of 20.2 keV. High Yield: • Filters block BOTH high and low photons, with the benefit of removing the low energy photons which only give you dose and the high energy photons which won’t help with contrast. • Rh/Rh is used for larger or denser breasts - because it’s higher energy compared to Mo/Mo. • Mo anode with Rhodium filter is also used to produce an intermediate energy spectra between a Mo/Mo and Rh/Rh combination. • NEVER use a Rh Target (21 kev) with a Mo Filter (20 Kev K edge) • Mo anode can also be combined with an aluminum filter, for a harder beam to penetrate denser breasts. • Some digital systems will use Tungsten / Rho and Tungsten / Silver- this creates a higher energy spectrum for increased penetration and lower dose. Contrast is lost - but some post processing can bring it back. 100

Focal Spot I mA I Exposure Tim e Focal Spot Size: As mentioned before, mammo requires a better spatial resolution than you get with normal imaging. To achieve this you use a smaller focal spot: High Yield: • Mammography uses a focal spot of 0.1 and 0.3 mm • General X-ray uses focal spots of 0.6 and 1.2 mm A smaller focal spot is not going to tolerate heat as well. To address this you have to use a lower mA (otherwise you’d melt the anode). The mA is limited to 50 for 0.1 mm, and 100 for 0.3 mm. Another issue is that since you are using a lower mA you need a longer exposure time. Generally you still try and keep the exposure time under 2 seconds. FRAT Version - Mammo Uses (1) Smaller Focal Spots, (2) Lower mA, and (3) Longer Exposure Time Spatial Resolution Screen Film Mammo 15 lp/mm Digital Mammo 7 lp/mm Digital Radiograph 3 lp/mm CT 0.7 lp/mm MRI 0.3 lp/mm H eel E ffect - R efresher I mentioned this before. Just remember the cathode side goes near the chest wall. Also, the loss of energy on the anode side (nipple side) is compensated for by angling the tube up to about 2 0 degrees. Effective Anode Angle = Anode Angle + Tube Angle Cathode Less More Cathode Anode J Less Intense More Intense

-Prometheus, Why do we have to angle the tube 20 deg? What is that doing to the energy on the anode side? It does nothing to the anode angle. The anode angle is not the same as the tube tilt angle. The tube is angled / oriented for a couple of reasons but it is easiest to just think about it as an attempt to maximize the effects of the heel effect by giving more x-rays to the chest wall (denser / thicker part of the tissue). i S L ** diagrams show a change in the tube tilt angle. Notice the anode angle doesn’t change (because it is built into the machine). Now - a possible trick could be to calculate the effective target angle. What this means is the anode angle plus the tube tilt. So for example, you would have the same field coverage (effective target angle) if you had: Anode Angle 8 + Tube Tilt 16 = 24 Or Anode Angle 16 + Tube Tilt 8 = 24 102

T he B eryllium W indow: Most diagnostic tubes use pyrex glass - this is NOT used in mammo because it causes excessive attenuation of the energies used in mammo. Instead a beryllium exit window is used. FRAT Version: • Regular Dx uses Glass, • Mammo uses Beryllium . Window Com pression Mammography is performed in compression (another unique feature). Why do they do that? There are lots of advantages. Mainly it makes for a more uniform tissue - gives you less overlap, less geometric blurring, and better detail. You would have a TON of call backs if you didn’t do it... even more than you do already .The most testable advantage is probably the dose / scatter reduction. Here is the full list of reasons: • Reduced Thickness = Less Scatter = Lower kVp can be chosen • Lower kVp and Less Scatter = Improved Contrast • Reduced Thickness = Less mAs needed = Less Dose • Breast Doesn’t Move = Less Motion Artifact • Breast Smashed Closer to Bucky = Less Geometric Magnification • Less Motion and Less Geometric Mag = Improved Spatial Resolution • Less Tissue overlap 103

Grid Anti-Scatter Grid: Since the breast is placed in compression and you are using lower kVp (both of which intrinsically reduce scatter) a smaller grid ratio is used. Mammo uses a 4-5 grid ratio (general x-ray uses a 6-16). What is a “Grid Ratio” ? Dose with the Grid - The grid removes scattered photons - which reduces some dose. However, the same technique can’t be used with a grid or you will underexpose. So, the p atj0 _ (_j/yy technique is turned up. Ultimately the dose is INCREASED with a grid. H= height, W= width between grids Bucky Factor is 2 for Mammo and 5 for General X-Ray M agnification - P art 1 Mammo is also unique in its implementation of the “mag view” - traditionally used for calcs. How it s done: The breast is moved away from the detector , and closer to the source (focal spot). Typically the distance to the source is cut in half -which doubles the mag. Focal Spot Magnification Magnification: Increases with: The patient far away from the detector. “Greater Object to Detector Distance” The source closer to the patient. “Less Source to Object Distance” Dose - Bringing the boob closer to the source is going to increase the dose. Inverse square law dude... 104

M agnification V ie w - P art 2 “T h e A ir G ap” “Dai Air Gap ” - I call it. So remember when we bring the boob further away from the target (and closer to the source) we are creating magnification. In doing this we are also creating a “gap” of air between the boob and target. This gap essentially acts like an anti scatter grid. Remember this ofif-axis scatter is coming out of the patient (in this case the patient’s boob). The gap allows it to miss the target. Key Point - No Grid for Mag Views, Instead You have an Air Gap. m m “Dat Air Gap” Off Axis Scatter - Hopefully missing the target. Magnification View ■ Part 3 - Remaining Trivia and Summary There are a few more testable details related to the Mag View - so let us fill in those gaps. The goal is to magnify the boob - so you can see those calcifications better. You don’t want to miss that low-grade DCIS in a 95 year old - the patient may not know she “needs” a double mastectomy. ' ■ v d I’ve mentioned that you create this magnification by moving the boob further from target. What I haven’t mentioned is the need for increased spatial resolution. You need to be able to describe the morphology of those calcifications. This is achieved by using a smaller focal spot. The other change to be aware of is a reduction in mA (so you don’t melt your smaller focal spot), and also made possible by the removal of the grid. Having said that - the “exposure time” is increased to compensate for the greater distance between the boob and the image receptor. Key Points: • Smaller Focal Spot (for better spatial resolution) • Less mA (about a quarter o f conventional “contact” mammo) • Longer Exposure Time (about triple that o f conventional “contact ” mammo) 105

C larification 1: M am m ogram s have high o p tical density com pared to general radiology. W hat does th a t m ean ? Unfortunately this question requires some discussion of the old fashion screen film. I get diarrhea cramps when I have to talk about screen films so I’ll try and be brief. If you can find a text book describing this stuff (usually they are written in ancient Sumerian) you might find a graph of what used to be called the “characteristic curve. ” This curve was the optical density and log of exposure. In other words in showed the relative changes in ^ “blackening” you’d get on the film at different exposure rates - for that g particular film. You see now all film is the same - the emulsion thickness, size, distribution of silver halide crystals... all these things change the characteristics of the film, and therefore it’s “characteristic curve.” Mammography film was traditionally composed of a single emulsion with very fine crystals and as a results had a very high sensitivity. You might hear people say the mammography film has a “high D Max” or high maximum optical density. This is useful especially at the periphery of the breast (the skin) where the thickness rapidly decreases. C larification 2: W hy does m am m a have a higher recep to r a ir kerm a (AK) com pared to general radiology ? As a refresher, AK is the density of x-rays (how many x-rays per unit of space - i.e. square millimeter). The further away from the source you get the less of them there is (they spread out). This obeys the inverse square law. Not all Air Kerma is the same. For example: • Entrance Air Kerma is the radiation incident on the patient • Receptor Air Kerma is the radiation used to generate the image. Receptor AK is gonna be less than entrance, as the x-rays get reduced by the attenuation of the patient and how far away the receptor is from the source. So, it is a little complicated but mammography is done at a closer distance. For example, the source to image distance for mammography is usually around 65 cm, vs the 100-cm SID used in normal radiography. Remember the inverse square law, even a little distance makes a big difference. 107

D igital M am m ography Digital systems have the major advantage of allowing you to change the contrast and brightness after the acquisition of the image by adjusting the window and level. Pixel Size and Spatial Resolution: Remember that smaller pixels = better spatial resolution. Additionally, you can lose spatial resolution from spread of electronic or light signal inside the digital or computed detectors. In general, most digital systems have lower spatial resolution (around 5-11 lp/mm) - relative to analog (1 1 -1 3 lp/mm). • Another point of trivia is that the MQSA does NOT have specific line pair requirements for digital; instead they are linked to the manufacturer’s specs. Dose: Digital machines have 15% less dose compared to analog (1.8mGy for analog, 1.6 for digital). This is because the beam quality is better. Digital also has fewer repeat exams (because you can window and level). Also, the dose is not fixed - like it is with screens; you can turn the juice up and down as needed. Noise Limited Images: The primary factor affecting the total noise is the number of photons interacting with the detector. Although digital images can have variable contrast, the noise is fixed after the exposure is taken. Dark Noise: This is an additional type of noise from electronic fluctuations within the detector element. The effect is proportional to the temperature of the detector - that is why coolers are needed. You see these more with underexposed regions. Flat Field Test: Imaging of a large piece of acrylic. This is done to improve image quality, and calibrate the digital detectors. Digital Artifacts: Ghosting: This is caused by a residual image from the prior exposure - burned into the detector. You see this when highly attenuating objects are placed in the beam. This is why lead is not allowed on flat panel digital systems. Pixels Gone Bad: This can manifest as a square or a streak. 108

PPVi PPV2 and PPV3 In an attempt to make things overly complicated the ACR and MQSA set up guidelines for data collection with sub categories for positive predictive value. Things with different types or categories make great multiple choice questions. First a quick refresher: “What is this positive predictive value” ? It’s the proportion of people who have a positive study and actually have breast cancer. Breast CA (+) Breast CA (-) Test (+) Test (-) Positive Exam With Breast CA Positive Exam With NO CA Negative Exam With Breast CA Negative Exam With NO CA PPV = Positive Exam with Cancer Positive Exam with Cancer People like to group statistics in these boxes sometimes labeled with letters. Letters are confusing - and test writers can fuck with the order, so don’t use the letters - just memorize what goes in the boxes and their relationship. Positive Predictive Value is going to be the amount of people with a positive test and disease (a correct test) over all the ones you called positive (the ones you got right + your overcalls). Notice everything is a “positive” since it’s a positive predictive value. The ones you called positive and were right, over all the ones you called positive. Positive Exams with NO Cancer (“overcalls”) Categories of PPV - Per Each Stage of the W orkup: PPVi - This refers to cases from positive screening (anything other than a recommendation to return to screening). In other words, BR-0, BR-3, BR-4, BR-5. “Call backs” - we call them. Benchmark for this is 4.4% A screening only facility only obtains data as PPVi ■ biopsy. because they don’t do call backs or PPV2- This refers to cases where biopsy was recommended. In other words, times you BR4 or BR5’d something. How many times you sent someone to the “room of tears” - where you tell them they need a biopsy. Benchmark for this is 25.4% PPV3- This refers to the results of the biopsy. Some people call this the “PBR’ or positive biopsy rate. Other people will call it the “biopsy yield of malignancy.” What will your examiner call it?... The most likely answer is the name you didn’t remember it was called - so remember all of them. Benchmark for this is 31.0% Synonyms: PPV3 = PBR = Biopsy Yield of Malignancy 109

- MQSA - Appropriate Target Range for Medical Audit Recall Rate 5-7% Cancers/ 1000 Screened 3-8 Specific Tasks That You Should Memorize Processor QC Daily Darkroom Cleanliness Daily Viewbox Conditions Weekly Phantom Evaluation Weekly Repeat Analysis Quarterly Compression Test Semi-Annually Darkroom Fog Semi-Annually Screen-Film Contrast Semi-Annually MQSA Rules for Male Residents on the M ammography Service: A tie must be worn (pink is the recommend color - although not required). General gaze should be in the downward direction (submissive posture) You will not speak unless spoken to first Penis must be taped either down or to the side Urination may only be performed in the seated position Misc MQSA Trivia: • Places that do mammo have to be “accredited and certified” i.e. pay money, every 3 years • The FDA is the evil overlord behind the MQSA Gaining the Privilege to Read a Mammogram: • During the last two years of training you have to read 240 mammography examinations (during a 6 month period) - under “direct supervision” • You have to have 3 months of formal training • You have to have 60 documented hours of mammo education Spatial Resolution and the Line Pair Phantom: • RSNA says: 13 LP/mm in the Anode - Cathode Direction • RSNA says: 11 LP/mm in the left-right direction • Hnda Says: MQSA requires a resolution of 12 line pairs per mm for screen-film, and manufacturer specs for digital (~ 7 lp/mm). Mean Glandular Dose: The MQSA has some breast phantom which is suppose to be an “average breast.” This thing is 4.2cm of compressed breast that is 50% adipose and 50% glandular. Testable Trivia: Dose under 300 millirads (3 mGy). This required dose is ONLY for the phantom, not a real human breast. This is measured WITH A GRID. The dose limit without a grid is 1 mGy. There is no actual regulation for what a human breast can endure. ***Summary - Phantom (not human) limit = 3 mGy with a grid. 1 mGy without a grid 110
