I ’ve made an effort to include a lot of repetition on topics (especially confusing topics) in this chapter. Th is is a deliberate act to help jam things into long term memory or even better generate a lasting understanding. It might feel redundant at times, but you ’11 thank me later. Just like with mammo, the differences between regular diagnostic X-Ray, and fluoro lend themselves easily to multiple choice questions. So what is different? Regular diagnostic x-ray is performed with mA ranging between 200-800 and very short exposure times. For fluoro to work it has to have very long exposure times. The trade-off is, that if you want long exposure times and you don’t want to melt your focal spot (or the patient), you will need to decrease the mA. Speaking of the focal spot - to limit geometric blurring, a smaller focal spot is used. Regular Dx Fluoro mA 200-800 mA 0-5 kVp 50-120 kVp 50-120 — \ ^ ........... Very short exposure times Longer exposure times 1 i L. . . Focal Spot 1.0 -1.2 mm Focal Spot 0.3-0.6 mm This vs T h at - “Last Im age Hold” vs Spot Im age If you tell the computer to hold the last fluoro image you have on the screen that is referred to as a “last image hold” or a “Fluoro Frame Shot.” This is different than a “spot image” which is basically a regular x-ray. The key differences worth knowing are: • Spot Image = Conventional X-Ray • Spot Image = More Dose • Spot Image = Large Focal Spot (because greater tube current is needed) • Fluoro Image Shot = More Quantum Mottle (less photons) • Both use a grid. • Spatial Resolution of a Fluoro Image Shot = around 2 Line Pairs per mm • Spatial Resolution of a Spot Image = around 3 Line Pairs per mm • **Spatial Resolution is better with the Spot Image Trivia: “5-10” digital spot films = the dose of 1 minute of fluoroscopy (assuming same FOV). 112

Fluoroscopic M echanism Subtypes There are two different types: Any guesses as to (1) The dinosaur one “Image Intensifies ”, and which one is more (2) The new one “Flat Panel Detector. ” testable? Im a g e In te n s ifie r: This is the basic set up. Image Intensifier Grid Patient jr “ Collimated X-Ray Beam X-Ray Generator / Tube Tube under the table. Collimation occurs prior to reaching the patient. Patient gets the juice - generates a bunch of scatter. Scatter gets rejected by the grid. Image intensifier. Is the tube always under the patient? Nope. It usually is (like in 95% of the systems). The notably exception is with GU radiology set ups which sometimes are set up with the bladder close to the receptor (to reduce focal spot blur etc...). In those cases the source is ABOVE the table. Is there any trivia you need to know about Source ABOVE the table systems? Yup. The operator (tech or radiologist) lens dose is higher. 113

Im a g e In te n s ifie r Smaller than input - so it Concentrates them Electrons -> Light “Minification Gain” Accelerating Electrons, Between Voltage Differences: “Flux Gain” Output Phosphor Focusing Electrodes Light -> Electrons Captures X-Rays, Turns them to Light Photo- Cathode Input Phosphor: Csl v> <> X-Rays Grid The purpose of an image intensifier is to convert x-rays to light and then back to electrons. Along the course of this conversion, the electron flux and energy are amplified by the image intensifier with the end result being a greater amount of light emerging from the output phosphor. To actually view the pictures, you must couple an image intensifier through lenses to a TV camera. Noticing the word ‘‘Gain ” is showing up on that diagram? Yup - Lots of “Gains” - all of which would make good distractors for each other. Complete discussion to follow. But first, lets have a more in-depth looks at what happens between the Input Phosphor and the Output Phosphor - the so called “Electrostatic Focusing Lens. ” 114

E lectro static Focusing Lens: The “electron lens ” is a series of electrodes that are used to focus the electron beam as it flows from the photocathode towards the output phosphor. Starting at the Input Phosphor: • X-Rays have recently been converted to Light. • Light to be converted into electrons via the Photo Cathode. • Notice a slight curve in the Input Phosphor? This is to maintain the same travel distance to the central region (necessary to undistorted focusing) Input Phosphor Focusing Electrodes: • A series of electrodes work to focus the beam and speed up the flow (“flux gain”). Output Phosphor: • Output Phosphor works to convert electrons into a pattern of light. • Note that we are not dealing with X-Rays. Relative to a conventional phosphor this can be much thinner (because it is electrons to x-rays). • The benefit of keeping this thin is improved (or maintained) resolution in the shrunken “minified” image. Output Phosphor When landing on the output phosphor the image is: 1) Inverted and Reversed This occurs as a result of “point inversion” - as all the electrons pass through the same focal point in their journey from input to output phosphor. 2) Reduced in Size. This process increases the light photons about 50x relative to the input phosphor. Input Phosphor Photocathode Output Phosphor/ Electron Optics 115

Gain “Brightness Gain” - This is an old term describing the ability of the I.I to convert x-ray energy into light energy and then increase the brightness. Think about this as the increase in emitted light from the I.I. compared to the amount of light you would have obtained if the I.I. was never invented (and you just directly exposed the phosphorescent screen). Testable Point: Brightness Gain is the combined effects of Flux Gain and Minification Gain. Brightness Gain = Flux Gain x Minification Gain “Flux Gain” This describes the increase in magnitude of light coming from the output phosphor, relative to the input. This increase in magnitude is accomplished with a high voltage between the photocathode and the output phosphor (25-35 kV). The voltage causes electrons to accelerate and yields a gain in energy. Frat Version: Accelerating Electrons Increases Brightness “Minification Gain” Electrons from the large photocathode surface are concentrated on a small output phosphor. This increases the number of electrons per unit area, and therefore the energy per unit area. Q: When you “Mag” something you are actually doing what? A: Minifying it less Q: Does minifying make the image brighter? A: Yes - because it is the same number of electrons being concentrated on a smaller surface area. Frat Version: Reducing Image Size (Output vs Input) Increases Brightness **/’// review all this again later in the chapter. “Conversion Gain” (Gx)- This is a term describing the efficiency of an I.I. in changing incident x-rays into light at the output surface. This is considered to be a “new term” that is often used to replace brightness gain as an expression of efficiency. An important concept is that the older an I.I. is, the more it sucks at this. 116

Dealing w ith th e E lderly Nothing is worse than having an elderly relative. It’s such a drag to have to try and keep them from falling, and pooping everywhere. Now, obviously we still want them to be full codes - even if they have metastatic cancer and end-stage Alzheimers — gotta keep cashing those Social Security checks. It is definitely ok to ablate 1.0 cm RCCs in the demented population as well - it’s like 40 RVUs.... uh I mean... it’s good for patient care. But I think we can all agree that it’s best if they are locked up in old people jail (“nursing homes” we call them). Just like a human... the I.I. also falls apart with advanced age and becomes a burden. On the prior page, I mentioned the terms “brightness gain ” and “conversion gain ” as measures of efficiency - which are sorta similar. Regardless of which term you are using, the important trivia to remember is that the ability of the intensifier to increase brightness deteriorates with the age of the tube. Q: What are the consequences o f this? A: More and More Dose is Requiring to Produce the Same Level of Output Brightness. Critical Point: Older I.I. = More Dose. So what do you do about this old I.I. situation? • Solution 1: Use an aperture with a large hole in it. The downside to this is it increases the image noise. • Solution 2: Let the Automatic Brightness Control system do its job and crank up the juice (use more radiation). The downside to this is it increases the dose. • Solution 3: Put it in a nursing home (old person jail), then get another one. The general rule for this is the Image Intensifier is usually replaced when the conversion gain falls to 50%. The down side to this is that it costs money to replace things, but it’s ok you can offset the cost by cutting the Resident’s benefits / salary....and hire a million$/year VP or chairman to oversee the cost savings transition. 117

Geometric vs Electronic Magnification Not all Mag is created equal. There are two primary types - and obviously the difference is testable. Geometric Magnification - This is the type of magnification that I discussed previously in the the book (most recently in the mammo chapter). As a quick refresher: to magnify something, you generally bring it closer to the x-ray source Source to Object Distance MORE Magnification Magnification Object to Detector Distance People usually think about the “inverse square law” for reducing radiation, but obviously it works the other way too. If you get closer to the tube you get more radiation— and it doesn’t double, it squares. Electronic Magnification (Zoom) - If you decrease the input field of view by half, then only one fourth of the input phosphor will be irradiated. If all other parameters are held constant, the brightness will also drop to one quarter. This gets the attention of the automatic exposure control which will ramp up the juice (on average 1.4 - 2.Ox dose per setting increase). Testable Trivia: Both Electronic and Geometric Magnification Increase Dose (Geometric increases it more). *Remember focal spot blur is going to occur with Geometric. Quick and Easy way to reduce dose = Don’t use Mag unless you need to. Automatic Brightness Control System (ABC). I mentioned this thing earlier. Remember it is a device that works to optimize image exposure. In fluoro - think about it watching the light from the output phosphor and adjusts accordingly - for contrast and density (via turing up the mA, kVp, or both). Note that in regular X-Ray automatic exposure control systems primarily messed with the mA (quantity) and not so much the kVp (quality). For fluoro, you typically only see ABC used during electronic mag. Spatial Resolution Q: What improves spatial resolution? A: Electronic Magnification Q: What is the main limiter? A: The quality of the display 118

More Meat - S patial Resolution vs M agnification - This can be confusing, so let me spend a little extra time on it. I’ll start by talking about electronic or zoom magnification. The image intensifier can, via various forms of magic and spell casting, vary the size of the input radiation field, but still keeping the output field the same size. If for example the input field is cut in half then the size of the object being viewed is also cut in half but the output is still fixed. So relative to the original setting, the object has undergone a 2x magnification - and a double in spatial resolution. **Remember the dose goes up because halving the input field o f view, while keeping all the other factors the same, would cut the image brightness to a 1/4 o f the original brightness. To compensate for this, the amount o f radiation at the input o f the image intensifier must be quadrupled to compensate for reduction in juice. More Radiation = More Dose. This kind o f trade off is highly testable. Question writers love this stuff - really gets their dicks hard. I Eo 10 c\i Larger Input Spatial Resolution LESS Radiation Dose LESS I Eo m cvi Smaller Input Spatial Resolution MORE Radiation Dose MORE The automated brightness device used in fluoro is making changes for both contrast and density, and as a result it is making changes in mA, kVp, or both. This is a little different then how it works in regular x-ray automatic exposure - where it is mostly a change in mA and not so much kVp. Because it's a mixed change - the AVERAGE increase in dose (per setting) will increase 1.4 -2.0 times. If we are talking about raw numbers in exposure (quantity) then in needs to be quadrupled as explained above. So - to be clear: The real dose increase via the ABC (for both contrast and density) is on average 1.4-2.0x. The theoretical quantity increase needed to maintain brightness is quadrupled. The take home message is dose goes up. 119

- Now let us pivot to the confusing part. Geometric Mag - This is the kind of magnification you get by fucking around with the object-film distance and focus to film distance. The general rule (under standard conditions) is to avoid this by following two rules : (1) Keep the object as close to the film as possible (2) Keep the focus-film distance as large as possible Why is it so bad? Well, distortion and unsharpness right ? Things that lower spatial resolution that go hand in hand with this type of magnification. So why am I acting like there is confusion ? Cuz.... Mammo. Fucking Mammo Dude. Here is one more reason to hate mammo. Mag Views - they improve spatial resolution so you can see those low grade DCIS calcs better. Remember all that air gap crap? NO grid etc.... ? Ok - so let me try and explain. Magnification is produced by moving the object away from the film and closer to the focal spot. So why doesn’t this just cause blur like in all those line diagrams? Focal Soot The answer is the focal spot... well that’s most of the answer Magnification requires a very small focal spot. Remember that the focal spot used in mag views is smaller. Less Blur 120 Focal Soot Focal Soot

Another Way to Conceptualize This: If the objects are moved farther away from the detector, the divergence of the beam will increase and allow the objects to be projected onto different detector elements. Therefore resulting in greater ability to resolve them as two (instead of one) which is the definition of spatial resolution. T This leads me into my final point on this topic. The idea of ‘''Receptor Unsharpness. ’ Technically are three contributors to “unsharpness.’ (1) Geometric - the stuff we have been discussing (2) Patient - Patients are not the ideal mathematical target. This is because you have a thickness that is going to vary - typically decreasing x-ray attenuation towards the edges. The gradual change in attenuation at the edges can (and does) produce an indistinct edge. You also have organs moving inside the body which create blur and contribute to unsharp edges. You get the idea... (3) Receptor - Limitations in the ability of the system to record exact resolution. I think about this like a wooden ruler. How long is an inch? (hold up fingers).... But is it really that long? Get a microscope and the line on the ruler is wavy right? So you need a laser for exact measurements. Remember real precision is based on the speed of light and all that bullshit from undergrad physics — i.e. the meter is technically defined as the distance travelled by light in a vacuum in 1 / 299,792,458 seconds. 121

OK ...so where am I ’m going with this ? You can think of receptor unsharpness as the ultimate limit on resolution. Resolution in a digital system is limited by the size of the pixels of the captured image. The relationship between how good the receptor “intrinsic Receptor resolution” is and the Focal spot size (R/F) will ultimately determine if geometric magnification will increase or decrease the spatial resolution. If R/F is greater than about 0.5, then magnification radiography will reduce unsharpness, a value less than 0.5 and magnification will increase unsharpness. For the purpose of multiple choice, when dealing with general radiography R/F « 0.5 (intrinsic receptor resolution ~0.1 mm, focal spot size ~1 mm) so resolution decreases (blurring increases) with increasing magnification. In mammography with 0.3 mm (contact) and 0.1 mm (magnification) that focal spot size -is gonna be small enough that you are going to achieve a ratio that is allows for unsharpness to decrease. 122

Dose & C om pensation For G eom etric Mag: So, if geometric mag is so bad - what do you do about it? You can try and collimate. Collimation is electronically controlled with positioning changes made to irradiate a small region of anatomy - you change to a smaller input surface. The trick is that you are actually minifying the image less, as a smaller input surface is projected on the same output surface. Best Position o f the I.I. and X-ray Tube? X- ray tube far away, with the I.I. close. Having the image intensifier as close to the patient as possible does 3 things: (1) Decreases patient dose. •The ABC tries to make pretty pictures and more incident x-rays are detected when the 1.1. is close so the ABC doesn’t increase mA (to increase the number of x-rays generated) to make an adequate image. (2) Decreases scatter to the operator. •Less x-rays are made to scatter and less scatter escapes to irradiate the operator. (3) Increases image sharpness. Decreases focal spot blur and magnification. Raising the Imaging Receptor will ? Increases the dose - the machine will increase dose to compensate for the “source to image” receptor distance What is the general radiation change with each magnification? -Increases dose by 1.4 — 2.0 times -You get less light out, the ABC turns up the juice to maintain the picture Where is the ideal place to stand? On the same side of the patient as the imaging intensifier -It is preferable to position the C arm x-ray tube under the table Double the distance from the tube does what to dose? Decreases it by a factor of 4 (inverse square law). 123

A S m aller Field of V ie w Does W hat to Dose? So, the important (testable) distinction here is smaller FOV from collimation alone vs smaller FOV from magnification. Sm aller FOV - Collim ation Collimation is a type of beam restriction. In the most general sense, a restrictor is a device that “restricts” the x-ray beam at the opening of the x-ray tube. They come in three main flavors with collimators being the most testable. Collimators do two things well and have one negative side effect. • Pro: They Reduce Dose • Pro: They Reduce Scatter • Con: They Alter Exposure • Dose Reduction: The smaller the field of view, the less volume of the patient is irradiated. Remember that even a little bit of collimation goes along way because area is a square function. • Scatter Reduction: The smaller the field of view, the less scatter radiation (per unit size). The opposite is true also - bigger FOV, more scatter. Now, this is not the same with primary radiation. Think about it like this picture => Scatter is like an “additional” radiation tacked onto the primary cost (which is not change by collimation alone). • Altered Exposure Factors: With the reduction in scatter we also have a decrease in image “blackening.” To maintain contrast you will have to increase exposure factors. This causes confusion... for the purpose of multiple choice collimation reduces dose and scatter. Scatter Primary Field Size 124

Sm aller FOV - M agnification As we discussed on the prior pages, the image intensifier can vary the size of the input radiation field, but still keeps the output field the same size. If the input field is cut in half then the size of the object being viewed is also cut in half but the output is still fixed. Remember the dose goes up because halving the input field of view, while keeping all the other factors the same, would cut the image brightness to a 1/4 of the original brightness. To compensate for this, the amount of radiation at the input of the image intensifier must be quadrupled to compensate for reduction in juice. More radiation = More Dose. Let’s use this as an opportunity to discuss the topic of “Minification Gain ” - a made up word used by PhDs to sound smart, in more depth. The idea of “Minification Gain” can be thought of like the magnifying glass focusing the awesome power of the sun to incinerate defenseless ants. “Now Witness the Firepower of this Fully Armed and Operational Battle Station!” I’ll explain... If we have two input screens. Both of these have 10 cm input screens. BUT one has an output screen of 5 cm, and the other has an output screen of 2.5 cm. Both have the same total amount of light coming in, but one is focusing that light on a more concentrated area. Similar to how the magnifying glass isn’t “getting extra sun”, it’s just focusing that sun on a smaller area through a lens. The outcome is a roasted ant. Larger Output Energy More Spread Out MATH MOTHERFUCKERS! 102 / 52 = 100 / 25 = 4 Minification Gain = “4 ” Smaller Output Energy Less Spread Out MATH MOTHERFUCKERS! 102 / 2.52 = 100 / 6.25 = 16 Minification Gain = “16” 125

OK... but now let us work this problem backwards. Remember that this system (the 1.1.) magnifies shit by changing the input (not the output). Although, the actual way this change in field size ( “change from 9 inch mode to 6 inch mode”) is accomplished is by changing the focal zone by applying voltage. The higher the voltage, the more the beam is focused. Change in Focal Zone (by spell casting) Unchanged Output Size Less Total Electrons Used, Since only seeing the central part of the beam Utilized Electrons Utilized Electrons The ratio of out to in, makes the one using just the central part of the beam less “minified.” This decrease in minification gives you the appearance of the TV screen that you have magnified something. The problem is that you have used less total electrons, and as you have experienced this “minification loss.” In vs Out Utilized Electrons Comparing in to Out... The “out” is much smaller - i.e. “minified” In vs Out Utilized Electrons Comparing in to Out... The “out is only a little smaller i.e. “Minified Less” 126

Ok putting this together now. Smaller FOV “Mode” (achieved by using voltage to move the focal point, so that central core of electrons are utilized). Now... to nearly complete this journey lets discuss the idea of “Brightness Gain.” Brightness Gain is the product of two unrelated things. 1- Minification Gain = Same energy in concentrated on a smaller target (like the magnifying glass) increases the density of the energy (Brightness). 2- Flux Gain = A gain in the ability of the electrons in the I.I. system to produce light photons. You may think, 1 electron in = 1 photon of light on the way out. BUT because of the acceleration process (1 electron can = 50 light photons). So this amplifies the process (or reduces it if you are using less electrons). Text Book Answer: Brightness Gain = Minification Gain x Flux Gain Practical Answer to Reason Through MCQs = Minification Gain = Brightness Gain • Less Minification (Smaller FOV) = Magnified = Less Brightness More Minification (Larger FOV) = Less Mag = More Brightness 127

But Prometheus! How Does that Cause More Dose ? Automatic Exposure Rate Control (AERC) circuit (the artist formerly known as Automatic Brightness Control - ABC). This guy has one job... keep the signal to noise ratio happy. As long as signal to noise ratio is happy everything will be fine. It does this by monkeying around with the X-Ray exposure rate on the input phosphor of the I.I. (or flat panel detector). Per my life changing discussion above, the brightness gain of the I.I. decreases as the magnification increases. This fucks up the SNR (less signal), and the AERC circuitry spazes out by boosting the exposure rate. How much does it increase the rate ? For the purpose of multiple choice, I would say equal to the ratio of FOV areas. Example = It’s a typical day on Fluoro. You are performing a post op swallow study on a 600 pound, unemployed, Hep C positive, chain smoker who has eaten through her gastric pouch procedure (again...). To improve the spatial resolution you decide to change from the standard 30-cm mode to the 23-cm magnification mode. This change (30 cm to 23 cm) will cause the AERC to increase the x-ray exposure rate equal to the ratio of FOV areas. In other words, by a factor of (30/23)^ or 1.7x. Ok... don’t freak out. The chance of having to work the problem out is very unlikely (although you never know what these clowns will ask). I’m just including this because I want to give you a very complete answer. Critical Trivia: The AERC has a limiter (courtesy of Uncle Sam). It can’t just go bananas and fry the patient. This max number is a highly testable piece of trivia. (87 mGy/min (10 R/min) ^memorize both sets of units - fuckery with units abounds on Multiple Choice 128

So - let us just back up a second and be clear. If you traveled back in time to the 1970s, and you went to the 1.1. store you’d find that they come in a bunch of different sizes (23, 30, 35, 40 cm). That is the standard FOV diameter. The smaller ones (23s) are used for cardiac imaging... since cardiac imaging has been totally hijacked by our good friends the Cardiologists. Ok., back on track. I.Is come in different sizes (just like a penis). Small ones for the Cardiologists. Big ones for the Radiologists (GI/GU). Then there are “modes.” So you can change the perceived FOV (as discussed in the preceding pages) in these mag modes (from 30 to 23 to 17 etc..). So what about collimation? When the magnification mode is engaged (resulting in focal point change, and electron focusing from a smaller central area) - the x-ray beam collimation automatically adjusts to match the x- ray beam dimensions on the now smaller FOV. X-Ray Tube Reduced FOV Mode Standard Collimator Adjusts for Coned in Input X-Ray Tube 129

Into the Weeds with Electronic Mag and Dose To touch on the next bit of fluoro related dose trivia, I need to briefly introduce the idea of KERMA and Kerma Area Product (KAP). I’ll mention it again later in the text. KERMA = Kinetic Energy Release per unit MAss. The best way to understand KERMA is to think of it as the TOTAL amount of energy deposited from the ionizing radiation. With that TOTAL amount divided by a unit of mass to give it a more quantifiable dose. Why did I capitalize TOTAL? Because when the radiation strikes a unit mass it will not only create some movement of particles (kinetic energy), but also some amount of heat. Just measuring what goes on with the electrons is not the TOTAL amount of energy (there is also that heat). As a result, some smart people figured out that the best way to measure both of these components was to measure it at the moment just before energy was transferred. Which brings us to the concept of “AIR KERMA” - which is an estimation of how many photons are in a unit of air prior to the energy striking the skin. The idea is that you are estimating the peak skin dose based on the potential transfer of energy. Kerma-Area Product (KAP) - Is the amount of Kerma (potential dose) multiplied by the cross sectional area of the x- ray beam. This provides a better way to measure the total radiation potentially incident on the patient. Think of it more as a measurement of total radiation used in the exam, more than the actual dose in the patient. KAP = Dose x Cross Sectional Area Conceptually understand that if you increase the juice, but concentrated it in a smaller area that you might not change your KAP (even though you increased the juice). Now that I’ve got that out of the way - the testable trivia related to mag and fluoro: • Electronic Mag increases the Air Kerma (and therefore skin dose). Why ? Should be intuitive - you turned the juice up. More juice concentrated on a smaller spot. • Electronic Mag does NOT increase the KAP. Why? Because even though you increased the juice you also decreased the cross sectional area. They cancel out - keeping the KAP the same. 130

If you are more confused now than you w ere 10 pages ago, just rem em ber these 3 things: MOST CRITICAL TAKE HOME POINTS - FOR LOWER DOSE: 1. Position the patient away from the source, and close to the I.I. / Detector 2. Use the smallest field of view by collimating (this also improves contrast) 3. Avoid magnification

High Level Control The US Government has one solution to all problems: Regulation - and not the good kind of regulation Warren G used to talk about (when he hooked a left on Two One and Lewis). Along those lines, the Uncle Sam has set regulations to limit Air Kerma rates - to minimize peak skin doses. That regulation is 87 mGy per min. WTF would they pick 87 and not 90 or 100 ? Seems pretty random. W ell... as is expected, government bureaucracy continues to lag about 30 years behind reality so they chose an official exposure limit of 10 “Roentgens” per min - which then coverts to about 87 mGy/min. Just remember 87 mGy. • 8.7 mGy or Air Kerma is about 1 Roentgen of Exposure. But what if your patient is getting disability payments because he/she is too fa t to get out o f bed? In that situation, even an entrance dose of 100 mGy/min (over the line!) would still likely result in an inadequate image. Fluoro machines have what is called “high level control, ” which allows you to turn up the juice on these fat cows. Uncle Sam allows this HLC up to 20 Roentgens/min (176 mGy/min). Testable Points: • Normal Air Kerma Limit = 87 mGy/min (10 Roentgens per min) • High Level Control (Really Fat Level Control) = 176 mGy/min (20 Roentgens per min) • In “high level mode, ” you must have audible or visual alarms (in addition to the normal time alarm used in normal fluoroscopy.) Gamesmanship - Watch your units!!! It’s the oldest trick in the book to change up units and keep the numbers the same. Don’t just memorize numbers - you gotta have those units on there. 132

Artifacts Pincushion Distortion: With a large field of view you can sometimes get the appearance of bent lines at the periphery (lines that should be straight). The inward bowing pattern is said to resemble a pincushion. S Distortion: This is a similar artifact to the pincushion, also seen more in larger fields of view. The etiology of this artifact is an interference of the earth’s magnetic field on the flow of electrons heading towards the I.I. Making it better? The addition of a mythical material called “mu metal” supposedly can deflect the magnetic field (and protect against vampires). Pincushion Distortion - Due to Large FOV S-Distortion - Due to Earth’s magnetic field Flare or Glare Artifact: If you have a transition from heavy attenuation to minimal attenuation - you can see bright white “Glare” at the periphery near the decreased attenuation. This is from an overproduction of x-rays in this thin area, to compensate for the nearby thick area. Another artifact (saturation) which is described on the following page can look similar with a gradient appearance - see discussion on the next page. Flare / Glare Artifact - Image becomes brighter with transition to less attenuation Lag Artifact: You move the I.I. and the ghosted image remains superimposed from the prior field. 133

Vignetting Artifact: Because distances from the focusing point to the outer phosphor tend to vary, with the closest path in the center and the farthest path at the edge, you can end up with a dark periphery and a light center. Vignetting Artifact - Edges are darker than center Saturation Artifact: If the dose is cranked up to try and penetrate a very dense object (classically metal), you can end up with regions around the metal appearing very bright. Saturation Artifact The I.I. System has many limitations - one of which is dynamic range. The way to think about this is that the receptor requires a goldilocks amount of radiation - too much and it saturates (turns totally white) too little and it under penetrates (too dark). You will see this saturation - which they can also call "flare" or "veiling" - in two scenarios 1- the incident X-ray beam passes through low density anatomy (like the lung), resulting in a higher flux than can be tolerated — makes it all white, which limits evaluation 2- the incident X-ray encounter metal — they all get blocked. The automatic brightness kicks in and turns up the juice. The metal still block the X-rays, but the soft tissue around it gets extra radiation - saturating the system - and making the white band / flare. 1 1 i iv^ ivj 1 0 “Saturated” 134

Fluoroscopic M echanism Subtypes There are two different types: (1) The dinosaur one “Image Intensifiers ”, and (2) The new one “Flat Panel Detector. ” I ’ve completed my discussion of the dinosaur one (the 1.1.). Now let us turn our attention to the modern type - the Flat Panel Detector SVs t e t f ' G rid V Rays V Entrance Surface of FPD “Carbon Fiber - to protect components Csl Needles Act as a Phosphor Turns Photons into Light Photodiode Array Absorbs light and converts it into electrons Read Out Elem ent ____ (T ransistors and G ates) Determine charge of elements in photodiode array and construct image. No television camera is needed in FPD systems As you can see all that I.I. stuff doesn’t happen with the modem system (oh, don’t worry you will still get tested on it). 135

Vocab: “Pitch ” There is a thing called pitch. Essentially it is the linear dimension of a detector element. This is different than the “pitch” you think about with CT (more on that later). “Fill Factor” There is a thing called fill factor. The system isn’t 100% efficient, only a portion is actually sensitive to light. The ratio of the sensitive area over the total area is the fill factor. As the detector element gets smaller, you get better spatial resolution, but the “fill factor” decreases. In other words, a smaller detector element will have superior resolution but will require more radiation. “M atrix” - The matrix is the number of detector elements (or pixels) on the surface of the FPD - in each dimension (horizontal and vertical). 1 Detector Element = 1 Pixel Math problem: If the matrix size is 1100 x 1100, and the FOV is 25cm, what is the pixel size (or detector element)? the formula is: Pixel = FOV / Matrix. But, I was trying to get sneaky with the units (a very common trick): 25cm = 250mm. 250/ 1100 = 0.23mm 136

Binning: There is a thing called binning. Taking several detector elements (DELs) and making a large DEL. The idea is the you reduce the amount of data (and reduce Quantum Mottle - less variation in x-ray photons from pixel to pixel). o o o o o o o o o o o o oooo 4 Pixels x 4 Pixels 2 Pixels x 2 Pixels 1 Pixels x 1 Pixels *No Binning * 2 x 2 Binning * 4 x 4 Binning Less Mottle means you can reduce radiation and keep the same noise. Larger DEL does reduce the spatial resolution When is “Binning” especially useful? -W ith large FOV (when there are too many pixels in the image) If there is no “binning, ” the spatial resolution changes in what way for different fields o f view (in a FPD system)? No change. If you have binning, large FOVs will have less spatial resolution (but can use less radiation to maintain the noise level). Remember “binning” is a FPD thing, not an I.I. thing What usually happens (without employing binning) with smaller FOVs? Dose is increased to reduce quantum mottle. If you employ binning, dose can be reduced. For example, if you combine 4 DEL you can reduce dose by 50% “Frame Averaging (Recursive Filtration) ” - This is an image process feature that adds several images together with different weight factors. Pro: It’s done to reduce quantum mottle and increase the signal. SNR improves. Cons: It increases the susceptibility to motion artifact and ghosting. *Critical Point = Binning Improves SNR oo oo oo oo oooo oo oo oo©o oo oo oooo 2 Pixels x 2 Pixels 1 Pixels x 1 Pixels * 2 x 2 Binning * 4 x 4 Binning 137

A rtifacts (FPD): Are artifacts seen with I.I.s the same as FPDs? Nope. FPDs do NOT have pincushion, S distortions, vignette, glare, or saturation artifacts Bad Pixel - appear as either white or black spots. One method to correct for this (often built into the system) is to interpolate in order to fill the data. Lag Artifact (Ghosting) can also occur with FPD. This tends to occur if the exposure uses very high radiation. S patial Resolution: What limits spatial resolution o f an I.I. fluoro system? -The television display system. The resolution of a TV depends on the raster (scan) lines, the bandwidth, and the FOV. Does the display limit the spatial resolution o f a FPD system? Nope, FPDs usually have displays with the same matrix as the image receptor. Vertical Resolution is limited by? The number of raster lines that the display monitor uses. You have to alternate white and black to reproduce a line pair image on the I.I. or FPD, so the max number of line pairs in the vertical direction is half the number of actual lines used. Because the bars in the line pair pattern may not totally line up with the raster lines, it’s less than that so you need to correct with the Kell Factor (Kell Factor is some derived factor: 0.7). R a s te r L in es x K ell F a c to r V e rtic a l R es o lu tio n = 2 x FO V So decreasing the FOV (mag mode) improves spatial resolution I f you measure spatial resolution at a diagonal (45 degrees to the raster lines) does it get better or worse? Better - spatial resolution improves by a factor of V2 over the vertical or horizontal resolution Better pure spatial resolution FPD v.v I.I. ? I.I. systems are better, and change with FOV. 138

Pulse Fluoro With conventional fluoro we have a long very low continuous mA. Pulse fluoro is pulsed. In other words, it’s NOT continuous mA but instead pulse of higher mA. Sorta like this: low mA «SRR-!SP : " " ' higher mA l l . 1 1 I I i ii n Regular Fluoro Pulsed Fluoro Now here is where the testable trivia, and potential trickery comes in. If you keep the rate at 30 pulses/second your dose is UNCHANGED. What? I thought pulse fluoro reduced dose? It totally can, but doesn’t have to. Here is what is happening: Regular Fluoro: Continuous low mA with higher “S” = mAs Pulse Fluoro: Pulsed high mA with lower “S” = mAs Regular Fluoro mAs = Pulsed Fluoro mAs. “S” = Time i.e. mAs So when does Pulse Fluoro reduce dose? If you drop the rate below 30 pulse/second. People usually use 15 pulse/second as an example - and I’ll show you why in a second. But first I want you to look at this diagram and understand why the dose starts to drop below 30ish. 20 “units” Same higher mA 30 F/ 20 “units” 10 “units” Throwing all the dose in a bucket Testable Trivia: • Pulse Fluoro is good for moving patients (Wiggling Babies) - Gives you sharper images with less motion blur • Pulsed Fluoro can reduce dose - when the frame rate is below 30 frame /second • People always use a drop of 30 to 15 frames per second as an example because that equals a dose reduction of 30%. Math to get there isn’t important, just understand it’s not a direct 1:1 thing. 50% reduction in pulse rate = 30 % reduction in dose. • Be careful how the question is worded - because a lower frame rate will have more mA per individual pulse - but the overall mAs will be decreased relative to regular fluoro below 30 frames per second. 139

Spatial Resolution is Limited By: FPD systems are limited by? - Detector element size (around 2.5-3.0 lp/mm) I.I systems are limited by? TV systems (1.0-2.0 lp/mm for GI, and 2.0-4.0 lp/mm for angio) 8 Factors Affecting Spatial Resolution: FOV - smaller FOV = better resolution Focal Sport Size - usually not an issue unless you move the relevant anatomy away from the image receptor Image Receptor Limitations - detector element for FPD and television for I.I Motion and Temporal Factors - motion creates ghosting Dynamic Range - This is only an issue for I.I systems - with variability in very dense or very transparent stuff Pixel Binning - Binning increases pixel size - reduced spatial resolution (but improves SNR) Frame Averaging - increases SNR (less mottle), more susceptible to blur Pulsed Fluoro - Dose is administered in pulses, instead of continuously. You get less motion artifact (better spatial resolution in moving objects) and overall less dose (if less than 30 frames per second). OA How is spatial resolution tested for? - Lead bar pattern How is distortion checked for? - Use a mesh screen or plate. Look for straight lines (not pincushion or S distortion). 140

M onkeying Around w ith th e kVp Depending on the study you are doing, you may need to monkey around with the kVp to optimize the exam. Barium vs Iodine: In the next few pages I’m going to talk about fluoro in Angio, but I might as well introduce this idea now. Remember this idea of a “k-edge” ? It’s the energy where absorption (and therefore image contrast) is maximized. Usually for a study using IV contrast (iodine) you are going to pick a kV of around 70 - which will place the average energy around 35 - landing it flush on the k-edge of Iodine (which is 33 keV). But what about Barium? Most of fluoro is barium, and the k-edge of barium is pretty similar to iodine (it’s like 37). So it s gonna be the same right? Nope.. .when you do a barium exam the amount of contrast you give is proportionally much higher than the dose of IV contrast. As a result you don’t have a problem seeing it. Instead the problem is penetrating it. As a result you need to pick a higher kV (like 100 or more). Summary: • kVp for Angio Study (Using low volume of Iodine) = 70 kV. Chosen to max out K-Edge • kVp for Barium Study (Using higher volumes of Barium) = > lOOkV. Chosen to max out penetration. kVp and Dose Earlier in the text I’ve mentioned that INCREASES in kVp result in LESS dose. This is a subject of confusion. This is how I think about it - patient dose is related to the photoelectric effect and to the amount of scatter that does not exit. If you increase the kVp you increase the average energy of photons - causing a drop in the photoelectric effect, and generally speaking the scatter that will exit the patient (which increases as the kVp does). This increase in kVp will cause a greater risk for the tech (from scatter). But Prometheus increasing kVp causes more overall x-ray production (rule o f 15%) and more overall x-rays (more bremstraahlung) - so more ionization ? True. Let me clarify - increased kVp slightly INCREASES organ dose - as you have more scatter, and that scatter travels further inside the body. BUT this is more than offset by reduced skin dose. So it depends on the wording of the question. Also, this is why if it’s a CT dose question - an increase in kVp is going to INCREASE dose - because you are rotating - spreading your skin dose. Also, if you assume that if you increase kVp by 15%, and decrease mA by 50% - then for sure you will have less dose — probably. Bottom Line: Increased kVp = less skin dose, slightly more organ dose. If you compensate for the increased kVp with a drop in mA then dose decreases period. For CT questions: increased kV = increased dose. ■ 141

Fluoro in IR Focal Spot: You are trying to visualize small things (tiny vessels in real time) - this means you need a small focal spot + large number of x-rays. To deal with this small focal spot, local heat exchanges must be added to avoid melting the whole unit. The anode angle is smaller when compared to conventional x-ray. The smaller angle leads to an increased heel effect. Fortunately, in IR the heel effect remains zero because you use a small field of view and small image detector (only allows the central portion of the x-ray beam to be imaged). Focal spots can be exchanged (large, small, micro) - depending on the need for resolution. kVp: As mentioned on the prior page, the best kVp to use with contrast is between 60-80 kVp (average beams hit that k-edge nicely). A higher kVp loses iodine contrast. Filter: There is a thing called an “equalization filter” or a “soft filter.” These reduce intensity but do NOT completely block the beam. They are used to “taper” the radiation profile, and are often employed when imaging the leg, arm, or pediatric patients. Grid: Since grids are usually used, the testable trivia is that they are often NOT used on extremities or peds. Digital Subtraction Angiography (DSA) This system works by taking a single frame mask image and subtracting it from another single frame contrast image - with the goal of removing anything that is not moving. This leaves the stuff that is moving (blood). Image 1: “Hold Your Breath!... No Breathing No Breathing No Breathing” Breath holding important because any moving parts won’t get subtracted (diaphragm etc..). Stuff that doesn’t get subtracted - commonly the diaphragm (because the patient didn’t obey your breathing instructions) and bowel gas will look dark on a white background. Image 2: Inject the IVcontrast. The contrast will be moving (through the blood) - and will hopefully be the only dark thing you seen. DSA Trivia: For the purpose of multiple choice, DSA is performed at a kVp of 70. This is an attempt to exploit the k-edge of iodine (33). 142

Patient Dose Most IR systems use a pulsed fluoro (helps reduce dose). 50% of the dose is delivered in the superficial 3-5 cm of skin/fat • The depth of this 50% depends on the kVp and filtration (higher kVp + Copper Filtration = more penetration) • For a body (or body part) measuring less than 10 cm (a baby for example) - the grid should be off • A thicker (fatter) patient gets more skin dose. This is because automatic brightness control sees less penetration - then cranks up the dose. • Additional high dose situations: lateral and oblique views Patient and operator dose doubles with a lateral view (compared to PA) • The typical dose is about 0.3 - 0.5 mGy per frame at the entrance skin position (lOx - 20x more per image than fluoro) Total Dose = (dose per frame) x (frame rate) x (duration x number of runs) “Source to Skin Distance” (SSD) = how close the patient is relative to the x-ray source. If you are using under the table positioning the SSD depends on table height. Small SSD = High Dose Short Angiographers should stand on a platform (or on the backs of a kneeling trainee) so that the source to image receptor distance is kept at 100 cm or more. “Dose Spreading ” The idea here is to change the angle of the gantry (especially in a long case) in order to spread the skin dose over a broader area - decreasing the skin dose to any specific location. “Best Place to Stand” You should try and stand / work on the image receptor side of the patient. You are trying to avoid the large amount of Compton scatter radiation produced where the beam enters the patient. Image Receptor Source Most of the Dose (close to the source) Image Receptor Angiographer -Standing in the Correct Spot the Dose (close to the source) -Standing in the WRONG Spot 143

Dose Area Product (Kerma Area Product) This measures the radiation dose to air in mGy - multiplied by the collimator area - then reported in mGy/cm. The measurement is independent of beam location. It’s true that as you move the beam away from the patient the intensity decreases- BUT, the beam spreads out more. These two things occur in equal amounts so the DAP (KAP) is NOT dependent on location. DAP (KAP): Low Dose to Large Skin Area = High Dose to Small Skin Area Magnification - will increase Air Kerma, but NOT KAP Obviously this doesn’t help you grade risk of a skin bum. Instead it is used to estimate total energy deposited in patient, effective dose, and cancer risk. If something reduces the DAP (KAP) it probably also reduces the scatter and the patient’s dose. Interventional Reference Point (IRP)- This describes the use of an ionization chamber with a set reference point (15 cm closer to the source than the isocenter o f the IR system) to measure radiation emitted from the source. Skin dose can be above or below this point. Because IRP ignores geometry, table attenuation, and back scatter, it probably over or underestimates patient’s skin dose every time - but it is currently the best thing available. Testable Trivia: The dose (outside lead) standing 1 meter from the patient is about 1/1000 of the dose received by the patient. 144

Regulatory Doses: Skin Doses: No High Level Control (HLC) Present = 10 R / min (87 mGy/min) HLC on = 20 R/ min (176 mGy/min) • During Image recording = no limit if pulsed High Level Control The maximum entrance exposure is 10 R/ min in a conventional setting. An option for high dose is “specially activated fluoro” or “high dose control”, where you can have rates up to 20 R/min. The trick is an audible alarm must be on when this is used. • Below 2 Gy - No action needed • 2-5 Gy - advise patient to watch for bums - especially 10 days post procedure • Above 5 Gy - procedure and dose should be reviewed by physics • 2 Gy - Early Transient Erythema • 3 Gy - Temporary Epilation (hair loss) • 6 Gy - Chronic Erythema / “Main Erythema” • 7 Gy - Permanent Epilation (hair loss) • 10 Gy - Telangiectasia • 13 Gy - Dry Desquamation • 18 Gy - Moist Desquamation / Ulceration • 24 Gy - Secondary ulceration Operator Doses: • You get about 0.1% of what the patient gets - at 1 meter • In 1 year - you typically get about 5 mSv • Regulatory dose limit is 50 mSv per year • Conceptus dose limit is 0.5 mSv per month High Yield Points: The tube goes under the patient, as far away as possible (largest SSD possible) • The receptor goes as close to the patient as possible * Use pulsed fluoro and collimate when possible Move the beam around as much as possible (don’t just bum up the dude’s skin in the same spot) * Avoid electronic magnification as much as possible • No grid on babies or extremities 145

e CHAPTER 7: ■ PROMETHEUS UONHART, M.D. - Just like with mammo (and fluoro), the differences between regular diagnostic x-ray, and CT lend themselves easily to multiple choice questions. So what is different? Most CT scanners today are “3rd generation” - which means the x-ray tube and the detectors spin around the patient in synchrony. CT X-ray tubes use tungsten alloy targets placed on high speed rotating anodes. CT tubes are designed to operate at reasonable voltages (between 80 -140 kV) with very high tube currents up to 1000mA. CT scanners have both small (0.6mm) and large (1.2mm) focal spots. Typically you need a large focal spot to handle all that power - lOOkW. Regular Dx ■ CT ■■■ ■ ; ................. ............ .. .............. mA 200-800 mAHIGH up to 1000 kVp 50-120 kVp 80-120 Focal Tube Spot 1.0 -1.2mm Focal Spot 0.6-1.2 mm The x-rays are filtered to remove low energy x-rays that would only increase dose. Filtration Mechanisms: Copper or Aluminum (6 mm) is used to filter the x-ray beam • Heavily filtered beam can have a half value thickness of up to lOmmAl Bow Tie filters are used to compensate for uneven attenuation of the beam by the patient. These filters attenuate less in the center and more on the edges. Bow Tie Filters are made of low Z materials - like Teflon (to reduce hardening differences). • The x-ray tube anode-cathode axis is positioned perpendicular to the imaging plane to reduce the heel effect. Bow Tie Filters: Compensate for uneven filtration Reduce Scatter Reduce Dose Bow Tie Filter Creation of Consistent Energy Hitting the Receptors 148

Scatter Reduction: • A collimator is used both at the x-ray tube, as well as the detector, with the purpose of shaping the x-ray beam ( “Defines the section thickness on a single slice”). The collimator also reduces some scatter. • Additional scatter reduction is further accomplished with “anti-scatter septa.” Which are grids, but you don’t call them grids - they are “septa!” • Along those lines - don’t call it a comeback (I’ve been here for years) The CT fires off x-rays as it spins. The table moves the patient through. The detectors (highly efficient scintillation detectors) have a lot of information - which looks like a wavy mess (Sinogram). Then a bunch of math takes place (filter back projection), and you get a picture. Modem setups use an “iterative reconstruction” math program that allows more noise, so you can have less dose at the same diagnostic quality... or at least that is the idea. Detector Types: There are two types of detectors; Scintillation Detectors (the modem type), and Gas Filled Detectors (the ones used back when dinosaurs roamed the earth). The Scintillator detectors are way more efficient and easier to produce. 3rd Generation Multi-slice vs Single Slice: For multi-slice systems the number of detectors in the Z direction (head to toe) determines the number of slices that can be simultaneously acquired. The MDCT can acquire images with “isotropic resolution,” - which means they can do non-axial reconstmctions without stretching pixels. High Yield Point: Minimal slice thickness is determined by detector element aperture width in a modem CT. Fixed and Variable mA Long ago when dinosaurs roamed the Earth, the scanners had fixed kVp and mA. Basically, they just had an on button. New scanners can adjust dose via two methods — more on this later in the chapter: • (1) Scout image - They actually use the data in the scout to estimate density, and adjust the juice accordingly. • (2) On the fly - as the scan is happening “flow with the go” - with continuous modulation Yes... “flow with the go” - that is how Rickson said it. That is how I say it. 149 “Filtered” in Filtered Back Projection refers to “Sharpening of the projection data prior to back projection.”

Vocabulary: What is this “Ray ” — A “ray” is a measure of total x-ray attenuation along a line from the focal point to a single detector Projection What is this “Sinogram ” - A “sinogram” is a bunch of squiggly lines that represent the data from all the projections of all the tube angles (0-360). What is this “Projection ” - A “projection” is all rays at a given angle of the x-ray tube. In other words, it’s a series of rays that pass through the patient - at the same orientation. Fundam ental Trivia: What kind o f x-rays are used with CT? Highly filtered, High kV (average energy 75 keV) What is the matrix size for CT? Each Pixel is ? The matrix is 512 x 512, with each pixel representing 4096 possible shades of gray (12 bits). Wait? How did you get that? 212 = 4096 What is the relationship between pixel width and height to voxels? They are the same. Pixel W x H = Voxel W x H. The difference is a voxel has a 3rd dimension (depth), which represents the slice thickness. A voxel is a cube, a pixel is a square. How do you calculate pixel size ? You divide the Field of View / Matrix Size How do you improve spatial resolution ? You need to make the pixels smaller (matrix larger). Remember that Pixel Size = FOV/ Matrix Is mAs the same on CT as it is on plain film? The conventional definition of radiographic mAs is not useful in spiral CT. You have to use “effective mAs”. Tube Current (mA) x Length of time that a given point in the patient is in the beam. Basically, the exposure time. Obviously the exposure time is going to be related to the collimated beam width and table speed. I f you turn down the mAs, what happens to your images? Less mAs = More Noise 150

Pitch Pitch is a super important (testable) concept. The big take-home is that you have a choice; dose or spatial resolution. If you overlap your scanning beams, you will have better spatial resolution but more dose. If you uncoil the beams, your dose becomes less - but your spatial resolution suffers. The concept is pretty easy and intuitive - but you need to know the vocab words to get the questions right. So lets learn them. Pitch: Pitch is defined as the distance the table moves during the time of a single revolution of the tube divided by the beam width. If the movement of the table is less than the beam width, you end up with a fraction (a number less than 1). If the Pitch = movement of the table is more than the beam width you end up with a whole number (a number more than 1). If they are equal, then obviously you get a 1. Table Movement Beam Width “Pitch of 1” - This mean there is no overlap between slices. “Pitch Greater than 1” - This means the table moved faster than the beam, and you have a gap between your slices. The gap means your spatial resolution suffers, but you got less dose. “Pitch Less than 1” - This means the table moved slow, and your slices overlapped. This overlap gets you increased spatial resolution, but your dose increases. Pitch Greater than 1 Table Moves Fast Creates a Gap Spatial Resolution is Decreased Dose is Also Decreased Pitch Less than 1 - Table Moves Slow - Creates an Overlap - Spatial Resolution is Increased - Dose is Also Increased Example: Cardiac CT (where you really care about spatial resolution) is done with a pitch of 0.2 (very small). Trivia: A pitch less than 1 is sometimes referred to as “over-scanning” 151

The Hounsfield Unit: CT scanners generate a picture based on attenuation data (how many x-rays are blocked), but the important thing to understand is that the data acquired is all relative. The standard density of water is used as a zero point. Anything less dense than water is a negative number, and anything more dense than water is a positive number. The formula used to calculate HU is: HU = 1000 x (attenuation of material - attenuation of water) / attenuation of water. What is the relationship between HU and X-ray attenuation? When HU increases by 10 HU, x-ray attenuation increases by 1% Does changing the keVmess with the HU? Yup Why? I thought values were zero’d to water, and standard etc... ? Number are based on attenuation (blockage / absorption) of x-rays. Remember that changes in photoelectric interaction are going to change x-ray absorption. In general - since RE. dominates at lower energy, low keVwill create a higher H. U. Obviously the closer you approach the k-edge of a given substance the more impressive the increase in attenuation (and therefore H.U.) will be. For example, IV contrast will have a HU of around 100 HU at 140 kV, which will increased markedly to 400 HU at 80 kV (where the average energy is going to land flush on its k-edge). Will filtration change the HUs? It can. Remember filtration will change the “quality” of the beam - the average energy, etc. In other words, it can be thought of as changing the kV. So if you have significant “hardening” of the beam - via removal of the lower energy portions, that will increase the average energy and in general lower your HUs. This is the genesis of the “cupping artifact” described later in the chapter. Testable Trivia Summary: • Density is relative • Water is used as a standard - the density of water is set to zero HU • An increased in 10 HU is typically a 1% increase in attenuation • HU will change with keV - typically becoming larger with lower keV (because you are getting more attenuation via photoelectric interactions, k-edge etc...) • Filtration / Beam Hardening can alter the HU measurements. Classic example is the “cupping artifact” where things appear less dense in the center, and more dense along the periphery. 152

W indow W idth and Level: It’s a basic concept that is highly testable. The “Level” is the midpoint of the gray scale display (the “center”). You want your level at the attenuation of the thing you are interested in. For example, if you are interested in bone - you want a high level. The “window width” (aka “window”) is selected based on what you are comparing. If you are comparing things with very different densities you want a wide window. If you are comparing things with similar densities (example white and gray matter), you want a narrow window. How this concept can be tested? Really there are two main ways: (1) you can ask a typical window for lung, bone, liver, etc... This is partly memorization (see chart) but you can sorta figure it out based on the principals I mentioned above. (2) They can ask you below or above what level will give you a white or black reading. This kind of question is easiest to solve if you draw it out. For example, if your level is set at 100 and you width is 300, you will have 150 above and 150 below at some gray scale (150 is half of 300). In this case, above 250 will be seen as solid white and below a -50 will be seen as black. A second example shows the same thing using a more narrow window of 150. W indow , Level in HU Brain W 80, L +40 Lung W 1500, L -400 Abdomen W 400, L +50 Bone W 1600, L +500 +250 +175 2,00 Level “+100” -50 Level “+100’ +25 153

H ow the M achine M akes A P icture I | | | | | | | Detectors y Raw Data Projection -Filter Back Etc... Mathematical Filter “Kernel’ 1 Ready to Read / Store The general overview is like this. You have detectors rotating around the patient which are collecting the raw attenuation data (lots of 1 ’s and 0’s). This data is then processed in an algorithm (either filter back or the new iterative reconstruction). After that a filter or “kernel” is applied - either smoothing or sharpening the data. Finally, it shows up at your workstation and you can window the crap out of it. On the next few pages, I’m gonna walk through these various steps - pointing out what I think is testable. 154

Acquiring th e R aw Data There are basically two methods for doing this: Axial Scanning and Helical Scanning. Axial The table is stationary. The tube comes “on” and takes a picture. The tube shuts off and the table moves up a slice. The tube comes back “on” and takes another picture. Advantages: Better spatial resolution in the z-dimension since full image sets are taken. No partial volume effect along the long axis. • Less Partial Volume Artifact. The artifacts of partial volume with helical CT are noticed more along a curved surface. Classic example = skull. The table moves at a constant speed. The tube is on the entire time. Advantages: • Primary Advantage = Way faster • Secondary Advantage = Post acquisition flexibility in the selection of slice location and lower probability of anatomic discontinuities between adjacent slices containing moving anatomy in the chest/abdomen scans. You also have less “stair step” artifact on reconstructions. Helical What if you Monkey with the Scanning Beam Width? • Increased Beam Width = Reduced Scan Time (larger coverage with 1 turn) • Increased Beam Width = Reduced Motion Artifact (less scan time) • Increased Beam Width = Increases Partial Volume (more divergent beam) • Increased Beam Width Does NOT change the radiation dose (mAs is unchanged, because even though the scan time is less, a larger area of tissue is scanned). s:'i What if you Monkey with the kVp? • Dose Goes Up: Unlike conventional radiography with automated exposure control, increasing kVp in CT will increase radiation dose to the patient. • Increased kVp = Image noise will decrease. • Iodinated contrast will be more conspicuous at lower kVp (such as 80 kVp), as the average energy of the x-ray beam will to the k-edge of iodine. T : 155

R aw D ate Projection Generating an image from the acquired data involves figuring out the linear attenuation coefficients of each pixel in the image matrix. In other words... math. Lots of math. Fortunately computers are good at math. There are a few different techniques for creating the image. Back Projection - This was the original way. After doing lots of math (literally millions of data points) - using sonograms etc... Eventually a value of attenuation is calculated for each pixel along a ray - traced through the patient. Filter Back Projection - This is the more modem way. Essentially a mathematical filter is applied prior to calculating the back projection. Just know it’s better than the old way - and it’s utilized by a lot of modem scanners now. Iterative Reconstruction - “Forwarded” information is compared to actual information and differences are used to correct the image. What you need to know is (a) it can correct for noise, so you can (b) use a lower dose. It requires some major computer power, that’s why older systems couldn’t use it. reconstruction algorithm (math) also referred to as a “filter.” What is actually happening is a tradeoff decision between spatial resolution and noise for each kernel. The selection is based on clinical parameters. “Smooth ” kernel generates images with low noise but with reduced spatial resolution. This is what you think about with a soft tissue kernel - like the “brain kernel.” “Sharp ” kernel generates images with higher spatial resolution but increased noise. This is what you think about with a “bone kernel" - the one you’d use for the skull, or the spine. Projection M ath em atical F ilter (K ernel) When people say “bone kernel’’ or “soft tissue kernel” - they are referring to a 156

S pecial Topic CT Fluoro Near real time imaging, with the CT image constantly updated (6 per second). Low tube currents (20-50 mA) are used to minimize radiation doses. . . . . . . . . Special Topic Dual Energy At a single photon energy' is it possible to tell two different materials apart? Nope How does dual energy CT Work? -Scan is acquired using both 140 and 80 kvp (instead of just 120). The H.U. of each pixel is obtained atboth energies. The image is dirtier, but you can do all kinds of stuff like characterize the material {what is the renal stone made of?), or do a virtual non con. It all has to do with different atomic numbers absorbing photons differently. S pecial Topic C ardiac Im aging Cardiac imaging is best performed during diastole. There are two main methods of gating; prospective and retrospective - I’m certain the differences in the two would make good multiple choice questions. -Prospective: “Step and Shoot” - R-R interval • Pro: There is reduced radiation b/c the scanner isn’t on the whole time • Con: No functional imaging. Susceptible to motion artifact. • Trivia: Always axial, not helical. You need a slow heart rate (50-65 bpm) -Retrospective: Scans the whole time, then back calculates • Pro: Can do functional imaging (evaluate contraction and wall motion) • Con: Higher radiation (use of low pitch - increases dose) 157
