Keep that dirty dick in your pants 48 hours prior to exam. 158

Signal to Noise Remember that noise is “Data that contributes nothing useful to the image.” People often make the mistake of thinking that Quantum Mottle = Noise. It’s true that Quantum Mottle (essentially a lack of photons) is the major contributor to noise - but it is not the only contributor. Scatter is also going to contribute “nothing useful to the image,” and therefore is a factor. Why do I bring this up? Because it will make understanding how increasing the number of x-rays does not lead to a 1:1 improvement in signal to noise. It’s also a Poisson distribution thing - but I think that is harder to conceptualize. Signal to Noise is a better way to think about “noise”. Remember this is a quantitative method for comparing the signal (stuff that contributes to the image) vs the noise (stuff that doesn’t contribute to the image). If your signal is really strong - you can tolerate more noise. If your signal is weak - even a little bit of noise can wreck your image. The bottom line is: Signal changes directly with increased x-ray flux. Twice the x-rays, Twice the Signal Noise changes by a factor of Vn . In other words, Twice the x-rays, V2 or 1.4 the Noise. Example 1: If mA goes from 200 -> 400 mA, SNR = 2/root 2 or 2/1.4 - so 40% increase in SNR Example 2: If mA goes from 200 -> 800 mA, SNR = 4/root 4 or 4/2 - so double the SNR *notice you needed 4x the photon flux to double the SNR All of the following will INCREASE (improve) the Signal to Noise Ratio: • Higher mA • Longer rotation time • Higher kVp • Larger slice thickness • Large pixel (Pixel Size = Field of View / Matrix Size) • Decreased pitch (having the patient move through the gantry slower) Why not just crank all that shit up to the max 7 Because your dose would be through the roof. It’s a trade off between burning the patient and having a diagnostic exam. Personally I prefer my exams to be diagnostic (and the patients extra-crispy) - but the ACR just won’t go for that. For multiple choice - the answer is always dose “as low as reasonably achievable” - which means a grainy mess is best. Repetition - Rehashing Filters - As mentioned previously, how noticeable the noise is depends on the reconstruction filter; using a sharp, high- resolution or “edge enhancement filter” will worsen noise (although spatial resolution improves). A smoothing filter, which is actually called a “noise reduction filter” by some will make the noise less noticeable (at the expense of spatial resolution). 159

Increasing mA doesn’t d ecrease noise WTF? But Prometheus ?!? How the fuck does noise increase with increased mA? What about all this quantum mottle shit you ’ve been talking about? This is confusing. Seriously, all you need to know is this: A) Increased mA = Improved Signal to Noise. B) Increased mA = Improved Signal to Noise - But NOT 1:1. You need 4x the mA to double the SNR. C) Increased mA = Decreased Quantum Mottle If you really really need a “deeper understanding ” - then the next 3 pages are for you. Don’t say I didn’t warn you. Statistics... Most people don’t understand it. Even “smart” people like Radiologists usually don’t understand it. Well Motherfuckers, that shit is about to change because I’m gonna blow your fucking minds with some knowledge. This thing =>, the so called “normal” distribution (also called the “Gaussian Distribution” - if you want to sound pretentious). The one that looks like a bell. This is the curve college professors have been using to fuck over students for decades. Test writers are still using it to fuck people over. Somebody at the CORE exam headquarters is probably drawling one up right now, figuring out how many standard deviations they will let pass this year. Besides understanding that test writers like these, the other thing I need you to know is that: The normal distribution is governed by two things: (1) mean and (2) standard deviation. Problem is this isn’t the only distribution. 3 SD SD (Standard Deviation) SD is the square root of the variance. Variance is the average of the squared difference of the mean (i.e the difference between expected results). 160

Poisson Distribution There are actually a bunch of different distributions - including this one called the “The Poisson Distribution” - which for some reason always makes me think of Aquaman (not sure why). Remember I mentioned that you need to know that normal distributions are governed by the mean and SD? I mentioned this because it’s the key different between normal and Poisson distributions. The shape of a Poisson distribution is governed ONLY BY THE MEAN. OK... stay with me now. X-Rays, and gamma rays, follow a Poisson distribution (not a normal distribution). BUT!! For the sake of simplification people who understand math decided to use normal curves to approximate them. It’s complicate! to why - just know that is what happens. distribution governed by the mean only, and make it governed by the mean and the SD. To convert the Poisson Distribution to a Normal Distribution equivalent we are going to just say the SD is the square root of the variable. What is the variable ? Let us just say it’s the mA. So now we have two factors - we have the mean, and we have our fake ass standard deviation. You know what? The curve drawls out pretty close to the normal mean curve. Want to be more exact ? It’s probably better to say that the variable “N” is : N = number of photons that land on the detector, which has a certain detector efficiency that is uniform, and is actually recorded successfully. Or you could just N = mA if you don’t want to hurt your brain. Also, it’s likely to be used as mA in test questions. I guess they could say kVp since technically that increases flux also. Those fucks.... I told that fuck down at the league office— who's in charge o f scheduling? 161

Noise Revisited So let us just back up and look at noise like this. Noise is the shit that limits your PRECISION, not necessarily your accuracy. Accurate but NOT Precise NOT Accurate NOT Precise Now let us think about this like targets. Anything that lands in the black is SIGNAL, anything that lands in the grey is NOISE. Lets play around and look at some examples: Precise but NOT Accurate Accurate and Precise More Noise Less Precise More Precise ■B This first example has only a few dots. Lets say this represents “low mA.” From a distance you might be able to make out that the center is “object of interest” but maybe not. A little bit of noise RELATIVE to the low signal makes a big difference. This one is shit. This represents “medium mA.” Little better this time right ? More signal makes the grouping in the middle “more precise.” BUT I want you to notice that the exposure in the grey has also increased. So total number of noise is up, but the relative amount of signal to noise is improved - thus it looks better. This represents “high mA.” This time it’s even better. More photons, better tighter group in the middle. Obvious bull’s-eye. BUT! more overall total impacts in the grey (noise). My point is to show you the difference between total noise and relative noise. Now let’s look at some math again. Less Noise 162

MATH!!?!?! BUT I’M A DOCTOR! I DON’T UNDERSTAND MATH! I’ll be gentle.... Review time: • We said that “N” was going to be our photon flux or mA. • We also said the V N was gonna be our stand in for standard deviation, so this will actually represent our raw noise. So to illustrate this concept with math: Noise = V N mA of 4 = Noise of 2 m A of 16 = Noise of 4 Goes Up Now lets talk about RELATIVE NOISE. • When Radiologists say noise they mean relative noise. • When Test Writers say noise they also mean relative noise! Relative Noise = V N / N SD = Noise V N = Noise Think about standard deviation as noise. Remember that a high standard deviation would make our target grouping less precise right?? We’d have more variation all over the place. Less standard deviation would be more points closer to the mean or the center. Relative noise is also called “the Coefficient o f Variation” So to illustrate this with math: mA of 4 — V 4 / 4 = 0.5 m A of 16 = V 16/16 = 0.25 Goes Down Now lets look at SIGNAL TO NOISE: . SIGNAL = N • Noise = V N So to illustrate with math: mA of4 = 4 / V4 = 2 m A of 8 = 8 / V 8 =2.8 m A of 16= 1 6 /V 16 = 4 *** so you need 4x the photons to double the signal to noise RATIO. Summary Think about Noise as the thing that fucks up Precision. More Photons = More Absolute Noise More Photons = LESS Relative Noise If a test writer uses the word “Noise” they mean Relative Noise It takes 4x the Photons to Double the Signal to Noise Ratio because of Math. 163

S pecial Topic H alf V alue Layer (HVL) - ■ ■ /■ You may recall the term “Half Value Layer” from earlier in the text. As a refresher HVL refers to the thickness of a material which can attenuate an x-ray beam to 50% of its original intensity. Let’s review some trivia related to this: • In conventional radiography, the HVL of soft tissue in a human is assumed to be 3 cm. • In CT, the HVL of soft tissue in a human is also assumed to be 3-4 cm • In mammography, the HVL of soft tissue in a human is assumed to be 1 cm (because of the lower energy beam quality). C ontrast Resolution Contrast resolution can be defined as the ability to discriminate between structures with very similar attenuation characteristics (CT numbers). On the next page I’m going to talk about CT Spatial Resolution - and I’ll mention the fact that CT Spatial Resolution is total shit compared to mammo (and even regular plain film imaging). CT Contrast Resolution is a different story. CT Contrast Resolution is excellent - way way better than plain film. Think about it - with CT you can tell the difference between white matter and grey matter in the brain. With plain film... it’s either bone, air, soft tissue, metal, etc... Why is CT Contrast Resolution So Good? Minimal Scatter Reaching the Detectors - This is accomplished via (1) very tight collimation (both pre-patient and pre-detector) and (2) by windowing . A tight window really maximizes your contrast resolution. What is the limiting factor for contrast resolution ? Noise. What will improve the contrast resolution? Essentially any factor that reduces the amount or visibility of noise - so all the stuff I mentioned before (mA, kVp, slice thickness, matrix size, FOV, and reconstruction filter). 164

Spatial Resolution Spatial Resolution (frequency) -This is the ability to distinguish small objects that are close together (tell that they are separate). Practically, a bar pattern is used to measure this (line pairs per mm). Line pair resolution for CT is typically 0.5 to 1 Lp/mm - which is shit compared to mammo (remember that was like 11 Lp/mm). Factors that affect spatial resolution: Focal Spot Size • A larger focal spot, means the object details are spread out over several detectors. This degrades and blurs the image • Smaller focal spot = better spatial resolution Magnification More magnification blurs the image Detector Aperture Size • As the detector size is reduced the cranial-caudal resolution (z-axis) increases • The in-plane “x-y axis” is NOT affected by aperture size. Number o f Projections • More projections, more data. Better Resolution Reconstruction Slice Thickness The thinner the detector element aperture, the better the spatial resolution in the Z direction Spatial Resolution as a function ofpixel size and display field o f view: “Display Field of View” (DFOV) = Space defined by the user based on the anatomy size to be displayed. It is always less than (or equal to) scan field of view. Remember that Pixel Size = DFOV / Matrix Size. So increasing the matrix size or decreasing the DFOV will make your pixel size smaller. Smaller pixels = Better Spatial Resolution. Spatial Resolution Take Home Focal Spot (Smaller = Better): Determines Spatial Resolution in the X-Y Plane... side to side. Detector Size (Smaller = Better): Determines Spatial Resolution in the Z Plane (the long axis or Cranial-Caudal direction) 165

• As pitch increases, so does the width of the slice sensitivity profile (SSP). • As the SSP widens, the slice thickness increases. Thicker slices = Less Spatial Resolution. Spatial Resolution as a function o f Pitch: Quick Review o f Pitch • Doses in helical scanning with a pitch of 1.0 are similar to those from axial scanning. • If the pitch is < 1.0 then the dose increases because the slices overlap. • If the pitch is > 1.0 then the dose decreases because energy is more spread out. • The relationship is proportional. A pitch of 2 halves the dose. A pitch of 0.5 doubles the dose. Patient Motion (why it is important to ask the patient to hold his/her breath... and also to stop masturbating - at least during the scan) Voluntary and involuntary movement leads to blurring. • Blurring = reduced image resolution Spatial Resolution Related to Filters: Sharp Filter = Better Spatial Resolution (trade-off is more noise) • Smooth Filter = Crappy Spatial Resolution (trade-off is less noise) 166

Pixel Size (Spatial Resolution vs Contrast Resolution): • Holding matrix size constant and decreasing FOV will decrease pixel size. This increases spatial resolution but decreases contrast resolution (less photons per box) • Holding matrix size constant and increasing FOV will increase pixel size. This decreases spatial resolution but increases contrast resolution (more photons per box). Factors That Affect Spatial Resolution Factors That Affect Contrast Resolution ,J . ........ . : ■ : .. ....... ' ' ' ' Number of X-Rays (mAs, kV, pitch). More Focal Spot (smaller spot = better) dose (less mottle) will improve contrast resolution. I Detector Width (smaller detector = better) Slice Thickness (thicker more x-ray quanta less noise). Nyquist Limitations Reconstruction Method ■'Sampling" (Oversampling better) (Iterative > Filtered Back) ' ■ ' ' :vr .-Lg' Reconstruction Filter (example - bone “sharp" algorithm gives a higher spatial Reconstruction Filter (Soft tissue Bone) resolution) 111!!. C ritical Trivia: CT vs Plain Film Radiographs: Spatial Resolution = Plain Film is Superior Contrast Resolution = CT is Superior 167

Radiation Dose M easures: CT S pecific “Variation within the Scan Plane” - In regular Dx imaging, the entrance skin dose is much larger than the exit skin dose. In CT, because the scanner spins 360 degrees - the dose is symmetrically distributed. Obviously, the center is still gonna get less (body phantoms show it to be about 50% less in the middle - compared to the edge). When you compare a head to a body phantom the center dose is similar to the skin (smaller diameter). Body Scans: Surface is about twice the central dose. Head Scans: Central and Surface are very similar “ZAxis Variation ” - The Z plane occurs along the length of the patient (or phantom). This idea of Z Axis variation refers to the “tails” of radiation along the edge of the area being scanned. As a result, the profile of radiation is not limited to the primary area being imaged. If multiple scans are performed these tails add up with the original scan. “C TD I” - CT Dose Index - This is the radiation dose, normalized to beam width. There are subtypes - the difference of which could easily be tested on a multiple choice test. “Weighted CTDI” - This is 1/3 the central CTDI + 2/3 the Peripheral CTDI (expressed in mGy) • “Volume CTDI” (CTDIvoi)- This is obtained by dividing weighted CTDI by the Pitch. Remember that Weighted CTDI is the intensity being used and can relate to mottle. 168

- DLP = CTDIvoi x the length of the scan in cm. “Effective Dose”for C T : Effective Dose = k x DLP. Remember that “k” is a body part constant. Effective dose is going to be in Sv. Phantom Size - These CTDI numbers are based on phantoms. The body phantom is 32 cm in diameter. If the patient is larger than the phantom, then dose is over estimated. If the patient is smaller than the phantom, then dose is under estimated. Average Dose (CTDI - in mGy) Corrected effective dose is different Adult Head = 58 (effective dose 1-2 mSv) • Adult Abd = 18 (effective dose 8-11 mSv) ACR Reference Values. • Peds Abdomen = 15 CTDIvoi: • 75mGy for Head, There is a “Reference dose” set by the ACR at 75 percentile . 25 mGy for Adult Abd, - doses above that should be “investigated” and reduced if . 20 mGy for Peds Abd (5 year old) possible. Z-Axis Modulation - Dose Reduction Strategy: One fancy way dose can be reduced is by adjusting (“modulating”) the mA based on the density of each slice. For example, when scanning through the lungs you can dip the mA down then bring it back up through the upper abdomen. “DLP” - Dose Length Product CTDI Vol • Underestimated for Peds patients • Overestimated for disturbingly fat patients. The ones that make you wonder how they can clean their butts. Do they use some type of a tool? Rag on a stick ? The way this works is by using a lateral scout to gauge the density at each slice. That data, combined with empirically determined noise prediction, allows the computer to determine a noise index. So you can say - I will tolerate a noise index of 10 or 12 or 15 - depending on the study and how important fine detail is going to be for the diagnosis. Then the scanner will adjust the mA based on that at each slice selection to keep you at the lowest mA possible still maintaining a threshold above that noise index. 169

Strategies to Reduce Dose to the Breast: • Do the scan at reduced mA (problem is the images look like shit) • Use a milliampere modulation (adjust based on density) * this is the preferred method Shield the breasts with bismuth - you get artifact and a degraded image (beam hardening can falsely elevate HU directly deep to the shield). Dose Related Trivia: • Dose of 1 Chest CT is about equal to 100 PA + Lateral CXRs • CT of the extremities has a very low effective dose (< 1 mSv) because they don’t contain any radiosensitive organs. • Embryo dose in CT A&P is around 30 mGy. • Individual dose monitoring is mandated if the occupational dose is favored to be greater than 10% the annual dose limit (500 mrem). • If you increase the kVp - you will increase your CTDIW on both a head and body phantom. Risk of Radiation Induced C ancer per Dose? » i -5% per Sv = Adult : -Up to 15% per Sv for Child : -About 1/10th that for someone older than 50 Pediatric Considerations: • Reducing mAs works to reduce dose. • kVp can also be reduced. Typical CT kVp is between 70-140 for an Adult. 70 is a good generic choice for Peds. • Reduced techniques are possible because x- ray penetration is greater in children • Dose Reduction in head CT are more modest than peds belly kV Modulation (a good option in Peds): How much kV reduction you can get away with depends heavily on the thickness of the patient. The limiting factor is noise from a lack of penetrating power. If the patient is thin (or a kid) reducing the kV will actually improve the contrast resolution from less Compton - assuming the kV selected still has enough power to maintain the signal. 170

Protocol Modification: Big Fat Ass Quantum Mottle - The Fat Man’s Burden. As you may recall, Quantum Mottle is the result of an insufficient quantity of photons striking the detector. The fatter you are, the more photons you absorb, and the less signal reaches the detector. The consequence is a shitty signal / noise ratio. Noisy images are a plague on the super monstrously gigantic fat (very fat) extra fat-fat patient. Solutions: • Eat Less and Exercise: Impractical due to the delicious taste of cheeseburgers and McDonald’s french fries (“freedom fries” I call them). Plus... cardio sucks. • mA: The simple solution is to increase the mA. As we discussed with all that fancy math you need to quadruple the mA to double your SNR - or cut your relative noise level in half. The tradeoff is that doubling the mA will also double the dose. • mAs: If you increase the time per rotation of the gantry more photons will strike the patient and also the detector. This is the “s” in mAs. There are two downsides for this. The first is dose, which just like mA increases in a linear fashion. The second is movement. Longer scan times always equal more movement artifacts. It’s true with babies and it’s true with super monstrous gigantic extra-large fat people. • Pitch: In the same way that increasing the scan time increases the photon load to the patient, you can lower the pitch in an effort to reduce noise. Scan time and dose will also both be increased. • kVp: Increasing kVp will generate more penetrating power in the photons. Seems logical to think this is the solution to dealing with a thicker mass of tissue. The problem is scatter. Scatter from increased kVp is a plague on the super monstrously gigantic fat (very fat) extra fat-fat patient, and the consequence is a decrease in contrast resolution. Also, the dose is increased - this time in a non-linear fashion (directly proportional to the square of kV). Typical CT kVp is between 70-140. It is sometimes appropriate to increase the kVp to 150 for a fat patient - and it is commonly done. 171

kVp Modifications - IV Contrast First, let us review the implication of changing photon energy (keV) on HUs. Yes, HUs are set to a “zero” by using a water phantom. However, the HU values always depend on the kV and to some degree filtration. This reliance on kV is all about the shit we talked about in the first few chapters, mainly atomic number and the “k-edge.” Refreshing Memories: An abrupt increase in the attenuation of photons occurs at a photon energy slightly more than the binding energy of the K shell electron of the particular atoms interacting with the photons. This is the sweet spot for photoelectric absorption of the photons - or the “k-edge”. Exploiting this is an important part of imaging (and more importantly - something that can be testable). Low Energy - Easily Attenuated K Edge - Spike Corresponds with Binding Energy Higher Energy - Pass Right Photon Energy This is taken advantage of by selecting a kVp between 65-90 when using contrast agents like barium (K edge 37 keV) and iodine (K edge 33 keV). Bat Prometheus Why 65-90??? Why wouldn ’tyou pick 37 or 33? Because the beam is “polychromatic” - with the kVp representing the peak, and a relative minority of photons. The average energy is going to be between 1/3 to 1/2 the maximum energy - placing the k edge right in the meat of the band - maximizing that spike in attenuation via the P.E. Basic Concept: • HU Values tend to increase as kV decreases • Changes as more pronounces with high Z material (i.e. iodine). Example of HUs with Changing kV Iodine 80kV = 400HU 140kV = 100HU Soft 80 kV = 60 HU 140kV = 50HU Tissue For the purpose of multiple choice, CT Angio study = kYp of 100 (120 in a big fat person). 172

Over Beaming and Over Ranging Over Beaming: This is radiation that extends beyond the active detector area and is not used for imaging. The important concept is that the amount of over beaming decreases the more detector rows you have. For example - we have two rows here. The top one shows only a few slices acquired simultaneously. The bottom one shows a larger amount acquired simultaneously. Hopefully you can see that the larger the beam width (more slices acquired simultaneously), the less the excess in dose. Collimated beam, striking the ACTIVE detector area. * Excess Dose ■ ■i m ■ ■m ■ ! Over Ranging: With spiral (helical) scanning, you need at least 1/2 rotation beyond the planned scan length so that you can create the first and the last images. This extra exposure is the “over ranging.” Critical Trivia: • It is not an issue with axial CT - only spiral (helical). • Proportional to pitch (more pitch = more over ranging) • Over ranging contributes more to the DLP for shorter scan ranges - classic examples including pediatric CT and cardiac CT • Over beaming decreases with an increasing number of slices that are acquired simultaneously. • Over ranging increases with an increasing number of slices that are acquired simultaneously. Ill 11 lilllijlll : : : . I ! W Sm ! " 1 1 t w Axial Scanning - No Over Ranging ■ Pitch > 1 V V •*— ► More Less Wasted Wasted Dose Dose Pitch Around 1 173

CT A rtifacts: Beam Hardening- As the x-ray beam passes through an object, the lower energy photons are removed preferentially, leaving a “harder beam” with an increased average energy. There are two artifacts associated with this. Cupping - The x-rays passing through the middle of a uniform shape (like a head) are hardened more than those traveling through the periphery (a shorter path). The harder the beam the slower the rate of attenuation. The manifestation is the center of the image appears darker than the peripheral portions. Normal Cupping Normal Dark Bands / Streak - This occurs in the setting of two dense objects. X-rays that pass through one are less attenuated than those that pass through both. The result is dark bands and streaks between those two objects. The classic location is bone or where a dense contrast was used. Fixing Beam Hardening: (1) Filtration - Pre hardening of the beam, to remove lower energy components before it hits the patient, and/or the addition of a bow-tie filter. (2) Calibration Correction - Using a phantom to allow the detector to compensate for hardening effects. (3) Correct Software - An iterative correction algorithm can be used. (4) Avoidance - Tilt the gantry or position the patient to avoid areas that cause hardening. 174

Partial Volume: This can occur in two main ways. Pattern 1 ( “Partial Volume Effect”): A dense object protrudes partially into the width of an x-ray beam. This results in divergence of the beam and manifests as shading artifacts adjacent to said object. Pattern 2: CT voxels are 3D cubes. If you have a dense thing taking up half the cube, and a sparse (low attenuating) thing in the other half of the cube the machine will average the two together giving you something that has intermediate density. The classic location is the skull base averaging with CSF or brain to sorta look like blood. 1/2 Light Density End Product will be an 112 Dark Density average of the two Both Overlapping in a Single Voxel Fixing partial volume: Make your slices thinner. If the noise is a problem, acquire thin slices then generate thicker slices by adding them together. Photon Starvation: High attenuating areas (classically the shoulders) can result in photon starvation manifesting as streaking. It’s seen when the beam travels horizontally - through the greatest area of attenuation. Fixing Photon Starvation: There are two main ways to fix this. (1) Automatic tube current modulation. If you increase the dose through the area of greater attenuation you can add enough photons to overcome this effect. (2) Adaptive filtration can be performed to correct the attenuation profile and “smooth the data” in the high-attenuation portions. 175

Undersampling: An insufficient number of projections used to reconstruct the CT can diminish quality, and result in misregistration artifacts. There are two main types. View Aliasing: This is when you have under sampling between projections. You see fine stripes radiating from the edge of (but at a distance from) a dense object. This is fixed by acquiring the largest possible number of projections per rotation - slowing the rotation speed. Ray Aliasing: This is when you have under sampling within a projection. You see strips appearing close to the structure. This is fixed by using specialized high resolution techniques - manufacturer employed. Metal Artifact: Metal causes streak artifact. It does this through several mechanisms: beam hardening, partial volume, aliasing, and having density ranges higher than what can be handled by the computer. Testable trivia is that metals with high Z (Iron, Platinum) tend to have more artifacts than those with lower Z (Titanium). Fixing Metal Artifact: • Tell the patient to remove it. • Increase the kVp (sometimes works). • Use thinner slices. • Certain interpolation software can help. Patient Motion: Motion can cause misregistration - which manifests as shading or streaking, especially on reconstructions. Fixing Motion Artifact: Tie the crazy patients down. Use a modem (fast) scanner. Align the scanner in the primary direction of motion (vertically above or below a chest scan - for breathing). “Over scanning” an extra 10% on the 360 rotation, with the repeated portion averaged. Gating - like in cardiac. Incomplete Projection - If parts of the patient are hanging outside the field, but still attenuating X-Rays this messes with the computer’s math. Examples include; arms hanging down or having the IV contrast on or near the patient. Fixing incomplete projections: Position the patient correctly. 176

Ring Artifact: A calibration error or defective detector on a third generation scanner will cause errors in angular position - resulting in a circular artifact. Fixing Ring Artifact - Recalibrate your dinosaur detector, or replace the broken part. Helical Artifact in the Axial Plane: Single Section You get the same artifacts with helical scanning modes plus you get some additional artifact from helical interpolation. The main place you see this is around the top of the skull (anatomy changing rapidly in the Z direction). The higher the pitch the worse this is. To minimize these artifacts you have to reduce the variation in the Z direction - use a low pitch, use 180 degree instead of 360 when possible, and using thin sections instead of thick. Testable trivia - this is why head CTs are still commonly done with axial scanning over helical. Helical Artifact in Multi-Section Distortion is more complicated on multi-section scanners, with a classic “windmill” appearance where several rows of detectors intersect. It worsens with increased helical pitch. A “Z-Filter” is done to reduce the severity of windmill artifacts. Stair Step Artifact: This is seen as a “stair step” on the edges of a multi-planar reformatted image, when you have a wide collimation of non-overlapping intervals. It’s less severe with a helical scanner - where you are getting some overlap. It’s fixed by making thin slices. Zebra Artifact: This is another reformat artifact, which can occur from helical data - secondary to the helical interpolation process (increases noise along the Z axis). The effect manifests as stripes (like a zebra) most pronounced on a 3D image. The effect is most significant away from the axis of rotation - noise is worst off axis. 177

^ CHAPTER 8: ^ k . u iltr m ©u » - PROMETHEUS UONHART, M.D. - Sound is not light. Sound requires a medium to travel in. It’s best to think of sound as a mechanical energy that produces vibrations when propagating through material. These vibrations produce alternating areas of: High Pressure (compression) and Low Pressure (Rarefaction). (Compression) Frequency: This is the rate of change between compression and rarefaction - given in Hertz. In other words, its the number of times the wave oscillates through a cycle each second. Wavelength: The distance between areas of compression. Remember this from general chemistry? Speed = Wavelength x Frequency Low Pressure (Rarefaction) High Pressure Wavelength Frequency 180

Speed is thought of as being constant (in a particular medium), so that an increase in frequency decreases the wavelength - and vice versa. Speed in Different Materials: This is based on the compressibility of something. Things that are very compressible (air) will have a very low speed. Things that are not very compressible (bone) will have a very fast speed. The ultrasound machine is stupid - and just assumes everything travels at 1540 m/s in tissue. The fact that the machine thinks speed is constant is a source of artifacts (discussed later). Speeds effect on frequency - None. The frequency is the same, irrelevant of the sound speed in various media. Therefore the wavelength changes in media. Wave Interference Patterns: Modem ultrasound machines make a bunch of different sound beams. These sound beams can either have “constructive effects” and help each other - increase the amplitude, “destructive effects” and hurt each other - decrease the amplitude, or complex effects and be complex. These interactions are important in shaping and steering the beam. As the waves collide they build each other up, or tear each other down, in a pattern that can be manipulated (lots of math), giving the probe the ability to steer the direction of the 181

Relative Intensity - The dB. A change of 10 in the dB scale corresponds to two orders of magnitude (100 times) and so forth. The dB is based on a log 10 scale. Reducing the sound intensity to 10% is -10 dB Reducing to 1% is - 20 dB Reducing to 0.1 % is - 30 dB Testable Point: A loss of 3 dB (-3 dB) represents a 50% loss of signal intensity (power). Testable Point: The tissue thickness that reduces the ultrasound intensity by 3 dB is considered the "half-value" thickness. 182

In teractio n s of U ltrasound w ith M a tte r Ultrasound interacts with matter by: Reflection Refraction Scattering • Absorption Reflection: Ultrasound energy gets reflected at a boundary between two tissues because of the differences in the acoustic impedances of the two tissues. A large difference in “stiffness” results in a large reflection of energy. Sound Wave Reaches an Interface between two similar tissues. Sound Wave Reaches an Interface between two different tissues. Impedance: This is defined as: Z = Density x speed of sound. People like to compare this to the compressibility of a spring. At a muscle-air interface, nearly 100% of incident intensity is reflected. Ever noticed how air has some serious shadowing? This is why gel must be used between the transducer and the skin to eliminate air pockets. Small Difference in “Impedance” Results in a Small Reflection Large Difference in “Impedance” Results in a Large Reflection 183

Refraction: “Bending o f the sound wave caused by a change in speed” This is the change in direction of transmitted ultrasound energy at a tissue boundary when the beam is not perpendicular to said boundary. Remember the frequency doesn’t change, but the speed might. What influences refraction ? Two Things: (1) Speed Change - which is based on tissue compression, and (2) the Angle of Incidence. This was described by some dude named Snell: No refraction occurs if the speed of sound is the same in the two media or with perpendicular incidence. In other words if one side of the equation (the angle side or the speed side) is 1/1 there will be no change. Sin A n g le 1 Sin A n g le 2 S p eed 2 S p eed 1 If it hits straight on, part of the beam will bounce straight back and part of it goes straight through. If it strikes at an angle, part will be reflected and the other part will be refracted - with the severity of this refraction depending on the speed difference of the two media. Trivia: “Total Reflection” It is possible to have complete reflection if the speed difference, and angle of incidence is great enough (exceeds the “Critical Angle”). Clinical Example o f Refraction: As sound passes from the tissue to fluid - there is a change in speed (because of the different compressive abilities of the tissues). This speed change creates a “bending” of the beam. You see this a lot along the edges of fluid filled structures (like the gallbladder). 184

Scattering: There are two main categories of reflectors: • Specular (smooth) - The reflector dimensions are much larger than the wavelength of the incident. *Strength o f reflection is highly angle dependent. • Non-specular (diffuse) - The scattering surfaces are about the size of a wavelength or smaller. * Angle has no effect on strength With higher frequency ultrasound beams, the wavelength becomes smaller and the boundary no longer appears smooth from the perspective of the small wavelength. In this case, returning echoes are diffusely scattered leaving only a small fraction of the incident intensity returning to the source. Truly smooth reflections are relatively independent of frequency. Smooth Reflectors: Will Reflect Depending on the Angle of Incidence Non-Specular: Will Scatter Everywhere - Not giving a fuck about the Angle of Incidence Breaking it Down: High Frequency = Small wavelength = Surfaces appear more rough = More Scatter. So Higher Frequency = More Scatter. Differences in Scatter Amplitude: The difference in amplitude of the returning echoes from one region to another corresponds with brightness changes on the ultrasound display (essentially the number of scatterers per unit volume). • Hyperechoic = High Scatter Amplitude - relative to average background • Hypoechoic = Lower Scatter Amplitude - relative to average background 185

Ahsorption: Sound energy gets turned into heat. This increases with frequency. “Attenuation ” is the loss of intensity of the ultrasound beam from both absorption and scattering in the medium. The degree of attenuation varies widely depending on the type of tissue involved. To quantify the degree of attenuation, people who like math decided to express it in dB as proportional to frequency. If you are forced into a math problem, the rule of thumb for "soft tissue" is 0.5 dB per cm per MHz or 0.5 (dB/cm)/MHz. It is proportional: • A 2-MHz ultrasound beam will have twice the attenuation of a 1-MHz beam; • A 10-MHz beam will have ten times the attenuation per unit distance. It is logarithmic: • The beam intensity is exponentially attenuated with distance. Testable Point: Half value thickness (HVT): This is the thickness of tissue necessary to attenuate the incident intensity by 50%, which is equal to a 3-dB reduction in intensity. As the frequency increases, the HVT decreases. Think it Through: You always use the high frequency probe for superficial stuff right? This is why. The higher the frequency the more the sound gets attenuated, so the depth sucks. If you need to see a deeper structure then you have to use a lower frequency probe. 186

Review Time What determines the strength of the echoes? Angle and Impedance What is Impedance? The degree of stiffness in a tissue. The differences in tissue impedance (stiffness) determines the strength of surface reflection. What is the unit used for impedance? The Rayl You will get a big reflection if? There is a large difference in impedance. Example skin and air - thats why you gotta lube it up (gel) otherwise you can’t transmit any sound. Is the speed of sound constant in tissue? Nope - changes (via wavelength) depending on the compressibility of the tissue (slow in air, fast in bone). Thats why you have “hearing bones” in your middle ear. How does the machine know what the speed is in the various tissues the sound has traveled through? It doesn’t. It just assumes it’s always 1540 m/s - which can lead to artifacts. What makes the sound have “bend”? Changes in the speed of sound - which occur as it travels through different media - creating a “bending” or “refraction” as described by Snells Law. More or Less Scatter with High Frequency Probes? More - smaller wavelength makes surfaces look rougher (non-specular). Causes scatter More or Less Attenuation with High Frequency Probes? More 187

Ultrasound Transducers and Related Trivia Ultrasound is produced and received with a transducer. The device works by taking electricity and running it through a crystal (which vibrates) to convert it into mechanical energy (ultrasound waves). On the way back, the waves (mechanical energy) vibrate the crystal and turn back into electricity so they can be recorded. Or something like that... close enough for what you need to know. Back in the stone ages these things had a single element resonance crystal, now they have a broadband transducer array of hundreds of individual elements. Piezoelectric Materials: This is a crystal (or ceramic) and is the functional component of the transducer. It could be quartz, but is usually lead-zinc-titanate (PZT). The magic of the material is a well-defined molecular arrangement of electrical dipoles. When mechanically compressed, their normally organized alignment gets disturbed from equilibrium; this can be measured and recorded. Damping Layer \ Crystal Matching Layer Resonance Transducers: These are made to operate in a “resonance” mode, where short durations of voltage (usually 150 V) are applied, causing the PZT to vibrate at a natural resonance frequency. High Yield Trivia: The operating frequency is determined from the speed of sound in and the thickness of the piezoelectric material. The only way to change frequency is to change the probe. *Wavelength changes to accommodate changing velocity in different media. High Yield Trivia: The thickness o f the transducer is equal to V2 the wavelength. Lower frequency is seen with thicker crystals and higher frequency is seen with thinner crystals. 188

Dampening Block: This thing sits behind the crystal and absorbs the backward directed US energy. It also dampens the transducer vibration to create a pulse with a short spatial pulse length - needed to preserve detail along the beam axis (axial resolution). The process of dampening introduces a broadband frequency spectrum. What you need to know about dampening blocks: The differences in a thin block and a fat block easily lend themselves to multiple choice questions. Thin Block Thick Block v Called a "Light Damping" Called a "High Q" Has a Long Spatial Pulse Length Has a Narrow Bandwidth _ Called a "Heavy Damping" Called a "Low Q" Has a Short Spatial Pulse Length Has a Broad Bandwidth High Yield Trivia: Low Damping (high Q) - Narrow Bandwidth - For Doppler, to preserve velocity information. • Heavy Damping (low Q) - Broad Bandwidth - Gives you high spatial (axial) resolution *fewer interference effects and therefore more uniformity Matching Layer - The matching layer gives the transducer an interface between the transducer element and the tissue. The key testable point is that it - minimizes the acoustic impedance differences between the transducer and the patient. It’s made of stuff that has an acoustic impedance intermediate to soft tissue and the transducer material. Testable Point: The optimal matching layer thickness is equal to l/4th the wavelength. 189

Transducer Arrays Broadly you can have Linear (which include curved) or Phased Arrays. Linear arrays are sequenced, whereas phased arrays are “activation / reactivation” types. Linear (Sequenced) - Each element is it’s own man. They fire and receive on their own - not giving a fuck what their neighbors are doing. There is no use of interference patterns / steering on any of that fancy shit. Trivia - The Width of the Transducer = The Width of the Sum of the Individual Elements Trivia - Linear Arrays are good for Peds, and good for superficial things (carotids, leg veins, testicles, thyroids). Curvilinear - This is still a “linear” probe, meaning that it still operates with individual elements firing on their own. The difference is the face is curved. Why curved? Scan lines diverge deeper into the image. This gives you a wider field of view for deeper structures. Used in ABD imaging. Phased - This is the arch nemesis of linear. Unlike the linear probe, phased probes operate as a hive mind - like the Borg from Star Trek. Resistance is Futile - as these groups of elements fire in multiples using interference patterns to steer the beam - operating like a search light scanning a dark room. Because of this steering the probes can be made smaller. Trivia: These are good for limited acoustic windows (in between ribs, transvaginal etc..). Phased -Smaller Probe Linear -Big Probe with the face of the screen mimicking the probe - flat for flat Linear Phased 256-512 Elements 64-128 Elements Large Small Sequenced Firing of small group of adjacent Elements arc - activated and reactivated — in a elements (20ish) phased pattern A rectangular field of view is produced (trapezoidal with curved). Time delays in electrical activation can make it possible to steer and focus, without moving the probe 190

Beam Properties There are two distinct beam components: a converging beam, which narrows out to a distance determined by the geometry and frequency of the transducer (near field), and a diverging beam (far field). The Near Field (Fresnel Zone): The convergence of the near field occurs because of the multiple constructive and destructive interference patterns. Beam intensity varies from: MAX-to-min- “Normal” Probe to-MAX in a converging beam. The near field length is dependent on: Transducer Frequency and Transducer High Frequency, Large Probe Diameter. Testable Point: •Higher Transducer Frequency = Longer Near Field •Larger Diameter Element = Longer Near Field The Far Field (Fraunhofer Zone) - This is where the beam diverges. As the beam diverges the ability to distinguish two objects close to one another is reduced. The divergence is less with a high frequency big probe and more with a low frequency small probe. Ultrasound intensity in the far field gradually decreases with distance. Intensity - The intensity of the beam is the power (measured in watts) flowing through a unit area. The power of the beam is not uniform along its length or width. This is caused by a variety of factors. The beam lacks clearly defined edges and the intensity will decrease from the center > outward - sometimes called “beam spread” The maximum sound pressure is always found along the acoustic axis (centerline) Divergence of the beam in the far field causes the power to be spread over a larger area. • Interference occurs from numerous point sources interacting in the near field. *Less with a broadband transducer Focal Zone 191

Focal Depth / Zone - The point at which the beam is at its narrowest and the area of maximum intensity. It’s the spot between the converging and diverging beams. You get your best echoes here and you get your best lateral resolution here. If you tried to image this round lesion in the far field - you would get shitty resolution because your field is diverging. If you stick the round lesion in the focal zone - you are going to maximize your beam density and get your best “lateral resolution” or “side-to-side” resolution. “Focusing” - This is a concept where you try and converge / narrow the ultrasound beam, again with the goal of improving the lateral resolution. Your two main strategies for focusing are (1) Mechanical, and (2) Electronic. Mechanical - By using a concave face you can set a fixed focal depth / zone. You do the same thing by slapping a concave acoustic lens on the front. Electronic - A shallow focus can be achieved by firing the inner transducers in a symmetrical pattern. A longer focus can be achieved by reducing the delay time differences among the transducers elements (resulting in more distal beam convergence). Lastly you imply multiple focal zones. All of these are phased array tricks. 192

The “Stand Off Pad” Scanning very superficial things is made much easier with a “stand off pad.” WTF is a “stand o ff pa d” ? - It’s basically a block of ultrasound gel - or something with low impedance that you can scan through. As a rare piece of practical knowledge (low yield for boards) if you have a burn patient or someone with very painful skin you can submerge their hand / foot in water and use the water as a standoff pad. That way you don’t have to touch their sensitive skin / wound with the ultrasound probe. Also works great in Peds - even sick kids like water. Try that shit sometime - tell your attending you thought of it and blow their mind - they will probably be so impressed that they will have you write it up (with their name on it of course). Why does it work? It works by moving things in the superficial region (the near zone) into the focal zone. Remember lateral resolution is shitty in the near zone (and in the far zone) — it maximizes right at the focal zone, so that’s where you want to put stuff. Superficial nodule - in the near zone S t a n d O f f P a d Superficial nodule -now in the focal zone 193

Spatial Resolution - There are three dimensions in US • Axial Lateral • Elevation (Slice Thickness) Axial Resolution: The ability to tell two closely spaced objects apart in the direction of the beam. For optimal axial resolution you need the returning echoes to be separate and not overlap. The minimum required separation between two reflectors is lA the spatial pulse length (SPL), otherwise the returning echoes will overlap. What is this “Spatial pulse length ” ? This is the number of cycles emitted per pulse by the transducer multiplied by the wavelength. Objects closer than XA Spatial Pulse Length will NOT be resolved. Lets Say This is the SPL Scenario A: M2 the length on the SPL is larger than the distance between those two nodules. Therefore they will look like 1 Big Nodule to the Scanner. In other words, you won’t be able to resolve them in the axial plane Scenario B: 112 the length on the SPL is sm aller than the distance between those two nodules. Therefore they will look like 2 Nodules to the Scanner Scanner. In other words, you will be able to resolve them in the axial plane Lateral Elevation (Slice Thickness) 194

Lateral Resolution: The ability of the system to resolve objects in a direction perpendicular to the beam direction. If you want to tell things apart, they must fit into separate beams. The thinner the beam, the more likely that each will fall into a separate beam. You can reason that improving lateral resolution has a lot to do with making your beam thinner. The beam is most skinny at the end of the near field (at the focal zone). Lateral resolution is worst in areas close to and far from the transducer surface (away from the focal zone). Is lateral resolution constant at different depths? No, Since the beam diameter varies with the distance from the transducer in the near and far field, the lateral resolution is depth-dependent. The best lateral resolution occurs at the focal zone (the interface of the near field and far field). Lateral resolution worsens in the deeper field. Is axial resolution constant at different depths? - Yep. Has nothing to do with depth or zones or any of that bullshit. It’s all about the Spatial Pulse Length (SPL) Will cranking up the transmit gain (transmit power) help? No - that widens the beam. You want a nice narrow beam. So try to use the minimal necessary transmit gain (power). Beam Frequency High Frequency beams are the most narrow (low frequency beams are wider). The higher the frequency the narrowing the beam, the narrower the beam the better the lateral resolution. Another more PhD’y concept is that a higher frequency will extend the Fresnel (near) zone - although this only confuses people because they think depth is decreased with frequency. They are right - depth does go down with frequency but that is because of increased attenuation. Just remember = Higher Frequency = Better Lateral Resolution Shitty Getting Better Shitty Again 195

Scan Density If you think about the probe working by sending a series (a bunch) o f individual scan lines out and then receiving data, it should be intuitive that more lines = more information and therefore better spatial resolution. Less Scan Lines Machine thinks it s only one More Scan Lines (“more density”) *Machine thinks it s two Higher Scan Line Density = Better Lateral Resolution So what is the relationship between transducer width and lateral spatial resolution? Remember I said that lateral spatial resolution changes with depth? And that it’s best in the focal zone (shitty up close and far away)? The length o f the near field varies depending on the diameter o f the transducer (single element or function total). Anything that effects the focal zone - effects the lateral spatial resolution. Longer Transducer^ Longer Near Field Length *and longer focal zone length Shorter Transducer Shorter Near Field Length • Lateral Resolution is best at the focal zone. • Lateral Resolution changes with depth. • Lateral Resolution is best with a narrow pulse (beam) WIDTH • Lateral Resolution is best with a higher frequency • Lateral Resolution is best with a higher scan line density. • Changes in Transducer Length Move the focal zone - and thus modify the lateral spatial resolution - at various depths. Larger diameter transducers tend to have longer focal zones - therefore better Lateral Resolution. 1 Testable Summary: I ■ 196

Elevation Resolution (Slice Thickness) - It’s the same as lateral resolution but is measured in the plane orthogonal to the image plane. This is usually the worst measure of resolution. It depends on the transducer element height. This type of resolution is why you can get volume averaging of acoustic details in regions that are close to the transducer and in the far field. Things to Know: • It’s similar to lateral resolution but in the orthogonal plane • It’s typically the weakest link • It depends on the height of the transducer elements • It’s a source of volume averaging before and after the focal zone (in the near and far fields) 197

S patial Resolution Sum m ary Improving Axial R esolution Improving Lateral R esolution In on R esolution Shorter Pulses (Smaller Spatial Pulse Length) Put the thing you want to look at in the focal zone. Phased array with multiple focal zones Use a fixed focal length across the entire surface of the array (downside is partial volume effects) Greater Damping “Low 0 ” (shorter pulses) Increasing the “line density” or lines per cm. Use a Thinner Crystal Minimize slice thickness - done by phase excitation of the outer to inner arrays Higher Frequency Probe (shorter wavelength) Higher Frequency Probe (less beam spreading) Dependent On Axial Resolution Spatial Pulse Length Lateral Resolution Transducer Element Width Elevation Resolution Transducer Element Height 198

Artifacts Ultrasound display equipment relies on multiple assumptions to assign the location and intensity of each received echo. When these assumptions are violated, you get an artifact. Assumptions: • All echoes originate from within the main beam • All echoes return to the transducer after a single 180 degree reflection • The amount of time an echo takes to return to the probe depends directly on the object depth. • The speed of sound in human tissue is constant - 1540 m/s • The sound beam and its echo travel in a straight path • Acoustic energy is uniformly attenuated Beam Related Artifacts: As stated above, there is an assumption that the detected echoes originate from within the main US beam. Side Lobe Artifact: The normal US beam looks like a bowtie, with a central main beam and off axis low energy beams on the side. These off axis beams are the “side lobes.” You get these side lobes from radial expansion of piezoelectric crystals, which happens more in linear arrays. If you have a strong enough reflector it could bounce back this low energy side lobe, which will be received by the transducer. This will violate the assumption that echoes originate from the main beam - and it will be incorrectly placed as if it did. This artifact is typically seen when the incorrectly placed echoes overlap an anechoic structure (Bladder or GB). Some people refer to this as “pseudo sludge” when it’s seen over the gallbladder. Testable Point: Side lobe energy is seen more with linear array transducers. Cystic Structure 199

Beam Width Artifact: The US beam first exits the transducer with the same width as the transducer. It then narrows to the focal zone and then begins to diverge in the far field. The beam will eventually diverge out past the original margins of the transducer. If this diverged beam encounters a strong reflector, it could send a signal back - which will be assumed to be from the main beam (within the normal width of the transducer) and will be erroneously displayed as such. The bladder is the classic place to show this, with peripheral echoes. A good “next step” question would be to show this artifact and have you improve it by (1) adjusting the focal zone to the level of interest and (2) placing the transducer at the center o f the image. Artifacts Associated with Multiple Echoes: These artifacts violate the assumption that an echo returns to the transducer after a single reflection and that the depth of the object is related to the time for the round trip. Reverberation Artifact: If the sound wave encounters two parallel highly reflective surfaces, the echoes generated from a primary ultrasound beam may be repeatedly reflected back and forth (like a game of pong) before eventually returning to the transducer for detection. This is recorded and displayed as multiple echoes, with the echoes that return after a single reflection being displayed appropriately and sequential echoes (which take longer to return to the transducer), erroneously placed at an increased distance from the transducer. This looks like multiple equidistantly spaced linear reflections. Comet Tail Artifact: A form of reverberation. This time our two parallel highly reflective surfaces are closer together which means the sequential echoes are closely spaced. The space between them may be less than lA the spatial pulse length (SPL) - which as mentioned above was the minimal distance needed for axial resolution. As a result, the displayed echoes will look like a triangle, instead of linear lines. Why a triangle and not a square ? The later echoes get attenuated and have decreased amplitude. This decreased amplitude is manifested on the display as decreased width. So you get a tapering triangle (or comet tail). Ring Down Artifact: The mechanism for this one is slightly different. Instead of encountering two parallel highly reflective surfaces, our sound wave encounters fluid trapped between a tetrahedron of air bubbles. The vibrations create a nearly continuous sound wave transmitted back to the probe. You see this as a line or series of parallel bands extending posterior to a collection of gas. 200

Mirror Image Artifact: Like other artifact in this category, this is created by the false assumption that an echo returns to the transducer after a single reflection. In this situation the ultrasound beam passes through a highly reflective surface, then gets repeatedly reflected between the back side of the reflector and the adjacent structure. This is displayed as a duplication equidistant from but deep to the strongly reflective interface. The classic location is along the liver / lung interface as shown - with liver parenchyma where you should have lung. Artifacts from Multiple Echoes Reverberation Two parallel highly reflective surfaces - Multiple equidistantly spaced linear reflections. Comet Tail Two parallel highly reflective surfaces - closer together (< 1/2 SPL) Triangle (comet) shaped Ring Down Artifact Fluid trapped between a tetrahedron of air bubbles Parallel band extending posterior to a collection of gas Mirror Image Trapped behind a strong reflector This is almost always shown with the liver on lung. 201

Artifacts Related to Velocity Errors: The assumption is that the speed of sound is 1540 m/s in human tissue. Sometimes the beam encounters media (air, fluid , fat, bone) where this doesn’t hold up exactly. If it travels slower, it takes longer to return and the machine thinks the depth is more. If it travels faster, it comes back to the monitor faster and the machine thinks the depth is less. Speed Displacement Artifact: The speed of sound slows down in fat, relative to liver. This means the beam takes longer to return and is perceived by the machine as being further away. This creates the appearance of a discontinuous and focally displaced liver border. Speed Displacement Artifact Refraction Artifact: As discussed earlier in the US section, speed difference in tissues causes refraction (as described by Snell). This change in the direction of the sound wave violates the idea that the beam is going straight and can cause three things: (1) the object can appear wider than it actually is, (2) the object can be misplaced to the side of the returning echo, or (3) the object can appear duplicated. The classic look (and location) for this is deep to the rectus muscles and midline fat. The so called “duplicated SMA, ” occurs secondary to symmetry of the geometry. The SMA will become a normal single vessel if you move the transducer to the side (removes symmetry of refraction). “Displayed Position” “Actual Position” Refraction Artifact 202

Artifacts Related to Attenuation Errors: Ultrasound beams are attenuated as they travel through the body (from scatter and absorption). The longer the echo travels, the more it will be attenuated (relative to an echo which traveled a shorter distance). ‘‘Compensation Amplification ” - This is a type of processing that ultrasound machines use on echoes which take longer to return to the transducer. Essentially, the echoes that return later are amplified more than echoes that return earlier. The end result is an image that appears more uniform in the deep field. Shadowing - If the ultrasound beam runs into a material that attenuates sound to a larger degree than the surrounding tissue, the strength of the beam distal to this structure appears weaker (darker) than in the surrounding field. Increased Through Transmission - This is the opposite of shadowing. Instead, the beam runs into material that attenuates sound less than the surrounding tissue and the strength of the beam distal to the structure appears stronger (brighter) than the surrounding field. Modes / Misc A Mode, B Mode, M Mode A Mode - “A” stands for amplitude. This is of historical context - and only used now by Ophtho to take eye measurements. This gives you processed information from the receiver versus time. An echo’s return from tissue boundaries and reflectors creates a digital signal proportional to echo. “A”mplitude is produced as a function of time. B Mode - “B” stands for brightness. Basically this is the conversion of A line information to brightness-modulated dots on a display. There is a proportional relationship of brightness to the echo signal amplitude. M Mode - “M” stands for motion. Essentially, B-mode information is used to display the echoes from a moving organ (like heart valves) from a fixed transducer and beam position on the patient. 203

Doppler The idea is that the frequency of sound changes with moving objects. This is usually explained by thinking about a car blowing it’s horn moving towards you (or away from you). Basically the sound wave gets crowded at the front, and will sound different depending on where you are standing in relationship to the moving object (origin of the sound wave). Understanding it really isn’t that important. It’s a thing.. .and because it’s a thing you can measure these changes (or shifts) in frequency to tell what direction things are moving (and how fast they are getting there). 204

D oppler A n g le: In an ideal world, the sample volume would be placed in the mid-part of the lumen, with an angle of incidence parallel to the vessel. If this angle is placed incorrectly the velocity calculation will be incorrect (especially if the angle is greater than 60). The angle should be between 30- 60. Why less than 60? Because of the formula for calculating it. Here let’s have a look at it, and it should make perfect sense. To make it less confusing I’ve written it in Egyptian Hieroglyphs, why would I do that? Because the only part of it you need to understand is the “Cosine” part. You can imagine if cosine was zero (which occurs at 90 degrees), then the numerator (top part) would be zero.... and zero over something is zero. A shift of zero would make you think the blood isn’t Dop = ■ < ] (C o s 0 > 3 ^ ) ' / V A How does the change in angle - change the Cosine? Because of the formula for calculating it. Notice that the cosine of the angle is on the bottom. Notice it’s Zero At 90 Degrees Looks like zero is best. So why more than 30? - Angles less than 20 degrees cause refraction and loss of signal. Aliasing also becomes an issue. But yes - theoretically zero is best. Why Not Perpendicular ? - If you placed the probe perpendicular to the vessel, it can give a false impression of no flow (occlusion). The other thing it can do is create a mirror image. 205

Pulsed Wave (Spectral) Doppler- This utilizes a single transducer for both reception and transmission. Blood flow velocity varies yielding a spectrum of doppler shifts instead of a single frequency. You can obtain the direction of blood flow (plotted above and below the baseline) and velocity. Color Doppler: This uses the gray scale image with a superimposed color blood flow image. Gives the direction of flow, this time with colors (red and blue). Color intensity varies depending on the flow intensity (things holding still are gray). This type of doppler obtains samples of each pixel multiple times and then displays the average shift. Testable Trivia: The spatial resolution is less compared to gray scale imaging (although smaller vessels are better seen). Testable Trivia: The doppler angle is not as important since the information is semi- quantitative. Power Doppler: Power doppler is different because it gives you a very sensitive look for the presence of flow without information on direction. You still get color but instead each pixel registers the total number of “frequency shifts”. Testable Trivia: Power Doppler does NOT exhibit aliasing (both color and spectral can). Testable Trivia: Power Doppler has NO dependence on the Doppler Angle - you can measure doppler totally perpendicular to the vessel. Testable Trivia: Power Doppler is extremely sensitive to flow (even very slow flow) 206

Doppler A rtifacts: Aliasing - Super high velocities are displayed as low (negative). On a spectral system it looks like the spectrum is cut off and “wrapped around the baseline”, reappearing on the opposite end. Testable Trivia: This artifact occurs when the doppler shift is greater than a threshold called the “Nyquist frequency” Nyquist limit (kHz) = 1/2 x pulse repetition frequency (PRF) Example: A frequency shift of 3.5 kHz requires a PRE of 7 kHz to avoid aliasing. Aliasing can be reduced or eliminated by: Decreasing Doppler shift - by either using a lower frequency transducer or using a doppler angle closer to 90 (increasing the angle) • Increasing the Pulse Repetition Frequency (which will increase your Nyquist) or selecting a sample volume at a lesser depth or increasing the scale. Tissue Vibration: This occurs secondary to turbulent blood flow. The doppler shows a mixture of red and blue colors. The classic example is a A-V fistula in a kidney post biopsy. Mirror Image: Just like the gray scale version, this occurs secondary to a vessel adjacent to a highly reflective surface, such as the lung. This results in the duplication of the structure being evaluated. The classic locations are the subdiaphragmatic region of the liver and the supraclavicular region. Twinkle Artifact: This occurs behind strongly reflecting surfaces such as calcifications. It manifests as a noisy spectrum with rapid fluctuation of red and blue colors. Testable Trivia: This artifact has a greater sensitivity for detection of small stones than acoustic shadowing. Testable Trivia: This is highly dependent on machine settings and how round the reflecting surface is (more rough = more twinkle). Pseudoflow (Pseudoblood) Artifact: Things that move like blood but aren’t. The classic example is ureteral jets. Flash Artifact: This is a burst of color filling the screen. It’s secondary to transducer or patient motion. The classic example is the “fetal kick!” Color Bleed - This looks like color extending beyond the vessel wall. It can decrease sensitivity to thrombus or stenosis. Making it better: Decrease the color gain. 207

Image Optimization This vs That: Output Power vs Receiver Gain: Output Power (‘‘Transmit Gain) - Cranking this knob up increases the brightness by adjusting the strength of the sound pulse SENT TO THE BODY by the transducer. When the image is too bright from high output power, this w ill degrade lateral resolution (think about it widening the beam). Receiver Gain - Cranking this knob up increases brightness by adjusting the strength of the sound pulse AFTER IT RETURNS to the transducer. Think about this like a post-processing technique. • BOTH CAN LIGHTEN or DARKEN THE ENTIRE IMAGE. ~ If Forced to Choose ALARA favors Receiver Gain is a first option TGC - Time Gain Compensation This is the button you push to make the image look better. What it actually does is progressively increase the amount of amplification applied with depth. The man who lives in the ultrasound machine (“computer” I call it), is trying to make the top and bottom uniform. This used to require manual manipulation of buttons, but it is now automated. What can you do to make this image better? - Answer is probably hit the TGC button. ' ' llPlipL :■ : ijlliS What you need to know: • Purpose is uniform brightness from top to bottom • It works by compensating for loss of echo strength caused by the depth of the reflector • Treats echoes differently depending on which depth they are returning from • You could manually manipulate it by wiggling buttons around (now automated with a button push) ■ 208

Harmonics -A Promethean Dialogue- A lot of discussion in ultrasound physics centers around how the sound changes as it goes through the body, and the various artifacts and such that result (so on and so forth, etc..ect..) - and there will be some of that in this dialogue, but first we need to have another look at the machine. Specifically, we should to focus on how the sonographic system is going to take received signal and make a diagnostic image. A basic way to look at it is like this: ^ Beam L Signal j L Image Former ♦ — As sound waves return to the probe the “Beam Former” will amplify and combine the contributions from the various of individual elements. The next step is the “Signal Processor. ” The “Signal Processor” will do several things - but the main thing relevant for this discussion is the task of filtering out undesirable noise. The way this works is the probe knows what frequency it sent out, so it has a general idea of what frequency is coming back. There will typically be some slight changes in frequency as the sound wave travels through the body and returns - and to deal with that a certain range of frequencies are deemed acceptable. But - anything totally out of whack is gonna be considered irrelevant dog shit and eliminated from the final processed signal. Something like this: Raw Signal This is the basic idea of how the signal processor can filter out unwanted frequencies to reduce noise. Harmonics can be viewed as a similar process - but with a slightly different goal. This time instead of filtering out the frequencies that are out of the expected range of the primary harmonic (the transmitted frequency) - the system does the opposite, deliberately filtering out the primary signal and instead preserving the signal from the second harmonic echo. 209

Before we discuss why you might want to do this - let us discuss how harmonic frequencies are generated in tissues (who does #2 work for ?) You can think about sound waves as area of compression (high density) and rarefaction (low density) occurring in repeating cycles. The areas of high density (compression) correspond with the positive direction of the wave or “high pressure” and the areas of low density correspond with the negative direction of the wave or “low pressure.” The reason I bring this up, is that as the wave travels through a medium (i.e. the cirrhotic liver of an IV drug user who is salivating at the chance to sue you), it doesn’t do so uniformly. The high pressure portion will travel faster than the low pressure portion. As this happens the shape of the wave changes, eventually forming even and odd “harmonics” from the original (fundamental) wave. 1st Harm onic 2nd Harmonic It’s important to realize that you need a significant difference between the high pressure and low pressure portions of the wave to actually form a harmonic under physiologic conditions. This is why harmonics are only formed from the highest intensity portions of the beam. Raw Signal Filter Also - for this change to happen you need some time for the wave to morph. This is why harmonic generation at the skin is basically zero. It’s a deep phenomenon. OK... so why filter out that primary harmonic and accept a “lesser harmonic’ 210

There are 3 primary advantages to doing this: (1) Improved Lateral Resolution. The reason for the improvement in lateral resolution is that the harmonic beam more narrow. Fundamental Beam with 2nd Harmonic Just the 2nd Harmonic (notice it is skinner) Remember that Lateral Resolution is limited by the WIDTH of the pulse. People will often write that as “beam width” rather than pulse width - but we are talking about the same thing since “beam” is just a series of identical pulses given off in rapid succession. Small beam width- reflectors close together are still resolved. Machine correctly identifies two objects Large beam width- reflectors close together are NOT resolved. Machine thinks it is just one reflector NARROWER BEAM WIDTH = BETTER LATERAL RESOLUTION 211

W hat about Axial Resolution ? Remember that axial resolution is limited by the length of the ultrasound pulse - the “Spatial Pulse Length” or “SPL,” ? Axial Resolution = Spatial Pulse Length / 2. When the bandwidth (Bw) is narrowed (via filtering out the fundamental frequency) you are going to lengthen the pulse length. Pulse Length = 1/ Bw Shorter SPL: PL = 1/Bw 1 st Harmonic Summary: Harmonics have a shorter bandwidth. This results in a longer spatial pulse length. Longer spatial pulse length = reduced axial resolution. Trivia: The solution to this problem is a technique called “pulse inversion.” The nuts and bolts of how “pulse inversion” works is probably beyond the scope of the exam. Probably... Before you spaz out and start googling it consider this - if the difference between you passing and failing this test comes down to knowing the inner mechanics of obscure ultrasound algorithms, as Kurt Osiander would say “you fucked up a long time ago.” 212

OK... so I said there were 3 Advantages. The first was IMPROVED LATERAL RESOLUTION. Then we had a brief tangent, so I could point out that harmonics can actually reduce axial resolution. Now - to finish up with these last two potential advantages. (2) Grating Lobe A rtifacts are Reduced / Elim inated. I guess before I get into why they are eliminated I should answer the burning question. “What the fuck are grating lobe artifacts ” ? The simplest explanation is that they represent off-axis sound. Normally the beam shoots straight down. However, sound energy can be emitted far off-axis (not on purpose - it just happens sometimes) - and these things are called “side lobes” or “grating lobes” - and the difference between those is two words is meaningless — seriously, don’t waste time going there. Main Beam Usually the presence of a grating lobe doesn’t mean shit because they aren’t strong enough to produce an echo. Having said that, if the grating lobes is directed at a strong reflector (gas, bone, etc...), their echoes have a chance of being received and recorded - creating an artifact (computer thinks the reflector originated from the main central beam - and will position it as such). So why are they eliminated ? Remember when I said you need a sufficiently strong beam to have a large enough difference between the high pressure and low pressure portions of the wave to actually form a harmonic under physiologic conditions ? Grating lobes are usually lower energy - so this difference isn’t present. In other words, they aren’t strong enough to produce a 2nd harmonic - and since that is the data we are using to make the pictures, they get filtered out with the primary harmonic. Grating / Side Lobe Grating / Side Lobe 213

(3) Superficial Reverberation is Reduced / Elim inated. There are several reasons why this occurs. The first one is depth. Remember Harmonics are generated deep in the tissue (it takes time from the high pressure - low pressure differences to morph the wave). As a result artifacts that are generated in the superficial tissues — for example reverberation between ribs will be removed. ~ RIB Reverberations - from strong reflector (ribs) Fundamental Beam with 2nd Harmonic NO Reverberations RIB Just the 2nd Harmonic - notice it is skinner Another reason is the energy of the reflected sound. As a quick refresher, reverberations occur when the sound beam is repeatedly reflected between two interfaces with a high acoustic impedance mismatch (difference). So you get a strong bounce back to the transducer face. At that point the computer who lives inside the probe recognizes that first echo and localizes it accurately. But... because it was such a strong echo some of it actually bounces off the transducer face and returns to that reflecting interface (as if it was a second transmitted pulse - although now at a lower energy). When that second pulse returns it’s location is falsely placed deeper in the tissue (since it took longer to make it back). This can happen multiple times - making a bunch of horizontal lines. The points that matter are that this strong reflection is almost always within the primary harmonic (which is filtered out), and the subsequent reflected pulses are not high enough energy to make a second harmonic. Therefore - harmonics can reduce or eliminate reverberation. Some people will refer to a “speckle” artifact - from wave interaction - that is also decreased with harmonics. 214

Harm onics Rapid R eview & Sum m ary As the ultrasound beam travels into the patient’s tissues the transmitted pulse is progressively distorted. However this occurs primarily in the central zone of the main beam where the intensity is high. A distorted pulse will give rise to distorted echoes and these have significant energy at harmonic frequencies. Tissue harmonics are made using the second harmonic component of the echo signal, with the fundamental frequency excluded. Undistorted echoes coming from lower intensity areas (fringes of the beam, side lobes, superficial tissues) are not seen. The beam changes as it passes through tissues The receiver can be set to accept this altered frequency What you need to know: • Harmonics involves transmitting at one frequency and receiving at another - “the second harmonic” they call it. • Possible because body tissues cause some distortion of the wave. • The wave has to travel some distance to become distorted enough to generate harmonics - as such harmonics are NOT produced in the near field (they haven’t traveled far enough) • This process IMPROVES lateral resolution (and can worsen axial resolution) • Reduction in artifacts - specifically reverberation & side lobe / grating but also the legendary “speckle” artifact - which I only mentioned once in passing because it’s probably low yield....probably. • Because you are dealing with a higher frequency, you do lose some depth penetration (remember higher frequency attenuates - is attenuated - faster). 215

' - ,y ; m - Compound Im aging - Having the electronic steering of the ultrasound beams from the transducer image an object in multiple different directions. This will sharpen the edges and cause loss of posterior shadowing (can make a cyst look solid). What you need to know: • Compound imaging uses electronic steering of the ultrasound beams from the transducer to image an object in multiple different directions.
