At least this is how spatial compound imaging works (there are other more complex mechanisms but - it doesn’t matter... probably). • Will sharpen the edges • Will cause loss of posterior shadowing Focal Zone Normal Scanning Compound Imaging (Peaking around the comers) Remember to optimize an image you want to drop your focal zone, narrowed appropriately, over the object of interest. WRONG! - Focal Zone Centered Too Low WRONG! - Focal Zone Too Wide CORRECT - Focal Zone Centered Tightly Over the Object - Maximized Resolution 216

This vs That - Harmonics vs Compound Imaging These toys are great, but sometimes they cause problems. The classic way to illustrate this is to think about a breast “nodule” in mammo. Normal Hypoechoic but not totally anechoic Some Shadowing - but not a ton Blurry Margins Reverberation Artifact Harmonics Looks more hypoechoic or almost anechoic Shadowing tends to be worse Blurry Margins stay Reverberation Artifact, side lobe / grating and “speckle are GONE Compound Hypoechoic but not totally anechoic Shadowing is GONE Margins are SHARP Harmonics can make a solid thing looks cystic (by turning it more anechoic). This can sometimes make it easier to see a “nodule” on the background breast parenchyma. Just make sure you flip it on and off to make sure it’s not a cyst. From a practical standpoint this is most useful in finding a lesion that has similar echotexture to the background, biopsy targeting, and evaluating implants (in my opinion). Compound = The big thing here is that you are dropping your posterior features. Lets you see there margins of the lesion better. A rtifact Harm onics Compounding Comet Tail More Visible Less Visible Ring Down Reduced Reverberation Reduced / Eliminated Increased Through Transmission INCREASED Acoustic Shadowing INCREASED DECREASED Speckle Noise Reduced Reduced Side Lobe / Grating Reduced 217

Safety First let us discuss some vocabulary related to Amplitude: This is the difference between the average value of the waveform. This is not constant throughout the beam. If you cut the beam in cross section you’d see that it’s more intense in the center, and less on the edges. I bring this up because it illustrates the need to discuss “intensity.” Intensity = power / area. • If the beam power increases the intensity increases. • If the beam area decreases (which happens during focusing) the intensity increases. • If you square the amplitude you quadruple the intensity. Some other important vocabulary words: • Spatial Peak Intensity = The greatest intensity found in the beam • Temporal Average Intensity = The average intensity over pulse times and listening times. Spatial Peak Spatial Average — Temporal Peak Temporal A verage how strong / intense an ultrasound beam is. peak and the Amplitude 218

Relative Intensity of Modes Remember the smaller the beam area the greater the intensity. Intensity = Power / Area. This is the easiest way to keep these straight - from a conceptual point of view. “B-Mode ” - Also known as “brightness mode” or “2D mode” - This is the standard grey scale mode that you use all the time. The field of view is fairly large, i.e. a big square. “M-Mode ” - This is more of a cardiac ECHO thing (although you can also use it for fetal HR). This mode focuses on the ability to see “motion” - for example a heart valve. It uses rapidly emitted pulses over a narrow beam to determine the velocity of that specific structure in question. Think about it as zeroing in on a very small structure to see if it is moving. Smaller beam = more intensity. “Doppler” - Think about the Doppler sample as occurring over a tiny little line - which is necessary to see those little shifts. Smallest beam = most intensity. This is a general way to think about these numerically: B-Mode is 10 M-Mode is 4 0 ---------------------------Doppler is 500 Within the doppler family spectral pulsed doppler tends to have the highest temporal average intensity. The reason is that the beam is held in a fixed position - not “spread.” Color flow mapping and power doppler mapping tend to have some beam scanning - they tend to fall between M mode and spectral pulse doppler. • Biggest Bad = Spectral Pulsed Doppler Mode • Made Worse With = High pulse repetition frequency (high limit on the frequency scale), and a shallow range gate. 219

Two acoustic output parameters are used as indicators of the potential for biologic effect. •Thermal Index (TI) - this is the maximum temperature rise in tissue secondary to energy absorption. This is based on a homogeneous tissue model with certain instrument parameters. •Mechanical Index (MI) -this is how likely it is that cavitation will occur considering peak rarefaction pressure and frequency. This index is the indicator of mechanical bioeffects (streaming and cavitation). This matters the most with contrast enhanced US. Cavitation - Sonically-generated activity in compressible bodies composed of gas and/or vapor. This can be sub-classified as either stable or transient. •Stable Cavitation - Micro bubbles are already present in the media. They expand and contract as the waves cycle (responding to pressure). This occurs at low and intermediate ultrasound intensities (as used clinically). The MI is an estimate for producing cavitation. •Transient Cavitation - “The violent one” - Bubble oscillations become so large that the bubbles collapse. This collapse results in shock waves rippling through tissue planes causing tissue damage. Testable Trivia: The rate of energy absorption increases with frequency. Eventually the rise in temperature slows secondary to conduction and perfusion with an eventual steady state. Testable Trivia: Thermal-induced damage is a threshold phenomenon (you get no tissue damage until a certain temperature is reached). Testable Trivia: Cavitation is most likely to occur with low frequency and high pressure. Testable Trivia: Spectral Doppler deposits more heat compared to gray-scale ultrasound. Testable Trivia: Per the NCRP - a risk-benefit decision when the TI exceeds a value of 1.0 and the MI exceeds a value of 0.5. Thermal Index for Soft Tissue (TIS) Used for soft tissues - i.e. OB scanning up to 10 weeks after LMP. Thernal Index for Bone (TIB) Used when the ultrasound beam impacts on bone at the focal region - i.e. OB scanning 10 weeks after LMP. Terminal Index for Cranial Bone (TIC) Used when the ultrasound beam impacts the skull - i.e. Trans-cranial scanning. 220

Obstetrics: The bottom line is ultrasound has been around for fucking ever, and there has never ever ever been a single case of a human fetus injured by it. Having said that, Radiologists are massive cowards so caution is still advised. Since temperature rises are most likely at bone surfaces and adjacent soft tissues, a baby with increasing mineralization (2nd and 3rd trimester) has the theoretical risk of the possibility of heating sensitive tissues such as brain and spinal cord. Sensitive tissues • Embryo less than eight weeks after conception • Head, brain or spine of any fetus or neonate • Eyeball (in a subject of any age). Testable Trivia: There are actually different “thermal indexes” for soft tissue, bone, and cranium. “Soft tissue Index” should be used in early gestation, “Bone index” should be used after 10 weeks (2nd and 3rd Trimester), when bone ossification is evident. General Recommendations (not at all evidence based) for 1st Trimester: - Pulsed doppler (spectral, power, and color) should NOT be routinely used - M-mode US is recommended instead of spectral Doppler US to document heart rate - Keep the TI under 1.0 (some sources say 0.7). - Scanning maternal uterine arteries with doppler is probably ok (as long as the fetus is outside the beam) Thermal Index: -Below 0.7 - for OB imaging -Between 1.0 - 1.5 - US should NOT exceed 30 mins -Between 2.5 - 3.0 - US should NOT exceed 1 min -Greater than 3.0 - US should NOT be used. 221
Blank for Notes: 222

Blank for Notes (or you could use this page to list all the exotic places you are going to vacation when you pass this test, finish training, andfinally start getting compensatedfor 15+years of horrible horrible sacrifice). “Hell, I can get you a toe by 3 o ’clock this afternoon with nail polish” 223

Nuclear medicine is unique in that instead of passing a beam of energy through the patient, you are injecting a tracer into the patient and observing a normal or abnormal distribution of that tracer. Stability / Decay: Everyone wants to be happy. I want to be happy, you want to be happy, and even isotopes want to be happy. We seek it in different ways. Some of us incorrectly thought that going to medical school would make us happy. Isotopes seek happiness through stability. There is this thing called the “band o f stability, ” that demonstrates the happy place atoms go to when they have a certain balance of protons and neutrons in the nucleus. It’s not exactly 1:1, but it’s pretty close. Neutrons “N” Protons = Neutrons (1:1) Beta Plus (Positron) or Electron Capture P > N Protons “Z” 224

As you can see from the chart depending on the imbalance of neutrons / protons different types of decay occur. Situation 1: “Lots of N eutrons, Not Enough Protons” Beta Minus Decay: The solution to the problem is to take one of your neutrons (you’ve got extra) and turn it into a proton. This change from a neutron to a proton results in the emission of a “beta particle” from the nucleus. WTF is a “Beta Particle ” ? - It’s basically an electron. Why not just call it an electron? Because “Real Electrons” don’t come from the nucleus. This pansy ass electron is a “beta male” electron - being Why is an electron (beta particle) being ejected? There are two reasons. (1) You need to change the charge of a neutron from neutral to positive. It’s best to think about neutrons as being 1/2 negative and 1/2 positive. If you eject the negative part, then you become all positive. The second reason (2) is to balance out the energy loss - going from a neutron to proton. In this task the proton doesn’t always get it done, so you have to emit a massless piece of shit called a “anti­ neutrino” just to balance out the energy. What kind o f a process is this (what fancy general chemistry vocabulary word) ? This process is an ISOBARIC transition. WTF does that mean? It means that the MASS DID NOT CHANGE. Yes, the atomic number changed (it increased - since you gained a proton), but because the mass of a neutron and proton are about the same (and the mass of an electron is pretty much nothing) the mass number remained constant. Is Beta Minus Good For Imaging? N o... it’s shit. But it can cause some damage. I f you had a Beta Emitter in a syringe would you want a lead shield or a plastic shield to block those beta particles (electrons) ? You want plastic - because plastic has a low Z. Lead’s high Z will actually result in Bremmstahlung x-rays (remember that’s one of the reasons why Tungsten is used as a target - high Z). £ > V 0 e © " " © o (wort? energy* ^ t ” "alanCin9 Po s - )ub omitted from the nucleus. 225

Situation 2a: “Lots of Protons, Not Enough Neutrons” There are two situations that can occur when you have too many protons and not enough neutrons, and just like in real life your choices depend on how much money you have. “The Rich Guy” - Beta Positive Decay It’s no big deal for the Rich Guy to buy a Ferrari or take an expensive vacation. It’s also no big deal for him to undergo beta positive decay. Why? Because he has 1.02 MeV and has no problem blowing it. Why does he have no problem blowing it? Because he didn’t work for it - his Daddy gave it to him. So here is how it goes down: A positively charged proton is converted into a neutral neutron. How does it do that? By giving up it’s positive charge in the form of a “positron” Just like in beta minus decay an addition POS neutrino is also emitted just to keep the energy balanced. What is this “Positron ” ? It’s basically an “anti-electron” or the bizarro superman (his exact opposite). It makes sense if you think about the proton needing to strip it’s positive charge away to become neutral. Is that the end of the story? Nope The Positron travels a Very Short Distance - Then it meets it’s arch nemesis, a real electron They fight and all our cold war fears are realized as they mutually annihilate each other. The result is the product of TWO 511 keV photons emitted exactly 180 degrees apart from each Where does this “511 ” number come from? Remember the 1.02 MeV needed to be considered a “rich guy” ? Well 511 keV + 511 keV = 1022 (or 1.02 MeV). Basically, they divided the money up and each photon got half. Positron emission is ALWAYS 511 keV and that’s why. 226

Situation 2b: “Lots of Protons, Not Enough Neutrons” “The Poor Guy” - Electron Capture If you weren’t bom with a silver spoon in your mouth it may be necessary to steal (capture) to be happy. Remember you are trying to change a positively charged proton into a neutral neutron. The rich guy had enough money (1.02 MeV) to bribe the positively charged part of the proton to leave. The poor guy doesn’t have that option. So instead of subtracting a positive from a positive to make a neutral - the strategy here is to ADD a negative to a positive to make a neutral. How do you pull this off? An electron is lured into the nucleus from the K shell. I imagine the Nucleus says something like “do you like candy?... I have some in the nucleus.” What kind o f a process is this (what fancy general chemistry vocabulary word) ? This process is an ISOBAR1C transition. Remember, just like Beta Minus, in Beta Plus the MASS DOES NOT CHANGE. Yes, the atomic number changed (it decreased - since you lost a proton), but the mass number remained constant. Is electron capture good for imaging? Yes - it’s excellent because it’s often coupled to a process referred to as “isomeric transition,” which results in the emission of characteristic gamma photons that can be imaged under a gamma camera. 227

T e ll M e M o re A b o u t th is “Is o m e ric T ra n s itio n ” After undergoing an isobaric transition (beta emission, positron emission, or electron capture), there is often left-over energy. This energy needs to be emitted prior to achieving final stability. One process for achieving this final stability is isomeric transition - i.e. emitting energy and dropping to the ground state. Gamma Emission: In most isomeric transitions, a nucleus will emit its excess energy in the form of a gamma photon. The energy of these photons is variable and depends on the energy differences between the intermediate and final states of the nucleus undergoing isomeric transition. Although the TOTAL energy emission for a given nuclei of a specific nuclide is the same, many times you have more than one intermediate state - with multiple different energy gamma photons.. This is why you see multiple peaks for different tracers (Gallium has 4, Indium has 2, etc...). Indium 111 4 9 Electron Capture (Isobaric Transition) 171 keV 245 keV Isomeric Transition Long vs Short - “Metastable” - In most cases, the time spent in the intermediate state is very short. The transition from isobaric to isomeric transitions is like “bam-bam.” However, there is a situation where the intermediate state is prolonged - a so called “metastable state.” Ever notice that Technetium is called “99m” ? Well that “m” stands for metastable. The main value of this is the ability to separate out the electron radiation (bad) and the photon radiation (good). Therefore by using a nuclide that has already undergone an isobaric (election emitting) transition, you can expose the body to the useful gamma emissions from the isomeric transition. Mo 99 -► Tc 99m Isobaric Transition via Beta Minus Decay *emission of a beta particle (electron) X Tc99 \ Hanging Out in “metastable” limbo for a couple of hours Isomeric Transition *emission of 140keV photons 228

Tell Me More About this “Isomeric Transition” - Continued Nemesis - Internal Conversion Gamma emission has an arch nemesis called “Internal Conversion”. The details of this probably aren’t super important so I won’t go into excruciating detail: just know that under certain conditions, the energy you would normally get rid of via gamma emission can be transferred to an electron within the atom. This transfer of energy is more than the binding energy and will expel the electron. The vacancy will lead to a downward cascade from a higher level - and result in either the emission of a characteristic x-ray or an Auger electron. Characteristic X-Ray Emission Bad because you are emitting particles - which can cause harm, not imageable gamma photons which contribute to the study. Augur Electron Emission Alpha Decay For Completeness - Alpha Decay = This tends to occur in heavier unstable atoms. Alpha particles are basically Helium nuclei (2 protons, 2 neutrons). Alpha particles are slow and fat. They can’t even penetrate a piece of paper, and are worthless for imaging. They can however fuck stuff up that is within close proximity - and therefore can be used in treatment situations (i.e. bone pain cancer mets via Radium 223). 229

Summary /Rapid Review Alpha Decay Heavy Unstable Atoms Lots of tissue damage ' - ■ ' '■ ; Beta (-) Neutron Excess (Proton Deficiency) Electron Emission Can Damage DNA (basis of Radionucleotide Therapy) Beta (+) —Rival Of Electron Capture Proton Excess (Neutron Deficiency) Has at least 1.02MeV Positron Emission , leading to two 511 keV photons which fly 180 degrees apart Electron Capture - Rival Of Beta (+) Proton Excess (Neutron Deficiency) Does NOT require 1.02MeV Leads to gamma emission (sometimes) and characteristic radiation, both of which may be used in imaging Neutron Poor (Proton Rich) Beta + D°eso7TT‘ 1 °SM e f ave Electron Capture Isomeric Conversion (Gamma Emission) vs Neutron Rich (Proton Poor) Beta Internal Conversion “Heavy” Alpha Capture Alpha 230

Production There are two main ways you can create tracers, both of which alter the Neutron/Proton ratio to make them unstable: (1) Bombardment (in a nuclear reactor or cyclotron) (2) Fission Bombardment: This is basically striking target elements with either neutrons (in a nuclear reactor), or with charged particles (alpha particles, protons, or deuterons) in a cyclotron. The downside to the nuclear reactor method is that you have to clean up the leftover parent element. This is referred to as not being carrier free. The cyclotron has the advantage of producing elements via transmutation, therefore you don’t have any parents to clean up -aka “Carrier Free.” -----------------------------— ......... ...... “Neutrons" I Nudeai Reacloi Example: 9842Mo + Neutron ^ " 42M 0 + Gamma This usually results in transmutation, so you don’t have to clean up the parent. -CARRIER FREE Fission: Neutrons are fired into large atoms (like Uranium and Plutonium) and split them into pieces. This basically just results in a bunch of random crap being made; Iodine-131, Xenon-133, Strontium-90m, Molybdenum-99, Cesium-137. Obviously the desired isotope also has a bunch of fission products (contaminants) which have to be separated out - can be done with chemistry. These guys demonstrate a bunch of different decay methods. Neutron Activation: Target atoms eat up neutrons to form a new isotope. These neutrons don’t really even need to be accelerated. Because the products are isotopes of the target atoms, they cannot easily be separated from each other. As a result these are NOT carrier free. These neutron rich products tend to decay via beta emission. 231

Radioactive Decay “Half Life” - Which usually means the physical half-life. The amount of time necessary for a radionuclide to be reduced to half of its existing activity. In addition to physical half life, you have biologic half life. The biologic half life is basically how long it takes to shit or piss half the tracer out. The “effective half life” takes both of these things into consideration. Let’s do a couple o f practice questions: 1) The nukes department has a supply o f 10 Ci o f 99mTc at 8:00 AM. The patient is showing up the next day at 8:00 AM the following morning — how much activity will be left? To answer this question you need to know the half life of the agent. They are NOT going to tell you that (see the list at the end of this chapter & book). The second thing you’ll need to do is figure out how many half lives are going down in the time period in question. The half life of Tc is 6 hours. 8am to 8am is 24 hours. 24 Hours 10 Ci 5 Ci 12 18h0urS hours 2.5 Ci 6 hours ° hours 1.25 0.615 Ci Ci So the answer is 0.6 Ci. Now lastly I want you to watch those units! It’s the oldest trick in the book to change them up. So it could be 0.6 Ci, or 600 mCi 2) 1-131 has a biologic half life o f 24 days. What is the effective half life? To answer this question you again need to know the physical half life of the agent. You have to memorize those. The biologic half life is something no one knows - if they want you to solve for effective half life they will give that one too you. The second thing you need to know is the formula of effective half life. The half life of 1-131 is 8 days. The formula is 1/Effective = 1/Physical + 1/Biologic 1/Te = 1/8 + 1/24 = 1/6 , or 1/Te = 3/24 + 1/24 = 4/24, reduced is 1/6 — so 6 days Gamesmanship: If you have a large mismatch with biological and physical half life, effective half life basically becomes the short one. In other words, if you breath the Xe out in 15 seconds it doesn’t matter what the physical half-life is. On the other hand, if your biological half-life is 10,000 years then you are 100% counting on physical half-life to clear it. How long do you have to keep radioactive material? The general rule is 10 half lives. 232

“Activity” is defined by the amount of disintegrations per second. Historically this was measured in Curie (Ci) which is 3.7 xlO10disintegrations per second. The new SI unit is the Becquerel (Bq), which is one disintegration per second. “Specific Activity” is defined as the activity per unit of mass (Bq/g). The longer the half-life, the lower its specific activity. How the Gamma Camera Works: A gamma camera takes photons emitted from the radionuclide, turns it into a light pulse, and then takes that light pulse and makes some voltage. The voltage makes a picture. I Collimator Crystal Photomultiplier Computer 233

Collim ator: There are a lot of unwanted photons (background, off-axis, Compton scatter, etc...). The purpose of the collimator is to reduce scatter and allow for correct localization of radionuclide events. It works by discriminating based on direction of travel. It can NOT tell the difference in photon energy (that’s done by the photon height analyzer). Parallel Hole Collimator: “The Work Horse” This can be modified based on the energy of the tracer being used. Low Energy (l-200keV)- Thinner Plates: 99mTc, 1231, I33Xe, 201 TI Medium Energy (200-400keV): 67Ga, 111 In High Energy (> 400keV) - Thicker Plates: 1311 (technically most energy peaks are medium) Sensitivity uv Resolution: These two guys have an inverse relationship. As one goes up the other goes down. A high sensitivity collimator will allow twice as many counts to be imaged but will degrade the spacial resolution. High sensitivity collimators are important with dynamic imaging (like the flow phase of a bone scan). Effects o f Distance on Sensitivity and Resolution: Distance has NO effect on Sensitivity (increased distance reduce counts by inverse square, but the increased distance allows for a greater field of view. NO change occurs in the net counts). Distance DOES affect Resolution (septa are no longer able to eliminate photons from oblique angles as distance increases). Septal Length (collimator depth): Short septa give a crappy spatial resolution, but better sensitivity. Long septa give excellent spatial resolution, but crappy sensitivity (noisier image). Hole Diameter: Wide holes = Highly sensitive, low resolution. Narrow holes = low sensitivity, high resolution. Parallel Hole Factor ■T V -X • ■ ■ Septa Length Long Septa: - Low Sensitivity (Noisy) - High Spatial Resolution Short Septa: - High Sensitivity - Low Spatial Resolution Hole Diameter Wider Hole: - High Sensitivity - Low Resolution Narrow Hole: - Low Sensitivity - High Resolution Septa Thickness Thick Septa: - Less Penetration - Less Available space for holes (Less Sensitivity) Thin Septa - More Penetration (Blur) - More Available space for holes (More sensitivity) In General: • You want to use long thick septa + wide holes for high energy • You want to use short thin septa + narrow holes for lower energy 234

Resolution Pearls: Pinhole Collimator: Magnifies and inverts image. Used for thyroids and other small parts. It’s usually cone shaped. Magnification occurs at a ratio of: pinhole to detector “F” / pinhole to patient “B” If F = B there is no magnification If F > B = there is magnification If F < B = object gets smaller. An increase in object-to- collimator distance degrades the system spatial resolution. The practical application is patient positing. The closer the detector is to the patient, the better the resolution of the images. In other words, if you move the pinhole far enough away from the patient it will make the image smaller. Magnification at the front (anterior) is greater than the back (objects are 3D), this is why large objects get distorted and pinholes work best on small things. Sensitivity for pinhole cameras is garbage. Converging Hole Collimator (Cone Beam): Holes are close together on the object side and far apart on the crystal/camera side. Magnifies WITHOUT inverting the image. Diverging Collimator: The opposite of converging. Holes are far apart on the object side, and close together on crystal/camera side. This takes a large object and minimizes it. The result is that you can image a large part of the body on a small crystal. So increased area of imaged object, decreased sensitivity and resolution. Collimator Type Parallel Hole “The work horse.” You want the collimator and detector as close as possible to the patient for the best spatial resolution (this is affected by distance). Sensitivity is NOT affected by distance. Pinhole Magnifies and inverts image - used for thyroids and other small parts. Large objects get distorted (front is magnified more than back). Sensitivity is garbage. Converging Magnifies without inverting Diverging Takes a large object and makes it small. Parallel s \ / ✓ X \ / ' V '/ Pinhole Converging Diverging ■ ) - < t - K 235

Scintillation Crystal- Once the photon emerges from the collimator, it impacts on the crystal. The crystal is made of sodium iodine doped with thallium. The crystal has the property that when struck with a photon it produces a pulse of light. The thicker the crystal the less photons will be wasted (just pass through). On this same principal, thin crystals will let more photons through (without generating light). Energy of the photons themselves also plays a similar role. Higher energy photons will pass right through the crystal (lower ones won’t). A thicker crystal is obviously better for high energy photons. So why not just make all the crystals super thick? Apparently you sacrifice spatial resolution with a thick crystal, because with a thinner crystal the photomultiplier tubes can sit closer to the event and more accurately localize it. * Thick crystal = Better Sensitivity, But Worse Spatial Resolution * Thin crystal = Better Spatial Resolution, but Worse Sensitivity Photomultiplier Tubes (PMT) - The PMTs detect the light and convert it into an electric signal or measurable magnitude. The more PMTs you have, the more light you pick up and the greater the resolution. The PMTs record two things: (a) location - on x and y axis, and (b) signal intensity “Z.” The X and Y coordinates go straight to the computer. The Signal Intensity (Z) goes to this gadget called a pulse height analyzer (discussed next). Pulse Height Analyzer - The function of this thing is to discard background crap, and only look at the photons from the tracer you are looking for. The background crap can come from multiple sources (compton scatter, iodine escape from the crystal itself, backscatter). The texts make a point in distinguishing the difference of background from a point source versus background from a person. Basically you should know that the Compton scatter in a person is a lot closer to the energy you want to image, but really degrades the images. FROM A POINT SOURCE Compton Edge Compton Scatter Tc Photon Energy Peak FROM A PERSON Tc Photon Energy Peak I ^0° You can set a window that will exclude all that crap higher or lower to the desired peak. Some cameras allow you to record multiple peaks (great for things like 67Ga and 11'In which have multiple peaks or dual tracer studies). 236

Key Concept: “Dow nscatter”- There is a thing called downscatter, which is super super high yield. Essentially high energy photons can spill into the window of a low energy emitter, mainly resulting from Compton scatter effects. Example 1: You are doing a V/Q scan using 133Xe and 99mTc. Xe has an energy of 8IkeV, whereas Tc has an energy of 140keV. The Tc Compton scatter is gonna range down from about 135 to 90 (as in the above diagram “from a person”). So if you inject the Tc first and image that will turn out just fine. But when you give the patient the Xe they will still have the Tc on board so you will be getting Xe peaks at 81, with a bunch of Tc Compton scatter all over it. So you totally hosed yourself, because the Pulse Height Analyzer can’t tell what is Compton scatter from the Tc and what is signal from the Xe. The solution is to image with the Xe first, because the 81 keV and downstream Compton effects from the Xe would not affect the peak at Tc (140) at all. Hopefully that makes sense. Otherwise just remember, LOWER PHOTON ENERGY TRACERS MUST BE USED FIRST. Example 2: If you are using 99mTc and 67Ga for a bone scan of the spine looking for osteomyelitis. Gallium has a bunch of photopeaks (93, 184, 300, 393) and an effective half life of 50 hours. You have to give the Tc first and image, otherwise you’d end up waiting 50 hours. Tc Photo Peak 140 237

SPECT and Matrix: Gamma camera imaging can be “static” or “dynamic.” When improved spatial resolution is needed “SPECT” can be performed by rotating a camera 180 or 360 degrees around the patient. The longer you image on SPECT the better it looks, but patients just can’t hold still forever - and movement degrades the images. Another thing to consider is matrix size. Matrix size: Size matters in most things in life, matrix being no different. A matrix size of 128x128 has superior resolution to 64x64. However, a larger matrix size means longer acquisition time (which patients hate), and reduced count density per pixel (which impacts image contrast) S tar A rtifact: If you have very focal intense energy you can sometimes see a star artifact, caused by septal penetration of the hexagonal collimator holes. This is typically seen in the thyroid bed after a high therapeutic dose (using a medium energy collimator, instead of high). / I MJ Star Artifact - From Septal Penetration *Star Pattern is seen if collimator holes are arranged in a hexagonal pattern 238

Quality Control for Gamma Cameras Possibly the most dreaded portion of radiology multiple choice exams (right up there with safety, and mammo MQSA). Daily, weekly, and quarterly tests are done on the camera (BY A TECH! NOT A RADIOLOGIST!!!) to make sure it’s functioning properly. As if this wasn’t bad enough, pinning down “official” recommendations is difficult as the NRC essentially says hire a medical physicist and do whatever the fuck they say. Honestly, if I was running the NRC I’d give similar advice. The problem is choosing consensus information for the purpose of multiple choice. The following is my best attempt at guessing “the right answer” for trivia that could be asked. Four parameters are usually tested for: field uniformity, window setting, image linearity and spatial resolution, and Center of Rotation (COR). Field Uniformity: So the photo multiplier tubes can have subtle variability in what voltage they assign to a given photon of light. Additionally, the crystal isn’t totally uniform, and has subtle variations in thickness. For exam purposes, you want to try and keep these two things as uniform as possible. A 2%-5% non-uniformity is allowed (1 % if SPECT). The test is known as a “Flood”, and is used to see if the camera can produce a uniform image along the entire crystal surface. It’s done two ways: (1) Extrinsically (with a collimator), and (2) Intrinsically (with NO collimator). You can use either a Na99mTc04 source or a Co57 source to perform the flood. The recommended counts for both extrinsic and intrinsic range somewhere between 5-10 Million (Siemens recommends 10 Million). • Extrinsic (WITH Collimator) - is done daily. This tests the collimator and crystals. • Intrinsic (WITHOUT Collimator) - is done weekly. A computer will check to make sure the field is uniform. You can also visually inspect the test images, to see if there is an obvious problem like a bunch of bullseyes, or a segmental region of failed PMTs. Norm al Bull’s Eye -“Flood” - For Appearance of Tubes Field Uniformity Indicates a Problem 239

Energy Window: The correct window needs to be used prior to each study, therefore this test should be performed daily. The most common approach to testing this is to use a symmetric window centered at the peak energy used in the imaging test. The source can be a syringe, a vial, or if absolutely necessary the patient. For Tc you would use a 20% window centered at 140keV. Image Linearity and Spatial Resolution: Lead bar phantoms with parallel lines, placed between the collimator and a 57Co sheet, are used to test image resolution and linearity. This only needs to be done weekly. Resolution is defined by the ability to differentiate between two distinct points (can you tell the bars are separate). Linearity is tested by looking to see if all the bars are straight (some distortion at the edges is ok). The bars should NOT look wavy. Quality Control for Gamma Cameras Continued Center of Rotation: Gamma cameras that are used for SPECT have to be routinely monitored for alignment offset at the COR (Center of Rotation). The test is done with 5 small 99mTc point sources along the axis of rotation. The axis should be straight, with minimal deviation. Most sources will say this is performed monthly. Bar Phantom — Lines should be straight (NOT WAVY) 240

Safety This is also an excruciating topic for Radiologists to learn about. As previously stated nukes is full of random trivia, and let’s be honest academic nuclear medicine tends to attract eccentric personalities... and they are writing the questions. Lead Aprons / Film Badges / Ring Badges Why don’t nuclear medicine techs wear lead? Two reasons: (1) Thin lead doesn’t stop gamma rays. To stop them it would have to be so heavy you couldn’t move. (2) When high energy gamma rays collide with a dense material that is not thick enough to stop them (lead apron) they rapidly slow down and lose energy which turns them into deeply penetrating Bremsstrahlung x-rays - which actually makes the dose worse. Correct Positioning on the Film Badge and Ring Badge * Film Badge: Should be worn on the collar at the chest / neck level • Ring Badge: Worn on dominant hand, index finger, label in towards source (usually this means towards the palm), under a glove (to avoid contamination) Instruments: • Sodium Iodine Well Counter - This is basically a small gamma camera, with just one PMT. There is a hole in the block of Nal crystal into which the sample is placed (so it’s surrounding the sample). So this gives you great efficiency in detection, but has a problem with being overwhelmed. If the sample exceeds 5000 counts per second (a lot less than a micro curie), it will be under reported. It’s good for in-vitro blood or urine samples. It’s good for "wipe test ” samples. • Thyroid Probe - This is a modified version of the Nal well counter. Its primary use is to calculate thyroid uptake values. The device has shielding, with a small opening that is pointed at the patient, at a precise distance. Dose is compared to a calibrated capsule of the same radionuclide. Well Counter 241

• Geiger -Muller Counter - this is a device used to detect small amounts of radioactive contamination. The device has a gas-filled chamber, which when it comes in contact with radiation becomes ionized and creates some voltage between a cathode and an anode - lots of beeping and clicking occurs. There are two ways that questions about this device are classically asked; (1) by just showing you a picture of one and saying “what is this”, (2) by testing the concept of “dead time.” What is this “dead time” ? Although the device is very sensitive, it is also vulnerable to being overloaded by a large dose of radiation. Then you have “dead time” as the ionization must dissipate before it can respond again. The maximum dose it can handle is about lOOmR/h. So if the device clicks once and stops it might be because of “dead time” (it might also be “dead time” for you). Trivia - The GM Counter can NOT provide info on radiation type • Ion Chamber - This is what is used when higher doses are expected. You don’t have the dead time problem, for reasons that don’t matter. Ion chambers can be used to detect exposure rates from 0.1 to lOOR/h (note the unit change). The dose calibrator used in most nuclear medicine departments is actually an ionizing chamber. Detects Ionizing Radiation (alpha, beta, gamma) For measuring dose rate. Used with higher rates. Very Sensitive Lower Sensitivity Great for Low-Level Radioactive Survey Stable across a wide voltage range - Excellent for accurate estimates (or exposure). Terrible for Very High Radiation Fields (“Dead Time”) Geiger - Muller Counter With Pancake Probe Ionizing Chamber 242

Device Trivia Personal Dosimeters “Pocket Ionization Detector” - uses a miniature ionization chamber. They give you real-time estimated dose, but must be charged and zero’d prior to use. These are not used anymore - which makes them high yield. “SolidState Dosimeter” - Accumulated dose or rate can be read real time with LCD display. “Film Badge” - Uses a thin metallic filter with a radiosensitive film. The degree of darkening (relative optic density) corresponds with dose. They can be damaged by temperature, humidity, etc... “Optically Stimulated Dosimeter ” - Replaced the film badge. Chips / Strips are placed under a filter. “Thermo-luminescent Dosimeter” - The ring badge. Should be worn under a glove, with the label facing the palm (target). Survey Meters G-M and Ionization Detectors - discussed above Well Counter Basically a small gamma camera, with one PMT. Susceptible to “dead time” at counts over 5000 per second. Good for urine and blood samples. Good for “wipe test” samples. Dose Calibration and Automated Dose Injection Systems Used to measure radiopharmaceuticals. Thyroid Uptake Probe Compares counts from region over the thyroid to a calibrated capsule of the same radionuclide. The probe is a cylindrical scintillator detector attached to a PMT. A positioning guide keeps the distance constant Intra-operative Probes Used for lymphoscintigraphy 243

Readout of activity is made in mCi. The range of most devices is 30 microCi to 2 Ci. Dose should be within 5% of computed activity, and this should be checked daily. * Consistency - should be within 5% of computed activity. Checked with reference sources (checked Daily) * Linearity - accurate readout for activities over the whole range of potentially encountered activities - checked with a large activity of Tc (around 200mCi) and decaying it down to less than the smallest activity you would measure for use. Or - the easy way - use a Calicheck© or Lineator© kit (which contains sheets of varied thickness of lead, simulating decay over time), (checked quarterly). * Accuracy - Standard measurements of radiotracers measured and compared to what the activity should be (performed at installation of the device and annually). * Geometry - Correction for different positioning and size (different volumes of liquids) of the sample (performed at installation and any time you move the device) Q/A on the Dose Calibrator (Ionizing Chamber): 244

Regulations: No bureaucracy is complete without lots of rules and regulations (and of course agencies to police such regulations). The Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) is where you will want to look for all your rules and regulations. Specifically part 19 (inspections), part 20 (radiation protection), and part 35 (human use of radioisotopes) . The “NRC” is the governing body that has been charged with the task of enforcing all these various directives. It is possible for individual states to reach an agreement with the Federal Government to enforce these rules on their own. These are called “Agreement States”, and the main thing to know is that they can be more strict, but not less strict than the national agency. NRC Trivia: - 10 CFR part 19: Notices, instructions, and reports to workers. - 10 CFR part 20: Standards for protection against radiation. - 10 CFR part 35: Medical use of by-product material. Radiation Safety / Contamination Accidents happen. Rules to know for answering questions about spills: First Things First - is it a MAJOR spill or a MINOR spill ? MAJOR Spills * Activity level greater than 100 mCi of Tc-99m is considered a major spill. * Activity level greater than 100 mCi of TI-201 is considered a major spill. * Activity level greater than 10 mCi of In-111, is considered to represent a major spill. * Activity level greater than 10 mCi of 1-123, is considered to represent a major spill. * Activity level greater than 10 mCi of Ga-67, is considered to represent a major spill. * Activity level greater than 1 mCi of 1-131 is considered to constitute a major spill. Major Spill - what do I do ??? 1. Clear area. 2. Cover spill with absorbent paper. Do NOT clean it up. 3. Clearly indicate boundaries of spill area. Limit movement of contaminated persons 4. Shield source if possible 5. Notify the Radiation Safety Officer immediately 6. Decontaminate persons Radiation Safety Officer needs to be notified immediately when a major spill occurs, to direct the decontamination process. Minor Spill = You Clean It Up Major Spill = Don’t Clean it Up, Call the Radiation 245

MINOR Spills (1) Protect the Patient: If a spill occurs while a patient is in distress, address the patient first. Once he/she is stable then address the spill. (2) Confine the Spill / Limit the Spread: Secure the area and make sure people aren’t tracking it all over the place. (3) Clean Up the Spill: Use gloves (wear shoes), and all other personal protective equipment including a radiation badge. If possible tongs or forceps are even better for grabbing stuff. Use a damp absorbent material to clean the spill (working from the outside to the center). (4) Survey Cleanup Items: Anything used in the clean up needs to be surveyed or presumed contaminated (held until they decay to safe levels - rule is 10 half lives). (5) Survey Cleanup People: People also need to be surveyed by the radiation safety officer (in a different area - to avoid count interference). What if ? • What if it gets on my clothes ? Take them off - they are contaminated. Clothes will be held by the RSO until activity has decayed to safe levels.. ..at which point they will be worn to the club. • What if it gets on my skin ? Wash with soap and water (don’t scrub so hard you break your skin .... you fucking psycho). • What if there is a Xenon leak? • Without alarming the patient (i.e. don’t yell “the radioactive gas is leaking!!” - even though that would technically be true), instruct all individuals to leave room as quickly as possible. Close the door. • Testable Trivia = The wipe test does NOT work on Xenon contamination. Piss and Shit ? What about piss and shit ? Surely the NRC has something to say about “excreta.” Surprisingly the government has not yet decided to regulate the bowel movements and urination of its citizens. In other words, for the purpose of multiple choice the flavorful urine and delicious poops of patients treated with radioactive materials can be flushed into the sewer in any amount. Even if the turd is so large that it wraps around the bowl like 3 times, and the janitor (or resident) has to cut it into pieces with a plunger to make it go down... that is still ok - per the NRC. Xenon Leak -Additional Resource - Youtube: “Seinfeld - George and the fire” For the purpose of multiple choice, that would not be the appropriate way to handle a Xenon leak. In George’s defense, he was simply trying to lead the way. They needed a leader. 246

Obviously it is best if the Radiation Safety Officer (RSO) is involved and doing all that stuff. In the real world that is what will happen. But the ABR feels that on an intermediate level test you should be able to perform the tasks of doctor, tech, radiation safety officer, and janitor (while receiving the salary equivalent of the janitor). Regulations Affecting the General Public Regulations demand the following: • Annual Dose limit of 100 mrem to the public • Not greater than 2 mrem per hour - in an “unrestricted area” • “Restricted Area” = Any place that receives a dose greater than 2 mrem/h Signs must be placed with the following slogans: • Radiation Area: Any place you could get 0.005 rem (0.05mSv) in 1 hour at 30cm • High Radiation Area: Any place you could get 0.1 rem (lmSv) in 1 hour at 30cm • Very High Radiation Area: Any place you could get 500 rads (5 gray) in 1 hour at 1 meter NRC Occupational Exposure Dose Limits Just like bowling... there are rules. This isn’t Nam. • Total Body Dose per Year = 5 rem (50 mSv) • Dose to the Ocular Lens per year =15 rem (150 mSv) • Total equivalent organ dose (skin is also an organ) per year = 50 rem (500mSv) • Total equivalent extremity dose per year = 50 rem (500mSv) • Total Dose to Embryo/fetus over entire 9 months - 0.5rem (5 mSv). If the fetus has already got 5mSv at the time of declaration - the NRC states you can get 0.5 mSv more for the remainder of the pregnancy. That’s seriously a rule. It basically says “Ahh... give him 0.5 more - he ’11 be fine ”. Unit Mischief - Be Prepared For Fuckery 1 rad = 1 rem, 1 rad = 0.01 Gy 1mSv = 100 mrem = 0.1 rem Gamesmanship “The General Public” Don’t expect the question writer to use the words “general public” in the question header. It’s much more likely they will describe someone - and have you understand that person meets criteria for “the general public. ” Examples: • Old Lady waiting for her scan in the lobby = “The General Public” • That Girl who works the front desk (the one with the big boobs) = “The General Public” • Homeless Guy looking for used cigarette butts around the corner of the nukes building (the one with the large urine stain on his pants) = “The General Public” 247

Recordable and Reportable Events The dose you order should be the dose the patient gets (sorta). The limit is 20% from the dose via the NRC (and 10% in some agreement states). You want to be a Radiologist? You better get your vocab straight on this stuff. Bean counters take this stuff pretty seriously: When does a medical event occur??? Well you have to meet two criteria: (1) You have to FUCK IT UP — Dude, are you fucking this up? • Wrong drug, Wrong route, Wrong patient, or Wrong dose (more than 20%). • OR Patient receives a dose to a part of the body other than the intended treatment site that exceeds by 50% or more the dose expected by proper administration and prescription (2) You have to HARM THE PATIENT: “Harm ” defined as either: A. Whole body dose > 5 rem, or B. Single organ dose > 50 rem Medical Events require you to call the doctor who ordered it, patient, and NRC/State and explain what happen and if anything needs to be done about it. Recordable events have to be documented (recorded) and kept for safe keeping - for 5 years* (most other records need to be kept 3 years) Recordable Event Reportable Medical Event Whole Body Dose < 5 rem Whole Body Dose > 5 rem Single Organ Dose < 50 rem Single Organ Dose > 50 rem There s some fuckery afoot Watch your units! 5 rem = 0.05 Sv = 50mSv You Done F’d Up (wrong drug, wrong dose - by 20%, wrong pt, etc... Reportable Event Whole Body Dose > or < 5 rem Single Organ Dose > or <50 rem Call NRC within 24 hrs Write NRC Letter within 15 days Present your testicles to the NRC for castration (within 30 days) Notify Referring Doctor within 24 hours Notify the Patient (or let referring do it) % Recordable Event Record Locally Institutional Review 248

Receiving, Storing, and Disposing of R adioactive M aterial Within 3 hours of receipt (3 working hours) you (the tech) have to survey packages when they arrive. This process involves a GM counter test at the surface and 1 meter from the package, as well as wipes of all surfaces of the package (>6600 dpm/300 cm2 is not allowed). Keep the package in a controlled area, like the hot lab (just in case A1 Qaeda invades your hospital). Contact both the shipper and the NRC if beyond allowable limits. Package labels and allowable limits White 1: No special handling, surface dose rate < 0.5 mRem/hr, 1 meter ~ 0 mRem/hr Yellow 2: Special handling required, surface dose rate <50 mRem/hr, 1 meter < 1 mRem/hr Yellow 3: Special handling required, surface dose rate < 200 mRem/hr, 1 meter < 10 mRem/hr *Seriously, these assholes couldn’t pick another color? - instead they choose “yellow 3 ” “Transport Index” - “T.I.” - This is the measured max dose at 1 meter. This is an actual dose rate measured at 1 meter (not an allowable dose rate), at the time of shipping • Radioactive Label 1: White 1 - There is no T.I. because the rate at 1 meter will be so low. • Radioactive Label 2: Yellow 2 - The T.I. is < 1.0 mRem per hour. • Radioactive Label 3: Yellow 3 - The T.I. is > 1.0 mRem per hour. “Common Carriers” - A truck that carries regular packages and radioactive material. The T.I. should not exceed 10 mRem / per hour. The surface rate should not be more than 200mRem. “Multiple Packages ” - Those shipped together; the sum should NOT exceed 50 mRem. — (switching gears gracefully) — Trivia: 5 mRem/hr (0.05mSv) is a “Radiation Area” 100 mRem/hr = “High Radiation” Area 249

Tc-99 I now want to transition into discussing Tc-99. It’s the workhorse of nuclear medicine, and its production / purity is likely high yield for multiple choice. First let’s talk (read) about how Tc is made. Vacuum s ' Step 2 : Radionuclide Purity Test You want to make sure you just have Tc, and you didn’t wash any Mo off as well. Mo that is in the sample is called “break through. ” The basis for the test is to look for the different photo peaks of the radionuclides. The sample is placed behind a lead shield. Mo is assayed for first. The high energy photons of Mo (like 740 keV) will NOT be attenuated by the shield, but the 140 keV Tc photons will be. • NRC allows no more than 0.15 micro Ci of Mo per 1 milli Ci of Tc, at the time of administration. Few pitfalls here: • (I) note the unit change from micro to milli, • (2) note the “time o f administration ” * If ratio is less than 0.038 at the time of elution, the material will be suitable for injection for at least 12 hours. Step 1 "M o / 99mjc Generation Because Molybdenum’s half life is longer than Tc (Mo = 67 hours, Tc = 6 hours), it can be made and shipped in a Tc generator. Mo adheres to the aluminum column tightly. As it decays, it does not stick as tightly and can be washed off with saline across the column. When it comes out, the Tc is stuck to Na (Na99mTc04). The piece of trivia to know is that it’s in a +7 valence state, and must be reduced to be used. This is accomplished with stannous ions. Saline Mo-99 Lined Aluminum Canister 250

Step 3: Chemical Purity Test Remember that the column is made of Aluminum Oxide, which can wash off, clump up with the Tc, and show up as liver activity, or cause sulfur colloid aggregation and show up in the lungs on liver spleen scan. The test for purity is performed with pH paper. The allowed amount is < 10 microgram A1 per 1 ml. Step 4: Radiochemical Purity Test As mentioned above, Tc comes out of the generator as Na99mTc04. So, to use it for anything useful it needs to be reduced (accomplished by adding it to SnCh). Thin layer chromatography is used to assess for purity. Limits for Free Tc * 95% Na99mTc04 * 92% for 99mTc sulfur colloid (MAA) * 91% for all other Tc radiopharmaceuticals Buckle Up Bitches: Questions can be sneaky.... My thoughts on some potential pitfalls: • When you check the Radiochemical Purity of 99mTc-MAA, you are checking for free pertechnetate (TcO 4) • Testing for Chemical Purity is NOT mandatory in NRC States . The 99Mo and 99mTc ratio must be known at the time of ADMINISTRATION, not elution . When you are checking for Radionuclide Purity (breakthrough Mo), you have to assay for Mo FIRST, before the Tc (to prevent issues with residual charge). • Technically 99mTc made in a generator is not considered carrier free because of the presence of 99Tc which is technically a radionuclide (but essentially stable since it has a half life of 200,000 years). Free Tc Free Tc can occur from a lack of stannous ions (reducing agents), or accidental air injection into the vial or syringe (which oxidizes). The way it’s shown on Tc scan is with gastric, salivary glands, and thyroid uptake. Free Tc: Gastric Uptake on Bone Scan ‘ Incidental Note of Sternal Met from Breast CA Aluminum Contamination Can Be Shown Two Ways: 1. Liver Spleen Scan + Lung = Aluminum Contamination 2. Tc scan + Liver Activity = Aluminum Contamination 251

Vocab What is it? Tested? Limit? Radionuclide Purity How much Mo in the Tc ? Tested in a dose calibrator with lead shield 0.15 microcuries of Mo per 1 millicurie of Tc Chemical Purity How much Al in the Tc ? Tested with pH paper <10 micrograms Al per 1 ml Radiochemical Purity How much Free Tc? Tested with Thin Layer Chromotography * 95% Na99mTc04 * 92% for 99mTc sulfur colloid (MAA) 91% for all other Tc radiopharmaceuticals Equilibria Equilibrium: A 2002 dystopian future sci-fi movie where both feelings and artistic expression are outlawed (similar to radiology residency). It’s also a term to describe when the concentration of parent and daughter isotopes are equal. Transient Equilibrium: This type of equilibrium occurs when the half life of the daughter is a little bit shorter than the parent. The classic example is the Moly-99 generator making Tc-99. A transient equilibrium occurs after 4 half lives (usually). Secular Equilibrium: This type of equilibrium occurs when the half life of the daughter is wav wav wav shorter than the parent. Apparently, in one of the “lesser languages” that I don’t speak -Latin or French “secular” means “long period of time.” Parent ("Moly) A t this “transient” point they are equal Daughter (99mTc) 252

This vs T h at - C ritical Organ vs T arg et Organ: “Critical Organ” - An organ that limits the dose of the radiopharmaceutical due to the increased susceptibility of the critical organ for cancer. This may or may not be the “Target Organ.” “Target Organ”- This is the organ you want the tracer to accumulate in. It’s your organ of interest. A few generalizations on Critical Organs: If you are trying to figure it out, it’s the organ that the tracer is going to spend the most time in. For example, Gallium ends up in the bowel. Tc RBC scans are going to end up passing through the heart a lot, unless they are heat treated then the spleen eats them. Tc-MAG3s and DTPA is going to be the bladder, but DMSA (which sticks to the kidney) is going to be the kidney. Why all the Controversy ? You will read conflicting information for many of these. Apparently, there is a fair amount of bickering among PhDs on how / when to calculate the dose to come up with these numbers. To know which organ to pick on the test simply read the mind of the person who wrote the question. Spleen: • -Octreotide • -Damaged RBCs . -In-WBC Proximal Colon • Sulfur Colloid (oral) • Sestamibi Bladder: • MAG 3 • 1123 MIBG • MDP *some source say bone Distal Colon: • Gallium Liver: -In-ProstaScint -M31 MIBG -Sulfur Colloid (IV) Gallbladder Wall: • HIDA Renal Cortex: • Thallium • DMSA 253

SPECT What is this SPECT? - The simple way to think about it is like this. CT is to plain film, as SPECT is the planar. Basically you take a gamma camera(s) (pretty damn similar to the one you use for planar) and rotate them around the patient. What this does is give you a 3D look — instead of a 2D look (like you get with planar). SPECT is a single photon system - unlike PET which uses two photons (from a positron annihilation event). Just like a regular planar scan, SPECT is not fast. Each projection takes like 30 seconds and the total scan time is around 15 mins. The matrix size is usually around 128 x 128 (for things that are not cardiac). The scan uses iterative reconstruction to make a picture. Because the data is isotropic volume, reconstruction in 3 planes is possible. Sensitivity is depth dependent, with radiation from different tissue origins attenuated to different degrees. Special collimators with longer holes are used to help improve the collection of photons. Testable Point: The big advantage SPECT has over planar is improved contrast from overlapping structures. Testable Point: SPECT is depth-dependent (PET is not * more on that later). Tuning Fork Artifact: Cardiac SPECT uses a 180 degree orbit. When a point source is imaged, it should look like a point source. If there is an error with the center of rotation (misregistration error) then it will look like a tuning fork (two lines in one direction and one line in the other). The same appearance can be seen with motion. 254

PET An Overview: Remember, there are two situations that can occur when you have too many protons and not enough neutrons. The “Rich Guy” situation - where you have 1.02 MeV to blow - results in the emission of a positron during the conversion of a proton to a neutron. This positron is going to travel a very short distance (less than 2mm) before it meets its arch nemesis the electron. The two fight to a draw, mutually annihilating each other. The annihilation event splits that 1.02 MeV in half, creating two 511 keV photons which are emitted exactly 180 degrees from each other. PET scanners work by detecting these 511 keV photon pairs - doing lots of math to determine their location of origin. SPECT v.v PET - There are a few differences. (1) SPECT is a few cameras rotating around the patient. PET is a set of complete rings/ detectors surrounding the patient. The PET detectors are much more sensitive. (2) SPECT is set up to detect medium-ish energy photons. PET is set up to detect a very high energy photon (511 keV). Remember that Iodine 131 - which is considered “high energy” in the planar world - has an energy of around 365 keV. So 511 is HOT. The main difference here is the scintillation crystal used. Just like in a regular planar camera SPECT used Nal systems - whereas PET tends to use a thicker more robust crystal (Bismuth Germinate (BGO), Lutetium Oxyorthosilicate (LSO), or Lutetium Ytrium Oxyorthosilicate (LYSO) (3) PET counts are way way way more (like lOx more). This results in an image with much less mottle. Those are the big ones... I’ll mention a few more as we proceed with the discussion. Let us begin first by discussing “localization.” 255

Localization / Coincidence Detection As mentioned before, PET has a complete ring(s) around the patient. As the 511 pairs fire off the photons are presumably going to land on opposite sides of the ring - 180 degrees apart. A true understanding of how this decay process results in a picture is complex and well beyond the scope of the exam (trust me, no one writing the questions actually understands it). It will be the same deal when we discuss MRI in the next chapter. In the simplest terms, the computer assumes that these two 511 photons travel in a straight line ( “straight line o f coincidence ”) reality is that the line is never exactly 180 degrees straight. The degree to which the line of response is not linear is referred to as “Non- Collinearity. ” The 511 keV photons will strike the detector elements, which are usually made of some type of scintillation material (related trivia on these later). Each photon is evaluated separately by the computer for 3 things: (a) spatial information , (b) energy, and (c) arrival time. The individual photons will be considered a “pair” if they: (1) Stay within a present energy window: example ~ 25-35% Full Width Half Maximum or "FWHM” - centered at the 511 photo-peak. (2) Stay within a certain timing window (example — 5-10 nanoseconds) - meaning they struck opposite sides of the detector at nearly the same time. If two photons follow those criteria they will be consider a pair, and the “event” which created them will be calculated along the “Line O f Response” or "LOR. ” Most (> 90%) of the generated photons are rejected in this process. Rejecting Scatter: Rejecting photons that don’t meet the criteria based on time of incidence and photon peak are mechanisms for rejecting scatter. In a perfect world, both photons should measure 511. If a photon undergoes scatter it will lose energy, so you can exclude scatter by saying you will only keep a photon at 510 keV or above. However, if you exclude photons that are just barely off of the 511 mark you will exclude a ton of true events as well. So, it turns into a balance of sensitivity and specificity. A tighter window gives you a more specific exam, but you are going to lose a lot of normal counts (and decrease your sensitivity). Various Degrees of “Non-Collinearity.” 256

The previous page described the scenario where a pair of photons would be considered to represent a “true coincidence.” This is actually only 1 of 3 possible scenarios where the computer will record an event. There are two additional types of “false coincidences” - which are basically tricks / fuck ups - Scatter and Random. True Coincidence - Two 511 photons resulting from an annihilation reaction are detected in the same coincident window. Scatter Coincidence - One of the photons has a Compton interaction and is deflected, but still hits the detector within the coincident time window (just not in the calculated location). Trivia: The Compton event lowers the photon energy. Therefore you can reduce this by using a more narrow photo-peak window. Random Coincidence: Two photons, from different annihilation reactions just so happen to land within the same coincident window - creating the false calculation that they occurred from the same event. Trivia: The number of random events increases with the dose of administered activity. Trivia: The number of random events is decreased with lower dose / counts and a narrower time window setting. “Noise Equivalent Counts” or “NEC” is a metric used with PET imaging to describe the signal to noise ratio. It is basically a ratio of true coincidences over total coincidences (true + false) with some random “k” factor to describe stuff you don’t need to know about. There is a very low probability of using the equation for any math. I’d just understand that if a question writer says “NEC” they mean SNR (signal noise ratio) in the context of PET. Trivia: If you went to the PET Scanner store and you were trying to compare models, the one with the higher NEC (assuming injected dose is the same) will be one that can achieve superior contrast-to-noise and signal-to-noise ratios. True Coincidence Scatter Coincidence Random Coincidence 257

Continuing with this discussion.... If the photons meet the selected criteria and a Line Of Response has been generated, that data will be compiled along with all the other LORs to generate a sinogram (buncha wavy lines). That sinogram is then converted into a picture that makes sense to a human via the magic of complex math referred to as “iterative reconstruction algorithms. ” Nuts and Bolts on the Detectors: PET uses Scintillators. Remember that Scintillators are materials that produce light when struck with ionizing radiation (in this case 511 keV photons). The light emitted by the scintillator is then “read” by a photomultiplier. There are several different options for crystal materials and there pros / cons are amendable to multiple choice trivia. THIS vs THAT - SPECT vs PET Crystals: • SPECT: Remember that SPECT uses Nal (activated with a trace of thallium) scintillator crystals. These crystals have an effective atomic number of 50.8, and do great with low-medium energy photons. For example, they absorb around 90% of the Tc99m photons. However, they are progressively less effective at higher energy levels (they only absorb around 30% of 1-131 photons). • PET: The crystals used for PET have to be able to handle those massive 511 photons. That means they need to be thick, have high density, and high atomic numbers. Intrinsic Resolution is bound to the crystal thickness. The thinner the crystal the better the resolution. The problem is a thin crystal has reduced sensitivity, compared to a thicker crystal of the same material. Density of the material plays a key role in improving resolution by allowing for a thinner crystal to be produced. • Higher Density Material = Potential for reduced material size = Improved resolution. • Reduced thickness also has the secondary benefit of producing less Compton scatter. PET S cintillator Trivia ■ - . ■ ■ . ■r'-S lfg k.. . ' W . • Material Absorption Characteristics Density Energv Resolution Light Decav Time Bismuth Original Germanate (Old (BGO) School) BEST Effective Z = 75.2 WORST 1.06 (g/cm3) WORST Slowest Decay Time WORST Lowest Light Output Gadolinium Oxyorthosilicate (GSO) Effective Z = 59.4 6.71 (g/cm3) Lutetium Most Oxyorthosilicate Commonly (LSO) Used Effective Z = 66.4 BEST 7.4 (g/cm3) Fastest Decay Time Highest Light Output Unadulterated Bullshit If the question writer wants to be a malicious asshole he/she can refer to “iterative reconstruction algorithms” as “ordered subset expectation maximization” or “OSEM.” 258

Inherent Limitations There are multiple inherent limitation to PET: • Crystal Thickness (number of detectors) - As we discussed on the prior page, the thick crystals you need to use to contain those super high energy 511 photons are going to result in decreased spatial resolution. This is actually the primary limiting factor that determines the spatial resolution of PET. The smaller the crystal used - the more detectors you can have, and the more detectors you have, the better your spatial resolution is. • Positron Range - The positron travels a little bit prior to the annihilation event. The detectors are tracking the position of the event - not the original location of emission. So you are always going to be slightly off. How off you are depends on the energy of the photon. F-l 8 (used for traditional FDG PET) has a maximum range of around 1mm in soft tissue. Rb-82 - used in cardiac PET tends to have a little more energy and can travel slightly longer (still only a few millimeters). ■ This idea of positron range is sometimes referred to as “the ultimate limit o f spatial resolution ’’for PET. I guess because no matter how awesome you design the camera, this part of the process cannot be made zero. • Angulation / “Non-Collinearity” - Remember when I said the two 511 keV photons are 180 degrees apart ? This is only sorta true. Because there is a little tiny explosion when the positron and electron collide sometimes there is some extra kinetic energy left over which wobbles the takeoff. Usually these things go around 1-3 +/- degrees from 180. So 180 is a generalization. But if you are setting the detectors up to think 180 , and it was actually 180.5 then you will be off a little bit in your localization. • Scatter - As previously discussed, the detectors try really hard to not get fooled - by having coincidence detection - but sometimes even the best of us make a mistake (or occasional typo). 259

2D vs 3D 2D Systems - These employ lead (or tungsten) septa to block unwanted scatter radiation. The downside to this is that you are also decreasing your sensitivity for un-scattered photons (you excluded the “good guys” too). 3D Systems - These systems do NOT use septa. This results in a large increase in sensitivity, and allows you to decrease the amount of tracer you are giving. Why can you decrease the dose of tracer? Because 2D systems are blocking a ton of activity with those septa (both good and bad). To pull off the 3D imaging you need faster coincidence detector. The classic spots you see 3D done is with CNS imaging (small object - lots of activity) and PEDs (also small object). Potential Disadvantages to 3D • Dead Time - It is possible to overwhelm (and temporarily paralyze) the detectors if you have counts rates that are very high. Another reason a dose decrease is sometimes a good idea. • More Random Events - 1 mentioned before that random events increase as your counts increase. The increased sensitivity of this mode can lead to more of these detections and a resulting low level background noise. • Scatter - Without those septa you are getting a lot of scatter. So, your coincidence detection has to bring its “A” game. Tim e of Flight Here is the idea, let us say you have a cancer in the anterior most part of your liver. The FDG uptake and resulting positron emission / annihilation event is going to take place in a location that is not in the center of the scanning bore. So one of the photons is going to reach the detector earlier than the other (which will have to travel a farther distance). TOF uses very quick detection which helps estimate the actual point of annihilation. The best situation to use TOF is large objects with low contrast (less background noise - to confuse the math). TOF results in improved spatial resolution and image contrast — especially in “heavier” patients. Trivia - SUV measurements with TOF images are higher than those on standard PET. “PET Dead Time” When a photon strikes a detector, the scintillation crystal produces a light flash which is collected by one or more photomultiplier tubes. The photomultiplier tubes determine the spatial position and energy of the event. After that, the coincidence processing occurs. During the time required to complete all of these tasks - the system will not respond to new events. This is “dead time.” Reducing Dead Time: You can use crystals with faster scintillation times - (LSO) or you can add more PMT’s. 260

Attenuation Correction Let us begin our discussion on Attenuation Correction by refreshing memories. In the prior pages, we were discussing the various methods the scanner uses to confirm a true event and localize that event along a “line of response.” In that discussion we mentioned that it is possible for scatter to trick the detector, producing an event that is assigned to the incorrect LOR. The additional issue is that when you assign the event to the wrong LOR (wrong spot in the body) you are also removing that event from the correct spot in the body. This removal of events can occur from both Compton interactions and Photoelectric interactions - and be defined with the vocabulary word “Attenuation.” *In PET it is primarily Compton scatter. Attenuation Correction works by assuming that 511 keV photons will be attenuated exponentially by a linear attenuation coefficient (the more tissue it passes through the greater the attenuation). “PET is depth independent. ” You may hear people say “PET is depth independent. ” Which is confusing since it’s intuitive that the more tissue you are passing through the more you are getting attenuated - so shouldn’t the opposite should be true ? The reasoning behind this statement is that both 511 keV photons (remember there are two created from a single event) both have to travel through the tissues at 180 degrees without interaction to meet criteria for an event. Still don’t believe me ? Look at this: This Arrow is “5” This Arrow is “4” This Arrow is “3” Units Long Units Long Units Long Notice that regardless of the depth, the sum of the distance traveled by both arrows is the same. 261

Now having said (written) that, this is really only true if the body is a homogenous attenuator (which it’s not). We are made of different stuff - bones, blood, fat, in some cases silicone, or even metal. In this way it is better to think of PET attenuation as being “Tissue Dependent. ” Before we get into that - let us be clear. I’m not trying to convince you that attenuation does not occur to a greater degree at increased depth - obviously it does. I’m trying to say that having two photons coming off at 180 degrees creates a scenario where things get evened out. This is different compared to the situation for SPECT where you have a single photon at one depth vs another photon at a different depth. This is why PET has a superior capacity to correct for attenuation relative to SPECT. As previously discussed, photons originating from deep structures will undergo greater attenuation relative to more superficial. If no corrections were performed the values measured from tumors deep in the body would appear artificially low (and those near the skin artificially hot). Attenuation correction is based on the data collected from a transmission scan utilizing a positron emitter 68Ge rod source, and then compared to the results of a “blank scan.” The “blank scan” is a QA procedure performed earlier in the day (more on this later). The transmission scan creates a very noisy “attenuation image” on which mathematical corrections based on variable attenuation along each LOR can be made. This methodology is under the influence of the F-l 8 FDG in the body. If you are using combined PET/CT (which is almost always the case) you can use X-Rays as your transmission source. Obviously, using x-ray via a conventional CT scan is going to produce a more better image that has a lot less noise. You should think about the CT part of PET-CT being performed for two main reasons: (1) so that you can see where things are and (2) for attenuation correction. This methodology is NOT under the influence of the F-l 8 FDG in the body. THIS vs THAT: PET vs SPECT-Attenuation Correction With PET the correction for nonuniform attenuation is applied to the data prior to reconstruction. This is considered an advantage o f PET over SPECT With SPECT the correction fo r nonuniform attenuation is integrated into the reconstruction process, increasing its complexity. Point Source - “Transmission Scan ” 262

Gamesmanship: Classic Trick 1: Metal Objects (classic = pacemaker) will generate overcorrected false increased activity at the site. This happens because the density of the metal is outside the conventional range that can be processed. You can see the same thing happen with other very dense objects including: calcified lymph nodes and IV contrast bolus injections (increased activity in the adjacent venous structures). This is why you always double check the uncorrected data when reading a PET. Classic Trick 2: A sneaky trick is to ask you to distinguish between corrected and uncorrected PET. The secret is to look at (1) the skin - which will be hot on uncorrected, and (2) the lungs - which will also be hot on uncorrected. Uncorrected Corrected " Dark Skin - Light Skin ■ Dark Lungs “ Light Lungs 263

Calculation of Standard Uptake Value (SUV): SUVs are not like HU (calibrated to water). There are multiple factors which go into the calculation of SUVs, and essentially make it highly variable from institution-to-institution and even patient-to-patient. The calculation is this: SUV = Tissue Radioactivity Concentration at Time Point 1 x Patient Weight Injected Dose Activity Fat vs Skinny: Fat has a low FDG uptake. So, it’s actually more accurate to use the patients lean body mass instead of weight (this is why the value is different from patient to patient). SUVs in fat people are overestimated (there is more sugar around for the tumor) - this can be corrected for by using lean body mass. Timing: The longer you wait between FDG administration and imaging, the more FDG uptake you get. At first the uptake of FDG in the tumor is rapid (rapid in first 2 hours), then it goes up slower. In other words, if you did “delays” you would get higher FDG values. Another point is that if scan (1) is done after an hour, and scan (2) is done after 2 hours, then scan 2 will falsely appear to have increased SUV. Basically, you need to do the scan at the same time interval after FDG administration if you want to compare apples to apples. Glucose Levels: This is one of those competitive kinetics things. The more non-labeled glucose floating around, the less FDG the tumor can drink. High glucose = Lower SUV. Size Matters: There is a size threshold for PET (usually 1 cm). Anything smaller than 1 cm, will be subjected to partial volume effects, and give a falsely low SUV. Smaller than 1 cm = Lower SUV. Dose Extravasation: FDG is given IV (most things are - except you breath Xenon and you eat Iodine). If you put all your FDG in the soft tissues of the arm, you have less circulating and get a lower SUV. Dose Extravasation = Lower SUV. Reconstruction Type: Iterative reconstruction can mess with the SUVs. The more iterations the higher the SUV. Attenuation Correction: Attenuation correction with CT, also adjusts for SUVs (the positrons have to go through denser stuff too). The method used for the computer to calculate attenuation correction varies between institutions which can make comparing SUVs difficult. 264

II ■ This has to do with differences in the field of view (FOV) from PET and CT. The classic example is a giant monster fat person who is so fat that they have several feet of blubber outside the CT FOV (and therefore not providing data for attenuation correction). The PET FOV is larger than the CT so it picks up the blubber but the eventual SUV calculation will be fucked because of the mismatch between CT and PET. Truncation A rtifact: < ( ) » Fat Ass in CT Scanner Same Fat Ass Now in PET Scanner -Lesion hanging half out of the FOV -Lesion now seen within FOV -Attenuation artifact / lack there of results in the lesion appearing artificially HOT at the margin, and artificially cold outside of the CT FOV Lesions that arc on the margin of the FOV in the CT scanner may appear hotter than they really are from attenuation over-correction. Lesions that are outside of the CT FOV - but are seen on the PET scanner may seem artificially cold because they received little or no attenuation correction. Bottom line = Giant Fat People can have artificially lower (or higher) SUV on peripheral lesions - secondary to FOV differences between CT and PET. 265

Pre FDG PET prep: Diet - Fasting for 4 hours prior to the test is the typical recommendation. If they just ate, they will spike their insulin and drive all the FDG into their muscles. Minimizing cardiac activity (you might want to do if you have a thoracic cancer) can be done with a 12 hour fast, and a low carb/ high protein & fat diet for 24 hours. Hydration - Oral hydration, and frequent voiding decreases the dose to the bladder and improves urinary visualization. M uscle Uptake - There are a couple of causes for diffuse muscle uptake; exercise (most places discourage this for 24-48 hours prior to the exam), eating or insulin use.
