More focal uptake (classic forearms from stress gripping) can also be seen. Insulin - If you have to have insulin, long acting insulins are better to avoid diffuse muscle uptake. Metformin is ok - it won’t mess with the muscles. Brown Fat - Excessive brown fat uptake can be distracting. You can decrease it by (1) making the room warm, (2) giving drugs - with the big two being propranolol and diazepam (Valium). 266

PET QA Just like there is QA on all the other pieces of equipment in nuclear medicine, PET also has a whole bunch of QA. I’ve chosen two QA tests which I think are the most high yield. I believe they are high yield because they are sorta similar things and that means they’d make good distractors for each other on multiple choice. Blank Scan - DAILY - This can be done two ways. 1. By using a positron source (68Ge or l37Cs) in the scanner all by itself. No phantom. No patient. Just a “blank scan,” irradiating the detector elements uniformly. 2. By using a uniform source - most commonly referred to as a “cylinder.” This cylinder is a 20 cm diameter phantom filled with the 511 keV positron emitting 68Ge/Ga. The “cylinder” is placed at the center of the FOV - and should also irradiate the detector elements uniformly. Don’t be tricked if the question writer refers to a scan that is performed with “nothing in the field of view.” This is the classic description of a blank scan, and it is correct for the “1” way described above. Obviously, for “2” you are placing something in the FOV (the “cylinder”) but this method is still referred to as a blank scan. Summary - both methods are “blank scans.” There are two reasons for performing a blank scan. 1. The uniform data allows you to have a “zero” set to help perform the attenuation correction calculation — you may recall I mentioned this in passing on page 246. This is done daily. 2. As the analog of the “daily flood” scan used for planar gamma cameras to evaluate the overall detector response. Just the flood or uniformity test you want to see an image that looks very uniform. No crazy dark lines. If you see a dark diagonal line or band that means a crystal / detector is fucked. You could also use a cylinder full of F-18, but because of the short half life (109 minutes) compared with 68Ge (270 days) I assume refilling that fucking cylinder everyday gets to be a significant pain in the ass. This “uniformity” portion of the scan is also done daily (some sources say weekly). 267

PET QA Continued Normalization Scan - MONTHLY (some sources say at installation, “after major service, when the studies look shitty, or at least annually.”) The scanner is made of a shit ton of detector elements (like 20,000). If you have even the slightest variation is the performance properties (i.e. one crystal is cut slightly thicker than another) you can get small variations in the line of response (LOR) counting. This nonuniform variability can be “normalized” by performing a normalization scan. This is performed similar to a blank scan - either by taking a point source (usually 68Ge) and rotating it around the periphery of the field of view, or using a cylindric source. The idea is to expose the detectors to a uniform flux of positrons. Trivia / Clarification - Regarding 68Ge / 68Ga You will sometimes (often) see 68Ge described as a positron emitter. This is not totally accurate and could be a source of trickery on a multiple choice exam — so allow me to clarify. 68Qe / 68Ga is a generator system - just like Tc and Moly. Germanium 68 will decay into Gallium 68 (not to be confused with Gallium 67). Gallium 68 is the 511 keV positron emitter. So why not just used Gallium 68 ? Same deal with Tc / Mo. Germanium 68 has a half life of around 271 days. That makes it ideal for transportation and in house production. Gallium 68 only has a half life of 68 minutes - which would be a massive pain in the ass for daily QA stuff. 268

Blank for Notes (or if you attend an abusive program - one that refuses to give you time off to study etc.., you could use this page to draw an appropriate tormenter - being kicked into a coma by a swarm of rabid skeeos). skeeos = pigeons “This is a very complicated case Maude. You know, a lotta ins, a lotta outs, lotta what-have-yous.” 269

Disclaim er - Strategy Overview: MRI is a bottomless pit of trivia. It’s very easy to fall into the trap of “trying to know everything” when studying this topic. That is potentially a colossal fuck-up. People literally do PhD work on the stuff and still don’t totally understand it. It boggles the mind how smart the people were who invented this stuff - as it’s very difficult even for “smart people” to get the basic concepts. I could dive into the reasons why “smart” people struggle with learning the topic - cliff notes is I don’t believe most doctors are “smart” - 1 just think they are good at memorizing and repeating stuff. It’s ok - that skill set will work here too. That’s what I’m trying to convey. You do NOT need to master the subject matter. The people writing the questions don’t even understand it that well. You just need basic concepts and that is what I’m going to give you. If you find yourself making a mean face and saying “he didn’t even mention -blank or blah!!!” - that’s probably because I deemed that shit not important. Yes - it’s possible I could leave a few things out, but I promise you that the knowledge contained in the next 50 pages or so is more than enough to get 90+ % of the questions right, and it’s way better than hunting through volumes of 1000 page physics texts trying to “understand” how all the intricacies work. How does MRI work? A certain percentage of nuclei in the body are considered “active.” WTF does “active” mean? Basically it means that there is NOT an equal number of protons / neutrons. I think of these atoms as being off balance. Off balance atoms will “spin” in the magnet and can be “knocked off balance.” The classic example is Hydrogen 1 - of which there is a shit ton in your body (fat, water, etc..), but you could in theory do MRI targeted at Carbon 13, Oxygen 17, Fluorine 19, and so on. Having said that - from here on out we are going to be talking about Hydrogen 1 nuclei - which is basically 1 proton. So when I say “the proton” - I’m talking about the solitary proton in a hydrogen nucleus. The MRI magnet is always “on” - constantly generating a magnet field. Prior to entering the magnet, the “protons” are normally randomly aligned, about half up and half down. When you stick someone in the magnet slightly more than half of them will align with the direction of the magnetic field. You can then slap them down with an RF pulse, and then watch how fast they bounce back up. The micro-environment that these protons live - be it water, fat, the liver etc... will be slightly different and the complex interactions within these environments will cause the protons to bounce back from the “slap down” at different speeds. The difference between how fast different protons bounce up is the “contrast” used to tell things apart - and make a pretty picture (after an incomprehensible amount of complex math). Think about it like this - you have a fat guy and a skinny guy on a track. One way to tell them apart is the time it takes them to run a lap. This is the general idea for MRI. 270

W hat is this “Coordinate System ” ? MRI employs a “coordinate system” - to describe which direction the proton(s) are pointing. A point worth making is that when you see an arrow pointing up or down or sideways - this does not represent a single proton, but instead the addition of all the protons as a “vector”. Protons aligned in the direction of the external magnet field are called “longitudinal magnetization.” This is the “default setting” when someone is in the magnet. If they are aligned perpendicular to the Z axis, then they are considered “transverse magnetization.” W hat is this “RF Pulse” ? In my overview I said that you could “slap down” the protons and then time how fast they bounce back up or “recover.” (Using the word “recover” makes you sound smart). The “slapping” is done by a RF pulse. The pulse is a radio wave. What? A “Radio-wave” ? Yup — Remember this thing? “Z” Longitudinal / y - * “X-Y Transverse r Radio-waves Microwaves Infrared Visible Light UV X-Rays / Gamma Rays Non-Ionizing Ionizing “Radio-waves” are just low energy particle waves - with much longer wavelengths (less frequency) than an X-ray. An important (testable) point is that they are NOT Ionizing. “Knocking the protons down, ” is done with a push. Pushing protons that are spinning in a magnet is a lot like trying to push your kid on a swing. You have to wait until he’s swinging back at you and you push him/her as he/she is moving forward. In other words, you have to time it just right. Another way to think about this is if you wanted to hand someone something but they were running. You would need to run at the same speed as them to be able to hand it to them. It’s an energy transfer. 271

So how do you know how “fast to run” , or “w hen to push” ? There is this thing called the Larmor Equation. C0 o y B 0 Written in English: Precession Frequency = Gyromagnetic Ratio x Field Strength in Tesla • CDo is the precession frequency • y is a proportionality constant called the gyromagnetic ratio • Bo is the external magnetic field strength given in Tesla. The Larmor equation describes the precession frequency of a nuclear magnetic moment and resonant frequency of a nucleus, and relates these aspects to the magnetic field strength. It basically says the precession frequency gets higher as the field strength increases. The kid swings faster. Precession Frequency (COo) is Directly Proportional to Magnet Field Strength (Bo) 272

W hat exactly does the RF pulse do? The RF pulse actually does two things: (1) it decreases the longitudinal magnetization, and (2) it causes the protons to sync up and precess in-phase (which establishes a transverse magnetization). <> VL Z, W hat do you m ean “sync up” ? Before the Pulse Let’s say we are look at this vector diagram from the air - maybe looking down on it from Donald Trump’s Helicopter. What you are seeing is the “transverse plane.” The normal (not-synced) situation is going to be a random rotating / spinning of the protons. Immediately After Pulse Immediately after the fury of the RF pulse - if we looked down on this thing we would see the protons all lined up together. They would be synced up or “in-phase. ” When can you m easure signal? Signal can only be measured when it is NOT in the longitudinal direction. If you are running next to someone at the same speed, you can’t really measure how fast they are going (it’s like an Einstein relativity thing). But, if they turn and run away from you, then you can measure that. It’s sorta like that. 273

W hat is this “ T1 w ? After you knock the protons down with an RF pulse, they will grow back up to normal size (their spin magnitude will re-orient in the direction of Bo). The time it takes for this to happen is different in different tissues. The process is referred to as “longitudinal relaxation”. Some people call this “spin-lattice relaxation ” because energy from the RF pulse is handed over to the surrounding lattice. The “1” of TI resembles a thermometer - which is useful in remembering that TI relaxation involves the exchange of thermal energy. Plotting the time vs longitudinal magnetization creates the TI curve. T1 - Recovery of Longitudinal Magnetization The return to longitudinal relaxation follows an exponential curve approaching 100% over time. TI is defined as the time at which longitudinal magnetization is 63% of its final value. Each tissue has a different TI and the greater the field strength the longer the TI (because net magnetization is greater in a larger field). Things with short TI signals are bright. Hence the phrase “intrinsic TI shortening” is something that is bright on TI. Things that have long T ls are dark. Is TI different in a stronger magnet? A stronger magnet makes TI longer. Protons in this stronger magnetic field have more energy (they precess faster), and therefore it takes longer to hand that over to the lattice. 274

W hat is this “ T 2 ” ? Remember I said the RJF pulse caused the protons to synch up and precess in phase (which establishes a transverse magnetization). As time progresses the protons will slowly fall out of sync and start doing their own thing again. This is the T2 Transverse relaxation. Time Elapses Plotting the time vs transverse magnetization creates the T2 curve - which resembles the downward portion of a ski slope. I like this analogy because it helps me remember that T2 is shorter than T1 (takes less time to go down a hill than up it). Transverse magnetization decay is also described by an exponential curve with T2 representing the time at which the signal has decayed to 37% of its original value of transverse magnetization (63% of it has decayed). Another term often used to describe T2 is “spin-spin relaxation. ” W hat causes protons to lose th eir transverse sync (T2 relaxation) ? There are two main reasons (1) inhomogeneities in the external field, and (2) inhomogeneities in the local magnetic filed - within the actual tissues & tissue spin interactions. Pure things (water) take longer to decay their transverse magnetization, and are therefore bright (the opposite is true of impure liquids). 275

T2 vs T2* ? The signal of T2 decays faster than various PhDs would predict based on tissue spin interactions alone. The reason for this is that math falsely assumes the main external field is absolutely homogeneous — it is not. This heterogeneous field creates additional interactions which further speed decay. It is because of this that T2* decay is ALWAYS faster than T2. T2* = Tissue Spin Interaction + Field Inhomogeneity; “random + fixed causes ” • T2 = Tissue Spin Interaction ; “random causes only” 12 Is there a way to “Fix” T2* and make it T2? Yes - but let me define a few things first. TR — Or “Time to Repetition” is the time between the initiation of two successive RF pulses RF Pulse RF Pulse RF Pulse RF Pulse RF Pulse RF Pulse TR 276

“Free Induction Decay” -FID - After you give an RF pulse the protons sync up and you start getting a signal. As time lapses this signal becomes less and less - decay via T2* (“random + fixed causes”) Technically an FID will be created by an RF pulse with any flip angle (90 degree as in spin echo or less than 90 degree as in gradient echo). 1 guess an exception would be a 180 degree pulse as it only inverts the longitudinal magnetization and doesn’t directly generate a transverse component - so pure 180 RF = no FID, but otherwise all Flip Angles cause them. ‘Free Induction Decay’ I thought you w ere going to tell m e how to “fix ” this T 2* thing? Alright this is how it’s done. Remember how we talked about T2 signal “decaying” to a “dephased” state over time? Well if you wait until it’s halfway through the dephasing process and hit it with a 180 degree pulse (the regular RF pulse we’ve been talking about before were 90 degree pulses), you can spin it all the way around and essentially restart the process. You will accomplish two things (1) you will clear out those inhomogeneities in the field making T2* turn into T2, and (2) you will create an “echo.” Immediately after 90 Degree RF Pulse - everything is lined up in sync Time is passing - starting to decay a little bit 180 Degree RF “Refocusing” Pulse Immediately after 180 Degree RF Pulse - everything is flipped to the other side Things start to return to phase 277

W hat is this “Echo” you speak of? If you are in a tunnel and you shout - your voice will trail off and then come back. That’s an echo. Something trailing off and coming back. When you perform your 180 pulse, the signal will build back up and come back. out of phase After 180 peaks in uniformity Pulse - the tip of the echo When do you deliver the 180 pulse? The 180 is given at the 1/2 the “T.E.” - or Time to Echo. TE 278

T1 and T2 - Clarified When people say “T l” or “T2” sequences they are not really telling the whole story. There is no such thing as a pure Tl or pure T2 - they are only “heavily weighted” as one or the other. Let me show you what I mean. Let’s look at the Tl and T2 curves. T l W elgkfred ^ ............. $- / #** 4/ / ^ TV > 0u # ♦ # ♦ f # f # a # ✓ V c ' f ♦ f # ✓ ^ 1 1 r-f I I : y ; ✓ N lr- V- .* / Tlrvie Short TR t t Notice that by choosing a short TR and short TE you maximize the Longitudinal Contrast (T1), and Minimize the Transverse Contrast (T2) When I say “contrast” I mean the difference between the different tissue curves (the dotted lines) Long TR Long TE W hat is “Long and Short” ? Depends on if you are talking about Spin Echo or GRE (I’ll explain the difference very soon). Short TR Long TR Short TE Long TE Spin Echo 250-700 ms >2000 ms 10-25 ms >60 ms GRadient Echo < 50 ms >100 ms 1-5 ms >10 ms I Ttrvie Short TE Notice that by choosing a long TR and long TE you minimize the Longitudinal Contrast (T1), and maximize the Transverse Contrast (T2) The Longer the TE, the Greater the T2 Effects. MRCP has a very long TE 279

W hat is this “Proton Density” ? So you have T1 and T2 weighted sequences, and then you have “Proton Density.” So what is this “proton density.” The best way to think about it - that PD is what you have left when you subtract the bias of T1 or T2 weighting. With all things equal the only thing you are measuring is how many protons are present in one thing vs another thing. So how is that accomplished? On the prior page, I showed you that a “short-short” - TR, and TE will result in T1 weighting - by maximizing the longitudinal relaxation differences between tissues and minimizing the transverse relaxation differences. I also showed you that a “long- long” - TR, and TE will result in T2 weighting - by maximizing the transverse relaxation differences between tissues and minimizing the longitudinal differences. Q: So what if you choose a long TR - to minimize longitudinal difference, and chose a short TE to minimize transverse differences ? A: Scan with PD weighting. ?t> MetgUfced. TLrvte a a TLrvte Long TR Short TE Sum m ary: T1 T2 Bullshit Distractor Proton Density Short TR Long TR Long TR Short TR Short TE Long TE Short TE Long TE 280

W hat is this k-Space and Im age M atrix ? First let me introduce this thing called a “Fourier Transform.” A fourier transform is a mathematical technique for converting data from the time domain to data in the frequency domain. K-space is a Fourier plane (like an x-y axis coordinate system) in which MR signal is stored. Turning K-space into an image requires an inverse two dimensional Fourier Transform (lots of math... which computers are good at). . ,> . ■. - Vy\ ■» ■ , * r, .. ■ ,• tik-, *- -■ *V*- •• >' y * f J * ? * " V ) ** 1 ; ' - • ' .V V- . -* ' • . '■ 1 2DFT \ II ■ T" \ 'wm \ . 1 / 4 ‘ \ i: 4 ■4 < * s- rp • • Trivia: • The center of K-space is made up of the information about gross form and tissue contrast. • The periphery of K-Space is made up of information about spatial resolution. Resolution Information t 1 Resolution Information * " “ 281

S patial Encoding - How Do You Localize the Signal From W ithin the Body ? - Localization of signal requires three steps: (1) Select the desired slice (2) Encode spatial information along the rows (3) Encode spatial information along the columns The localizing gradients have identical properties they are just applied at different times and in different directions. Because you have three gradients in three planes, you can localize anything in the body. (1) Selecting the Desired Slice: The use of a slice selection gradient (SSG) is used to select the area of interest. This gradient is placed perpendicular to the desired slice plane. The slice selection gradient determines the view (axial, coronal, sagittal, even oblique). This vs That Localization Gradients can be turned “on” or “off.” Superconducting “main” magnet is always on. Selective Pulse: On top of this gradient, an RF pulse is applied - at the same frequency as the protons in the slice plane you want to sample. That way only the protons in this plane will be affected. This pulse is called a selective pulse. A RF Pulse at the Desired Slice “Selective Pulse’ Slice Selection Gradient Dealing with the 180 degree refocusing pulse: In a spin echo sequence you apply a 180 degree RF pulse after your 90 degree pulse. Apparently this jacks up your nice field (especially along the edges). To deal with this, before and after the 180 degree RF pulse you place two identical gradients (which cancel each other out) and correct for errors around the edges. 282

(2) Encoding Spatial Inform ation ~ in Vertical Direction (Phase Encoding): This is the second step in the process. This gradient is applied causing protons in the same row perpendicular to the gradient to have the same phase. All protons at this point will have the same frequency. Phase encoding is much longer than frequency encoding - this is why it’s done on the thinner portion. They don’t want to lose time including the tip of the nose on phase encoding - this is why it’s side to side in the head. The opposite is true in the abdomen, where it’s front to back. Breast is an exception to this rule (I’ll touch on that later). Contribution to duration o f study: The number ofphase encoding steps contributes to the duration o f a 2D imaging sequence. Duration = TRx Npy x Nex TR = Repetition Time NPy = Number o f phase encoding steps Nex = Number o f excitations Phase Encoding Gradient (3) Encoding Spatial Inform ation ~ in the Horizontal Direction (Frequency Encoding): This gradient is applied perpendicular to the phase-enc direction, which results in the modification of Larmor frequency over the duration of its application. The end result is column of protons which have identical frequencies. Testable Trivia: This encoding gradient is applied at the same time as the readout. Frequency Encoding Gradient 283

Pulling it Together Lets walk through the steps of a basic “spin echo” type of sequence. Spin Echo Sequence ~TR 90 180 RF SSG — PEG ------ FEG Echo TE +— // 90 “ / / ---------------- - / / ----------------- (1) Slice Selection: This SSG is applied at the same time as the 90 degree RF pulse (called a selective pulse). (2) Phase encoding and frequency encoding gradients are applied. (3) Next a 180 refocusing RF pulse is given. Before and after this 180 pulse, two identical SS gradient lobes are applied to help correct undesired spin on the edge of the slice. (4) Lastly a second FEG is applied at the same time as the read out echo. This process is repeated for as many phase encoding steps you do. 284

Slice Thickness For each slice to have a thickness, a “slice” or “band” of nuclei must be excited via the excitation pulse. As I mentioned previously, the slope of this slice selecting gradient will decide the difference in precessional frequency between two adjacent spots on the gradient. Steep “Large” SS Gradient Slope = Large difference in Precessional Frequency Shallow “Small” SS Gradient Slope = Small Difference in Precessional Frequency • • • > • • • > m • m > m m m y mm m y y Less Gradient = Thicker Slices More Gradient = Thinner Slices T3 C CO CO ~o TJc CO CD “Shallow” / Small SS Gradient wc CO E C/3C CO “Steep” / Large SS Gradient After the gradient is applied, an RF Transmit Pulse is deployed at a range of frequencies (or bandwidth), covering the desired area. This is referred to as a Transmit bandwidth. Thicker bandwidth will result in a thicker slice. Thinner bandwidth will result in a thinner slice. "D c CO qCQ — —' E lc CO I— Summary: Thinner Sheer with Steep (Large) Slice Selection Gradient, and Thin Transmit Bandwidth Thicker Slicer with Shallow (Small) Slice Selection Gradient, and Thick Transmit Bandwidth Same “Shallow” / Small SS Gradient 285

M odifications -VERY VERY VERY HIGH YIELD TOPIC- This next topic is probably the single most high yield topic for the purpose of multiple choice (artifacts is probably a close second). “Modifications'” - is going to be a discussion of the choices you make and how those choices have consequences. The focus will be on 3 main topics: (1) Table Time (2) Spatial Resolution (3) Signal to Noise 1: Table T im e $$$$ Stunna' Island Money and the Power There are a variety of things that can subtly mess with table time. However, for the purpose of multiple choice - all things being equal, this is the formula I want you to use. TIME = TR x Phase Matrix x NEX • TR = TR is the time between each RF pulse (usually measured in milliseconds) • Phase M atrix = The is the data the system collects from each phase encoding step. In other words, how many phase encoding steps. • NEX = The number of times each set of phase encoding steps is repeated. Often referred to as “The Number of Excitations.” Or perhaps more easily thought of as “how may averages (excitations) per TR ?” This is the only equation you need for both spin echo and gradient echo sequences. Unless they tell you otherwise - assume this is what they want. Please resist the urge to over complicate this. Putting Laundry Away TR = How long To fill each drawer Phase Matrix = How many drawers in your dresser NEX = How many times you have to open the same drawer, to pack in more socks. *1 read a similar analogy in the book “MR1 in Practice ” 286

What about slice thickness ? Thinner slices is more time right? Ugh.... this is exactly the kind of thinking you need to avoid. Stop trying to understand it and just memorize the fucking formula! OK... Fine! It’s probably true that using a narrower RF pulse bandwidth will take longer to excite, which may result in a slightly longer scan. BUT for the purpose of multiple choice (assuming it’s a 2D standard sequence) I only want you to consider the big 3; TR, Phase Matrix, and NEX. But wouldn’t thinner slices mean more Phase Encoding Steps? Don’t think about it like that. Think about it as “phase matrix.” If you are going to eat the entire block of cheese, making 30 thin slices vs 10 thick slices doesn’t change the calories - it’s still the same block of cheese. Same Block of Cheese = Same Calories Are there any situations when the “Big 3 ” Formula doesn't hold up? Yes (2). The first one is Fast Spin Echo. For reasons that will be explained later , Fast Spine Echo acquisition time is approximately proportional to 1/Echo Train Length The second one is 3D Imaging. Prior to this everything I’ve been talking about has been 2D, and unless they specifically tell you that it’s 3D you should assume that it’s 2D. In 3D Volume imaging you are acquiring information in blocks instead of slices. This has the advantage of improving spatial resolution (you can really shrink your slice thickness) plus there is no slice gap. 3D imaging also offers improved SNR plus the ability to manipulate the angle of obliquity (great for rectal and cervix cancer staging). So why not do 3D all the time? The scan times are long - which means money gets lost... which means the hospital administrator (non-MD) can’t afford a second Rolls Royce. Because what’s better than 1 Rolls? You guessed it... 2 Rolls. Take Home Point: 2D: TIME =TR x Phase Matrix x NEX 3D: TIME = TR x Phase Matrix x NEX x # Slices 287

But what if.. As I’ve mentioned previously there are a whole bunch of other random things you can fuck with that are extremely low yield and will only confuse you. * *Do NOTfucking memorize these you maniacs! Those include (but are not limited to): Combing TGE and EPI = TR x Ny x NSA / (ETL t g e x ETL e p i) Phase oversampling Parallel imaging Partial Fourier But what if... Some other sources of confusion / fuckery include the definition of “scan time.” This is the Bill Clinton - “it depends on what the meaning o f the word is is ’ ” - level of absurdity. Just assume “scan time” = data acquisition time. No need to try and guess how many seconds it takes the scanner to prepare to acquire data or the average tech to press the scan button. Although there is no limit to the fuckery that can take place on an exam like this, I’m willing to bet that this will not be part of the question header: “Your new MRI technologist (who has Clinodactyly) is performing a TSE on a 1987 Seimans model A ” Hmmm.... what if the tech had coffee this morning ? 288

But Prometheus - I ’ve read that magnet strength changes scan time ! Why didn’t you include that in your formula ?!! Lets talk about TR... just as a quick refresher W hat is this “TR” ? TR is the time between excitation pulses. - Duh. BUT! And this is often a point of confusion - it is NOT the time between each excitation pulse. Hold on... I’ll put it together. Instead think about TR as the time between exciting slice 1 and then exciting that same slice 1 again. Not slice 1, and 2, 3, 4 (as below). This is TR SLICE 1 ^ SLICE 2 This is NOT the Fucking TR SLICE 3 SLICE 4 SLICE 1 Now - hopefully, it’s a little easier to remember that for each “TR” each slice is selected, phase encoded and frequency encoded. Don’t think about slices being selected together but instead in sequence. Slice 1: Slice 2: Etc... Selected Selected Encoded Encoded Frequencies call out — Echo Frequencies call out — Echo Echo is recorded Echo is recorded This is why the maximum number of slices available depends on the TR. Longer TRs = Ability to select more slices Shorter TRs = Can’t select as many slices Example With Made Up Numbers: TR of 500 ms may allow for 10 slices TR of 2000 ms may allow for 40 slices 289

W hat is this “Phase M atrix” ? The is another way of saying “number of phase encoding steps.” You can also think about this as how many lines you need to fill before the scan is done. 1 Line = 1 TR. This is why you multiple #PE steps x TR to calculate scan time. W hat is this “Num ber of Excitations” ? It took me like 3 hours to finish the shading on that upper lip. It s probably the best drawling I ’ve ever done. How much time do you want to spend on shading that upper lip? If you are ok spending 3 hours shading that thing then sure... it will look awesome. But... you may have better things to do. Excitations are just like this. Sampling the same data (and recording an average) multiple times to make a better picture. Sure you shaded the lip... but more shading can only make it better. More steps/averages = better picture More steps/averages = more time OK... one more time putting it all together Scan Time = TR x Number of Phase Encoding Steps (Ny) x Number of Averages (NSA) Example: Phase Matrix of 256, You Must Complete 256 TRs 290

BUT Prometheus! What About the Magnet Strength!?! When you think about magnet strength I want you to think mostly about Signal and Noise (not scan time). Remember - super basic here. You put the patient in the scanner (magnet is always on) and a certain number of nuclei align with the field. Not all of them. Some... Lets do this... You have a 0.5 T blur machine because technical fees buy Ferraris, and Ferraris are awesome. At 0.5 T 51% of the nuclei are aligned with the field. So 49% are against it. So the Net is 1%. You got 1% to work with here. More than enough to find that disc budge (and obscure the pancreatic mass in all that blur ). Your good friend at the Academic Center has a 3T (and drives a Honda Civic). At 3 T 90% of the nuclei are aligned with the field. So 10% are against it. So the Net is 80%. He has got 80% to work with. More than enough to find that disc bulge (and miss the pancreatic mass because he only reads neuro, all his other skills have atrophied, doesn’t recognize the finding and gets sued for 10 million dollars). The point here is that if you have a stronger magnet you will have more available net magnetization to pull from and the SNR goes UP. If you have a weaker magnet you have less magnetization to pull from and SNR goes DOWN. To compensate for the terrible SNR you can (probably should) do some things and those things will make the scan time longer. What kinds o f things? Increasing your TR. Longer TR = Better SNR. Shorter TR = Shitty SNR *Other one is the other one* Longer TE = Shitty SNR Shorter TE = Better SNR If you choose to lengthen the TR to improve SNR - you will increase the table time. So in this way a weaker magnet could “increase” table time. BUT unless they tell you the TR has been changed then don’t assume that it has. Keep it basic. Chances are the person writing the question doesn’t actually understand physics. *These percentages are made up — pulled directly from the darkest deepest crack in my ass. I ’m ju st trying to make a point about Signal and Noise. 291

BUT Prometheus! 1 Read that Stronger Magnets Increase the TR! That’s Opposite o f What You Just Said!?! So... let us be clear about some things. There is NOT a knob on the scanner that turns down or up the field strength. It is like ultrasound. If you want a different frequency you need to pick up a different probe. You want 1.5 T instead of 3 T then you need put the patient on a different scanner. BUT you know what there is a knob for? Changing the fucking TE and TR. That’s how you decide how heavily T1 or T2 weight something is or PD or whatever.. Remember the TR controls the amount of recover in the longitudinal direction that is allowed to recover before the next pulse is applied. Long TR: You are giving time for full recovery. Therefore, you are allowing more to be available to be flipped on the next go around. • Short TR: You are NOT allowing for a full recovery. So when the next RF pulse comes you have less available signal. This is why a Long TR improves SNR, and a short TR makes it worse. BUT.... What About the Magnet...? T1 recover depends on the ability to hand energy back. Free water is terrible at this. So it has long T1 (dark on Tl). If you have proteins in the water they slow down the movement (like thickening the milk shake) and make the energy transfer more efficient (proteinacous cyst = Tl bright). Tissues with fast Tl will have more signal recovered in a given TR relative to tissues with a slow Tl. With a larger magnet strength you will have more energy to give back, so it takes longer to recover. In this way stronger magnets can be thought of as decreasing the available signal available at a given TR. So to compensate for this you could increase the TR. We are talking about making changes for image contrast here (not SNR - like the prior page). Bottom Line = Field Strength doesn’t do dick to TR. You set the TR. If you choose to increase the TR to improve your Tl signal tissue contrast then you increased the TR. Don’t be blaming shit on the magnet when you are the one that turned the TR knob up. Should you assume that the test writer has inferred that stronger magnets always have increased TR? I wouldn’t but if you want to be sure then simply read the mind of the person who wrote the question to see if they had that bias. Images Contrast = The degree o f T l / T2 weighting. 292

2: Spatial Resolution Factors affecting spatial resolution: Spatial resolution is governed by the size of a voxel. The voxel size is determined by the matrix, field of view, and slice thickness. € FOV Phase Voxel = Slice Thickness x I X I Matrix Size Phase Field o f View: The smaller the field of view, the better the spatial resolution. So, why not make the FOV super super small? Because you will get aliasing or wrap around artifacts from signal outside the FOV. FOV Read Matrix Size Read Matrix Size: The matrix size corresponds to the image width and height (in pixels). The larger the matrix the smaller the pixels. Pixel = FOV / M atrix Unfortunately, no one can be told what the matrix is, you have to see it for yourself. Gradient: A gradient with higher amplitude (more intense) or one applied for a longer period of time results in better spatial resolution. Makes sense since the point of a gradient is to localize stulf. Slice Thickness: For a thicker slice you can either increase the Transmit RF pulse bandwidth (the slice selection pulse) or you can decrease the slice selection gradient. For a thinner slice you would do the opposite. The thinner the slice, the better the spatial resolution. Summary - Better Spatial Resolution If: -Sm all Voxel -Sm all Field of V iew -Larger M atrix -Thinner Slices Steep (Large) Slice Selection Gradient, and Thin Transmit Bandwidth 293

3: Signal to Noise Noise is random variation in the signal that leads to its degradation. In MRI the primary source of this noise is the patient’s body. Overall the SNR depends on voxel size (bigger is better), field strength, and some random sequence parameters. Voxel Size: Anything that makes your voxel bigger improves your SNR (the opposite of spatial resolution). • Thicker slices = Higher SNR • Increased Transmit RF Pulse Bandwidth • Decreased Slice Selection Gradient • Larger FOV = More SNR • Smaller Matrix = More SNR Field Strength: A stronger field gets you more signal. Greater Field Strength = Greater SNR. RF Coils: Smaller surface coils improve your signal (increased SNR) compared to a coil within the scanner. Number o f Excitations per Slice (number o f averages): The more excitations you perform the more signal you get (increased SNR). The main trade off is a prolonged imaging time. Receiver Bandwidth: A fat bandwidth gives you a rapid sampling of data, a narrow bandwidth gives you slow sampling of data. Since noise is constant, the fatter band will pick up more noise. So, fat bandwidth = decrease SNR, narrow bandwidth = increased SNR. Maximizing TR (long) and Minimizing TE (short) - Peaks your signal. Sum m ary - B etter SNR If: Stronger M agnet Long TR Big FOV Large Slices Shallow (Small) Slice Selection Gradient, and Thick Transmit Bandwidth More NEX Short TE Sm all M atrix Sm all Receiver Bandwidth Appropriate Coil Size 294

Potential Fuckery - Receiver Bandwidth vs Transm it Bandwidth Don’t confuse these terms - they sound similar and would make good distractors. Fat Receiver Bandwidth = Decrease SNR, Fat Transmit Bandwidth = Large Slice = Increased SNR Narrow Receiver Bandwidth = Increased SNR. Narrow Transmit Bandwidth = Thin Slice = Decreased SNR Proton Density - Signal to Noise Specialist If you ask a MSK guy the utility of Proton Density he/she may tell you “I like it for the meniscus.” If you ask a Neuro guy the utility of Proton Density he/she may tell you “I like it for MS Plaques in the Posterior Fossa.” If you ask a physicist the utility of Proton Density he/she may tell you “it has a good signal to noise ratio.” All of them are correct - for the same reason. Proton density has excellent signal to noise. Why? • Remember the ways you increase SNR ? • Long TR, Big FOV, Large Slices, more NEX, • Short TE and Small Matrix.r-.r-: ^ • Remember how you make a Proton Density? Long TR and Short TE. See? - Proton Density is basically designed to have excellent SNR T ra d e o ffs : T h in g s to T h in k A b ou t: • Thicker slices will improve SNR • Thinner Slices are needed for Spatial Resolution • Thinner Slices are Noisier • Signal Can directly be improved by increasing the NEX - but increasing NEX from two to four, doubles the scan time, but increases the signal by only the square root of two. • Shortening TE will improve your SNR - and it doesn’t mess with table time. BUT the loss in T2 effect can wreck your image contrast. In general you should only drop the TE to improve SNR with Tl sequences. • Larger receiver bandwidths may decrease SNR but they also decreases mismatch artifacts like chemical shift or magnetic susceptibility. Thinner receiver bandwidths may increase SNR but they also increase mismatch artifacts like chemical shift or magnetic susceptibility. 295

Modification Signal to Noise Spatial Resolution Duration of Exam Thicker Slices Increased Decreased No Effect Larger Field of View Increased Decreased No Effect Larger Matrix Decreased Increased Increased Greater Field Strength Increased No Effect No Effect Greater Receiver Bandwidth Decreased No Effect Decreased Greater Transmit Bandwidth Increased Decreased No Effect More Excitations per Slice “More Averages ” Increased No Effect Increased Utilizing Partial K Space Sampling Decreased No Effect Decreased 296

MRI Sequences There are a bunch of sequences, but two general categories: Spin Echo - which uses a 90 degree flip angle and a 180 degree refocusing RF pulse Gradient Echo - which uses a LESS than 90 degree flip angle, *and NO 180 RF pulse Spin Echo: Spin echo is composed of a 90 RF pulse followed by an 180 rephasing pulse. This rephasing pulse is done at 1/2 the time of echo Spin Echo Sequence JR — ----- 180 rephasing pulse is done at 1/2 the time of echo A (TE). The sequence is repeated (TR) until k ^ A r \j \J ' space is filled. ggQ ■ ________ j f - FEG Why do you give this 180 pulse? In an ideal P E G €=§5 f j ~ world the magnetic field is homogeneous, but we don’t live in an ideal world. The pulse is given to try and improve the heterogeneous / nature of the field and this is the reason spin |— echoes give us T2 and not T2*. As discussed in the spatial encoding section, the slice selection gradient is applied with the 90 degree RF pulse. Then you have the Phase encoding and the frequency encoding gradients. The 180 degree pulse is flanked by self canceling slice selection gradients. Lastly the frequency encoding gradient fires again with the read out echo. High Yield: Duration = TR x Phase Matrix x Nex TR = Repetition Time Phase Matrix = The is the data the system collects from each phase encoding step Nex - Number o f excitations Contrast: The contrast on spin echo sequences is determined by the TR (interval between 90 degree RFs), and the TE (interval between the 90 degree RF and the receipt of the echo). As mentioned above, a short TE and TR gives you Tl weighting, long TE and TR gives you T2 weighting, and long TR & short TE produce a proton density sequence. High Yield Fact: Because a line in k space is filled at each TR, the TR contributes to the duration of the sequence. So, much so that long TR times are the reason a true spin echo is of historic significance. 297

Fast Spin Echo Fast Spin Echo The idea behind the FSE sequence is to reduce the TR, which I already mentioned is a major contribution to the duration of the study. This is done by applying multiple 180 RF pulses each resulting in an echo. Echo Train Length - Number of echoes in the same TR. Fat Signal - There is a “normal” phenomenon called “J coupling” which occurs between the nuclei of lipid molecules, causing intrinsic shortening of T2 signal. The fast repetition of 180 degree pulses mess up the J couples and cause the T2 of fat to lengthen. Testable Point: T2 fat signal is longer with fast spin echo (interferes with J coupling). Testable Vocab: With each progressive echo train the transverse signal gradually decreases. This is called “T2 Blurring” Testable Point: Acquisition time is approximately proportional to 1/ETL 298

Inversion Recovery Inversion Recovery Sequence -------------------------- TR --------------- TI 1 RF SSG 180 A 90 180 A IJO m V \J U iT m PEG FEG i Echo ------4-, 180 ■; / i A r -II- Tl 100-180 msec 180 r h ”. 180 \j 180 I I f TI 1700-2200 msec n r IT 180 Jl This time instead of starting with a 90 degree RF pulse, you start with a 180 degree “preparation” pulse. You wait for the relaxation of the thing you want to saturate (water, fat, myocardium) to hit its null point then you slam it with the 90 degree pulse. This way that particular tissue gives no signal. “TI” - The time between the 180 and 90 pulse. “STIR ” - Short TI Inversion Recovery, employs a short TI (120- 160 ms) to suppress fat, which is based on the TI for fat. Often used on MSK imaging to suppress the fat within bone marrow to allow visualization of fluid (edema) within bone. “FLAIR ” - Fluid Attenuated Inversion Recovery, employ a TI designed to suppress water signal (approximately 2000 ms). Frequently used in Neuro-imaging to suppress CSF signal (based on the TI relaxation time of CSF) to allow visualization of edema or inflammation (i.e. in multiple sclerosis). Testable Point: Relative to other fat suppression techniques - STIR is much less “susceptible” to magnetic susceptibility (metal) and field inhomogeneity Testable Point: STIR can NOT be used with Gadolinium. Gd+ enhanced tissues have a similar TI as fat, so they may get totally nulled out. Testable Point: A tradeoff for Inversion Recovery sequences is a longer study as the extra pulse and subsequent “time to inversion (TI)” increases the time to repetition (TR). 299

GRE Sequences Gradient echo sequences are different than spin echo sequences because they (1) use a flip angle less than 90 and (2) do NOT have a 180 pulse. The advantage to this low flip angle is faster recovery, shorter TR/TE times, and a faster scan. The flip angle is used to determine how much transverse and how much longitudinal magnetization is going to be used. Testable Point: Because there is no 180 pulse you are dealing with T2* (not T2). For the same reason GRE is more susceptible to susceptibility artifacts. Testable Point: Gradient sequences have a lower Specific Absorption Rate (less heating). I f there is no 180 pulse, how do you create an echo? A bipolar readout gradient (basically a frequency encoding gradient) is used. Vocab: An echo in gradient is called a “Field Echo” I f gradient is so fast and has a nice low SAR, why don’t we use it fo r everything? The signal to noise ratio isn’t that great. Plus, you get more susceptibility artifacts. “Steady State ” Because the TR is shortened in GRE you can get stuck with permanent residual transverse magnetization - where the magnetization never completely goes away. What is often the case is that the TR is shorter than the Tl and T2 of the tissues, so T2* dephasing dominates. The two main flavors in GRE imaging are classified depending on how this residual transverse magnetization is handled. Spoiled (incoherent) GRE: Gradients and/or RF pulses are used to get rid of the transverse magnetization (T2) that is persisting in the steady state. Refocused (coherent) GRE: The steady state is preserved by using a “Rewind gradient.” Therefore these sequences are T2* weighted. These refocused sequences can be partial or full. An example of the full refocus is the SSFP (steady-state free precession) which is used in cardiac imaging to provide a fast sequence with high signal to noise. Spoiled (Incoherent) = Basically Tl Refocused (Coherent) = Basically T2 TR Alpha RF Alpha SSG PEG FEG Echo % f EG (FID) EG (FID) 300

Echo-Plannar Im aging (EPI) “T h e Noisy O ne” 90 180 RF v l r SSG PEG FEG Echo — . . •> ' — [.....I..... i......n r z T f 1 » a i 3 1 E r I 1 I E6 67 TE EPI can be done with spin echo (90 + 180) or gradient echo (90 + a bunch of gradients). EPI is MRI’s fastest acquisition method, on the order of 100 ms/slice. One RF pulse is used to acquire data for an image (aka single shot). The sequence works by turning the phase encoding and frequency encoding gradients on and off very rapidly - causing a very fast filling of k space. Compared to a regular GRE: The sequence is more vulnerable to magnetic susceptibility, gives you better tissue contrast, and is faster. Artifacts linked to EPI: Magnetic Susceptibility - can be improved with segmented sequences (instead of single shots) Ghosting - Gradient imperfections mess with spatial encoding Chemical Shift - A narrow readout bandwidth is used. As mentioned before, a fat readout bandwidth improves chemical shift (lets a bigger range in), a narrow one does the opposite. Testable Trivia: • Echo-planar is the technique of choice for Diffusion Weighted Imaging • Echo-planar is highly vulnerable to magnetic susceptibility (even more than normal gradient sequences) 301

Diffusion The base sequence is either a fast GRE or an echo planar. Generally speaking, diffusion weighted imaging works by using two very strong and symmetric MR gradients. The acquisition is repeated in each of the 3 dimensions in space with b-factors of 0 and b-factors of 1000. The signal differences are based on mobility and direction of water. Scenario 1 (no net movement): The first gradient fires dephasing the spins. The molecules do not move. The second gradient fires rephasing the same molecules - giving you high signal. Scenario 2 (net movement): The first gradient fires dephasing the spins. The molecules move out of the way. The second gradient fires missing the original protons - gives you low signal. Vocab: The direction of diffusion is described as isotropic (movement in all spatial directions) and anisotropic (movement in a single direction). “B-Factor” - The best way to think about “B-Factors” & Diffusion is to consider it a type of tissue weighting - just like TE and TR - which as we’ve discussed are the factors that determine it you produce a T2, TI, or PD. The “B-Factor” controls how much a tissues apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) contributes to the study. The higher the “B-Factor” the greater the diffusion weighting. B Zero = If you set the contribution of diffusion to zero, and assuming your TR and TE are long - which they usually are - then you basically have a T2. This is why people call the B Zero a “poor mans T2.” In other words - if there is something with high signal it’s because it has a long T2 time. B 500 or 1000 = With an increase in “B-Factor” - usually to 500 and then 1000 - the image weighting changes from T2 - to become more and more diffusion. In other words - if there is something with high signal it’s because it has a low apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC). ADC (Apparent Diffusion Coefficient); - As I’m sure you know an ADC is needed to read diffusion correctly. The ADC map is calculated by obtaining (a) set of images without a diffusion gradient (B0 “poor mans T2”), and (b) set of images with the diffusion gradient (B500-B1000). A negative logarithm of the ratios is performed. Low signal is true restriction, high signal is T2 shine through. 302

Additional Sequences - Mentioned For Completeness: Perfusion imaging • Perfusion MRI quantitates cerebral micro-vascularization parameters. These parameters include regional blood flow and blood volume and mean transit time. Functional MRI (fMRI) • The basics underlying fMRI are increased blood flow to local vasculature that accompanies neural activity resulting in local reduction of deoxyhemoglobin. Deoxyhemoglobin acts as a contrast agent because it is paramagnetic (thus alters T2* MR signal). Take home point = fMRI depends on T2* effects. In fMRI procedures: First the patient performs a task, then Blood Oxygen Level Dependent (BOLD) imaging is done before and after the task. The pre and post task images are then subtracted and overlaid on a gray-scale brain image to localize the signal origin. Angiography: 2D time o f flight (TOF) MRA, 3D-TOF MRA, Phase contrast MRA • 2D-TOFMRA: Uses a gradient echo sequence where a saturation pulse is employed to null venous or arterial blood flow. Has a SMALL VOXEL SIZE. • 3D-TOFMRA: Is collected as a 3D volume, as opposed to slices, and allows for smaller voxels than 2D. 3D-TOF is well-suited for high flow arterial systems like the Circle of Willis. Benefits include a higher SNR than 2D, a shorter imaging time, more smooth vessel contours, and better saturation (which limits venous circulation). • Phase contrast MRA: Uses bipolar gradients to create contrast from flow. High velocity encoding time (VENC) is needed for arterial imaging, and low VENC for veins and sinuses. Phase contrast MRA is a quantitative image and can measure mean blood flow velocity and direction. • In general: TOF MRA is faster and less sensitive to signal loss from turbulent vessels than Phase Contrast MRA. Phase contrast MRA has advantages in increased background suppression and decreased sensitivity to intravoxel dephasing. 303

MRI Spectroscopy WTF is this “M R I Spectroscopy ” ? The general idea is that the various metabolites that exist on the cellular level (choline, lactate, N-acetylaspartate “NAA,” etc... etc...) occur in different concentrations depending on the pathology. For example, “NAA” is a neuronal marker. Things that destroy neurons (like tumors) will decrease NAA. So, in general the lower the NAA the higher the grade tumor. I talk about this stuff in volume 2 o f Crack the Core. How is this sorcery performed ? The physics is pretty similar to conventional MRI. In fact, you can used a variety o f sequence types including vary basic spin ECHO. Some difference worth knowing are: (1) Instead o f using the information from the frequency gradient to provide locational information - that data is used to help identify the different chemical patterns in a methods similar to the way NMR worked in your undergrad organic chemistry class (electron shielding, resonance frequency, etc etc... so on and so forth). Localization is a little more complicated - the short version is that you can use three orthogonally selective slice with 90 degree pulses. The signal will then be produced from the intersection o f the three planes “STEAM” they call it. There are other methods too “PRESS” is one - it’s beyond the scope of the exam (probably). (2) Fat and water are deliberately avoided. In conventional MRI you are using signal from the entire region o f interest (example the brain, CSF, skull, and scalp) to make up the picture. If you tried to do that with MRS the peaks o f fat and water would scale out to be sky scrapers, with the remaining metabolites like little ants or lego men / women. This is avoided in two ways: (a) you need to choose a voxel for MRS that is within the brain - avoiding the high fat areas of the bone marrow and scalp - “outer volume” suppression is also sometimes needed - to suppression signal from the boxes around the target, (b) you need some method o f water suppression - either an inversion sequence o f chemical shift suppression. This entire process becomes difficult if you are using MRS on trial lawyers (bones for brains) - but that situation is beyond the scope o f the exam (probably). Why do is see “PPM” on these diagrams ? PPM stands for parts per million. You might think this relates to the quantity o f that specific metabolite but you’d be wrong. It is actually the percent of the Larmor Frequency (lppm = 1 millionth o f the Larmor frequency). This is important because each metabolite will have a unique frequency distribution. For example NAA is a 2.0 ppm. Why are the numbers counting backwards on the scale ? — “because I’m your mother thats why.” How does one tell how much of something there is ? You look at the signal intensity (“Y axis”), but you’ll find the ratios are overall more useful. Other random physics tips ? Good “Shimming” is important - it is necessary for adequate signal-to- noise. If your lines look flat - “shimming” is probably a good option to choose as a fix. What about TE - Does that matter ? This is complicated. Yes... it does matter, and the nuts and bolts of it would require a discussion o f J-Coupling. I don’t want to go there. Just think about it like this: Initial Exam: short TE ~ 30 msec, is often used to start because it has a good signal to noise and demonstrates a broad range of metabolites (with both short and long T2 relaxation times). This Follow Up Exam : For More Characterization: You can sample multiple voxels with longer TEs. This will only show metabolites with a long T2 ~ 270 msec (NAA, Creatine, Choline). 304

Fat Saturation Techniques There are two broad categories. •Inversion Sequences (STIR) that are based on the inversion time “T.I.” of fat •Those that exploit the resonance difference between fat and water protons. STIR - As described previously, this is a short inversion sequence (180, followed by a well timed 90). It does great with metal artifact. You can NOT use Gd with it. Selective Pulse: Remember one of the limitation of STIR is that you can’t give Gd with it - so this is the method used most commonly for contrast enhanced studies. Water Unlike STIR which works by capitalizing on the differences in inversion times. Selective saturation works by capitalizing on the resonance difference between protons within their respective microenvironment. For example, protons in fat precess about 74 Hz slower than water at 0.5 Hz. This precession difference gets more exaggerated with a stronger magnet - per that Larmor equation which I mentioned near the beginning of the chapter (Precession Frequency = Gyromagnetic Radio x Field Strength in Tesla). The fat saturation is a 3 step process. (1) Preparatory Pulse - This is done by dropping a very narrow RF pulse just at the resonance of fat. (2) Spoiler Gradient - This results in dephasing of the protons primed by the predatory pulse - eliminating their ability to produce a signal. The remaining protons (the one’s that aren’t fat) will be the only ones left able to give signal. Dephasing “Spoiler” Gradient - (3) Proceed with the scan as normal - sending the first RF pulse immediately after the spoiler gradient. Trivia: This requires excellent field homogeneity Trivia: This is NOT typically done with very low field strength magnets (less that 1.0T) because you can’t accurately separate the peaks between fat and water. Only Fat Peak Selected RF Pulse Sent at the Specific Resonance of Fat Fat Flipped Water NOT 305

Fat Related Topics: In Phase and Out of Phase: The chemical environments of fat and water are different for protons. This causes these protons to precess at different rates. To create “in and out of phase” images a spoiled GRE is performed when the protons are spinning with each other (about 4.4 msec at 1.5T) and directly out of phase of each other (2.2 msec at 1.5 T). Think about this like two runners on a track. At the beginning of the race they are lined up together. The faster of the two (water) will get out to a lead - and at 2.2msec (assuming a 1,5T magnet) will be directly across the track from the slow one (fat) - this is when they are “opposed.” At this point in time their signal will cancel out. As the race continues eventually the fast one (water) will begin to lap the slow one (fat) - and at 4.4msec will have gone all the way around and cut him. At this point they are in phase. The cycle repeats every 2.2msec (assuming a 1.5T). The idea is that microscopic fat will drop out on the out of phase. Out - Signal Loss In - Signal Normal Out - Signal Loss From Fat Liver From Fat Liver From Fat Liver Start / Peak: The signal drop out that you can visualize starts around 15% fatty infiltration and peaks at 50%. Yes... it peaks at 50%. You will actually see less drop out at 75%. The reason is at greater that 50% there are fewer water molecules (relative to fat) to cancel out the fat signal. What i f it’s a 3T magnet? Then the changes occur twice as fast. 1.5 T: Out of Phase 2.2 msec, In Phase 4.4 msec, Out of Phase 6.6 msec, In Phase 8.8 msec 3.0 T: Out of Phase 1.1 msec, In Phase 2.2 msec, Out of Phase 3.3 msec, In Phase 4.4 msec 306

Does it matter when (chronologically) you obtain the out of phase sequence? In other words in you obtain out of phase at 2.2 msec, and in phase at 4.4 msec is that the same as getting out of phase at 6.6 msec and in phase at 4.4 msec ? Yes/No - But ultimately yes. The No - If you are looking for Hepatic Steatosis, then it does NOT matter. If the liver is fatty you will see signal loss on out of phase no matter when you get it. Out of phase is out of phase, and the reason out of phase is out of phase is because of the precision differences in fat and water. The water is lapping the fat on the track. The Yes - What about Hemochromatosis? The iron liver. Remember way back to the beginning of this masterpiece of a chapter when I talked about T2*? The quick refresher is basically loss of signal occurs faster when you have an inhomogeneous field. The 180 degree pulse of Spin Echo is used to try and smooth things out. Remember in and out of phase is a GRE sequence - so no 180 degree refocusing pulse. Therefore T2* is alive and well. Think about iron as a “magnet within a magnet” - and that iron liver magnet is going to fuck the field up in that local area. So you end up having T2* effects that are even more pronounced. That is why signal is lost with Iron. Totally different mechanism than the fat. In other words, Signal is lost as a function of time. The longer you wait the darker it gets. Whatever you obtain second will look darker than the thing you obtained first. 2.2 msec -Signal OK 4.4 msec -Signal Struggling 6.6msec -Signal Lost Consequences - Let me show you what happens if you don’t obtain the out of phase at 2.2msec (or 1.1msec on a 3T). 2.2 msec - Out 4.4 msec - In 6.6 msec - Out The Correct Way - Here we didn’t wait and obtained out of phase at 2.2msec, and in phase at 4.4 msec. Patient A - has signal loss on out o f phase = FAT Patient B - has signal loss on In phase = IRON The Wrong Way - Here the tech was distracted by Facebook and missed the out o f phase at 2.2msec, did get the in phase at 4.4 msec, and then snagged the out o f phase at 6.6 msec - thinking no big deal. Patient A - has signal loss on out o f phase = FAT Patient B - also appears to have signal loss on out of phase = FAT? * you see if you wait - you can’t tell why the signal loss happen, and could falsely assume its from fat drop out 307

Chemical Shift Artifact (The Fat Artifact): This will be mentioned again when 1 talk about artifacts, but I want to just touch on it briefly as it’s related to fat. Again, because of the difference in proton environments between fat and water precession differences cause an artifact in the read out (frequency encoding) direction. There are two subtypes. Type 1: This one manifestation is a bright rim on one side and a dark rim on the other. This can occur on either Spin Echo or GE sequences. Testable Trivia: Chemical shift increases with field strength (it’s not seen below 1 T) Testable Trivia: Chemical shift decreases with increased gradient strength Testable Trivia: Chemical shift decreases with a wider read out bandwidth Type 1 Chemical Shift Artifact Frequency Encoding Direction Type 2: India Ink Artifact (Black Boundary) - This type of chemical shift artifact shows a black line in all directions of the fat-water interface. On Gradient Echo sequences, if a voxel has about 50% fat and 50% water the signals will cancel out - leaving this black line. Testable Trivia: Using Spin Echo sequences will get rid of the India Ink, but NOT the Chemical Shift. Type 2 Chemical Shift Artifact “India Ink” 308

MR Contrast (Gd+) Broadly speaking MRI contrast can be grouped into either “positive agents” which cause bright Tl signal (shortens Tl), or “negative agents” which produce magnetic inhomogeneity from susceptibility leading the T2 shortening. Gadolinium: This is a highly toxic metal that can cause all kinds of badness even in small doses - including liver necrosis. To solve this issue, Gd is complexed with a chelate. The toxicity, clearance routes, bio-distribution, and relaxation properties are all attributes to the chelating agent (DTPA). How do Gd+3 cause a Tl shortening? • Short answer: Gd is strongly paramagnetic. “Paramagnetic” means it can be magnetized, and when it does, it tends to be polarized in a way that is the same as the magnetic field - locally augmenting it. • Longer answer: Gd+3has seven unpaired electrons, and the interaction of these ELECTRONS causes augmentation of the external magnetic field. The field of the electrons is very short so the contrast agent needs to be right next to the tissue to have an effect on it. Why does Gd+3 “only” shorten Tl ? It actually doesn’t. It just shortens Tl more. You could say that the “dominant effect” is on Tl. The reason for this preferential choice is complicated, and has a lot to do with relaxation rates of tissues in the body. The easiest way to conceptualize it is to remember that most tissues in the body have a natural disparity in their baseline relaxation times, with Tl being about 5 times (sometimes 10 times) longer than T2. So the effects are more noticeable on T l. If that doesn’t make it clear then make the following flash card. On one side write “why does Gd preferentially shorten T l” on the other side write “IT JUST FUCKING DOES.” Many MRI physics concepts can be learned using a similar flashcard set. Does the Tl shortening effect o f Gd change with different magnet strengthens ? Yes, it gets stronger with a stronger field strength. However, the caveat is only between a 0.5 T and 1.5 T is the increase significant. The change from 1.5 to 3.0 T is much less noticeable - although still present. What about T2 effects ? At weak concentration the T2 effect is essentially nothing. However, at high concentration (classically seen as a “pseudolayer” the bladder) T2 effects dominate. MRI Perfusion techniques use this T2 effect by using a concentrated bolus in the first intravascular passage - causing a drop in both T2* and T2 signal. Tl Shortening = Bright T2 Shortening = Dark 309

MR Contrast (Gd+) - Continued There are a ton of different MRI contrast agents. I tried to pick the “main ones” and give a few pears I think could be testable about each. Yes... you probably need to know both names because the question writer could be an asshole and give you the name no one uses. Name Everyone Uses Name That No One Uses But Will Probably Be the One Used on the Test Structural Type Ionic vs Non-Ionic Misc Trivia Magnevist Gadopentetate (Gd-DTPA) | Linear Ionic Oldest FDA Approved Agent Probably has the highest NSF Risk Multihance Gadobenate (Gd-BOPTA) | Linear Ionic 5% Hepatocyte Uptake Eovist Gadoxetate (Gd-EOB-DTPA) Linear Ionic 50% Hepatocyte Uptake Gadavist Gadobutrol (Gd-BT-D03A) Macrocylic Non-Ionic High Viscosity Low (none?) Risk of NSF Extracellular Agents: These are the most clinically used agents that have a brief vascular phase, followed by an equilibrium in the extracellular space. All o f these agents are very hydrophilic. Blood Pool Agents: Keeping the agent in the blood can be done two ways (1) by using a large Gd ligand, or (2) by binding the Gd to a big sugar or protein molecule Pharmacokinetics: • Elimination is primarily renal • Some agents (Gd-BOPTA and Gd-EOB-DTPA) have more excretion in the bile • In general, Gd cannot pass through a normal (intact) blood brain barrier. • Although there is that brain autopsy paper that showed Gad deposition in the dentate nucleus , globus pallidus, and skull. Which means absolutely fucking nothing, but made people pretend it was a big deal. Also prompted a bunch of frivolous litigation against Radiology - because “what else do we not know ?” - so why not blame all medical maladies on the MRI contrast. Dumb! Everyone knows vaccinations are the real evil. • Chuck Norris famously sued everyone because “MRI medicine poisoned his wife.” • A cobra once bit Chuck Norris in the leg. After 3 days of agonizing pain, the cobra died. Storage: Gadolinium contrast agents are stored and administered at room temperature. CT Contrast is sometimes “warmed” prior to administration. 310

MRI Artifacts A lot of artifact testable fodder has to do with phase vs frequency encoding direction. So let us review that briefly, then we will discuss the artifacts. When we sample signal for each plane we have an “X” and a “Y” direction for the grid - one will be phase the other frequency. You CAN do it either way, and sometimes flipping them is a way to deal with flow (motion) or wrap around (aliasing) artifacts. What influences the choice? A - Scan Time. Remember that the number of phase encoding steps is a key component to the table time equation. More steps = More time. That is why scan time is the most important consideration, because scan time = money (and despite what Academic Radiologists will tell you: money = happiness). The Hospital Administrator who went to school for 6 years and makes triple what your Chairman makes would agree also. As a general rule phase encoding direction is the shorter distance. • Front-Back in the Abd, • Side-Side in the Head. • Sagittal Direction for 3D Spine Shorter Direction - “usually ” the phase encoding direction B - Artifact Reduction. Moving the pulsation from the aorta off of that liver lesion. Moving the respiratory artifact off the posterior 1/3 of the breasts. Notable exception to the “General Rule” = Breast MRI is typically PE in the Side to Side Direction, to keep chest wall movement artifact off the breasts. C - Coil Design Restrictions. 311

Image Process Artifacts: These include aliasing, chemical shift, truncation, and partial volume. Aliasing: This error occurs when an area is under sampled. You get wrapping of anatomy from under sampled portions. This occurs in the phase encoding direction. Fix: • MAKE THE FOY BIGGER: Seems obvious. The follow-up question is this... what is the cost? Why not just make the FOVfucking huge every time to avoid this? The answer is spatial resolution. Large FOV = Shitty Spatial Resolution. Ever try and look at the spine on a body MRI? Think you can grade narrowing/stenosis accurately on that mother fucker? The reason it is blurry is because your FOV is large. Large FOV = Shitty Spatial Resolution. So the counter to the counter is that you have to increase phase encoding steps to maintain the spatial resolution -which will lengthen the time of the scan, cost the imaging center money, and get you a threatening email from your Insect Overlord (hospital administrator). I, for one, welcome our new insect overlords • Flip the PE/FE directions to move the artifact off a specific area of interest (sometimes this works - other times it is just not possible). This also increases table time (costs money) if you move the PE direction into the longer part of the body. • Surface Coils - This works by minimizing signal from tissue outside the FOV. Surface coils won’t detect signal far away from it. • Sat Bands - Eliminating signal from tissue outside Gamesmanship: A tricky way to show this or ask this would be to show you wrap around (“aliasing”) artifact in the AP direction and ask you how phase/frequency directions were selected. This makes you work backward to realize that the scan was done in a non-traditional way (remember that heads are usually done side-side). The artifact is occurring in the AP direction. So, that must be the phase encoding 312

Chemical Shift: As discussed (page 291), the protons of different molecules precess at different frequencies. The difference between water and fat is around 220 Hz at 1.5 T (even worse at 3 T). This shift in the Larmor frequency is the so called “chemical shift.” The type 1 error occurs in the frequency encoding direction (opposite of most of artifacts which are P.E.). Fixing Type 1: • Usually this doesn’t cause a big enough problem to need “fixing” but for the purpose of multiple choice exams here are a few solutions. • Swap PE and FE positions to move the artifact off the area of interest. • Use STIR or other fat suppression techniques • Increase the Receiver Bandwidth Fixing Type 2: • Adjust the TE • Perform Spin Echo Sequence (remember this only occurs with GRE) Partial Volume: This is just like in CT. You have different signal intensities in different adjacent structures. If they overlap in a signal voxel - you get averaging. This can result in an intermediate signal from mixing of high and low intensities. Making it better: Make your pixels smaller. More slices in the z-direction. 313

Truncation I Gibbs: The transformation of K space through inverse fourier transform ideally (but never) resulting in a block of data. Ripples in this data - especially at abrupt intense tissue changes result in the appearance of lines. You classically see these at high contrast interfaces (skull -brain, Cord-CSF, and meniscus/fluid). The CSF-Cord interface is the most classic - mimicking a syrinx. If prompted I would say the cause is limited sampling of free induction decay. It can be seen in both the frequency encoding and phase encoding directions but is more commonly seen in the phase encoding because many times a phase encoding matrix that is smaller than the readout matrix is selected to reduce time. Gamesmanship: The classic way to show this is sagittal view of the spine - looking like a syrinx. Making it better: • Short answer = more matrix. • Long answer = Decreasing the bandwidth or decreasing pixel size (more PE steps, less FOV, more matrix). Improvement penalty: Increased acquisition time and reduced per-pixel signal to noise. Gibbs at the Skull - Brain Gibbs - Mimicking a Syrinx 314

Motion: This can be seen with both voluntary and involuntary (cardiac, respiratory) movements. Motion creates difference between frequency encoding (which is fast) and phase encoding (which is slow). You will see ghosting or smearing - primarily in the phase encoding direction. You can also see the classic pulsation artifact from the aorta. Making it Better: • If the motion is “voluntary” you should start by yelling at the patient (“SIR! SIR! PLEASE STOP MASTURBATING! - You are creating a motion artifact that is degrading imaging quality!). • Perform Breath Holding - This is the best method for respiratory motion. • Respiratory Gating (increases acquisition time) • ROPE (Respiratory Ordered Phase Encoding) - Phase encoding steps are ordered with respiration • Breathing Navigator - An echo from the diagram determines its position, then timing and acquisition are based off this. • Apply a fat sat band across the abdomen • Switch the phase encoding direction. • Fancying Shit like BLADE / PROPELLER - which use radial sampling methods (like a helicopter “blade” or “propeller”) to oversample the center of k-space. This gives you redundant information for error correction. Patient Related Artifacts: These include Motion and Magic Angle Pulsation - From the Aorta Breathing Artifact 315

Flow: This is a type of motion artifact, related to blood flow. Blood flow causes ghosting in the phase encoding direction. GRE sequences are more susceptible to this than SE sequences. SE Sequences - Flow looks dark. Moving blood that got hit with the 90 pulse, moves out of the way prior to getting the 180 pulse. Therefore, it doesn’t have any signal. GRE Sequences: In flowing blood looks bright. Making it better: • Apply a saturation band adjacent to the imaging section - 90 pulse followed by crusher gradient. W hat M akes A Flow Void? The dark hole of a patent vessel is called a “flow void.” This is generally a spin echo imaging finding (as stated above - flow looks dark on SE). ■ 90 180 90+180 1 ■._ No Echo ♦ 11 No Flow, gets 90 & 180- makes an Echo Slow Flow, gets some 90 & 180 - makes an Echo Fast Flow, gets 90 but misses 180 - No Echo Why does this happen? To make an echo, the proton must be exposed to both a 90 pulse and a 180 pulse pulse. If the blood is flowing fast it will get hit with the 90 degree, but miss the 180 degree pulse. This means no echo, and thus no signal. 316

Magic Angle: This is an MSK artifact seen with tendons. You see this with short echo time (TE) sequences where the focus forms an angle of 55 degrees with the main magnetic field (magic angle phenomenon). This will NOT be seen in T2 sequences (with long TE). This phenomenon, is reduced at higher field strengths due to greater shortening of T2 relaxation times. Magic Angle: You see it on Short TE (T1, PD, GRE) Then it goes away on Long TE (T2) Why does it go away with a long TE ? Remember “T2” is 37% of the original signal. Tendons that interact with the magnet have a decrease in tissue interactions. The result is a prolonged time needed to get to that 37%. However, if you wait long enough (longer TEs) those differences equal out. This is why longer TE sequences (classic “T2”) are not troubled by the mischief of this sinister phenomenon. 37% Magic Angle Voodoo ■ increases tendon 1 Normal “T2” of \ CT 2 „ from % a tendon is 10-20ms \ around 1-10 ms Y 37% i d / i 1 I i At a long enough TE there is no difference between signal from normal tendons and those manipulated by the sinister magic angle 40 ms 10 ms 10 ms 20 ms 10 ms 317

Cross Talk: RF and FT pulses are not perfectly rectangular. So if they are placed close enough together then you get excitation of neighboring section more than once in a single repetition. These lead to partial saturation and lower signal. So all sections (except the ones on the ends will be subjected to this). RF Related: These include Cross Talk and Zipper Artifacts Making it better: • Increasing the gap between sections. • Interleave slices (all odds, then all evens). Testable Trivia: The 3D images are not susceptible to this artifact because the entire volume undergoes excitation with sections within the volume acquired with gradients. Zipper A rtifact: There are lots of random stray RF signals (radio, tv, etc...). Remember that the RF pulse is a “radio-wave.” Anyway, if you have defective or inadequate shielding you can get a “zipper” of high signal - 1-2 pixels in width running across the image. This typically extends in the phase encoding direction. Gamesmanship: A possible scenario for this is that anesthesia left the pulse ox monitor in the room. Making it better: • Try closing the door (if it’s open). • Remove all electronic devices from the room. • Call the tech people to repair the faulty RF shielding. • Alternatively, you could write an email, cc 15 people, and use the phrase “patient care”. That fixes most problems. Ideal Slice Profile 318

External Field Related: This is Magnetic Inhomogeneity: Inhomogeneous Fat Suppression: If the field is actually homogeneous you get a uniform fat saturation by applying an RF pulse with the resonance of fat protons. In the real world, local field inhomogeneities cause fat protons to precess at difference frequencies. These differences allow certain areas of fat to resist suppression - which can mimic edema. Making it better: Using an inversion recovery - STIR, especially in the setting of metal. T2FS: Fat Sat in Marrow is ruined by Improved with STIR Metal messing with the local field Shimming Shimming is a thing that can be done to improve field homogeneity.When the magnet comes from the manufacturer the field homogeneity is terrible. To correct for this a process called “shimming” is used. There are two types of shimming, passive and active. Most of the time a combination of the two is used; passive to get the thing going in the right direction and active to optimize the field for each patient. “ Passive Shimming - A phantom is scanned and the position of the shim plates is adjusted until the field becomes homogeneous (are at least better than it was). This is done at the time of installation. • Active Shimming - This is done by using the electromagnetic coil, and can be done after each patient (or sequence). Essentially, gives you the chance to have a homogeneous field (or nearly) regardless of the size of the patient. 319

Magnetic Susceptibility: This includes diamagnetic, paramagnetic, and ferromagnetic Susceptibility (includes “m etal”) - This refers to the ability of a substance to become magnetized by the external field. Obviously metals have large susceptibility. Calcium hydroxy apatite and accumulations of gadolinium chelate can do the same thing. Generally speaking, susceptibility affects all pulse sequences, but is most severe with GRE images and least severe with SE (because of the 180 degree refocusing pulse - to lose those T2* effects). A less prominent version occurs at tissue interfaces (bone and muscle, or air and bone). The classic location for this is the transition from paranasal sinus to skull base.
