NIS SECTION 1 i ,W ; DUTIES OF THE OA OFFICER v C B ) | - PROMETHEUS LIONHART, MJ>. - j The QA officer has several important duties including (1) Reading hand washing statistics to the faculty once a month, (2) Stirring up trouble, and (3) Making everyone’s lives miserable. Who is the QA Officer, and why does he hate puppies, sunshine, and colors? He is a man with no soul. Bom without warm blood, only cold black oil fills his veins. He is the official trouble maker of the Radiology department. This vs That: QC, QA, and QI The terms QC, QA, and QI are often used interchangeable to describe the various sinister deeds of the QA officer. However, there are differences and those differences are potentially testable. QC - Quality Control As a resident you are responsible for doing all the work in the department. You can think about the work you do as “generating a product.” The product you are making is a radiology report. That product (your report) is checked for quality to see if it meets certain requirements. This “checking for quality” is quality control. Example 1 "The Readout ” - The attending performing a readout is doing the QC. Your product (the shoulder MR read) is checked by your attending to make sure it meets acceptable parameters. Note that this happens multiple time a day. Example 2 “Berating the Tech ” - You (the Resident) may find it therapeutic to periodically yell at the Tech - For example, your attending disagrees with you on that shoulder MR read. You said the labral tear was 270 degrees and he thinks it’s 290 degrees. He makes a big deal about this and embarrasses you in front of the med student who wants to do ortho and thinks he’s learning something by watching the readout. To make yourself feel better you decide to yell at the Tech. The next case that comes through is a chest x-ray on a 500 pound vented ICU patient. You notice the right costophrenic angle is partially clipped. You immediately call the tech room and berate them for their poor quality. You mention the phrase “patient care” a lot to really make them feel bad. You don’t let up until the tech begins to cry. You like the way it makes you feel and decide that a career in academics is right for you. This process of checking the film for quality is an example of QC. Example 3 “Bean Counting” - Bean Counters check your ultrasound reports to make sure you mention all the stuff you need, so medicare will pay. “Can you put an addendum on that and mention the IVC???” The process of checking your reports for the IVC is an example of QC. Quality Control = Checking to see if the product meets the acceptable parameters. Think about this as Yes or No. This is NOT a process for improvement. No solutions are provided. It is simply - “good dog” or “bad dog.” 357

QA - Quality Assurance This is a set of activities for checking quality in the processes by which products are created. Example - Lead Tech trains the other techs to position the patient’s arms up during the body CT scan. The Lead Tech (an overweight man with a mustache) periodically gets off Facebook (he is an avid Farmville player) and pokes his head in the scanner room to make sure the patient’s arms are up. If they aren’t up then he has to hold a training session, which means less time for Farmville, which means he gets PISSED! During the training session the Lead Tech yells “you did it wrong!” The point is to “prevent” a defect in the product. This prevention is typically done with periodic audits of the operations of the system. Ql - Quality Im provem ent In my example where the Lead Tech has to retrain the other techs at the expense of his Farmville time, this would be an example of QI. So, to be clear: QA - The Lead Tech performs the audit, by checking to make sure the patient’s arms are up. After noticing the error has already occurred (arms are down) he reacts and yells “you did it wrong!” QI - The Lead Tech retrains the techs to make sure they understand the importance of keeping the arms up. During the training he raises his arms many times. He is an overweight man and sweats a lot. The perspiration stains under his armpits make the other techs very uncomfortable so they look at the floor. He becomes angry that they aren’t looking at him during his demonstration and questions their commitment to patient care. The Lead Tech demands that a check list be attached to the scanner and a mark must be made on the paper indicated that the arms were placed up prior to the scan. The whole scene is just ugly, and the techs never make that mistake again. The QI intervention is a great success. Another concept regarding QA, QC, and QI is this idea that an active improvement is best. This is how I remember this. Remember when your PD/Chairman said they had to take your book fund / travel fund away because of Obama? The real reason was they hired a QA officer and needed to free up some cash to pay him. Most QA officers are paid in silver (30 pieces). For that QA officer to have a job he/she has to continuously stir up trouble, and he/she does that by looking for projects. Why??? Because “we can always improve.” “I for Improve, I for QI” Quality Assurance = This is a STABLE process in which errors are handled in a reactive usually retrospective fashion. Example: Yelling “YOU DID IT WRONG” after someone did it wrong. This doesn’t actually prevent future errors, it just makes the person who made an error feel like shit. 358

Quality Control Quality Assurance Quality Improvement Errors are identified in Retrospect. Errors are identified in Retrospect. Blame is assigned for fucking up. Errors are identified prospectively and retrospectively. Less emphasis on individual blame. Identification occurs AFTER the product is developed but prior to release. Example: Attending checks you out. Process is Stable - no change in behavior Process is Dynamic - attempts are made to change behavior. *The Modern System - or The Good One. "I would greatly prefer golrv-g to M§M thaw checking out flyujtlwwg with him,." Making People Miserable - The H aw th o rn e Effect: Monitoring your work for “training purposes” - falls under the purview of the QA Officer. People behave differently when they think they are being watched. At the institution I trained I used to have a lot o f “unproductive conversations” while on night float. Me and my teammate on call would discuss a variety o f topics including (but not limited to); How much I hated various Attendings and the Program Director(s), Which female Residents/Attendings I would like to have sexual encounters with (with the priority debated and ranked), My thoughts on various political opinions (Mexico will pay for the wall!), gun rights - including my desire to buy a fully automatic Israeli assault rifle (for home protection), making / blow shit up with homemade tannerite, the hoax o f global warming, how all the religions are wrong (except the one I practice), various drugs I’ve tried - including DMT (the spirit molecule), That time I told a Surgeon to go fuck him self That time I told most o f the ED to go fuck themselves That time I told that Medicine Intern to go fuck himself. Obviously these are not conversations that I would want to be made public or listened to by anyone. If you take in house call I imagine you have similar conversations, or have similar conversations in the privacy o f your own home. Now imagine if your chairman / PD told you he placed a recording device in the call reading room. Those conversations probably wouldn’t happen, and you’d be a lot more productive. That is the Hawthorne effect - you shut up and play by the rules when you are being watched. 359

Making People Miserable - T h e W e b e r E ffe ct: Along the lines of making people miserable - the QA Officer enjoys collecting data on mistakes and errors. Who better to collect miss rate data on then the new guy? People love to talk shit about the new guy. New attending shows up and everyone starts looking at him/her very closely. He said this or that. He missed this or that. Look at the way he eats! If you ever switched schools growing up you probably experienced this first hand. It happens with drug adverse reactions a lot. The reporting of “adverse events” is highest in the first year, then starts to taper off. The “talk shit about the new guy effect” Stirring Up Trouble - T h e S a fe ty C h am p io n The pretentious title of ‘‘Safety Champion ” is bestowed upon one who reports the wrong doings of others to an authority. Think about this person as the QA Officer in charge of safety. Trivia: “Safety Champions focus on the System not the individual” - uh huh. The system will now be punished.... said no one ever. Stirring Up Trouble - B e n ch m a rk in g What is this Benchmarking? Basically how well you are doing something relative to a standard measurement or comparison to your peers. The process to perform yourself some benchmarking is as follows: (1) Turn the thing into something that can be measured objectively. For example, patient satisfaction. Mr Metastatic Cancer Patient did you enjoy laying in the MR scanner for the last hour? Can you rate the experience on a scale of 1-10? Here are some smiley / frowning faces to help you decide what number to pick. (2) Once you have an objective measurement then you can compare it to a national standard or another institution. At our hospital the average patient satisfaction score for oncology patients undergoing total spine MR is a 3/10. At the rival hospital the same patients give an average score of 4/10. The goals of the process is to look for ways to improve. What are they doing better than us? Well they give their patients a lollipop and a sticker after their agonizing MR experience. Perhaps we should try that too. Benchmarking = The measurement of how well an organization’s policies are doing, with the goal of determining where improvement is needed. Examples: • Report Turn Around Time • Compliance with Critical Finding Reporting • Peer Reviews Safety Champion = The Asshole responsible for the safety process and safety reporting systems. 360

Stirring Up Trouble - Best Practices A “Best Practice” is a method that shows superior results compared to other methods. On the prior page, we discussed the use of benchmarking to see how much our metastatic cancer patient population enjoys laying in the magnet for hours without moving. We were distressed to find out that they don’t like it very much. We also discovered that our competition offers lollipops as an incentive to raise customer satisfaction scores. Offering the lollipop is an example of a practice that is showing superior results relative to not offering the lollipop - i.e. a “best practice.” However, a critical detail is that “best practices” continue to evolve. So a new strategy is employed to see if even better results can be obtained. Instead of having our elderly, poorly mannered, former public school bus driver, secretary pass out the lollipops we hire a 20 year old, large breasted college student to administer the candy. Satisfaction scores sky rocket among our metastatic prostate cancer patients - and this is found to be the new “best practice.” Stirring Up Trouble - Key P erform ance In d icato r (K PI) This is the metric for which the QA Officer will judge you buy. A poor performance here and he may recommend “additional training.” In other words, it could be the bottom line ($$$) or it could be how fast you get that allergy bracelet on once they hit the front door of the ED. Example - The KPI for the President of the United States is probably the unemployment rate. That’s the thing most people will quote for how well he is doing (at least from an economic point of view). Example - In Radiology this might be a tum-around time metric or simply the bottom line in the department. How much money the slaves (residents/fellows) saved you by taking in house call at night. Stirring Up Trouble - Dashboards A “dashboard” is a tool that displays the most important information all on one screen or page. Examples might include an “urgent” work-list or daily RVU counter. Dashboards need to provide immediate feedback (you read the study, it drops off the list) and be simple to be effective. KPI = Metrics to determine how well the organization is doing. Includes both financial and non-financial metrics. Example: • Customer Satisfaction 361

Making People Miserable - The Value Equation: You probably thought that “value” was just a made-up term that hospital administrators throw around to justify cutting your salary (and increasing theirs). To prove to you that “value” is an actual thing, people who don’t understand math decided to make it seem like a quantitative thing by creating a “formula” to calculate value - using one measurable thing (cost) and two things that can’t be objectively measured (service and quality). SERVICE x QUALITY V A L U E = 55s One way to think about this is what the hospital administrator wants out of you. Providing a service (reading of CT scans) at a high quality (low error rate) at the lowest cost possible. So to get the highest value possible you want the service x quality to be high, and the cost to be low. Catch that? Cost low... i.e. you get paid less. Don’t worry that money you are saving the system will still manage to find a way back into someone’s pocket - be that your Hospital Administrator or your Chairman in the form of a kickback for increasing the value (paying you less). Remember what that word really means next time you hear someone telling you that it’s your job to “Add Value.” 362

Making People Miserable - PDSA Cycle - “Improvement Cycle” A potentially endless cycle of torment - imposed by the QA officer. The idea behind a PDSA Cycle is to test quality improvement interventions. A PQI project might be an example. Think about this like the scientific method. You want to improve turn-around time for the daytime oncology clinic cancer followup pan scans. You decided to try and incentivize the residents on the body CT service to pick up the pace by telling them that if they can’t make the metric that they will need “Additional Training” i.e. more night float. You set up a metric, and let the residents try and make it for 3 months. After the 3 month mark you study the results, and based on those results you decide if the plan worked or didn’t work. I f it didn’t work ? You modify it by making the punishment more severe - torture ? Yes. But not of the body, of the soul. I f it did work ? You still aren’t satisfied. You want perpetual improvement. So you set the bar even higher and start the cycle of torture over again. Act - If it worked you keep offering the “incentive”. If it didn’t work you’ll need to try a different plan (attach the stone of sorrow?). Your different plan will start the cycle over with a test, study and act period all over again. ACT Study - You Study the results - did they pull it off? Plan - Improve turn around time by creating an incentive for residents on that service PLAN STUDY DO Do the Test - You let them try for 3 months Key Trivia: The ABR specifically “suggests” the use of PDSA cycles when doing you semi­ voluntary PQI projects. 363

Making People Miserable - Lean If the QA Officer had his way he would run the hospital just like a Japanese car factory (“LEAN” is the workflow style developed by Toyota). Testable Point - This overall strategy involves “engaging” the workforce. *Not just thinking problems are solved at the management level. Huh? Basically - the techs, reading room coordinators, and even the lowest of the low (the residents) understand the inefficiencies in the process and can have great insight in improvement of the process. Testable Point - LEAN = PULL. Memory strategy; Meat Heads (who are big not lean) only do bench press (a Pushing exercise). Real athletes do power cleans and snatch - bench is for idiots. WTF is "PULL” ? This vs That: PUSH vs PULL PUSH = This is really something that happens in industry and not really in the hospital. But it’s a vocab word so it’s potentially testable. The basic idea is that you make something even if no one is buying it. On January 1 - the board of directors got together and said we are going to make 500 cars a month. Even if no one is buying them, we are still going to PUSH 500 cars off the assembly line into the warehouse. Why? Cuz that was the plan. This seems stupid right? On January 1st the Chairman says we are going to take 300 chest x-rays a day. Regardless of the number ordered or the number of patients in the hospital, 300 chest x-rays are getting done. Makes no sense... so this isn’t the one to pick on the test. PULL = This is the idea that you PULL resources to match the demand. The floor orders 10 portables, one tech can handle that and one resident can read that. The floor orders 100 portables - gotta PULL a tech in to help. I’d say the resident would get help also, but this isn’t a science fiction book. These scenarios have to be at least partially grounded in reality “Kandan” Another "Getting Lean” Concept, that basically means don’t let your supplies run out, otherwise you can’t do the next case and you have to wait around. So, when supplies run low, you get a refill. Memory Tool - Kandan = Kan Dan. Dan is the guy who gets you more supplies before you run out. Hey Dan... Kan you restock the PICC Lines? We only have 2 left, and I usually drop at least one on the floor per case. If anyone Kan.... Kan-Dan. Dan Kan get you more supplies. 364

“S tandard W ork” “Getting Lean ” concept that is basically the idea of standardizing work (when possible). Some people might disagree, but I think structured reporting style is actually a faster way and safer way to read. That way you hit all the stuff you need to, in the same order, and minimize the thinking. It makes everyone’s reports look similar which is nice for the ordering providers - makes finding the one or two things they actually care about easier. As opposed to people who dictate in blob format waxing poetically about normal structures. Which of these two looks more “lean” to you? The liver has a normal contour and morphology. No enhancing lesions. No focal hypodense lesions. No fatty infiltration. There are 3 normally-oriented hepatic veins which drain into the IVC. The right, left, and main portal vein are patent. Liver: Normal “W aste” Lean Concept - you want to eliminate waste... get lean. ,.V: i. Don’t make more than you need. Don’t make a shitty product (going back and fixing stuff wastes time). Be efficient with how you do stuff (if you have two patients in the ED both need ultrasound, - scan them both in one trip). Don’t spend time doing ancillary staff’s job (looking up phone numbers and tracking people down) - it’s a huge time sink. Anything that wastes vour time is waste. “Just in T im e ” m Yet another "Lean ” Concept. Basically this is having a smooth continuous workflow. Example: The ultrasound tech has been doing multiple portable exams, but waits until 7:59 to drop the tech worksheets on your desk, because he/she leaves at 8:00. That way they can bail before you can ask for additional images, or complain about how shitty their work is (you labeled the bile duct the portal vein... again). In addition to ducking work, this also causes a work flow issue for you, as you were just sitting there with a clear list thinking you were going to leave your swing shift on time, and now you are stuck till 9:30 cleaning up this ultrasound dump. Had they provided you with a few ultrasounds at a time, you would be way more efficient with your time. This would be a “just in time ” way to do things. No dumps... you are working continuously through the time you have... just in time. 365

Making People Miserable - DMAIC Methodology This is very similar to the PDSA method - which essentially follows a common sense approach. But instead of just saying that, the QA officer tries to act like they invented something by calling it another vocabulary word. Define -> Measure -> Analyze -> Improve -> Control In other words, state the problem, find a way to quantify or measure the problem, guess where the problems are, make an intervention, and then see if it worked. If they work - they act like a control to keep you from sliding back into your pattern of screwing up. Trivia: DMAIC is “Data Driven” Trivia: DMAIC is the Primary Tool for Six Sigma Projects (although DMAIC is not exclusive to Six Sigma), it can be used for any random project. WTF is Six Sigma ? Six Sigma is a process to identify and remove trouble makers and minimize variability. The name “Six Sigma” describes the standard deviation of defect-free products it creates - in this case 6. What that actually means is that 99.9999998% of all opportunities to produce the product are expected to be free of defects (3.4 defective features per million opportunities). This seems like a reasonable goal for a Radiology resident to be held accountable to. 99.999998% "Six Standard Deviations = Six Sigma" 3 per Mi.LLi.oKs T his vs T h a t - Lean vs S ix Sigm a Lean = Focus on Eliminating Waste Six Sigma = Focus on Eliminating Defects 366

Stirring Up Trouble - This vs That: FMEA v s PCA: Failure Mode Effects Analysis (FMEA) - This is a mechanism to PROSPECTIVELY identify error in a process. Let’s look at an example of how you can set up the analysis. FUNCTION Primary Function of the resident is to take in house overnight call so Attendings don’t have to Potential Potential Failure Effects of Mode Failure Type Misses Changes - Stuff At Pisses the Night ED Off Might Hurt Someone Important Turn Metric gets Around the attention Time of Hospital Sucks Administrator “S ” Potential Causes of Failure Trainees are stupid Insufficient Fear of Consequences Insufficient Fear of Consequences ( Current O Process Controls In-Service Testing Milestones g Public Humiliation “QA Meeting” “D” 3 9 In this example we have examined the Trainees in their primary function (taking call so the Attendings don’t have to). The potential failures are listed with the possible consequences of those failures. “S” = Is a subjective score of Severity. - Rated 0-10 The potential causes of failure for each “mode” are listed, and “O” = The objective number of how often these occur The current processes for controlling the failures are listed and “D” = A subjective score on how effective those detection processes are is given. Successful detection is defined as - picking it up after it happens but prior to it effecting the consumer. In this case methods for determining which trainees will be high risk for misses on call are given. Testable Trivia • It’s a prospective process • It’s super subjective - therefore total bullshit • Fake math equations: Risk Priority Number (RPN) = S x O x D and Criticality Index = S x O 367

Root Cause Analysis (RCA) - This is a mechanism to RETROSPECTIVELY identify error in a process. Trivia = Used for both Active and Latent Errors Trivia = Retrospective Process (remember FMEA was prospective) This is essentially a “systems analysis”, used to discover the highest level cause or “root cause” or “evil at the bottom.” RCA uses a multi-disciplinary approach to collect data and reconstruct the events that lead to the error. Trivia: RCA is the appropriate next step after a sentinel event P.D. is an Asshole No Time Off To Study Poor Lotta Sick ► “Trainee” Call Ins Morale w What does “multi-disciplinary ” mean ? It means you let the nurses and techs pretend they are important and invite them to the meeting. There is always at least one of them that is really excited to be there - usually a male nurse / tech who was going to go to med school but insert excuse here (as if becoming a doctor is as easy as simply signing up). Both active errors (sharp end - i.e. doctor cuts the wrong vessel during surgery) and latent errors (blunt end - system errors, like EMR allows for incorrect dosing of a medication) are evaluated for. Why is an RCA stupid / almost never true ? Errors are usually multiple - like the swiss cheese idea (multiple holes). The idea that one single cause is to blame is silly. Having said that - one single resident / tech / nurse can usually be blamed - (and should be) at the end of the process. 368

Swiss Cheese Trivia = Adding layers isn’t always the answer, sometimes that bogs the system down, increasing its inefficiency. Sorta like government bureaucracy - the cure to bureaucracy isn’t always more bureaucracy (you socialist pig!). Short Run Goal = “Quick Fix” - Place immediate policy / countermeasure so that it doesn’t happen again (amputating more than one wrong limb in a week is frowned upon). In other words, when you amputate the wrong leg on Monday - and blame it on the resident, you don’t want to amputate the wrong arm on Tuesday. Mainly because the resident you blamed it on Monday will be fired, and if you try and blame it on another resident the higher-ups might get suspicious that you are actually the problem. So you want to place a short term fix - threaten all the residents that you will make them all take call on Christmas if this happens again. This is not a long term fix (plus some of them might be Jewish / Muslim / or even worse not acknowledge the existence of a Celestial Dictator) Long Run Goal = This is the long term fix - a system fix. Mark the right leg pre-op before the patient is sedated. 369

NIS SECTION 1 THE TRAINEE CAUSED A COMPLICATION - PROMETHEUS UONHART, M.D. - i yg* Any time I enter a patient care area, the first thing I do is look for the person I’m going to blame if something goes wrong- usually a tech or nurse. If I were working in academics, I would choose a “Trainee.” What is a “Trainee ? ” A Trainee is the new word for resident. It was developed by bureaucrats to help maintain the hierarchy of academic medicine, with the eventual goal of providing no salary or benefits (why would you pay the trainee?). When can a “Trainee” be referred to as a Doctor? There are only two scenarios: (1) If there is a menial task that requires a physician signature (writing scripts, doing H&Ps, consenting patients, etc....) or (2) There has been a complication and someone needs to be blamed. Trivia: How many people die per year from medical error? Between 44,000 and 98,000 Trivia: Where does that crazy stat come from? The Institute of Medicine put out a paper called “To Err is Human” in the 1990s throwing all doctors under the bus and embarrassing / discrediting the practice of medicine. The stat is from that paper. I’m sure the people who wrote the paper have all been promoted for their courageous efforts to make themselves famous at the expense of their peers. W hat is this “M edical Error” ? Either: (1) Failure of a planned action to be completed as intended. a. Example - 1 meant to stick the catheter in your jugular vein, but at the last second your carotid artery jumped into the path of my needle. (2) Using the wrong plan to achieve the aim. a. Example - 1 thought your cancer was an abscess so I tried to drain it spilling mucinous tumor all over your peritoneum making you stage 4. My Bad. Vocab : Safety vs Error: Safety = No risk of accidental injury Error = Fucking up the thing you planned to do or having the wrong plan to start with. 370

Fundamental Errors: (1) Shitty Communication - Basically the left hand doesn’t talk to the right hand. You have lots of different providers, EDs, Dr. Oz, etc providing medical advice and therapy and they don’t talk to each other. One doc puts you on a med, the next takes you off, then Dr. Oz sells you miracle weight loss potion. a. Buzzword = “Non-System ” b. Buzzword = Decentralized Health Care Delivery (2) Idiot Test Writers - License Exams don’t focus on error a. Turns out memorizing what chromosome Wilms tumor is on doesn’t make you a better / safer doctor. (3) Blood-Sucking Lawyers - In America, Lawyers or “Scum Sucking Fuck Pigs” (the technical term for a lawyer) ruin everyone’s fun. People are afraid o f getting sued, so errors aren’t reported. If errors don’t get reported, then mistakes never get corrected. (4) $$$ - Providers are not incentivized to emphasize safety, and since the real reason 99.9% of people actually go into medicine is to get paid - if you aren’t paying me to do it, then I’m not doing it. Want me to do something? Then pay me... or take the hospital administrator strategy and threaten not to pay me, and keep the extra money for yourself. Types of Errors (per the model classification) (1) Diagnostic Error - Family medicine wants a non-con chest to rule out a PE. (2) Treatment Error - Family medicine wants you to drain that gluteal abscess (which is actually a massive necrotic tumor). Now the track is seeded and they have to resect his entire posterior compartment - leaving the patient unable to stand up. (3) Preventive Error - Grandma falls out of bed. Happens at home - no ones fault. Happens at the hospital - it’s your fault (the Trainees fault). (4) “Other Error” - Communication Errors and Equipment Errors. You say Mag Sulfate but the Nurse hears Methotrexate. 371

This vs That: - Latent vs Active Errors Latent Error - Conditions in the system which allow the error to occur Example 1: Have you ever pulled up to a stop light, and you went a little too far into the intersection so you put the car in reverse and back up a little bit so you don’t get hit? But then a song comes on the radio that you really like and you forget to put the car back in drive - so you are sitting there waiting for the light to turn green with the car in reverse. You are truly an accident waiting to happen. This is a latent error. Conditions in the system that allow the error to occur, i.e. Justin Bieber on the radio distracted you - so you forgot to put the car back in drive. If you didn’t have music distracting you - this wouldn’t have happen Example 2: Sharps box is bolted to the wall away from the procedure area, so you have to walk the needles over to it in a crowded room. Why the fuck is the box on the other side of the room? System Failure = Accident Waiting to happened Example 3: Shitty EMR allows you to potentially enter lethal doses of pain medicine. Active Error: A mistake that immediately causes an injury. You did the fucking up. It’s tough to blame the system - although you can still try and blame the resident. Example 1: Administration of the wrong dose of medication Example 2: Cutting the hepatic artery while trying to remove the gallbladder. This vs That: Blunt End vs Sharp End: Blunt End = Think about the blunt end as the part you are holding - not the part doing the actual damage. It’s the tool you are wielding. Latent error occurs at the “blunt end.” The blunt end might be the EMR which allows for a medication to dosed incorrectly. Sharp End = Think about the sharp end as the part actually doing the damage. Active Error occurs at the “sharp end.” Think about this as the surgeon holding the “sharp end” of the scalpel. He is the active user / operator. He is the one making the error. Another example would be the nurse who actually gave the wrong medication. Trivia: The sharp end is the “last line of defense” against an error. 372

If you haven’t figured it out yet - Radiology is hard. It’s very very hard. But at the same time it’s simple (and made complicated by poor teaching at most academic centers). It’s hard because in the real world the volume is high and you have to maintain constant concentration for hours and hours without a lapse. This is why Radiology is much tougher than Medicine or even Surgery. What??? I thought radiology was supposed to be easy? Yeah... we all did. They lied to us. Turns out it is hard. Medicine and Surgery docs have a ton of walking around / bullshitting time during their day. They sleep on call (at least part of the night), and they have other people checking their work (Nurses, Pharmacists, etc...). Radiology is just you, and you get no break. There are 2 main categories for fucking up in Radiology: • Perceptual - Majority - (60-80%) This is when you didn’t see it. You called the pancreas normal... it wasn’t normal. There was a cancer. This is by far the most common type of mistake. This is why you have to have a search pattern, and you have to be a fucking robot / zombie that never breaks the search pattern. Academic Radiologists often push this idea that “communication” is why people get sued. It is... in a minority of cases. The main reason radiologists get sued is missing the finding. Slip / Lapse • Cognitive / Interpretive - (20-40%) You saw it but you called it the wrong thing. “Hypodense lesion in the cirrhotic liver is likely focal fat - no follow-up needed” .... Wrong ! - that’s a 6 cm HCC. You’ve heard the expression “you can’t fix stupid”.... Search pattern won’t help you here bro, you better read some books. Actually, if you find yourself making these kinds of mistakes you’ll want to try and switch into an administrative role - something non-clinical like QA Officer. Or even better, try and get into medical legal work so you can get paid helping to sue other Doctors, who are actually way better / smarter than you. You can wear a nice suit, and strut up to the witness stand. Practice that strut in the mirror, it’s important to have the swagger to convince the jury that you know what you are talking about. Knowledge based error Radiology Error - This vs That Satisfaction of Search When I was a young cub my father told me “never marry your first girl­ friend.” This turned out to be very applicable to Radiology as well as life. Marrying your first girlfriend is a satisfaction of search. 1 found one who likes me, no need to look for anything else. You saw the aneurysm - then you stopped looking (30% are multiple). You saw one fracture, stopped looking - missed the other fractures. His report us three pages lowg. So he has wo idea what he's talteuwg abouut. 373

“Never Event” • As the name infers this is something that should “never happen”. • Testable Trivia = This vocabulary word was invented by the National Quality Forum (NGF) “Sentinel Event” • Serious Injury / Death OR a close call. • Trivia - this always triggers an investigation • Trivia - 15 Gray exposure is a sentinel event • Classic Example - Wrong patient, wrong site, wrong procedure. • Next Step = Root Cause Analysis “Adverse Event” • Injury Cause by medical Care. • Trivia - It does NOT have to be someone’s fault. It does NOT mean an error occurred Example — Guy with horrible emphysema needs a lung biopsy. The procedure is done with “perfect” technique by the Attending while the Trainee watches in awe from the comer of the room. The patient develops a small pneumothorax - which is promptly treated with a chest tube. The development of a pneumo in this case is an “Adverse Event” - but it happen because of unfavorable conditions / bad luck not because of an error. Never Event Sentinel Event Adverse Event I Specific “Bad” Event, on a list written by the NGF Injury, Death, or Close Call Injury, but not definitely an error Adverse Event: Iatrogenic injury that does NOT imply error or blame. 374

Second V ictim The wire pulls back as you are placing the monster IVC retrieval device and you run the pointy end right through the side of the right ventricle. The patient turns very pale and flat-lines. You immediately shout “the Trainee pulled the wire back!” - even though he wasn’t even scrubbed in yet. You shout it a second time with confidence so that the techs will remember you said it when they testify in the malpractice case (and the firing of the trainee). Homicide isn’t a billable diagnosis. • First Victim = The Guy you stabbed in the heart with the filter retrieval device • Second Victim = You.... • Tertiary Victim = The Medical Student / Tech / Trainee you blame it on Second Victim is a concept that refers to buyers remorse / PTSD that you get after you’re really hurt someone. This vs T h at - M istake vs Slip The difference occurs at the “level of intention” - You make the decision to act - you are driving in an unfamiliar city and you come upon an intersection. You must make a choice (turn right or turn left) - if you turn the wrong way that is a “mistake.” If you decided to turn right, but for some reason turned left instead this is a “slip” - your intention was correct... you just fucked up. M istake = Error that occurs during active problem solving. You choose to turn right, but should have turned left M istake = In a just culture you get re-trained *more on this later Slip = Error that occurs during automatic cognitive task. You choose to turn right, but your body turned left instead. Classic example - calling your wife your girlfriends name while in the throes of passionate lovemaking. Slip = In a just culture you get consoled. *more on this later Unfortunately wives are not just in these particular setting - you will likely be punished (which is only appropriate for reckless behavior). Slips can NOT be prevented with additional training, but can be prevented with “forcing functions.” If you bite your lip, you won’t call out anyones name and this won’t happen. Or... I guess you could not commit adultery. 375

Skill-R ule-K now ledge C lassification Regarding Hum an Error This system describes a thing called “cognitive mode.” It’s sorta of a spectrum: Knowledge ► Rules ► Skill “Skill” is the guy who ties your shoes for you. If you have ever attempted to teach someone how to tie their shoes you will realize how complicated it is. Lots of little steps - making a loop, pinch, hold, pull, and its gotta be just right. When you were a little kid this took massive concentration to do. Now you totally take it for granted and you can tie your shoes without even looking down while having a conversation about something else. It’s almost like someone lives inside of you, and just takes over with little tasks like this. It’s the same guy who drives you to work in the morning when you are tired and not paying attention. Where does that guy come from??? Lots of reps. After you tied your shoe 10,000 times you started being able to do it without paying attention. It’s on auto pilot. Practice doesn’t make perfect, it makes “permanent.” This is the “skill” cognitive mode. ** Skill-Related Errors are Slips / Lapses - You call your one kid the other kids name. Or much worse you call your wife your girlfriends name... oops. In Radiology a good example would be a right/left error. “Knowledge” This is the opposite side of the spectrum. This is when you first learned to tie your shoe. You know the moves but you have to really think about it. Every step is like you are performing brain surgery and the slightest distraction can result in a catastrophic failure and the need to re-start the knot. *** Knowledge-Related Errors are Mistakes - This is when you nwagu>u) c-a^ be confusing. -c i u j n i 1 i ii i 11 j • Bspedaiiu If mow. art net luck up because you don t know what the hell you are doing. \ r j ' j familiar with the an.atomy. Usually preceded by the phrase “how hard can it be?” „ „ fi.i'uai.i'vgs, or pathology. “Rules”- This is somewhere in the middle. You operate with formulas for decision making (sorta like the ER). If X then Y. Patient has “Trauma” , Patient gets “Pan Scanned. ” The ■presence of a human. body Is an. in.dic.ation. for the generation, of a confuted tomogram thereof. 376

Blam ing it on th e Train ee Must Read For Anyone Considering a Career in Academics You’ve made a terrible mistake and you need someone to blame it on. If you are an Attending, the answer is obvious - blame your “Trainee.” If you are a Resident - 1 would try and pin it on a Medical Student or Nurse / Tech. You might be able to blame the Attending if he/she is new to the department or not well liked by his/her peers. That’s more tricky and you could experience what Ron Paul referred to as “blow back.” In other words, they may crush you. What if you feel compelled (the hospital lawyer / risk management guy is making you) to confess your mistake to the patient or family? You are supposed to handle it in a specific way— “the sacrament o f confession (1) Break it down in a wav they can understand. Usually when a patient asks me a question I use the most complicated medical jargon / anatomy possible to alpha dog them / make them feel stupid and hopefully discourage them from wasting my time with another question. But in this situation you are supposed to break it down. a. Example - “Remember when I said I would try and biopsy the liver ? I missed and biopsied the blood vessel that brings the liver blood. Without that blood your transplanted liver got very thirsty and sick.” “Biliary Necrosis” is the fancy doctor word. b. Example - “Remember when I said I would try and drain that infection in your belly with a tube? I accidentally thought your bladder was the infection and put the tube in it instead.” “Iatrogenic Bladder Rupture” is the fancy doctor word. (2) Take responsibility. The whole point of this is to convince the patient not to sue you. To achieve that goal you want to seem honorable / likable. So this is the rare exception where you want to actually own up and take responsibility. Now when you have your departmental QA meeting... that is when you want to throw the Trainee directly under the bus. It’s best to not give them any warning. Just say - “it would be best - for your education - if you came to the QA meeting. Please sit in the front row where everyone can see you.” (3) Apologize-Again I’d never advise this under normal circumstances, but you are trying to not get sued. (4) Explain what will be done to prevent similar errors. American culture is all about getting famous. For most; fame is even more desirable than getting rich. To take advantage of this cultural psychopathology you can tell the patient that they have sparked an entire policy / rule and that they will go down in the annals of medicine as a famous teaching / learning case. They may even get a painting in the department. There is no greater honor in academic medicine than having a painting of you, with a smug look on your face, hanging in some random room or hallway. 377

“G etting Sued” B-ehlkvet every image is a hw.kw.nkv belkwj and behLkvd that hum an beikv0 Is a lawyer Since you made the terrible mistake of being a doctor, your punishment is to spend your career worrying about getting sued and eventually getting sued. This can only be prevented by (1) working at a VA, or (2) quitting medicine and going to law school (so you can do the suing). What gets you sued? * Errors in diagnosis are the most common cause of malpractice suits against Radiologists * Imaging findings related to breast cancer are the most common subject of malpractice suits against Radiologists * Communication Errors (with referring M.D., or failure to recommend another test) is actually a much less frequent cause of litigation - when compared to interpretive error. Whang, Jeremy S., et al. "The causes of medical malpractice suits against radiologists in the United States. "Radiology 266.2 (2013): 548-554. What must be proven? All 5 Elements must be proven for a successful medical malpractice case: 1. A duty was owed: You owe that duty once you read that case, or do that procedure. 2. A duty was breached: You didn’t follow the standard of care. 3. The breach caused an injury: Your failure to follow the standard of care is what hurt the patient. 4. Deviation from the accepted standard: You didn’t follow the standard of care in your specialty (this is similar to #2). 5. Damage: The patient must have gotten hurt (physical or emotional). If they didn’t get hurt, it doesn’t matter if you were negligent or not. Basically, it was your case, you missed the finding or didn’t communicate a critical finding (or other violation of the standard of care), and the patient got hurt. If they didn’t get hurt, then you are ok. If they got hurt but you followed the standard of care you are ok. - If you drive an expensive car, you will also be more susceptible to character assassination, which will aid the prosecution. 378

Error Prevention (Cover yow.r A s s Hide) Human Factors Engineering: people are oblivious to their Ii/vaoteejuacles. More commonly referred to as “ergonomics.” Basically taking the human into consideration when you are designing a piece of equipment. In the medical field this is more about the design of a system built around known human weakness. Examples: People fuck up when they are tired = 80-hour work week People forget shit - so we use a check list. People have egos and want to be rich and famous so they write a book trying to take credit for the invention of the check list - and title it with the pretentious word “manifesto.” Human Reliability Curve: This is a human reliability curve. The idea is that with improvement in “system factors” that human performance increases - but never achieves 100% PoiVt feel bad, oiA-ty I am*, see it Resource Allocation: More resources = more reliability. You pay the doctor more than the janitor, because the doctor’s job poses more safety risks... Well, sometimes you pay him more. If he is a resident physician you pay him less - but use guilt based strategy “I thought you went into medicine to help people?” to get him/her to accept less money. But - you still in general put more into resources for important things - to try and achieve near 100% reliability, and less resources into things where safety is less of a concern. Trivia = You can’t achieve 100% reliability if your system contains humans “It can only be attributable to human error - HAL 9000, 2001 a space odyssey 100% 379

Culture of Safety I doiA-'t give a flyiivg fuck, about the cystic artery, the gallbladder is the cockroach of the abdorue Basically - to create an environment that emphasizes safety you need to first (1) recognize that complications occur. If you aren’t having complications then you are not doing enough procedures. To reduce the number of complications you want to create an environment where (2) people can confess their sins without punishment so that mistakes don’t get repeated. You want the “Trainees” / Techs to not be afraid of you (3) so they can tell you if a mistake is going to happen. You have to be willing to (4) commit resources - usually time into developing safety protocols - checklists, QA meetings, etc.. Trivia: The “AHRO Patient Safety Culture Survey” is the tool used to measure the culture of safety at a given institution. Trivia: This AHRQ survey is brought to you by the bureaucracy of the Dept of Health and Human Services. Steep Authority Gradient Fear and punishment are the two primary tools available to an Attending in academic medicine. They keep those no good, lazy, Trainees in check while the courageous Attendings take naps in their offices and get coffee. However, as amazing as it may sound, this type of culture actually contributes to more errors. The reason is the Trainee is terrified of getting fired / humiliated publicly , and/or being given more call. So he/she covers up mistakes. If mistakes aren’t out in the open - you are doomed to repeat them. Bottom line: Steep Authority Gradient = More Coffee Breaks / Mid-day naps for Attendings Steep Authority Gradient = More Errors and Less “Culture of Safety” Trivia: Communicating in an authority gradient requires an “SBAR” style to avoid immediate death and destruction. S - Situation • B - Background A - Assessment R - Recommendation The way to think about SBAR - is like the old oral boards style of case taking. Describe the findings (situation and background), make a diagnosis (assessment), and if necessary give a recommendation. 380

This is an engineering concept that keeps something bad from happening. The protection is built into the system. You can’t fuck up even if you wanted to. • Example - You can’t put the car in reverse accidentally while driving 80 on the interstate. • Example - You can’t prescribe 1000 grams of morphine - it’s not an option on the EMR button thing. Forcing Function 381

NIS SECTION 1 PATIENT SAFETY GOALS ° PI&ONIiTIHIIKmS y®[MAimrff MM* - Because yo u ’ve worked hard, went to school fo r 8+ years presumably making near-perfect grades, and completed the most competitive graduate training in the country you have been rewarded with J00K+ debt and a lower middle class salary. As a result, some o f you may have considered taking a part-time job on the side to make ends meet. Just in case the only job you can find that will work around your call schedule is hammering rail road spikes for a company that uses equipment from the 1800s (which has been known to malfunction andfire spikes through the frontal lobe o f their operators - Phineas Gage style), your executive function may become impaired leading you to impulsively quit being a Radiologist and go back to school to become a nurse. Don ’t worry, you have prepared fo r this scenario as part o f your modern radiology non­ interpretation skills training. That s right - National Patient Safety Goals including how to reconcile meds, how to transfuse blood, how to label medications on the tray prior to the procedure, etc... are all included on the official NIS study guide - and are therefore fair game. Who issues these patient safety goals ? The Joint Commission. They come out every year. Blood Transfusion- How do you identify the correct person? You have to use two separate indicators. You can’t just ask him his name because maybe he’s Vietnamese and like all Vietnamese people his name is Nguyen (seriously it’s like 40%). You gotta go Name + Birthday, or Name + Med Record Number (or hospital ID number). Trivia - what can NOT be used as identifying factor? - Basically anything that changes - classic examples are (a) Patient’s Location, or (b) Patient’s Room Number As a nurse, what is the verification process for giving blood? (1) Check to make sure the blood is blood... and not platelets or something (2) Match the Patient to the Blood. The blood bank should label them - it’s a compatibility thing. Remember 2 Identifiers for the patient... in case they are a Nguyen. (3) Two nurses should be involved in the verification.... In case both the nurses are also named Nguyen. 382

What gets Labeled? The rule on this is - before the procedure starts you are supposed to label medicines, and I mean ALL the meds that are not labeled. This includes medicines in syringes, cups, and basins. If you know what’s good for you, you better label those fucking basins. What if there is only one medication being used? You still have to label it. Is there any exception to the rule? Yup - In the 2014 recommendations they say label stuff “not immediately administered” - implying things that are immediately administered don’t need to be labeled. They even go as far as defining this exception very clearly “An immediately administered medication is one that an authorized staff member prepares or obtains, takes directly to a patient, and administers to that patient without any break in the process”. Where are you supposed to do the labeling? In the area where medicines and supplies are set up. What do you have to write on the labels? You better write small - because they pretty much want everything on there. Medication name, Strength, Quantity, Diluent and volume (if not apparent from the container), Expiration date when not used within 24 hours, Expiration time when expiration occurs in less than 24 hours. Exceptions to the label-writing rules? You don’t have to put expiration date and time - if you are doing a short procedure. What if the person doing the preparing / labeling is not the person doing the administering? Then verification (both verbal and visual) is done by two people qualified to participate in the procedure (the janitor doesn’t count). M edication Labeling 383

Once upon a time I was an intern - and trying to figure out what medications patients were on during an admission H&P made me want to stick my head in a dirty toilet (just to experience something less terrible). “I take a pink pill, and a blue pill ” Medication reconciliation is awful and it’s one of the reasons people who aren’t interested in dedicating their lives to poop, pus, and note writing choose radiology over internal medicine, to avoid these protracted horrible conversations. The idea is - these people are on tons of meds, of which they may or may not even be taking. You are supposed to compare the meds they should be on (and actually using) to new medications that are ordered and resolve any discrepancies. Testable Point - If the patient is unwilling or unable to provide you an accurate list, a “good faith” effort is enough to meet the guideline requirement. Testable Point - You are supposed to provide the patient with written information on the medications they should be taking when they are discharged. Testable Point - Medication Reconciliation is supposed to be done at (a) Admission, (b) Transfer, and (c) Discharge. Central Venous Access Trivia: The Groin is dirty... usually. Mine is very clean. But most people have dirty ones given the proximity of the groin to some of the dirtier parts of the body. It should be used last. IJ and Subclavian first. Trivia: You are supposed to have a standard cart of supplies. Trivia: You are supposed to use a sterile barrier during insertion, hub cleaning, and injection of ports. Trivia: If they don’t need the catheter - remove it. M edication Reconciling 384

Wrong Site If you amputate the wrong leg people are going to make a huge deal about it. I get it, mistakes happen. “To Err is Human ” as the Institute of Medicine would say... but for some reason wrong site procedures are unforgivable. Trivia: Any procedure where the patient can’t say “Hey! I thought the osteosarcoma was in my other leg?” makes it high risk. In other words big risk = general anesthesia or deep sedation. Trivia: Pre-procedure verification can (a) be done more than once - if that makes you happy, (b) has to be before the procedure - with the actual time dictated by the hospital, (c) it’s optimal to involve the patient. “The cancer is in your left leg right?” - “Right” - “Right or Left?” - “Left” Trivia: Mark the site of the procedure. Especially if it’s possible to do it on the wrong side. Example = you have one nose, might not need to mark that... but you have two ears... gotta mark those. Trivia: For the spine you need to do a fluoro spot film (to be interpreted by the Department of Radiology a week later) for locating and marking the exact vertebral level. Trivia: Gotta mark the site before the surgery starts. Can’t mark it after you amputate the wrong leg. Also, by the book you want the patient awake when you mark the right (left) site. Trivia: You gotta mark with something that won’t just fall off or wash off during the prep and draping. In other words - you can’t use a sticky note (or other adhesive marker). Trivia: What if the patient’s religion forbids them from being marked (or wearing cotton and wool at the same time) ? You’ll need to use a written alternative process. Trivia: Other examples of when you need a written site mark alternative? - Teeth, Mucosal Surfaces, or Premature infants (the mark could cause a permanent tattoo). Trivia: Best time to get a permanent tattoo? When you are a premature infant. Trivia: The Time Out - What if you are doing two procedures on the same patient? You need to perform the time out before each procedure (you can’t do them both in a row at the beginning). Trivia: The Time Out - You need to agree on (a) Correct Patient, (b) Correct Site, and (C) Correct Procedure. Also , you have to document the time out happened. Trivia: I f the patient is demented (or smells bad) and you can’t (or don’t want to) ask him/her for identifying factors - who else can you obtain this information from? Basically anyone including relatives, guardians, homosexual domestic partners, and even healthcare providers who “know the patient” 385

Patient Specific Assessment - This is a fancy way of saying a “pre-procedure note.” This is required before any kind of image guided procedure, and involves review of the H&P, sedation risk (if they are getting sedation), and review of labs (H&H, coags, etc...). I know... it sounds horrible. The good news is you (the Radiologist) don’t have to be the one to do it. A “Qualified Assistant” i.e. Nurse Practitioner or PA can do it for you. This is why having one of those is 100% essential if you do procedures - otherwise you will be stuck spending part of your day talking to icky smelly patients. In academics you can just make your Resident do it. Trivia - Who can perform it (3) : • Radiologist Performing the Procedure, your “assistant” - NP/PA, • or even the referring Provider - even better! Informed Consent: Who can obtain it? - You (the Physician) or if you are smart you can have a “qualified assistant” - in other words a Resident (in academics) or a PA/NP in private practice. Who can give it ? Patient or a legal representative - they have to be the one listed as the go-to in the chart. Ultimate Responsibility? Unfortunately it’s you (the Physician performing the procedure) - if they have additional questions, etc... you are supposed to answer them. Components of the Consent: • What the procedure involves ? - We are going to put a tube through your grandmother’s skin into her stomach so she can eat. • What is the benefit ? Although there is probably no longer term survival benefit given her advanced Alzheimer’s and other co-morbidities - BUT this will really decrease the guilt you have about not visiting her in the nursing home. Now you can tell yourself you “helped her.” • What are the risks? It will periodically get clogged and they will transfer her to the ED in the middle of the night to unclog it/ give her IV fluids. They will probably call you and wake you up in the middle of the night to come in and give consent for her etc... • Reasonable Alternatives ? Let her die in dignity with hospice. • Risks of Refusing ? Your lack of understanding of medicine coupled with American culture will make you feel very guilty for not “helping her”, and you may get accused of “killing grandma” by one of your drunken relatives at the next family barbecue. 386

When is consent not valid ? (1) Patient has dementia or delirium (can’t understand the procedure, risks/benefits etc). (2) Is a minor (and not pregnant) What to do? Gotta get consent from an appointed representative What about in emergencies? You can treat without consent to prevent serious disability or death / or to alleviate great pain or suffering. Universal Protocol Process: public Health is code for "wash your fucteiuq (1) Pre-Procedure Verification hawds " (2) Mark the Site (3) Time Out This is your checklist (manifest) to make sure you don’t amputate the wrong leg. Site Marking Trivia: Person doing the procedure should do the marking (limited circumstances for allowing the NP/PA to do it) - It must be visible AFTER draping / prepping - in other words - near the site of the actual procedure. Tim e Out Trivia: - Should occur - IMMEDIATELY PRIOR to the procedure Everyone in the room should agree (including anesthesia, nurses, etc...) Multiple Procedures = Multiple Time Outs , prior to each procedure (you can’t do them all at the beginning) Special Situations: You do NOT have to wait on consent in an emergency. You can provide care with no consent to prevent death, serious disability, or prevent great pain. Emergency 387

NIS SECTION 4 THE MYTHICAL "JUST CULTURE" - PMMiYHiUS LD@HHARYff M M - In my experience as a Resident / Medical Student I never encountered what is described as a “just culture.” I’m not sure it actually exists, although a description is present. Obviously, a description of something doesn’t mean it’s real. Here is a description and drawing of the Chubacabra; reptile­ like creature known to drink the blood of goats. It has a leathery or scaly greenish-gray skin with sharp quills running down its back. It is said to be approximately 4 feet high, and hops in a fashion similar to a kangaroo. Chupacabra (Goat Sucker) seen with the same frequency as a “just culture” in academic medicine. There is a similar amount of evidence for their existence. The idea of a just culture is that behavior operates on a continuum of Good to Bad. That certain behaviors should be punished while others should be handled with a “hug” and an “”It will be ok bud”. Good Boy Bad Boy Human Error Risk Behavior Reckless Behavior 388

Human Error (Slip) - Console — In a “just culture” you would not blame people for making human errors. You can’t be perfect all the time. Sometimes you just don’t see that fucking nodule, or you thought the ureteral stone was a phlebolith. In reality, we all know what would happen - the Trainee isn’t meeting his/her milestones and requires additional training (more call). People Feel Bad when they make mistakes = A just person would tell them it’s ok. “Console them”. A just culture would never punish someone for trying to do the right thing. At Risk Behavior (Short Cut Taking) - In a “just culture” you would almost expect humans to try and take shortcuts - especially when the non-MD hospital administrator is telling them their pay depends on how fast they get the allergy band on, through put in the ED, turn-around time for ED studies etc... People are going to try and cut corners. People will try and take short cuts to meet the unreasonable expectations they are given = A just person will “coach them” or “counsel them” that you aren’t supposed to do that... with a wink and a nod. I cannot approve of the shortcuts you are taking... everyone heard me say that right? Classic example = “Driving 5 mph above the speed limit” Reckless Behavior (Ignoring Critical Safety Steps) - Not following the checklist “manifesto” ??? That shit is unforgivable. Don’t you know that Harvard invented the checklist? There were never checklists before that book - it’s truly a revolutionary idea. “5 thousand” years old - this planet is and only in the last 10 did a human finally become smart enough to invent a checklist. Have you read this book? This guy is using a checklist! You can’t ignore the checklist. Ignore The Checklist? Even in a just culture = Your ass is fried chicken. Classic example = “Driving 30 mph above the speed limit in a school zone” shit where you. eat, you. will get parasitic womcs.
