NIS SECTION 5 COMMUNICATION STANDARDS ° PMMIITIffliUS IM M URT, MM* - Communication Practice Standards: **Disclaimer - the following tirade Reflects my (the author’s) understanding of the practice parameter. **Disclaimer - my opinion on this document does in no way represents those of the ACR, or ABR. They are my own opinions. Do communication standard exists? - Yes. There is a document written by the ACR referred to as “ACR Practice Guideline for Communication of Diagnostic Imaging Findings.” Is this document helpful ? In my opinion it is 10% helpful, and 90% very very very harmful. When I first read this document it completely blew my fucking mind. It appears to place you (the Radiologist) at significant litigation risk for every single case you read, if you don’t call the provider and read the entire report to them (then demand a re-back). Although, the document explicitly claims this is not its intention. Prometheus those are strong words - why do you feel like this ? I’m deeply concerned about the section on “non-routine communication” specifically part iii. Here is a brief summary of this section: Section 2 “Non Routine Communications” ** this is from the 2014 revision. i — Findings that suggest a needfor immediate or urgent intervention (i.e. Pneumothorax) — ok, totally reasonable. ii - Findings that are discrepant with a preceding interpretation of the same examination and where failure to act may adversely affect patient health - ok , also totally reasonable. iii - Findings that the interpreting physician reasonably believes may be seriously adverse to the patient s health and may not require immediate attention but, if not acted on, may worsen over time and possibly result in an adverse patient outcome - This is where they destroy you. They define these findings as “acute infectious processes, possible malignant lesions, or other unexpected findings that may impact patient care if not treated. ” In other words... anything. Literally any positive scan. Pulmonary nodule? Well that could turn into cancer - gotta call on it. Possible pneumonia vs atelectasis ... gotta call on it. Renal hypodensity that is 16 H.U.s probably a cyst but recommending an ultrasound just to cover my ass... gotta call on it. So if you don’t work in academics and have residents to call on all these findings you literally cannot get any work done. It’s incredibly to me that this was put into writing. Absolutely incredible. 391

But you can just send them an email right??? They specifically say in this document that you have to confirm receipt. How do you know they read that email?? Could have gone into the junk folder. Plus there are HIPAA concerns about sending emails. They strongly encourage telephone or in person communication. Are they not merciful by letting you do it on the telephone? I assume to make it even more impossible to follow that for the next edition only in person communication will be ok. You need to walk down to their clinic and tell them about the 3 mm nodule in person. Is the document actually legally binding? NO Well, they say it’s not. They state that this document is not intended to set a national standard of care, and that the Iowa Supreme Court ruled on this. Unfortunately, the wording is such that in my opinion you are truly at the mercy of the court. Plus they throw this line in there: “a practitioner who employs an approach substantially different from the guidance in this document is advised to document in the patient record information sufficient to explain the approach taken. ” Per the document who is the ultimate judgement regarding the course o f action up to? It is up to the Practitioner (Radiologist) - who takes in all the practical circumstances. Then why all the hate Prometheus? I’m just worried about the following situation. Remember regardless of what it said - this is still an official guideline from our main national body: Lawyer: Why didn’t you communicate the finding of a 1 cm indeterminate renal cyst ? Radiologist: I did Lawyer: Really? Where is it documented ? Radiologist: It’s impression line 3 - and I gave a specific follow-up recommendation Lawyer: But did you call? Radiologist: N o... I assumed the ordering provider would read the report for the multi-thousand dollar exam they ordered. Lawyer: You assumed??? ACR - your professional leadership “suggests” that you call on findings that could potentially harm the patient at some point in the future. Radiologist: That guideline is not legally binding. Ultimately the judgement to communicate is up to the practitioner. Lawyer: So you exercised poor judgement - since 10 years later this lesion turned out to be a very slow-growing malignancy. Did you document in the chart the reason you didn’t communicate this finding- as suggested in the document, since you decided to deviate from their suggested course of action? Radiologist: I surrender.. .(begins to cry) take all my money. I’ll sleep under the bridge Lawyer: Excellent - My secretary will tell you who to make the check out to. 392

Have your group meet with medicine and surgery and come up with a standardized communication protocol. We will call you on these 15 things. These other things we will send an email or text or something. All other minor things, will be in the report. Then you document this meeting happen. Now you have established an “institutional standard of care.” Practical Solution (this is what I did with my private practice group) : Ok do you have any other beefs with the paper? Yup. The paper specifies that the person generating a radiology report should be “a physician.” Not a radiologist.... But a physician. Neurologist, Cardiologists, ER, Ortho etc.. please go right ahead and read the imaging. In fact, we will train you to read it. Are they not merciful? They do at least take a stand against nurses generating reports? Yes... real helpful. The financial security of the specialty is now safe. Is there anything in the paper that you agree with? I like the stance on “informal reads.” The so called - “curb side” read, or “can you look at this real quick?”, “just a quick wet read - can you stage this cholangiocarcinoma real fast for me?” The ACR doesn’t like this, says it should be avoided and if you have to do it you should document it. I almost completely agree. (1) I don’t work for free. You want me to give an opinion on that study? Put in an order for official interpretation and I’ll read it. (2) There is no greater way to fuck up then by looking at a study quickly with some medicine doctor over your shoulder asking you to point out basic anatomy. Yes... that is the liver. You will break your search pattern, and cause confusion. Try to avoid this. I f you are an IR doc, and you order a study then read it (it’s a self-referred study), who should you notify when you have a critical result? The patient. Yes... you actually have to talk to the patient. I know, its horrible. My advice? Get an NP or PA to make the call for you, and document. I f you are reading a study referred by some random 3rd party (Lawyer, Insurance Company, Employer, etc...) and you have a critical result, who do you notify? Again you might have to actually talk to the patient. My advice? If you encounter this situation, delete your report and put the study on your partner’s desk. you. cawwot in itia te eye cowtact with the cllku-cXaws or their lurk, w ill latch owto you.. if we dow t coiwplaiiA. about th iw gs they dovct tewow that we're reading studies. 393

NIS SECTION CHARTS & GRAPHS Flow C harts This is a type of chart that uses symbols chained together to illustrate a process. The standardized shapes for the major symbols are below. Realize medicine is "not as advertised” NO Squeeze ■ Your Butt Cheeks B Together Mi Processes mapped with flow charts can be high level or low level. High Level • Abstract Description • “I got up, and I went to work” Low Level • Detail focused / micro functions • “I got up at 5:58am, when my alarm woke me. I used my left hand to turn the alarm off, turning the alarm button 90 degrees in a clockwise direction from the on position to the off position ” 394

Fishbone D iagram (Cause and E ffect) This type of chart is used when multiple causes contribute to a problem. The process is supposed to allow you to “drill down” to what the real cause by asking “why?” Sometimes the method is called a “5 why?” method. Causes r f’roblerri J L Effect Spineless Leaders Giving Away Procedures Decline of Radiology Pointless Internship Brilliant ideas like “Starting a Clinic” 395

P areto C harts This is a chart named after Vifredo Pareto, who accomplished many great things in his life including popularizing the term “elite” , marrying a women 30 years his junior (WIN!), and collecting a large number of cats - which he famously treated better than his house guests (he fed the cats first, and made his dinner guests wait). Oh, he also made a chart. The chart is essentially a prioritization exercise. I got 99 problems, which l(s) is the major contributor. You take causes to a problem, and rank them in the order that they contribute to that problem. The problems sum together to 100% of the problem (bars are ranked in descending order, creating a concave function). The point is to show how 2 or 3 things can cause 80% of the issue. The entire purpose of the chart is to highlight the most important factor among a set of factors. The idea is that fixing the main cause may fix the problem. Example: Problem: Poor Resident Moral 350 -...... ...... — 100% 250 Frequency of Event Cumulative Percentage Of Event Being No Time Extra Treated Off To Call as Like Study R4 Diseased for Cattle Exam, with High Failure Rate Debt 0% 396

S h ew h art C harts (Control C hart) This is a “process-behavior” type of chart. This is best explained by thinking about the chart as a tool to analyze turnaround time for ED studies. Yes there is variation. Some residents are faster than others. Some cases are more complicated than others. The time of the day matters (I’m sleepy right after lunch). So that type of variation is expected - you would call this “noise”. Investigation of this would be a huge waste of time. Spikes above or below this expected variation (above your upper This allows you to tell the , N . . signal from the noise, control limit, or lower control limit). These are considered special. It’s the WTF happen there? Why did it take 6 hours to Signal = Special Cause of turn around that ED study? That should be investigated. Maybe Variation the reading room coordinator put the study on the wrong list and Noise Common Cause didn’t notice because something really interesting was going on Variation with her Facebook feed. Upper and lower “controls” are chosen to distinguish between normal variation (noise), and special or unusually variation (signal). Staying between the controls demonstrates a stable process. 397

R eceiver O perator Curve - ROC This is a graph to describe how good your test is. The perfect (accurate) test will have an area under the curve of 1.0. The worst piece of shit test will have an area under the curve of 0.5 (50% chance, i.e no better than a coin flip) The graph shows the tradeoff between sensitivity and specificity. Questions You Can Ask About ROC Curves: (1)What are the axes of the graph ? Sensitivity, and 1-Specificity (2) What kind of a line would have no predictive value ? The straight one as above (3) What kind of a line would occur from random chance? Same question, just different wording - still the straight one as above. (4) Where is the “Ideal” or “Perfect” or “Gold Standard” test? Top left comer. (5) When the accuracy improves how does the curve change? Shifts towards the upper left comer. 398

NIS SECTION 7 RANDOM STUFF im This vs That Validity vs Reliability - Validity = Does it do what it is supposed to do? Does the tool measure what it’s supposed to measure? Does the simulation device actually prepare you for a real case / accurate mimic a real patient? Reliability = The degree to which the tool produces consistent / stable results. Example = you step on the bathroom scale and it says you are fat. You get off and step back on 10 seconds later and is says “Still fat.” This is reliable. Tools can be reliable but not valid and vice versa. Validity Sub-Type Trivia (using the example of a sim-lab) Face validity = Does the doll have a human-looking face? Or does the virtual colon thing look like the real colon on endoscopy? Constmct Validity = Can it determine if you suck or not? Which should be the overall point of simulation. In other words - does the test do what it says it does ? There are two subtypes of Construct Validity: Discriminant Validity = Does the simulation test have the ability to discriminate between things that are not supposed to be related. Classic example - a test that measures narcissism (a disorder that plagues the high ranks of academic medicine) is not simply measuring self-esteem. Convergent Validity - Does the simulation test have the ability to determine if two measurements of things that should be related - are actually related (the opposite of discriminant). Classic example - a test that measures self-esteem can show that measures of similar constmcts, such as, confidence and self-worth, are also related. Content Validity = Does it teach you what you need to know? Concurrent Validity = Does the simulation test correlate with other similar performance tests ? Predictive Validity = Does success in the sim lab = success in the real world ? 399

Image Gently Formed as an attempt to capitulate to the corporate media driven frenzy of radiation-induced cancer. It’s basically a social marketing campaign, with the end result being increased liability risk from having to try and interpret shitty low dose scans all the time. It started out as a Peds thing - which is actually reasonable in some cases. Then QA officers (who’s job description is “trouble maker”) decided to start applying low dose protocols to 95 year old patients because saying you “reduced dose” sounds great on paper - especially if you aren’t the one who has to try and read (and accept liability risk) for the shitty exam. Trivia = There were 3 phases of propaganda in this social marketing campaign Phase 1 = Convince the Radiologists it’s a good idea (easy since most don’t understand physics, and those who do are huge pussies and will do whatever they are told without questioning) Phase 2 = Convince referring Physicians it’s a good idea. There may actually be a secondary gain to this - in that Family Medicine doctors who might have been afraid to order a CT on a 90 year old because of “radiation” could feel more comfortable knowing it’s “low dose.” • Phase 3 = Convince the public that it’s a good idea. “Radiation hormesis” — it’s a real thing. Google it. If you can convince people that colonics are good for them with late night infomercials than you can certainly sell them on needing a CT once or twice a year to help stimulate and activate repair mechanisms. Trivia = “Step Lightly” = what you call it for Pediatric IR Trivia = “Image Wisely” = what you call it for Adults. Radiation Risk Discussing with Patients - Obviously you want to avoid talking to patients at all costs. But sometimes you just can’t avoid it. Here are some tips on discussing Radiation dose Risk vs Benefit- What are the risks of not doing the exam? I understand that you are concerned about developing a malignancy from the radiation exposure of your PET CT Scan but you already have widely metastatic pancreatic cancer. Without the test your “Cancer Doctors” can’t tell if the chemotherapy is working or not. Oh... you aren’t taking chemo - you refused that too? You are using “homeopathic juice therapy” to focus your natural immune system.... Comparison to other exposures - Hours in an airplane or number of chest x-rays What’s my risk of harm from this single exam? Risk models are developed based on populations. There is no way to assess a specific individuals risk from a scan. 400

Biologic Effects of Ionizing Radiation (BEIR) Committee - Also known as BEIR 7 Report What were the sources o f data used? Atomic Bomb Survivor data, medical radiation studies, occupational radiation studies, and environmental radiation studies. The primary source was the bomb data. What was the primary task o f this committee? To develop the best possible risk estimate for human exposure to low-dose (<100 mSv), low-LET ionizing radiation. What did the BEIR 7 Report conclude? That there is a thing called a linear, no-threshold dose response between radiation and the development of cancer in humans. More specifically, what does BEIR 7 accuse medical imaging of? Giving everyone cancer. BEIR 7 predicts that 1 in 100 will get a solid cancer or leukemia from a dose of lOOmSv above background. Is BEIR 7 legit? N o... it’s probably a load of monkey shit. If you look at how they get their data, they use a risk model that is “x” times excess relative risk + (l-“x”) times the excess absolute risk. In this equation, “x” is determined by committee! In other words... they just made it up. Basically, all estimates are based on multiple models and assumption with confidence intervals that are subjective and partly based on opinion. Cancer estimates from BEIR 7 are not proven facts.... Having said all that, the test will probably want you to treat them as such. Who knows... just say linear no-threshold based on bomb data. 401

Appropriateness Trivia - Related to Unnecessary Exam /Dose Reduction “Choosing Wisely ” • Uncomplicated Headache = Should NOT Get CT “Low Probability for PE” (negative D-Dimer) = Should NOT Get CTPA No significant medical history and younger than 70 = Do NOT need pre-op CXR • Suspected Rotten Appendix in Kid = NO CT *** Until you’ve “considered” ultrasound first (note the word “considered” - you don’t actually have to do it) • Simple Adnexal Cyst < 5 cm (or 1 cm in Postmenopausal) = NO Follow up needed — w hat was ovo the fhijs.lc.ai exam.? you. \zwow, the doctor thivug. ACR Dose Index Registry (DIR) The two reported parameters (CTDI Vol and DLP) are sent from participating institutions to a facility that uses the data to track performance and adjust protocols. Trivia - CT Reference Values (“helping you know if you are in the ballpark”) • CT Head = 75 mGy CT Abd (adult) = 25 mGy CT Abd (peds) = 20 mGy ACR Appropriateness Criteria In an attempt to get promoted, various academic Radiologists got together in 1993 and formed committees to decide what tests are appropriate. The idea was to help ordering providers get the right test. Testable trivia: •Appropriateness Rating •Rating 1-3 = Usually NOT appropriate •Rating 4-6 = Might be appropriate •Rating 7-9 = Usually appropriate •There is also a 1-6 Relative Radiation Score for each test. 402

G etting Paid The only reason to go to medical school and endure residency / fellowship is the chance to make enough money to buy a Porsche and/or Boat. This is still not guaranteed, as hospital administrators make more and more and doctors make less and less every year. Step 1: Getting Credentialed Testable Trivia revolves around two processes: (1) Focused Professional Practice Evaluation (FPPE) - This is basically current medical staff checking your work (proctoring) you for the first few studies you read or a committee evaluating if you can do a new procedure or not. (2) Ongoing Professional Practice Evaluations (OPPE) - This is a continued review of your performance - typically q6 months, that is used for reappointment. Your department determines the scope of the review. Step 2: Getting A Paycheck Testable Trivia Regarding Payment: Resource Based Relative Value System (RBRVS) - This is a system based on some Harvard study , that said doctors should get paid based on the time it took to do something and how difficult it was to do it. RBRVSs are adjusted based on geography with a “Geographic Practice Cost Indicator”, so that you make more in LA than you do in rural South Dakota or New Mexico. Omnibus Budget Reconciliation Act of 1989 (OBRA 89) - This is the name of the Medicare payment reform law. Participating vs Non-Participating Physicians - Participating - If you agree to take what medicare gives you and not try and get the rest of the money owed to you by the patient. - Non- Participating - You refuse to sign the agreement, and can attempt to collect the rest of the money from the patient. The government tries to discourage this by only paying you 95% of what is paid to the participating physicians. 403

Sustainable Growth Rate System (SGR) - This was put in place in 1997 to control the growth of Medicare. It was based on domestic product per capita. This was totally flawed, and would have resulted in you making no money at all and being homeless eating out of the dumpster behind McDonalds. The only thing that kept you from enjoying two day old Big Macs is a “patch fix” congress did yearly (at the stroke of midnight). The AM A tried to repeal the SGR, but until 2015 lacked enough funds to “convince” members of congress to act on its behalf. On April 16, 2015, the Medicare Access and CHIP Reauthorization Act of 2015, was signed by Obama - ending the use of the SGR. Having said that - this occurred after the first CORE exam was written. So, it’s possible questions on this topic haven’t been updated to account for this change. Impossible to say... Two Lists of Diseases/Procedures Made by Two Different Bodies for Billing: 1. Current Procedural Terminology Codes (CPT) - The AMA editorial panel is actually in charge of these. They create a “uniform” description of the procedure. 2.Intemational Classification of Diseases (ICD) 9 - This is a list of diseases made by the World Health Organization (WHO). The “9th” version of this list was the most popular one. In October of 2015 - ICD 10 was bom, and the world has never been the same. Two Ways the Government Punishes you for Going to Medical School: 1. Physician Quality Reporting System (PQRS) — Medicare uses carrots (stick to come later) to force you to report “quality clinical data” on certain conditions. 2. Meaningful Use - Forcing you to have an electronic medical record, and then prove (by random button clicking) that you are using it. 404

Off Label • Using an approved drug / device for something other than its original intended use is called “off label” use. • The FDA is in charge of monitoring the safety of medical devices and drugs • The “Practice o f Medicine ” doctrine allows doctors to use things off label. • For regular clinical work, off label use does NOT have to be disclosed while obtaining informed consent • For research studies, off label use MUST be disclosed while obtaining consent If you arc using a medication / device off label, you can’t market the product as your own special product. C ontrast Extravasation: What is the typical outcome? Most of the time there is no significant sequelae. Is the rate o f injection a risk factor? N o... but, it can make the severity worse. What are the “severe” complications? - Compartment Syndrome - This is the most common, and related to mechanical compression. The primary risk factor is the volume of contrast extravasation, into a small compartment (hand, foot, or ankle). - Skin Ulceration / Necrosis - What is the treatment? There is no consensus - so you shouldn’t be asked. If you do get asked, I guess I’d say elevate the affected extremity above the heart. Hot vs Cold compress — no consensus on a difference. Off Label Example: Using Gadolinium for an Arthrogram. 405

CT C ontrast - R eactions and M anagem ent: Statistical Trivia - How Often Do Reactions Occur? Incidence of Any Contrast Reaction (mild or serious): 0.2-0.7 % * Incidence of Serious Contrast Reactions: 0.01-0.02 % Who gets contrast reactions? * Greatest risk is prior reaction (5x increase for reaction). Atopy (2-3x increased risk of a serious reaction) What is the idea behind premedicating? * The goal is to block histamine. This is typically done with a steroid + an antihistamine. C ontrast Reaction M anagem ent Routine Premedication Method 1: Prednisone: 50 mg by mouth at 13 hours, 7 hours, and 1 hour before contrast Diphenhydramine (Benadryl): 50 mg IV (or PO) 1 hour before contrast Method 2: . _ Methylprednisolone: 32 mg by mouth 12 hours and 2 hours before contrast Emergent Premedication Diphenhydramine (Benadryl): 50 mg IV (or PO) 1 hour before contrast Method 1: Methylprednisolone 40 mg or hydrocortisone sodium succinate 200 mg IV Q4 until contrast study Diphenhydramine (Benadryl) 50 mg IV 1 hour prior to contrast injection Method 2: * For Allergy to NSAIDS, or history of Asthma Dexamethasone sodium 7.5 mg or betamethasone 6.0 mg IV Q4 until contrast study. Diphenhydramine (Benadryl) 50 mg IV 1 hour prior to contrast injection Method 3: * For People who can't / shouldn’t get steroids - infected, or at risk for bowel perforation Diphenhydramine 50 mg IV 1 hour prior to contrast injection. : A y ! 406

Why is the concentration for IV Epi different than SubQ ? The way to think about this is that you need a large volume of fluid for IV - because you are going to push it around. Alternatively, you wouldn’t want a large volume of fluid under the skin. The dose is actually the same (0.1 - 0.3 mg) it’s just the dilution volume that is different. VV:"' 1 ' ■ SC or 1M: 1:1,000, 0.1 to 0.3 ml (0.1 to 0.3 mg) IV: 1:10,000, 1 to 3 ml (0.1 to 0.3 mg) C ontrast Induced N ephropathy (CIN): Define CIN? The stone age (“historic”) criteria is an absolute increase in the serum creatinine by 0.5 mg/dL. There are more modem definitions that require an absolute increase of up to 2.0 mg/dL, or a percentage of change in the baseline serum creatinine of 25 to 50 percent - depending on the study. The reality is CIN is way way way over diagnosed, and there are at least 2 large studies showing that most things that are called “CIN” are just normal variation in Cr that occur in patients who are sick in the hospital. How often does CIN occur? Real answer is almost never. If asked on the exam, I would say something like less than 10% even with moderate kidney disease. When does CIN occur? The typical course is a spike in Cr within 24 hours of getting contrast, peaking around day 4, then returning to baseline at day 10. When should you check a Cr? * Age >60; * History of renal disease (dialysis, renal transplant, renal cancer) * Solitary Kidney * Bad Hypertension (that requires medication) Diabetes Mellitus * Metformin 408

What is the deal with metformin? The trick is metformin is NOT a risk for developing CIN. However, if you do develop renal failure while on metformin you could get a lactic acidosis. What do you do if you are on metformin? The typical recommendation is to stop the drug at the time of the study and hold it for 48 hours. If they didn’t develop CIN - they can restart after 48hours. How Does One Prevent the Plague o f CIN? There are several techniques all of which are bogus. However, for the purpose of the exam, intravenous hydration, preferably with isotonic fluids such as 0.9% saline or Lactated Ringer’s, at 100 ml/hr for 6-12 hour before contrast administration and 4-12 hours after contrast administration is the best. The reason it “works” is that it artificially lowers the Cr on the labs. Gadolinium Contrast agents: Gd is easier than iodine contrast agents, with regard to necessary trivia. Just remember these things: - Don’t give Gd to patients with a GFR < 30 or with acute kidney injury. - Check a GFR (within 6 weeks is adequate) with patients with renal disease, transplant, over the age of 60, diabetes, or a history of hypertension. CPR: They have changed the CPR rules Again. Don’t worry I’m sure the changes are c\ idence based. Testable Trivia: • Current Sequence: (1) Chest Compressions, (2) Airways, (3) Breathing. The American Heart Association believes it’s critical to survival to begin circulating un­ oxygenated blood first. # Compression rate is 100/min, at 2 inches depth (to the beat of the disco song “Staying Alive”). * Ratio is always 30:2 with one exception; the two person pediatric which is 15:2 409

PART 3: MOSTATISTICS V S * 412

There is just no escape from this crap. It has been on every major certifying / board exam you’ve taken, and will be on every one to follow. The Normal Distribution: This will look familiar as it is how every “good test” will turn out. Naturally occurring phenomena will distribute as a bell-shaped “normal” or “Gaussian Distribution.” Data can be “skewed” to one side or the other. The “negative” or “positive” skewing is described by which side the tail is on. The relationship to the mean is the most likely testable trivia. A negative skew has the mean after the tail. A positive skew has the mean before the tail. 413

Precision and A ccuracy: Precision - This is the immunity to variation. “The dart hits the same spot every time.” A wider confidence interval = a less precise study. Accuracy (unbiased) - This is the immunity to systematic error or bias. “The dart hits the center.” W hat is this “S ta tis tica l S ignificance” ? You often hear people say “the study was significant, with a p value < 0.05.” What the hell does that mean anyway? Basically, they are saying that the result is unlikely to have occurred by chance. You can flip a coin that lands on heads 50 times in a row, it’s just not very likely to occur by chance. If you say the p value is < 0.05, then you are saying the likelihood it occurred by chance is less than 5%. In other words, the investigator is 95% sure the result did NOT occur by chance. C orrelation and Causality: Just because two things seem to occur together (correlation), doesn’t mean one is causing the other. The example I like to use is ice cream sales and death by drowning. You will find that the more ice cream is sold, the more people die in swimming pools and the ocean. Why is ice cream so deadly? Should we ban it? The people who believe the MMR vaccine causes autism probably think we should, but they don’t understand the difference between correlation and causality. When do people buy ice cream ? - The Summer When do people go swimming ? - The Summer Warm weather is actually the reason these things go up together, they are not actually causing each other. You can make a similar graph comparing global |ce Cream Sales temperature rising with a decrease in the number of pirates. 414

Errors People will talk about error as either “type 1” or “type 2.” Type 1 Error: The is a false positive. You called the CXR back for a “nodule” but it turned out to be a peanut caught in one of the patient’s many fat folds. Alternatively you could think about this as a fire alarm. A type one error is when the fire alarm has gone off, but there is no fire. A false positive. Type 2 Error: This is a false negative. You read the CXR as negative because you thought the 7 cm mass was just a “funny-looking rib shadow.” Or using our fire alarm example you could think about this as - the house is on fire, but the alarm does NOT go off. Obviously this is the worst kind of error. Ways to Remember this: Type 2 Error will cost you “twice” as much when you get sued - remember that it’s the bad one. The missed cancer, or the missed fire. Type 2 Error is a false negative or a “false-false,” so “two” false for two. • Type I: "Ifalsely think hypothesis is true" (one false) • Type II: "I falsely think hypothesis is false" (two falses) 415

S ta tis tica l Epidem iology Incidence: The number of NEW cases occurring in a particular time period. Number of NEW cases of disease Incidence Rate = ------------------------------------ Per unit time Total Number of People at Risk Prevalence: The number of cases of a certain disease in a particular moment in time. Number of cases of disease _ . _ . At a particular time Prevalence Rate = ------------------------------------ Total Number of People at Risk Sensitivity & Specificity Both sensitivity and specificity measure validity (the ability to detect people with or without disease). • True Positive (TP) = Test shows cancer, Patient has cancer • False Positive (FP) = Test shows cancer, Patient does NOT have cancer. * Same as a Type I error • True Negative (TN) = Test shows NO cancer, Patient does NOT have cancer. • False Negative (FN) = Test shows NO cancer, Patient has cancer. * Same as a Type II error. 416

Disease + Test I want to discourage you from using the A, B, C, D method for doing statistical problems. This can get you in hot water if the question writer flips the axis of the square (which they love to do). It’s just better to understand what you are actually measuring. Sensitivity = This is the ability o f the test to detect true disease. In other words, the amount of true disease detected with the test / total people with the disease. True Positive / True Positive + False Negatives. Specificity = This is the ability o f the test to detect people free o f disease. In other words, the amount of disease free people called negative by the test / total number of disease free people. True Negative / True Negative + False Positives A very specific test rules in disease. (SPIN ; SPecificity rules IN) 417

Accuracy = Think about this as how often the test was right: True Positive + True Negative / TP + FP + TN + FN Positive Predictive Value: This is the likelihood that a person with a positive test actually has the disease. True Positive / True Positive + False Positives Negative Predictive Value: This is the likelihood that a person with a negative test actually is disease free. True Negative / True Negative + False Negative PPV and NPV are influenced by the prevalence of disease. If we test in a high prevalence setting, it is more likely that a persons with a positive test result will truly have the disease, compared to the situation where we would test a population with low prevalence. In other words, if you see a spiculated nodule on the Chest CT of a 70 year old smoker it’s probably a cancer (true positive more likely in a patient population with high prevalence). High Prevalence = High True Positive This is why you should only screen patients with a high prevalence of a disease. Think about all those criteria to get low dose CT screening for lung cancer (older, lots of pack years, etc...). The big issue is the opposite is also true. Low prevalence populations will have higher false negatives. Validity - Is the test doing what it claims it does? An x-ray is a pretty valid test for looking for a fracture (it has the ability to show who has a fracture and who does not). Important qualities of a valid test are that it is highly specific and highly sensitive. Validity is similar to accuracy. Power - Type II errors are bad (there is a fire, but the alarm doesn’t go off). The ability to prevent this from happening is called “power.” By convention, a study needs a power of 0.8 (80% chance of the fire alarm going off for a fire). The most important thing to remember is that the larger the sample size the greater the power. Larger protests have more power. Sensitivity and specificity are characteristic qualities of the test. The population does not affect them. 418

Risk: Absolute Risk: The more common a disease is, the higher the risk of catching it. Most people will say that the incidence of a disease is the most important risk factor. That is why epidemiologists defined “absolute risk” as essentially the same thing as disease incidence. The incidence o f disease is 1:100, then the absolute risk is 1:100. Relative Risk: Ever wondered how many times the guy with multi-drug resistant TB can cough on you on your plane ride to Chicago (or Tucson) before your PPD converts? This is the best way to think about relative risk - exposure to risk factors increases the risk of getting the disease. RR = Incidence of disease among persons exposed to risk factor Incidence of disease among people who did NOT get exposed to risk factor. Absolute Risk Reduction: The general way to think about this is the difference between the people who did not get the drug (control group event rate) and the people who did get the drug (experimental group event rate). This is the inverse of number needed to treat. ARR = (Control Event Rate) - (Experimental Event Rate) Number Needed to Treat: How many drug addicts do you need to put in rehab before you actually get one clean? The math requires knowing the absolute risk reduction. NNT = 1/Absolute Risk Reduction. Attributable Risk: How much lung cancer is attributable to smoking? AR = (Incidence of disease in exposed) - (Incidence of disease in those NOT exposed.) 419

Odds Ratio: The absolute risk calculations require a prospective study. Odds ratio can be done using a retrospective study. The basic idea is “what are the odds that a disease will develop in an exposed person?” This is how to work it out: Number of Cases WITH Exposure Odds of exposure in case = Number of Cases WITHOUT Exposure Number of Controls WITH Exposure Odds of exposure in controls = Number of Controls WITHOUT Exposure Odds of Exposures in CASES Odds Ratio = ____________________________ Odds of Exposures in CONTROLS Cases WITH Exposure x Controls WITHOUT Exposure Odds Ratio= F Controls WITH Exposure x Cases WITHOUT Exposure 420

Specific Factors Affecting the Spatial Resolution of CR • Laser Spot Size: Smaller = Better • Phosphor Pate Density / Thickness: More Thick = More Light Spreading = Less Resolution • Sampling Frequency (Rate of Light Sampling): Increasing the sampling frequency results in a smaller pixel pitch with improves the spatial resolution. • Imaging Plate: You are supposed to use the SMALLEST plate size reasonable for the anatomic area of interest. The reason is that for fixed matrix size CR systems using a smaller plate for a given field of view improves your spatial resolution. • Increasing x-rays will NOT improve Maximum spatial resolution Specific Factors Affecting the Spatial Resolution of DR • Spatial Resolution for DR is Superior to CR because the pixel detector is built into the DR flat panel - • Direct systems that avoid lateral dispersion of light have better spatial resolution • Spatial Resolution for Flat Panel Detectors is limited to the DEL (detector element); smaller detector elements = better spatial resolution. Direct Conversion Indirect Conversion Directly converts x-rays to electrical signal X-Ray -> Light -> Electrical Signal Detector material is amorphous selenium Phospor material is usually thallium doped cesium iodine Signal does not “laterally disperse”, as the applied voltage separates the electrons and holes made by x-rays Light can scatter (worse with thicker crystal), better if columnar structure is used. Fill Factor is high (near 100%) Moderate fill factor (depends on size of pixel) Higher Detector Quantum Efficiency (DQE) Moderate Detector Quantum Efficiency (DQE) 428

Pulsed Fluoro • Conventional fluoro = long very low continuous mA. • Pulse fluoro is pulsed (NOT continuous mA but instead pulse of higher mA). • Pulse Fluoro is good for moving patients (Wiggling Babies) - Gives you sharper images with less motion blur • Pulsed Fluoro can reduce dose - when the frame rate is below 30 frame /second • People always use a drop of 30 to 15 frames per second as an example because that equals a dose reduction of 30%. Math to get there isn’t important, just understand it’s not a direct 1:1 thing. 50% reduction in pulse rate — 30 % reduction in dose. • Be careful how the question is worded - because a lower frame rate will have more mA per individual pulse - but the overall mAs will be decreased relative to regular fluoro below 30 frames per second. 432

W in d o w in g - T riv ia S u m m ary Window Level (or Center) Thing you change for “Brightness” This is the midpoint of the gray scale display (the “center”). You want your level at the attenuation of the thing you are interested in. For example, if you are interested in bone - you want a high level. Widows Width Thing you change for Contrast This is selected based on what you are comparing. If you are comparing things with very different densities you want a wide width. If you are comparing things with very similar densities (example white and gray matter), you want a very narrow window width. Above the upper limit of the width everything will look white. Below the lower limit of the width everything will look black. A narrow (decreased) window width Increases Contrast. A wider (increased) window width Decreases Contrast. WL - “Brain” ? W 80, L +40 WL - “Stroke” ? W 30, L+30 WL - “Lung” ? W 1500, L - 400 WL - “Abdomen” ? W 400, L + 50 WL - “Bone” ? W 1600, L +500 435

Doppler Ultrasound - Trivia Ideal Doppler Angle ? The angle should be between 30- 60. Theoretically the best angle is zero. Doppler Angle of 90 Degrees will ? Look like no flow If you are looking for slow flow, you should ? • Use a low pulse repetition frequency (PRF) • Use Power Doppler Power Doppler does Not depend on ? The Doppler Angle Power Doppler does Not provide information on ? The Direction of Flow Power Doppler will Not demonstrate this artifact ? Aliasing (both color and spectral can). Aliasing Artifact occurs when ? the doppler shift is greater than a threshold called the “Nyquist frequency” Aliasing Artifact Can be reduced by ? • Increasing the scale • Increasing the Pulse Repetition Frequency (which will increase your Nyquist) • using a lower frequency transducer or using a doppler angle closer to 90 (increasing the angle) (which will reduce the doppler shift) Artifacts from Multiple Echoes Reverberation Two parallel highly reflective surfaces - Multiple equidistantly spaced linear reflections. Comet Tail Two parallel highly reflective surfaces - closer together (< 1/2 SPL) Triangle (comet) shaped Ring Down Artifact Fluid trapped between a tetrahedron of air bubbles Parallel band extending posterior to a collection of gas Mirror Image Trapped behind a strong reflector This is almost always shown with the liver on lung. 443

Image Size = - 1:1- Equal to Patient “The work horse ” Pinhole Distance Sensitive - amount of magnification decreases as object gets farther from the collimator. If the object is as far from the pinhole as the pinhole is the camera crystal there will be zero magnification. The FOV increases with distance. D evice T riv ia Personal Dosimeters “Pocket Ionization Detector” - uses a miniature ionization chamber. They give you real-time estimated dose, but must be charged and zero’d prior to use. These arc not used anymore - which makes them high yield. “SolidState Dosimeter” - Accumulated dose or rate can be read real time with LCD display. “Film Badge” - Uses a thin metallic filter with a radiosensitive film. The degree of darkening (relative optic density) corresponds with dose. They can be damaged by temperature, humidity, etc... “Optically Stimulated Dosimeter” - Replaced the film badge. Chips / Strips are placed under a filter. “Thermo-luminescent Dosimeter” - The ring badge. Should be worn under a glove, with the label facing the palm (target). Survey Meters G-M and Ionization Detectors - discussed on prior page Well Counter Basically a small gamma camera, with one PMT. Susceptible to “dead time” at counts over 5000 per second. Good for urine and blood samples. Good for “wipe test” samples. Dose Calibration & Automated Dose Injection Systems Used to measure radiopharmaceuticals. Thyroid Uptake Probe Compares counts from region over the thyroid to a calibrated capsule of the same radionuclide. The probe is a cylindrical scintillator detector attached to a PMT. A positioning guide keeps the distance constant. A Chi-Squared test can be used to evaluate the reliability of consistent operation. Intra-operative Probes Used for lymphoscintigraphy 449

Dose U nits Exposure (C/kg) Charge (C) in the air created divided by the mass (kg) of that air. Absorbed Dose (Gy) 1 Gy = 100 rads Energy deposited per kilogram of material (J/kg = Gy), Organ Dose = Total energy to that organ / Total mass of the organ. Equivalent Dose (Sv) Absorbed dose of different types of radiation creates different levels of biologic damage (thus measured in Sv). A weighting factor is used to adjust the value. For example, an alpha particle can do more damage than an electron. EqD = Dose x Weight Factor Weight Factor = for x-rays and gamma rays this is 1, Weight Factor = for alpha particles it’s 20. Effective Dose (Sv) Closest measure of cancer risk, since it considers both radiation and tissue type. This takes into account whether radiation has been absorbed by the specific tissue. In other words, you are taking into account the type of radiation and the variable sensitivity of the organ / body part. Bone Marrow and Bowel are Radiosensitive (higher wT). The Brain is relatively insensitive (lower wT). If all the dose is absorbed then 1 Gy = 1 Sv. If you are dealing with organs or specific body parts you have to use a “tissue weighting” conversion factor. EfD = Equivalent Dose x Tissue Factor “wT” 455

Deterministic Effects Stochastic Effects (“Random”) Has a threshold Has NO threshold Severity is dose related Severity is NOT dose related Probability of effect increases with dose Does Not include Cancer Risk Includes heritable effects and carcinogenesis (NOT cell killing) Dose U nits - Fluoro Air Kerma (AK) (Gy/min) Measures the intensity of the x-ray beam. Absorbed dose in Air Energy transfer from the primary interaction of photons on tissue atoms. NOT the secondary production of scatter electrons which also contribute to the overall dose. Relates to Deterministic Risk Decreases with distance from the x-ray source — inverse square. Kerma-Area Product (KAP) (Gy-cm2) Accounts for both exposure and area exposed - best thought of as “the whole beam.” KAP = Dose “AK” x Cross Sectional Area Relates to Stochastic Risk Independent of the source distance. You can measure it at any point along its path and it will be the same. Collimation will DECREASE the KAP. Cumulative Air Kerma (CAK) (Gy) Total of all the AK values for individual exposures, Described at a specific interventional reference point: 15 cm toward the x-ray tube measured from the isocenter of the fluoroscopy machine. 456

Dose U nits - CT CTDI “CT Dose Index” mGy This is the radiation dose, normalized to beam width. CTDI numbers are based on phantoms. The body phantom is 32 cm in diameter. If the patient is larger (a big fat pig) than the phantom, then dose is over estimated. If the patient is smaller (peds) than the phantom, then dose is under estimated. “Weighted CTDI” mGy This is 1/3 the central CTDI + 2/3 the Peripheral CTDI (expressed in mGy) “Volume CTDI” mGy This is obtained by dividing weighted CTDI by the Pitch. 2/3 CDTIp + 1/3 CTDIc / pitch “Reference Dose ” set by the ACR at 75 percentile CTDIvoi: • 75mGy for Head, • 25 mGy for Adult Abd, • 20 mGy for Peds Abd (5 year old) DLP “Dose Length Product” mGy - cm CTDI - Vol x the length of the scan in cm. It is appropriate to add DLP from series to series. “Effective Dose”fo r CT mSv Effective Dose = k x DLP. Remember that “k” is a body part constant. 457

MR - Safety Related Trivia Zone I No Restriction This is basically outside the building. Zone II No Restriction This is the waiting room and the dressing room. This is where you can screen patients and control access to Zone 3 and 4. Zone III Restricted Room This is typically the control room, where the MRI tech does his/her thing. There should be some kind of a lock on the door between zone 2 and 3. Zone IV Restricted Room This is the actual MRI scanner room (the same room as the magnet) Quenching - Only if ? There is a fire or trapped patients or staff member trapped Prior to the quench ... Get the fuck out of the room (zone 4) Quench if code ? A code is NOT a reason to quench Who should push the Quench button ? Medical Student, Tech, Non-English Speaking Observer.... Anyone but you. 5 G Line ? It is an implanted device safety thing, not a pulling (translational force) thing Hearing Damage Trivia: • Noise - from the gradient set. • Worse with Echo Planar • Damage to the fetal ossicles is part of the theoretical (complete bullshit) reason fetal Neurostimulation ? High-bandwidth readouts and rapid gradient switching (echo-planar imaging) are the usual culprits SAR Formula ? Bo2 x Alpha2 x Duty Cycle (B = magnet strength, Alpha = Flip angle, Duty Cycle = how short your TR is). SAR Limit ? 4W/kg Doubling the TR will do what to the Duty Cycle? Double the TR - will half the Dutv Cvcle 466

Biostatistics - Trivia Summary Precision Accuracy Definition This is the immunity to variation This is the immunity to systematic error or bias Type 1 Error The is a false positive. The fire alarm has gone off, but there is no fire. Type 2 Error: Incidence Rate This is a false negative. The house is on fire, but the alarm does NOT go off. New Cases of Disease Total Number of People are Risk Prevalence Rate Sensitivity Number of Cases of Disease Total Number of People at Risk True Positive True Positive + False Negatives. *Sensitive Exams Rule Out Disease Specificity Accuracy True Negative True Negative + False Positives *Specific Exams Rule IN Disease True Positive + True Negative TP + FP + TN + FN Positive Predictive Value True Positive True Positive + False Positives Negative Predictive Value True Negative True Negative + False Negative 468
